candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM CHEUK TING (林卓廷)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Allsop NPJ

principles.principle_label: Mens rea for s.30(1)(b) disclosure offence
principles.paraphrase_en: For the offence under section 30(1)(b) of the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant knew or suspected that the investigation in question was specifically in respect of a Part II offence.

The proper construction of section 30(1)(b) requires the disclosure to convey, in substance, that the individual is subject to an investigation for an offence alleged or suspected under Part II of the Ordinance.

While statutory provisions are to be interpreted purposively, the court cannot attribute a meaning to a provision that the statutory language, understood in context and in light of its purpose, is incapable of bearing.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the primary purpose of the disclosure offence under section 30.

relationships.target_case_name: China Field Ltd v Appeal Tribunal (Buildings) (No.2)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the limits of purposive statutory interpretation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI QINGBIN (李慶彬)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Gummow NPJ

principles.principle_label: Automendacity principle inapplicable to false documents
principles.paraphrase_en: The automendacity principle, which requires a document to 'tell a lie about itself' to be considered false, applies to charges of using a forged document under the Immigration Ordinance but does not apply to charges of using a 'false' travel document under Section 42(2)(b).

principles.principle_label: Objective falsity as actus reus requirement
principles.paraphrase_en: For an offence under Section 42 of the Immigration Ordinance, the statement or document must be objectively false in fact. This objective falsity is a required element of the actus reus, regardless of whether the mens rea is based on knowledge of falsity or lack of belief in truth.

principles.principle_label: Materiality is objective, not requiring defendant's awareness
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution does not need to prove that the defendant was aware that the false particular in a document or representation was material. Materiality is determined objectively based on whether the false particular is important to immigration officers performing their duties under the Ordinance.

principles.principle_label: Inaccuracy alone does not constitute falsity
principles.paraphrase_en: Merely making a statement or using a document containing an inaccurate particular does not automatically mean it is 'false' for the purposes of Section 42 offences. The falsity must relate to a particular that is material, as defined in the context of the immigration control framework.

principles.principle_label: Date of birth as material particular
principles.paraphrase_en: A date of birth in a travel document is a material particular because it is an important matter relied upon by immigration authorities to ascertain the identity of the document holder.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Chan Kam Ching by holding that the automendacity principle applicable to forgery under the Crimes Ordinance does not apply to charges of using a false travel document under the Immigration Ordinance.
The judgment agrees with and applies the reasoning in Ma Michael that the knowledge limb of mens rea under Section 42(1)(a) implicitly requires proof of the actual falsity of the representation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WOO TIM WING
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Possession of items - inference of intent
principles.paraphrase_en: Mere possession of an item that could be used for lawful purposes is insufficient, by itself, to infer an unlawful intent. The court must consider the totality of the circumstances.
A person's presence at a location while wearing attire common to a group (e.g., demonstrators) is not, on its own, sufficient evidence to infer a specific criminal intent.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TONG WAI HUNG (湯偉雄)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Lord Sumption NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: For liability under unlawful assembly or riot offences, the defendant must be shown to have acted as part of an assembly with other participants, not merely to have engaged in disorderly conduct alone. The conduct must demonstrate a sufficient nexus with other participants to justify regarding them as acting together.

principles.principle_label: Common purpose and mutual assistance for riot
principles.paraphrase_en: An essential element of the common law offence of riot is that each participant must have the intention to mutually assist one another, by force if necessary, against any person who opposes them in the execution of their common purpose.

principles.principle_label: Physical presence requirement for principal offender
principles.paraphrase_en: For participatory public order offences where the statute defines the offence in terms of taking part in an 'assembly' or being 'assembled together', the statutory language imports a requirement of physical presence at the scene for liability as a principal offender. This statutory requirement cannot be displaced by common law doctrines.

Mere presence at the scene of an unlawful assembly or riot is generally insufficient to establish liability. There must be some intentional activity in furtherance of the unlawful assembly or riot, such as encouragement, promotion, or participation through words, signs, gestures, or wearing the badge of the rioters.

The common law doctrine of joint enterprise is excluded as a matter of statutory construction for offences that require physical presence as part of an assembly. Persons who play important roles in unlawful assemblies or riots while not physically present at the scene cannot be convicted as principal offenders under such statutory provisions.

relationships.description: The judgment follows Lam JA's approach in SJ v Leung Kwok Wah regarding the participatory requirements and 'corporate nature' of unlawful assembly offences.
The judgment applies the principle from R v Cook that mere presence in an unlawful assembly or riot is generally insufficient to found liability at common law.
The judgment cites R v Abdul Mahroof in relation to the conditions that must be met for establishing participation in a riot.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TANG PUI YEE
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Woodcock in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal from a magistrate is conducted as a rehearing on the evidence, but the appellate court will only overturn the magistrate's findings of fact or credibility assessments if those findings are plainly wrong.
The Vye direction regime, which governs how a defendant's good character should be directed to a jury, is not applicable in Hong Kong, as established by the Court of Final Appeal.

When a defendant is of good character, a judge must consider this not only regarding the defendant's propensity to commit the offence but also, where the defendant has testified or made statements, regarding their credibility.
A professional judge or magistrate is not required to articulate their entire thought process in a judgment, provided they demonstrate awareness of the relevant legal principles.
The test for dishonesty involves both an objective and a subjective limb, and a judge must consider evidence relevant to the defendant's subjective state of mind, such as intention or belief.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the Court of Final Appeal's holding that the Vye direction regime does not apply in Hong Kong.
The judgment cites this English case as the source of the 'Vye direction' which was held not to apply to Hong Kong.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NAWAZ AKMAL
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Woodcock in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For offenses involving motor vehicles, legal ownership through registration with the Transport Department is not an essential element of being considered an 'owner' under the relevant ordinances. Custody, control, and possession of the vehicle can be sufficient to establish the actus reus.

On appeal from a magistracy conviction, the court conducts a rehearing and will not disturb the magistrate's findings if they are based on cogent reasoning and are not flawed. The appeal must demonstrate that the conviction is unsafe or unsatisfactory.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG CHUNG HANG SIXTUS (梁頌恆)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Stock NPJ and Mr Justice French NPJ

principles.principle_label: Mens rea presumption in statutory construction
principles.paraphrase_en: The mental element required for a statutory offence is determined through statutory interpretation. The presumption of mens rea serves as a starting point, but this presumption can be expressly or impliedly displaced by the statute's proper construction.

principles.principle_label: Two-step approach to mens rea displacement
principles.paraphrase_en: When interpreting a statutory offence, the court must first determine whether the presumption of mens rea is displaced. If it is displaced, the court must then determine what mental element, if any, replaces the displaced mens rea requirement.

principles.principle_label: Fifth Kulemesin category for likely consequence limb
principles.paraphrase_en: The 'likely consequence' limb of the unlawful assembly offence (where conduct is likely to cause reasonable apprehension of a breach of the peace) falls within the fifth Kulemesin category, which dispenses with any mens rea requirement regarding that likely consequence.

principles.principle_label: Objective test for likely apprehension of breach of peace
principles.paraphrase_en: For determining whether conduct was likely to cause any person reasonably to fear a breach of the peace, the test is objective. The subjective intentions of the defendants do not matter.

principles.principle_label: Culpability despite absence of mens rea requirement
principles.paraphrase_en: Even when an offence falls within a category that dispenses with mens rea requirements, it may still incorporate significant blameworthiness through its built-in requirements, making the description 'strict or absolute liability' inappropriate in such contexts.

relationships.description: The judgment follows Hin Lin Yee's approach to determining when the presumption of mens rea applies and what replaces it if displaced.
The judgment applies the refined categories from Kulemesin for analyzing statutory offences and their mental element requirements.
The judgment finds the appellant's reliance on this case misplaced as it reached a different conclusion about whether the presumption of mens rea is displaced.
The judgment cites this case regarding the constitutional right of peaceful assembly.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Leung Kwok Wah
relationships.description: The lower court judge relied on this judgment in construing the likely limb as falling within the fifth Kulemesin category.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. LI KWOK WING AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Contempt protection scope for court participants
principles.paraphrase_en: The power to hold persons in criminal contempt extends to protect anyone with a duty to perform at court, including witnesses, ensuring they can go to and from court free from molestation or fear of molestation.

principles.principle_label: Mens rea for witness interference contempt
principles.paraphrase_en: For contempt in the form of witness interference or victimization, there is no requirement to prove a specific intent to interfere with the administration of justice.

principles.principle_label: Intent requirement depends on contempt form
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether specific intent or basic intent is required for contempt depends on the actual form of contempt before the court and the public policy considerations involved.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from R v Runting that contempt power protects court participants from molestation.
The judgment applies Butterworth's holding that witness interference contempt does not require proof of specific intent.
The judgment follows Wong Ho Ming's analysis that intent requirement varies based on contempt form and public policy.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM HOI YAN
judges: Before: Hon Tam J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for trafficking offences involving more than one type of dangerous drug, the court must adopt a combined approach.
There are three established tests to cross-check whether a sentence for drug trafficking is manifestly inadequate: the absurdity test, the ratio test, and the conversion test.
The court must determine which of the three cross-check tests is the most appropriate to apply based on the specific circumstances of the case.
As part of the sentencing process, the court must assess the defendant's role in the offence to determine the appropriate position within the sentencing guideline band.
When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple charges, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is just and appropriate.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing methodology and cross-check tests established in Chan Yuk Leong for cases involving multiple types of dangerous drugs.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. HUI CHI FUNG
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish liability for criminal contempt, the court must be satisfied that the defendant's conduct was intended to prejudice or interfere with the ongoing administration of justice and was inherently likely to do so.
The mere failure of a defendant to appear in court to answer bail is not, by itself, sufficient to constitute criminal contempt.
For a finding of criminal contempt, there must be additional conduct beyond bail non-appearance that amounts to an interference with the due administration of justice.
There must be a real risk that the conduct in question would undermine public confidence in the administration of justice.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHO TING KAI (曹挺楷) AND OTHERS
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Chan NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: When interpreting statutory offences, courts must determine whether the presumption of mens rea has been displaced, applying the analytical framework established in Kulemesin to identify whether the offence falls into the category of strict liability with a statutory defence.

principles.principle_label: Role of Court of Final Appeal on factual disputes
principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Final Appeal does not function as a second appellate tier to re-examine factual findings or alleged errors by lower courts that do not raise points of law of great and general importance.

An appellate court will not interfere with factual findings made at trial if there was a proper evidential basis for those findings, and it was open to the trial court to reach those conclusions on the evidence presented.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from Kulemesin to determine whether the presumption of mens rea has been displaced for the statutory offence in question.
The judgment cites Kosar Mahmoo to support the principle that the Court of Final Appeal does not act as a second appellate tier for factual disputes.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. TANG LIN LING
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Mens rea for criminal contempt (photography in court)
principles.paraphrase_en: For criminal contempt involving photography in court, the prosecution does not need to prove a specific intent to obstruct justice. It is sufficient to prove the defendant's basic intent to perform the act of taking photographs, coupled with the act itself, where the action is inherently likely to interfere with the administration of justice.

principles.principle_label: Actus reus and mens rea requirement
principles.paraphrase_en: A finding of guilt for criminal contempt requires proof of both the requisite actus reus and mens rea for the specific charge.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHIN CHIN FAI
judges: Before: Hon M Poon JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court must consider the overall sentence to ensure it is just and proportionate to the offender's total criminality, not merely the sum of individual sentences.
Committing new offenses shortly after discharge from a previous sentence for similar conduct is an aggravating factor that justifies a more severe sentence.

principles.principle_label: Steps to inhibit investigation as aggravating factor
principles.paraphrase_en: Taking deliberate steps to conceal a stolen vehicle's identity or hinder police investigation (e.g., changing plates, painting over logos) demonstrates planning and aggravates the offense.

principles.principle_label: Court of Appeal's power on renewed application
principles.paraphrase_en: If an applicant renews a refused leave application before the Court of Appeal and it lacks merit, the court may order loss of time pursuant to statutory authority.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HUNG SZE HO
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Anthony Kwok

principles.principle_label: Application of Kulemesin alternatives to indecent assault
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of indecent assault under section 122(1) of the Crimes Ordinance is not one of absolute liability. Following the framework in Kulemesin, the third alternative applies, meaning a defendant has a defence if they held an honest and reasonable belief that the complainant was aged 16 or over, even if the conduct was consensual.

principles.principle_label: Extension of principle to s.146 offence
principles.paraphrase_en: Through statutory interpretation, the offence under section 146 of the Crimes Ordinance is sufficiently similar to the section 122 offence in language, nature, subject-matter, and legislative purpose. Therefore, the reasoning in Choi Wai Lun applies, and the third Kulemesin alternative likewise governs liability for the section 146 offence.

The analysis for indecent assault offences under sections 122 and 146 differs from the analysis for the offence of unlawful sexual intercourse with a girl under 16 under section 124, which has been held to be one of absolute liability.

The recognition of intermediate bases of criminal liability (between full mens rea and absolute liability) in cases like Hin Lin Yee and Kulemesin has altered the legal landscape. Earlier decisions that concluded an offence was one of absolute liability after finding the presumption of mens rea displaced were made when only a stark choice between the two extremes was available.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the reasoning of Choi Wai Lun in applying the third Kulemesin alternative to determine the mental element for the offence under section 146.
The judgment applies the framework of five possible bases of criminal liability, particularly the third alternative, as reformulated in Kulemesin.

The judgment distinguishes the present offences from the offence in So Wai Lun, noting that decision was made before the recognition of intermediate liability and concerned a different statutory provision held to impose absolute liability.
The judgment cites Hin Lin Yee as part of the development in the law that recognized intermediate bases of criminal liability.

candidates.case_name: HIGH FASHION NEW MEDIA CORPORATION LTD (suing on behalf of itself and also in its capacity as the sole shareholder of LONGFORD INFORMATION AND TECHNOLOGY CO LTD) v. LEONG MA LI
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Chu VP and G Lam JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person subject to a court injunction can be held liable for contempt if their agent breaches the order, provided the agent was acting within the scope of their authority. The principal cannot avoid liability by denying knowledge or claiming willful ignorance of the agent's actions.

For a finding of vicarious liability for contempt based on an agency relationship, the specific allegations regarding the nature of the agency, the scope of authority, and the principal's control must be properly pleaded to allow for a fair inquiry and presentation of evidence.

A key factor in establishing an agency relationship for the purpose of attributing liability is the principal's ability to exercise control over the agent's actions concerning the subject matter of the injunction.

principles.principle_label: Fiduciary duty not automatic with company seal
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no automatic principle that an employee who receives a company seal from a company officer for company purposes thereby owes fiduciary duties to that individual officer personally.

principles.principle_label: Legal relations distinct from corporate officer
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a bank account is held in a company's name, the bank's legal relationship is with the company, not the individual officer. The use of an officer's personal seal on company documents does not necessarily create or affect a legal relationship between that officer and the bank.

relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the principle stated by Slade J regarding when a person bound by an injunction is liable for an agent's breach.

relationships.target_case_name: Crown Times International Ltd v Chan Yim Ping & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment notes that this Hong Kong case adopted the principle from Hone v Page as correct.

relationships.target_case_name: China Metal Recycling (Holdings) Limited (in compulsory liquidation) v Chun Hei Man & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment notes that this Hong Kong case also adopted the principle from Hone v Page as correct.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. C.H.P. AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Macrae Acting CJHC, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: In cases involving a continuing course of conduct or a sequence of events, it is not necessary for the mens rea to be present at the very beginning of the actus reus. It is sufficient if the defendant possessed the requisite mental state at some point during the ongoing transaction or sequence of events.

principles.principle_label: Jury direction on sequence of events
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge is not required to provide a legal definition of what constitutes a 'sequence of events' for the jury. It is appropriate to direct the jury to use their common sense to determine, as a matter of fact, whether the proven conduct formed part of a sequence of events that resulted in the victim's death.

principles.principle_label: Mens rea for murder in a sequence
principles.paraphrase_en: For a murder conviction where death results from a sequence of events, the prosecution must prove that at some point during the conduct which caused death, the defendant intended to cause the victim grievous bodily harm.

A person who has charge, custody, or care of a child can be found guilty of cruelty through acquiescence or connivance if they turn a blind eye to the child's suffering and fail to intervene, even when the injuries or risks to the child are obvious.

principles.principle_label: No requirement for Kevin Brown direction
principles.paraphrase_en: A Kevin Brown direction (regarding jury disagreement on an ingredient of the offence) is not required merely because the jury might have differed on which specific acts within a sequence of events occurred, provided they were properly directed and would have been satisfied as to each legal ingredient of the offence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the single transaction rule established in Thabo Meli, holding that mens rea can be superimposed during a continuing actus reus.
The judgment follows Fagan, which itself followed Thabo Meli, in applying the principle that mens rea need not coincide with the inception of the actus reus in a continuing transaction.
The judgment references a submission that a Nedrick direction on intent was required, but the court's reasoning addresses and implicitly rejects the necessity of such a direction in this context.

candidates.case_name: SAIF PARTNERS II L P AND ANOTHER v. JOE ZHIXIONG ZHOU
judges: Before : Hon Cheung, Chu and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Jurisdiction for service on foreign defendants
principles.paraphrase_en: The High Court's authority to allow service of originating process on a defendant outside Hong Kong is derived solely from statute, specifically the Rules of the High Court, and does not stem from any inherent jurisdiction of the court.

The court must consider whether it is permissible to bypass the specific statutory regime governing service out of the jurisdiction by using alternative procedural rules, such as those allowing for substituted service or dispensing with service.

relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the principle from Kayden Ltd that jurisdiction for service on foreign defendants is purely statutory.
The judgment notes that the principle of statutory-only jurisdiction for foreign service, followed in Kayden Ltd, originated from this Privy Council authority.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BORCHERT HOLGER TORSTEN
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A magistrate errs in law by treating witness credibility as the sole issue in a case when the legal elements of the offense, particularly mens rea, must be separately determined.

principles.principle_label: Inference of mens rea for AOABH
principles.paraphrase_en: In cases of assault occasioning actual bodily harm, the requisite mental element (mens rea) may properly be inferred from the circumstances and is not solely dependent on the victim's credibility.

principles.principle_label: Test for quashing conviction on magistrate error
principles.paraphrase_en: The test for whether a magistrate's error constitutes a material irregularity warranting the quashing of a conviction is whether it is just to allow the appeal and quash the conviction.
A material irregularity occurs when a magistrate's focus is diverted entirely to witness credibility, thereby failing to properly consider the necessary legal ingredients of the offense.

relationships.description: The judgment cites authority [3] for the test governing when a magistrate's error justifies quashing a conviction.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YIP KA YU
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and Anthea Pang JJA in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LU YANBO
judges: Before: His Honour Judge W.K. Kwok

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may allow the prosecution to amend charges by splitting an original charge into multiple separate charges.
A person who joins a conspiracy after it has already been formed is equally guilty as the original conspirators.
A conspiracy remains a single conspiracy even if some conspirators drop out and others join during its course.

To establish conspiracy, the prosecution must prove: (a) conspirators were acting in pursuance of a common criminal purpose; (b) each conspirator joined the conspiracy at some time (not necessarily the same time); (c) each conspirator participated in the conspiracy.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Sheik Abdul Rahman Bux
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles established in R v Sheik Abdul Rahman Bux regarding the elements required to prove conspiracy.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. QUINTERO BERNAL DIEGO-JHOVANNY AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Campbell‑Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The defense of duress excuses criminal liability when a person commits a crime because they were compelled to do so against their will by threats of death or serious injury, creating a deemed absence of mens rea.

principles.principle_label: Elements for duress to go to jury
principles.paraphrase_en: For the defense of duress to be put before a jury, there must be some evidence supporting all relevant elements of duress by threats. If any element lacks evidence, the defense fails from the outset.

The defense of duress is unavailable if the defendant could reasonably have been expected to take evasive action to avoid committing the crime, as the imminence of the threat is central to the defense.
The threat supporting a duress defense must be immediate or almost immediate; greater opportunity to avoid the crime undermines the defense.

A preliminary ruling on duress serves to establish an agreed understanding of the applicable legal principles to determine whether sufficient evidence exists for the defense to be put before a jury, not to decide the defense in advance.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. QUAN MENGFAN
judges: Before: HH Judge E Yip

principles.principle_label: Objective test for money laundering knowledge
principles.paraphrase_en: The test under section 25(1) of the Organized and Serious Crimes Ordinance is objective, assessing whether a reasonable person with the defendant's knowledge and circumstances would have believed the property represented proceeds of crime.

The term 'dealing' under the money laundering offence includes opening or maintaining a bank account and allowing it to be used to receive or transfer tainted funds; physical possession of the money is not required.
The prosecution may proceed wholly on admitted facts under section 65C of the Criminal Procedure Ordinance without calling witnesses.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSIM SUM KIT, ADA
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Gummow NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A statutory reverse onus provision engages the presumption of innocence when it places the burden of proof on the defendant regarding an essential element of the offence, which typically involves blameworthy conduct that constitutes the substance of the crime.

When assessing whether a reverse onus provision infringes the presumption of innocence, it is not determinative whether the ultimate fact the defendant must prove is characterized as an essential ingredient of the offence or as a matter of defence.

principles.principle_label: Legitimate aim of diminished responsibility provision
principles.paraphrase_en: A statutory provision that places the burden on an accused to prove diminished responsibility in order to reduce a murder conviction to manslaughter pursues a legitimate aim.

principles.principle_label: Rationale for reverse onus in diminished responsibility
principles.paraphrase_en: A fundamental justification for a reverse onus in the law of diminished responsibility is that the issue depends on the defendant's state of mind at the time of the offence, which is peculiarly within the defendant's knowledge.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as noting that the principles regarding reverse onus provisions and the presumption of innocence are well-established.
The judgment references this case as one of the leading decisions in Hong Kong on reverse onus provisions and the presumption of innocence.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Hung Chan Wa & Another
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this case, which found a derogation from the presumption of innocence where a reverse onus relates to a critical aspect of the offence involving blameworthy conduct.
The judgment follows the approach established in this case, particularly regarding the characterization of the ultimate fact a defendant must prove.

The judgment indicates that the approach in this case runs counter to the subsequent approach adopted by the Hong Kong courts, though it cites its reasoning on the rationale for reverse onus in diminished responsibility.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TONG YING KIT
judges: Before: Hon Toh, Anthea Pang and Wilson Chan JJ in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The actus reus of an offence under Article 24 of the National Security Law consists of organizing, planning, committing, participating in, or threatening to commit any of the activities listed in Article 24(1) to 24(5), provided that such conduct causes grave harm to society or was intended by the defendant to cause such harm.

For an offence under Article 24 of the National Security Law, the prosecution must prove that the defendant's conduct either caused grave harm to society or was intended by the defendant to cause such grave harm.

principles.principle_label: No parity of mens rea in incitement
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no legal principle requiring parity of mens rea between an inciter and the person incited. An incitement offence can be established even if the person being incited (e.g., an undercover officer) never intended to commit the substantive offence.

principles.principle_label: Incitement by publication to general public
principles.paraphrase_en: The publication and circulation of material (like a newspaper article) to the general public can constitute the offence of incitement or encouragement to commit a crime, even if it is not addressed to any specific person, provided the defendant intended to encourage or persuade any person to commit the crime.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of inciting material as a whole
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining whether material amounts to incitement, the court must look at the advertisement or publication as a whole. The overall impression and meaning conveyed by the words used must be assessed to see if readers are being persuaded or incited to commit an unlawful act.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from R v Most that incitement can be committed by publication to the general public.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MA CHUN KIT
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A jury direction on dishonesty in theft must be properly focused on the correct actus reus and require the jury to fully consider the defendant's knowledge and beliefs. A misdirection on this central issue can render a conviction unsafe.

principles.principle_label: Status of the Ghosh test in Hong Kong
principles.paraphrase_en: The Ghosh test for dishonesty, despite academic criticism, represents the law in Hong Kong. Arguments for its replacement are distinct from arguments about the interpretation of its objective limb.

A confession cannot be used to convict if it was obtained by an inducement or hope of advantage. A misdirection on this prerequisite condition constitutes a material irregularity that can render a conviction unsafe.

A Mushtaq direction (directing the jury to disregard a confession unless sure it was not obtained by oppression or other unreliable means) must be given where there is an issue about the reliability of a confession. The scope of the direction (which confession statements it applies to) must be carefully considered.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites Mo Yuk Ping to affirm that the Ghosh test represents the current law in Hong Kong.
The judgment applies the Ghosh test as the established legal standard for dishonesty in Hong Kong.
The judgment applies the principle from Mushtaq requiring a specific jury direction when the reliability of a confession is in issue.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN WAI HO
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.principle_label: Elements of attempt to obtain property by deception
principles.paraphrase_en: A conviction for attempting to obtain property by deception under s.17(1) of the Theft Ordinance and s.159G of the Crimes Ordinance requires proof of the defendant's subjective dishonesty and that the deception was operative in inducing the victim to part with property.

An appeal against conviction may be based on the grounds that the conviction was against the weight of the evidence, that evidence was improperly admitted or rejected, or that there was insufficient evidence to support the conviction.

A magistrate must properly evaluate all relevant evidence, including defense witness testimony and the defendant's contemporaneous reactions, when determining whether the defendant acted with subjective dishonesty.

principles.principle_label: Fair assessment of record of interview
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering a record of interview, the court must identify and separately assess the inculpatory and exculpatory portions, making a fair overall assessment of the evidence.

For a deception charge, the prosecution must prove that the alleged false statements were actually false and that they materially induced the victim to act, creating a reasonable doubt if either element is not established.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSE SUI HING
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The absence of specific evidence (like DNA) is not automatically determinative of guilt or innocence when it has been formally admitted as a fact in the proceedings.
A magistrate is not required to consider defenses or factual scenarios that were not properly raised or presented as part of the evidence before the court.

A defendant must clearly articulate their defense in evidence; it is insufficient to merely suggest that another person had access to the locus in quo without providing substantive evidence to support that claim.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TANG KA MAN
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Specific intent for wounding with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: For the offence of wounding with intent under section 17, the prosecution must prove the defendant had the specific intent to wound and to cause grievous bodily harm; mere recklessness or foresight of harm is insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Mens rea standard for conspiracy to wound
principles.paraphrase_en: For conspiracy to wound, the prosecution must prove the defendant had a specific intent to wound and cause serious bodily injury; general intent or recklessness is not sufficient to establish guilt.

principles.principle_label: Judge's role in assessing jury conclusion
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge may properly observe that, based on the facts and circumstances, a reasonable jury would conclude the mental element of an offence was proved, without thereby applying an incorrect standard of proof.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Chung Chi Fai as having approved the English decision in R v Belfon regarding the distinction between intention and recklessness in wounding offences.
The judgment applies the principle from R v Belfon, as endorsed in Chung Chi Fai, that recklessness and foresight cannot equate to the specific intent required for wounding.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIN LING
judges: Before: DHCJ Anthony Kwok

principles.paraphrase_en: The appropriate percentage discount for the 'no harm' factor in sentencing is fact-sensitive and depends on the specific circumstances of each case, including the nature of the substance involved.
A specific percentage discount for the 'no harm' factor awarded in a previous case is not binding on a judge in a subsequent case with different facts.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes the facts from Cheng Ling Ling, noting the substance here was only table salt and not a Part I poison.
The present judgment adopts the general sentencing approach established by the Court of Appeal in Cheng Ling Ling for determining the discount for the 'no harm' factor.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG WING YI
judges: Before: Hon Campbell‑Moffat J in Court

principles.principle_label: Judge's role in no case submission
principles.paraphrase_en: When deciding a 'no case to answer' application under section 16, the judge's role is not to determine what findings of fact they or the jury would make, but to assess what findings a jury could, may, or might potentially make based on the evidence.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of evidence in no case submission
principles.paraphrase_en: In a 'no case to answer' determination, the judge must not assess the quality or credibility of the evidence, as that would usurp the jury's function. The judge must assume the jury will accept all admissible and probative evidence.

The test for a 'no case to answer' submission is whether, assuming the jury accepts all admissible and probative evidence and receives proper judicial directions, they could, may, or might find the issue in dispute proven beyond reasonable doubt.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of primary facts and inferences
principles.paraphrase_en: Both primary facts and inferences drawn from them fall within the jury's province. The judge must consider how both could be affected by future evidence or the absence of defence evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Galbraith that primary facts and inferences are matters generally within the province of the jury.

candidates.case_name: ALLIED WELI DEVELOPMENT LTD (In Liquidation) v. CHUANG YUE-CHIEN EUGENE AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge KC Chan in Chambers (Paper Disposal)

principles.principle_label: Directors' fiduciary duties to creditors in insolvency
principles.paraphrase_en: When a company is at or near insolvency, directors owe fiduciary duties to consider the interests of creditors, and implementing transactions that deplete company assets for no consideration to the prejudice of creditors constitutes a breach of those duties.

In complex, high-value transactions, the complete absence of relevant documentation from a company and the inability of examined persons to produce it or recall details can support an adverse inference against the parties responsible.

A complaint that a pleaded case of conspiracy defrauds pleading principles and that judicial reasoning is inapplicable to demurrable pleas of fraudulent conspiracy, when based on a selective application of such principles, has no reasonable prospect of success on appeal.

principles.principle_label: Appeal prospect for shadow directorship plea
principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal against a refusal to strike out a plea of shadow directorship has no reasonable prospect of success.

relationships.target_case_name: Leung Chin Sing, Rabo v Ko Chun Hay, Kelvin
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case's observation regarding adverse inferences from missing documentation in complex transactions.

candidates.case_name: RE AN APPLICATION BY MR TIMOTHY WYNN OWEN KC
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When interpreting Article 29(4) of the National Security Law, the court must consider its relationship with other NSL provisions, including Article 62 (which establishes NSL supremacy over inconsistent local laws), Article 4 (which requires protection of human rights including freedom of expression when safeguarding national security), and Article 5 (which mandates adherence to the rule of law in national security cases).

principles.principle_label: Distinct roles of Secretary for Justice
principles.paraphrase_en: The Secretary for Justice's role as guardian of the public interest in applications for ad hoc admission of counsel is distinct from and should not be conflated with the Secretary's prosecutorial role in criminal trials.

Determining the constitutionality of legislation involves established principles like legality and proportionality, but the analysis is not simple or straightforward and may require examination of legal developments in other common law jurisdictions.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes the Secretary for Justice's role in opposing this admission application from his position in the Perry case, noting the different capacities involved.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUNG CHUN FUNG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and A Pang JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A Liberato direction, which cautions against concluding guilt merely from preferring prosecution evidence over defence evidence, is not required in every case where factual issues are disputed. It is particularly inapplicable where there is no direct conflict between live prosecution and defence witness testimony, such as when the defence calls no witnesses and the prosecution case relies on documentary or other non-witness evidence.

principles.principle_label: Sufficiency of general burden of proof directions
principles.paraphrase_en: The core principle from Liberato can be sufficiently conveyed to a jury through general directions on the prosecution's burden to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt, provided the summing-up contains nothing that contradicts that principle.
Whether a particular factual assertion (e.g., whether a defendant heard a specific utterance) is accepted is a matter of fact for the jury to determine.

Determining whether a jury dispersing during deliberations renders a trial unfair or a verdict unsafe requires a fact-sensitive analysis, considering factors such as the quality of the judge's summing-up.

A trial judge is not obliged to comment on every aspect of the evidence, such as the absence of corroboration from neutral witnesses for a particular piece of testimony, as this remains a matter for the jury's evaluation.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Liberato, finding its specific direction unnecessary as the case did not involve a direct conflict between prosecution and defence witness testimony.
The judgment applies the principle from Zhou Limei that a judge is not obliged to give a Liberato direction on every disputed factual issue.

relationships.target_case_name: Law Chung Ki & Anor v HKSAR
relationships.description: The judgment applies the CFA's principle that the Liberato principle can be sufficiently conveyed by general burden and standard of proof directions if nothing in the summing-up contradicts it.
The judgment cites Woodward & Ors for the principle that assessing the fairness of jury dispersal during deliberations requires a fact-sensitive analysis.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LUNG HON LAM AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge David Cheung

principles.paraphrase_en: A statement made by one defendant under caution cannot be used as evidence against a co-defendant, and the court should not compare out-of-court statements from different defendants to assess their truthfulness. However, parts of a defendant's cautioned statement that are favorable to a co-defendant may be used to support that co-defendant's defense.

To establish that an accused had 'reasonable grounds to believe' property was tainted, the prosecution must prove the accused had grounds for believing it was tainted, and those grounds must be objectively reasonable such that any reasonable person with the same knowledge would have held that belief.

In determining whether a defendant had reasonable grounds to believe property was tainted, the court must assess what a reasonable person, sharing the defendant's knowledge, would have been bound to believe. If the defendant does not give evidence, the court must first identify the facts or circumstances that would lead a reasonable person to that belief.

The prosecution retains the burden of proving the circumstances that show the defendant either knew or had reasonable grounds to believe property was tainted. It is insufficient for the prosecution to merely assert the property was tainted or to conclude the defendant had such knowledge or belief without evidence.

Where a bank account is held in a company's name and an individual is merely a signatory, the individual's lack of knowledge about the account's transactions, under the principle of separate corporate legal entity, does not necessarily imply anything illegitimate or suspicious that would establish they had reasonable grounds for belief.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Wai Man & Ors v HKSAR
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a defendant's out-of-court statement is not evidence against a co-defendant and courts should not compare such statements for truthfulness.
The judgment cites this case for the principle that parts of a defendant's cautioned statement favorable to a co-defendant can support that co-defendant's defense.
The judgment applies this case's interpretation of the principle from R v Myers regarding the use of favorable parts of a co-defendant's cautioned statement.
The judgment cites this case for confirming the test for 'reasonable grounds to believe' as propounded in Seng Yuet Fong v HKSAR.
The judgment notes that Carson Yeung's case confirmed the earlier decision in this case regarding the test for 'reasonable grounds to believe'.
The judgment follows the test from this case, as confirmed in Carson Yeung, for determining whether an accused had 'reasonable grounds to believe'.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FAN TING YAN, YUKI
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge Wong Sze-lai, Lily

principles.paraphrase_en: A witness's credibility and reliability can be established by considering the inherent improbability of their alleged actions, the lack of contradictions in their testimony, and their performance under cross-examination.

A court can find that documents contain false particulars based on credible witness testimony that the named individuals did not purchase, sign, or pay for the policies in question, and that the defendant knew of these falsities.

A court may find that no valid contract existed where credible evidence shows the purported parties did not intend to enter into an agreement, did not sign the documents, and did not perform their obligations (such as paying premiums).

The inherent improbability of a person's alleged conduct (e.g., purchasing expensive insurance only to let it lapse shortly after) is a logical and reasonable factor supporting a finding that the event did not occur as alleged.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SHUM HIU LUN
judges: Before: Hon Pang, M Poon & A Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Final Appeal has resolved disputes regarding the 'common purpose' element, which is a requisite for both unlawful assembly and the offence of riot.

principles.principle_label: Court's power to convict on multiple charges from single act
principles.paraphrase_en: Section 101D of the Criminal Procedure Ordinance may confer power on the court to convict on all charges arising from a single act, but assault and riot are not considered 'true alternative charges' for this purpose.

principles.principle_label: Video evidence consistency with witness testimony
principles.paraphrase_en: Video footage can provide independent evidence of events, and when a witness's testimony is completely consistent with such footage, it supports the witness's credibility and the factual findings.
If the purpose of an act was solely to attack an individual without any broader common purpose, charging riot may violate the prosecution basis and constitute improper application of riot principles.

relationships.description: The judgment follows the Court of Final Appeal's resolution of disputes regarding the 'common purpose' requirement for unlawful assembly and riot offences.
The court distinguishes this precedent as inapplicable to the present case because assault and riot are not considered 'true alternative charges'.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG MING-CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and Anthea Pang J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may infer that a person is in possession of drugs found in a vehicle if that person has been given the key to that vehicle, as this demonstrates a level of control inconsistent with the drugs belonging to another person.
A court is entitled to reject alternative, innocent explanations for a defendant's actions if those explanations are found to be inherently improbable given the surrounding circumstances.

An appellate court will not interfere with the factual findings and inferences drawn by the trial judge if the judge correctly identified the issues and was plainly entitled to draw those inferences from the evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM LAI SIN
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.paraphrase_en: A sentencing court may exercise discretion to grant a further sentence discount when a defendant commits an offense to maintain a relationship with a partner or because they were used by that partner.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing discount for efficient trial conduct
principles.paraphrase_en: A sentencing court may grant a discount to reflect the defense's conduct before and during trial, particularly where such conduct saves time and shortens the trial.

A sentencing court may exercise discretion to reduce a sentence to account for the genuine and pressing need of a defendant to care for vulnerable children, especially when they are deprived of both parents.
When imposing sentences for multiple counts, the court may order them to be served concurrently, taking into account the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate.

candidates.case_name: SAIF PARTNERS II L.P. AND ANOTHER v. JOE ZHIXIONG ZHOU
judges: Before: Hon Anthony Chan J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The standard of proof for establishing a breach of a court order in contempt proceedings is beyond all reasonable doubt.
A court order requiring an account of profits and income received personally from specified investments is limited to those specific sources and does not extend to other profits or income.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from Re Bramblebvale, as previously adopted in Hong Kong case law.

relationships.target_case_name: Bruno Arboit v Koo Siu Ying
relationships.description: The judgment applies the holding from Bruno Arboit v Koo Siu Ying, which itself applied Re Bramblebvale.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. WONG WING-YI
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP and Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The criminal liability of an accomplice under the doctrine of joint enterprise does not depend on the defendant physically performing the actus reus. Liability arises if the accomplice's actions fall within the agreed scope of the joint enterprise or were foreseen as a possible incident of it.

principles.principle_label: Prima facie case test for discharge
principles.paraphrase_en: The test for determining a no case to answer application under section 16 is whether the evidence could, may or might lead a properly directed jury to convict. The judge's concern is not with present proof but with potential future proof to the satisfaction of a jury.

principles.principle_label: Overlap between joint enterprise and accessorial liability
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrines of joint enterprise and traditional accessorial liability are distinct but may provide overlapping bases for establishing a defendant's guilt. The prosecution is not required to choose exclusively between them.

A judge has the discretion under the relevant rules to waive or remedy any non-compliance with procedural requirements for an application, provided the non-compliance is not wilful. This discretion must be exercised judicially, considering factors such as prejudice to the prosecution.

When deciding a section 16 application, a judge must consider whether there is sufficient evidence for the offence charged or for any other offence for which the accused might be convicted on that charge. The case may proceed to trial on an alternative offence even if the primary charge is not made out.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the authoritative principles on the doctrine of joint enterprise as settled by the Court of Final Appeal in Chan Kam Shing.
The judgment cites Galbraith for the principle that both primary facts and inferences from them are generally within the province of the jury when assessing a no case to answer submission.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. LAI CHEE YING
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Appeal by Case Stated vs rehearing
principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal under Section 105 of the Magistrates Ordinance (by way of Case Stated) is distinct from an appeal under Section 113 (by way of rehearing). The appellate court in a Case Stated appeal does not reassess the weight of trial evidence, including witness testimony; it is a review.

A magistrate's finding of fact constitutes an error of law (perversity) if no reasonable magistrate, properly directing themselves, could have reached that conclusion. This requires showing misdirection, misunderstanding of facts, consideration of irrelevant matters, or overlooking relevant matters.

For criminal intimidation, the relevant questions are the intention behind the threat and its effect on the recipient (or the likely effect on a person of ordinary firmness). These are determined by the specific circumstances of the persons involved at the time the words were spoken.

In assessing a threat for criminal intimidation, the court must consider whether the threat was of a kind to overcome the ordinary free will of a firm man, which is informed by whether the intended recipient was in fact alarmed.

principles.principle_label: Logical fallacy in treating denial as admission
principles.paraphrase_en: A suspect's denial or rejection of a proposition during a police interview cannot, without more, be logically turned into an admission or confession of that same proposition.

relationships.target_case_name: Lo Tong Kai v The Queen
relationships.description: The judgment applies the legal principle from Lo Tong Kai regarding the relevant questions for determining criminal intimidation.
The judgment cites Kwan Sing Kit as a comparable case where the parties had a long history of conflict, which is relevant to assessing the context of the threat.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU TING YIN
judges: Before: Hon Campbell‑Moffat J in Court

principles.principle_label: No case to answer - sufficiency of evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A no case to answer submission should succeed if the prosecution evidence, taken at its highest, is insufficient to support a conviction, either through direct evidence or through irresistible inferences that can be drawn from the evidence.

principles.principle_label: Attempt - acts more than merely preparatory
principles.paraphrase_en: For an attempt charge, the prosecution must identify acts by the defendant that go beyond mere preparation and show that the defendant had embarked upon the commission of the crime itself.

principles.principle_label: Attempt to traffic in dangerous drugs - elements
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of attempting to traffic in a dangerous drug requires proof of an intention to traffic and the performance of acts that are more than merely preparatory to the commission of the substantive trafficking offence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI KWAN CHIU
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a conviction of criminal negligence, it must be established that the defendant's actions or omissions fell below the standard of care expected of a reasonable person in those circumstances, and that this failure caused the harm.

A defendant may not be criminally liable if they held a genuine and honest belief that their actions were sufficient to ensure safety, even if that belief was mistaken, provided the belief was held in good faith.

A conviction for child neglect cannot be based solely on a single act (like removing safety equipment) without considering the totality of the circumstances and the defendant's overall assessment of risk and supervision.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ASIF AHFAQ MEHMOOD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Campbell‑Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant is tried on indictment, if the jury finds them not guilty of the specific charge, they may still convict of another offense if the allegations in the indictment, after removing unproven elements, expressly or impliedly include the particulars of that other offense within the court's jurisdiction and the defendant could immediately defend against it.

A defendant may offer to plead guilty to a lesser offense than charged, but the prosecution is not obliged to accept that plea. If the prosecution rejects it, the plea is considered a nullity or withdrawn, and the trial must proceed on the original charge as laid in the indictment.

The jury has no duty to return a verdict on a plea to a lesser offense that the prosecution has not accepted, as that plea is not properly before them. The jury's task is to consider the charge as prosecuted.

It is not appropriate for defendants to identify a charge of their own choosing, indicate a willingness to plead guilty to it, and then ask the court to place it before the jury as an alternative, especially when the prosecution does not accept that charge.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the test from Lillis for determining when a jury may convict of an alternative offense not specifically charged.
The judgment cites Maxwell in the context of discussing the duties of a trial judge regarding alternative verdicts.
The judgment cites Coutts regarding the broader question of a judge's duty to leave lesser included offenses to the jury.
The judgment notes that a quote relied upon by counsel is referenced in Coutts but not found in the original Maxwell (Nolan) report.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. QUINTERO BERNAL DIEGO-JHOVANNY
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Purpose of preliminary rulings on law
principles.paraphrase_en: A trial judge may provide a preliminary ruling on a point of law to facilitate a common understanding among counsel and enable them to advise their clients, but such a ruling should not pre-judge factual matters or decide whether a defence should be left to the jury before evidence is heard.

A judicial ruling made before trial evidence is presented should be confined to clarifying the law and should not transform into an advisory opinion on the applicability of a defence to the specific facts, as this risks improperly influencing the defendant's tactical decisions.

principles.principle_label: Defence counsel's role in seeking rulings
principles.paraphrase_en: It is unusual for defence counsel, who is privy to the defendant's instructions and evidence, to seek a preliminary ruling from the judge on the viability of a defence based on hypothetical facts before any evidence is adduced.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSIM SUM-KIT, ADA
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Diminished responsibility as complete defense to murder
principles.paraphrase_en: If the defense of diminished responsibility is proven, the accused cannot be convicted of murder. This defense only becomes relevant after the prosecution has proven all elements of murder beyond reasonable doubt.
The prosecution must prove all elements of the offence of murder beyond reasonable doubt before any defense of diminished responsibility is considered.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Hughes' explanation from R v Foye regarding when the defense of diminished responsibility arises in a murder trial.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v LEE CHUNG FAI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: HH Judge Anthony Kwok in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For the offense of conspiracy, the required mental element is the intention to be a party to an agreement to carry out an unlawful act.
A conspiracy agreement can be established once the foundational agreement is in place, even if specific terms and details require further discussion and refinement at a later stage.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Archbold for the legal principle regarding the mens rea required for the offense of conspiracy.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOW KA SHING AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court

principles.principle_label: Actus reus for unauthorized article offence
principles.paraphrase_en: The actus reus of the offence of taking an unauthorized article out of prison is established when a prisoner hands an item to another person for removal without notifying the Correctional Services Department to conduct the required security check, thereby making the item unauthorized.

principles.principle_label: Mens rea for unauthorized article offence
principles.paraphrase_en: The mens rea for the offence is established when the accused is aware that the article is unauthorized and intends for it to be carried out of prison without the knowledge of the Correctional Services Department.

principles.principle_label: Inference of knowledge from furtive conduct
principles.paraphrase_en: Knowledge that an article is unauthorized can be inferred from furtive conduct, such as secretly inserting an item into a document after an officer has left and failing to follow the standard registration procedures for handover.
A conviction requires the prosecution to prove both the actus reus and mens rea of the offence beyond a reasonable doubt.

When sentencing a prisoner for a new offence committed while serving a sentence, the court may order the new sentence to run consecutively to the existing sentence, having regard to the totality principle of sentencing.

candidates.case_name: KOT SEE FOR v. LAM MAN CHEUNG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Lam VP and Barma JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Where there is sufficient evidence for a substantive offence, adding a charge of conspiracy is generally undesirable unless it serves a distinct purpose.

The protection against self-incrimination under Section 33 of the Theft Ordinance is not automatically rendered inapplicable by the inclusion of an additional charge of conspiracy to defraud, if offences under the Theft Ordinance are the obvious charges.

A statutory provision can abrogate common law privilege against self-incrimination by necessary implication, but this requires a purposive interpretation where Parliament's intention to override the fundamental right is clear.

principles.principle_label: Separate privilege test for separate offences
principles.paraphrase_en: When multiple charges are possible, the test for privilege against self-incrimination should be applied separately to each offence, considering whether answering would create or increase the risk of proceedings for that specific offence.

principles.principle_label: Human rights development in compulsion cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal position regarding compelled disclosure has developed to place higher regard on relevant human rights considerations, moving beyond earlier authorities.

relationships.target_case_name: Pacific Harbor Advisors Pte Ltd v Winson Federal Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Pacific Harbor that adding a conspiracy charge does not automatically remove Section 33 protection when Theft Ordinance offences are the obvious charges.
The judgment follows Khan v Khan's approach of considering privilege separately for each offence and applying Section 33 protection by analogy to conspiracy charges.
The judgment notes that Khan v Khan was distinguished in Pheby v Paier, but suggests this was without consideration of relevant authorities.

relationships.target_case_name: International Management Group v Lun Sheung Mei Serina
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as holding Section 33 could not cover conspiracy and forgery charges, but notes it did not consider Khan v Khan.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that statutory provisions can abrogate common law privilege by necessary implication.

relationships.target_case_name: Johnstone v United Norwest Co-operatives Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment suggests the legal position has developed since this unreported decision, with greater regard for human rights considerations.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOY KIN YUE (蔡健瑜)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Lord Hoffmann NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: To be guilty of taking part in an unlawful assembly, a defendant must have a participatory intent; mere presence at the scene is insufficient.
An innocent passer-by caught up in an unlawful assembly is not guilty simply by being present; some intentional activity in furtherance of the assembly is required.
The intention to become part of an assembly corresponds with the actus reus of acting as part of that assembly.

principles.principle_label: No requirement for extraneous common purpose
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution does not need to establish any extraneous common purpose or mutual assistance among participants in an unlawful assembly.
It is not necessary to show a prior agreement between participants, and applying the doctrine of basic joint enterprise in this context is confusing.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies and builds upon the principles expounded in Lo Kin Man regarding participatory intent for unlawful assembly.
The judgment cites the Queensland Court of Appeal's holding that mere presence is generally insufficient for liability at common law.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Leung Kwok Wah
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that intention corresponds with the actus reus of acting as part of an assembly.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Leung Chung Hang Sixtus
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that it is unnecessary to prove a defendant intended their act to be provocative in nature.

candidates.case_name: INVEST GAIN LTD v. NOVEL GOOD LTD
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Proper notice requirement for contractual claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A party's claim under a contract may fail if proper or effective notice as required by the contract is not given, regardless of the underlying merits of the claim.
The court must interpret contractual clauses that purport to confine or limit a party's liability according to their proper construction and effect.
A court may consider the merits of a claim even after finding a procedural defect (like improper notice) that disposes of the claim, particularly where the matter may be subject to appeal.

candidates.case_name: MAK MEI LING v. DR. POON NAI YUN
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Andrew Li in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Breaches of the Rules of the District Court can render an affidavit defective and insufficient for procedural purposes.

principles.principle_label: Expert report requirements in medical negligence
principles.paraphrase_en: In medical negligence cases, the required expert medical report must address both liability and causation, and must be filed and served simultaneously with the statement of claim.
An expert report that lacks analysis connecting the defendant's treatment to the plaintiff's alleged injuries or symptoms fails to establish the necessary causal link for a medical negligence claim.

candidates.case_name: YINGGAO RESOURCES LTD AND ANOTHER v. THE HONGKONG AND SHANGHAI BANKING CORPORATION LTD
judges: Before: Master Alexander Tang in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The test for remoteness of damage in contract comprises two limbs (actual and imputed contemplation), which together express the principle that a party is only liable for losses that were, at the time of contracting, within their reasonable contemplation as a possible result of breach. Both limbs are assessed objectively.

principles.principle_label: Amendment of pleadings - bound to fail test
principles.paraphrase_en: An amendment to a pleading should only be disallowed at an early stage if the proposed claim is bound to fail. The court will not refuse an amendment merely because the losses claimed may not ultimately be recoverable as a matter of remoteness, if it cannot be concluded they must be regarded as not flowing naturally from the breach.

principles.principle_label: Cause of action - essential factual elements
principles.paraphrase_en: A 'cause of action' consists of every material fact that must be proved to entitle the plaintiff to succeed. To identify these facts, the litmus test is whether they must be pleaded to sustain the claim and avoid it being struck out. The pleadings must be reduced to their essential factual elements for analysis.

principles.principle_label: Loss as element in contract vs tort
principles.paraphrase_en: Loss is not an essential element of a cause of action for breach of contract, but it is an essential element of a cause of action in tort for breach of a duty of care.

Where loss is an essential element of a cause of action (as in tort), the essential element constituting the cause of action is that the plaintiff suffered some loss as a result of the pleaded breach, not that the plaintiff suffered a specific type or quantification of loss. Therefore, substituting or changing the pleaded losses may not necessarily introduce a new cause of action.

relationships.target_case_name: Jackson v Royal Bank of Scotland plc
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Walker's observation from this case regarding the two limbs of the remoteness test being the practical expression of a single principle.
The judgment applies the approach from this case that requires boiling pleadings down to their essential factual elements to determine the cause of action.
The judgment follows the ratio from this case that the essential element in a tort claim is suffering loss from the breach, not suffering a specific loss.

relationships.target_case_name: Steamship Mutual Underwriting Association Ltd v Trollope & Colls (City) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment notes counsel's reliance on May LJ's statement from this case regarding the test for new causes of action not being cut and dried.

candidates.case_name: KWOK CARMAN v. CHENG SAI WAH
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Phillis Loh in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Relevance of expert evidence at interlocutory stage
principles.paraphrase_en: At an interlocutory stage, the court should not preclude a party from adducing expert evidence merely because factual matters related to that evidence (such as reasons for non-compliance with treatment) are contested and will be determined at trial.
Psychiatric expert evidence can be relevant and of probative value to issues of causation and quantum in a case.

principles.principle_label: Granting leave to adduce expert evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may grant leave for a party to adduce expert evidence when it is deemed appropriate for the proper determination of the case.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites and summarizes the ruling in Wong Yin Chau where the court allowed an appeal for leave to adduce psychiatric expert evidence.

candidates.case_name: HOU HSIAO BING v. REN BAOGEN
judges: Before: Mr Recorder William Wong, SC in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The party alleging a shared common intention that qualifies or contradicts the written terms of a contract bears the burden of proving its existence on the balance of probabilities.
The court's primary role is to interpret the contract based on its objective terms and the parties' intentions as expressed therein, not to rewrite the contract for the parties.

principles.principle_label: Limits on implying terms into contracts
principles.paraphrase_en: A court cannot generally read words into a contract as part of construction; it may only ascertain the parties' common intention on a matter where the express terms are silent, and only if doing so is necessary for commercial or practical coherence.

relationships.description: The present judgment clarifies that the PolyU case does not establish a general power to read words into contracts, but was a specific instance of ascertaining common intention where the contract was silent.

candidates.case_name: CHIU KWAI YUK for herself and on behalf of members of the family of LEE CHI WAI, deceased v. LEE TAK WAH t/a HATCO EXHIBITION PRODUCTION CO AND OTHERS
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge Harold Leong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An expert witness must provide objective, unbiased opinions based on evidence, not personal feelings or emotions, which may introduce bias.
For an injury or death to be compensable under employment legislation, it must arise out of and in the course of employment, not from personal circumstances unrelated to work.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for employment-related death
principles.paraphrase_en: The claimant bears the burden of providing sufficient evidence to establish that a death arose out of employment; failure to meet this burden results in the claim not being established.

candidates.case_name: MAK MEI LING v. DR. POON NAI YUN
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Andrew Li in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.principle_label: Failure to comply with court orders
principles.paraphrase_en: A party's persistent failure to comply with court orders and procedural deadlines, despite being granted significant extensions of time, is a serious matter that can justify enforcement action by the opposing party.

A report from a treating practitioner, as opposed to an independent expert, may be insufficient to establish a claim if it fails to address key legal elements such as causation, the standard of care, and the specific injuries alleged.

A plaintiff has a duty to act diligently in progressing a claim, including taking timely steps to secure necessary expert evidence, and a significant delay in doing so can be a relevant factor in assessing procedural conduct.

principles.principle_label: Proper use of summons for enforcement
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a party fails to comply with a court order, it is proper for the opposing party to issue a summons to compel compliance and seek appropriate relief from the court.

candidates.case_name: CHAU YUEN HEUNG v. JEKCO ELEVATORS LTD
judges: Before : Hon Marlene Ng J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Duty of care for lift maintenance contractors
principles.paraphrase_en: A company that holds itself out as specializing in lift repair and maintenance owes a duty of care to all users of the lifts it maintains.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in negligence claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff carries the burden of proving their case against a defendant to establish liability for injuries suffered.

principles.principle_label: Test for relevance of discoverable documents
