principles.paraphrase_en: The test for whether documents are discoverable is whether they are relevant to the issues between the parties or the questions in the action.
Proof of a fact in litigation is not necessarily limited to direct evidence; it can be established through indirect or circumstantial evidence.

principles.principle_label: Amendment of pleadings based on new discovery
principles.paraphrase_en: A party may responsibly amend its pleadings to plead a proper case when new documents are discovered that provide a basis for such amendments, even if the documents do not provide direct proof.

relationships.target_case_name: Thorpe v Chief Constable of Greater Manchester Police
relationships.description: The judgment cites Thorpe for the proposition that matters can be 'in question' in litigation even if not explicitly identified in the pleadings.
The judgment applies the well-known Peruvian Guano test to determine the relevance of documents for discovery.

candidates.case_name: TANG HO HEI v. CHAN PO MEI
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Chu and Au JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A negligent defendant is liable for the full extent of the plaintiff's injuries, even if the plaintiff had a pre-existing susceptibility or condition that made the consequences more severe than they would have been for an ordinary person.

principles.principle_label: De minimis non curat lex inapplicability
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal principle that the law does not concern itself with trifles (de minimis) does not logically extend from property damage to personal injury; minimal damage to objects does not preclude significant injury to a person.

Statutes that deal with the same or similar subject matter (in pari materia) should be construed together as forming a single, coherent system, interpreting and reinforcing each other, even if their primary nature (e.g., penal vs. regulatory) differs.

Where expert evidence from both parties agrees that a delay in a hazardous environment could cause or worsen injury, and the plaintiff's evidence establishes such a delay occurred, a specific finding on the precise duration of the delay is not necessary to establish causation.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from CMY v Tam that a negligent defendant must take the victim as found, rejecting the argument that it is limited to physical peculiarities.

candidates.case_name: SUN MING LOK, A MINOR SUING BY HIS MOTHER AND NEXT FRIEND, HUI MAN CHONG v. CHOY WING HO AND ANOTHER
judges: Before : Hon Marlene Ng J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Standard of care for medical specialists
principles.paraphrase_en: A medical professional's conduct must be judged against the standard of the ordinary skilled person exercising and professing to have that particular special skill, not the highest possible expert skill.

The Bolam test for professional negligence only applies where the matter requires the exercise of a special skill. It does not apply to situations where giving advice or making a decision requires no special skill.

Expert evidence must be relevant and necessary to resolve pleaded issues. The court may refuse to direct expert evidence if it would lead to proliferation of unhelpful issues, lengthen the trial, and increase costs, especially where its relevance and necessity are not concretely demonstrated.

principles.principle_label: Case management decisions on expert evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not lightly interfere with a case management decision concerning expert directions made by a lower court.
cited_statutes: Rules of the High Court Order 58 rule 1

principles.paraphrase_en: An expert's evidence that amounts to no more than a personal opinion of what they would have done, rather than evidence of a responsible body of professional opinion, fails to meet the initial criteria for applying the Bolam test.

relationships.target_case_name: Ng Wing Man, a mentally incapacitated person by his wife and next friend, Ho Siu Fun v Everbest Port Services Limited
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that good reasons are needed for an appellate court to interfere with a case management decision on expert directions.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that an expert's mere personal opinion does not constitute evidence of a responsible body of professional opinion.

relationships.target_case_name: Midland Bank Trust Co Ltd and another v Hett, Stubbs & Kemp (a firm)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in support of the principle that a personal opinion from an expert is insufficient to meet the Bolam criteria.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that the Bolam test does not apply where no special skill is required for the task in question.

relationships.target_case_name: Shenzhen Futaihong Precision Industry Co Ltd v BYD Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment follows the approach recommended by the Court of Appeal in this case regarding the court's discretion to refuse expert evidence that is not relevant or necessary.

relationships.target_case_name: SUNeVision Holdings Ltd v Hong Kong Science and Technology Parks Corporation
relationships.description: The judgment notes that a submission based on Wong Hoi Fung in this case is at variance with and should not be followed over the approach in Shenzhen Futaihong.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG KAI CHUN, suing by his mother and next friend, LAM SZE MAN v. HOSPITAL AUTHORITY
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Leung in Chambers

candidates.case_name: YINGGAO RESOURCES LTD AND ANOTHER v. THE HONGKONG AND SHANGHAI BANKING CORPORATION LTD
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: When a claim in tort for breach of a duty of care arises from and is concurrent with a contractual relationship between the parties, the test for remoteness of damages is the contractual test, not the tortious test.

The test for remoteness of damages in contract consists of two limbs: first, damages arising naturally from the breach; second, damages arising from special circumstances actually known to the contract-breaker at the time of contract.

principles.principle_label: Assumption of responsibility as limiting principle
principles.paraphrase_en: The rule in Hadley v Baxendale is a rebuttable presumption about the parties' intentions, and the concept of assumption of responsibility provides a principled basis for determining whether a party is liable for particular losses, often serving as a limiting principle.

The issue of causation does not always need to be determined at trial; it can be decided on an interlocutory application (such as striking out or summary judgment) if the facts are sufficiently clear for the court to make a proper determination.

relationships.target_case_name: Wellesley Partners LLP v Withers LLP
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Wellesley that the contractual remoteness test governs concurrent tortious and contractual duties arising from the same relationship.
The judgment applies the two-limb test for contractual remoteness of damages established in Hadley v Baxendale.

relationships.target_case_name: Galoo Ltd v Bright Grahame Murray (a firm)
relationships.description: The judgment cites Galoo as an example where causation was determined on a striking out application, not at trial.

relationships.target_case_name: Mulvenna v Royal Bank of Scotland Plc
relationships.description: The judgment cites Mulvenna as another example where causation was determined on an application for summary dismissal of a claim.
The judgment cites The Achilleas as an authority where the concept of assumption of responsibility acted as a limiting principle for remoteness.

candidates.case_name: LAM KAI KWONG v. UP CREATION LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Leung in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Expert evidence admissibility - relevance to issues
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence is only admissible if it addresses specific questions of liability or causation that the court must determine, and parties should focus their expert evidence on those relevant disciplines.

Questions regarding the condition, quality, or probability of events based on degrees of factual circumstances are matters for the court to determine based on witness evidence and pleaded cases, not for expert opinion.
An employer's liability for workplace accidents may depend on the provision of appropriate protective equipment, which is a factual dispute to be determined by the court based on evidence.

candidates.case_name: CHAN KI v. GENERAL SECURITY (H.K.) LTD
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge Levy in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: CB v. COMMISSIONER OF POLICE, SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE, SECRETARY FOR SECURITY
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Gummow NPJ

principles.principle_label: Causation test for positive BOR4 obligations
principles.paraphrase_en: The correct test to determine whether the absence of specific legislation caused a breach of the government's positive duty under Article 4 of the Bill of Rights is whether enacting such legislation was 'the only effective solution' to prevent the breach, not whether its absence 'substantially contributed' to the breach.
cited_statutes: Hong Kong Bill of Rights Article 4

principles.paraphrase_en: The Hong Kong Special Administrative Region Government has a positive constitutional duty to implement practical and effective measures to protect individuals from the activities prohibited under Article 4 of the Bill of Rights, which includes forced or compulsory labour.
cited_statutes: Hong Kong Bill of Rights Article 4

principles.paraphrase_en: The proper legal test for establishing causation in any case depends on the specific context and the purpose for which the causal question is being asked.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows ZN(CFA) in holding that the government's positive duty under BOR4 is to have practical and effective protective measures in place.
The judgment applies the reasoning from ZN(CA) that the enactment of a specific criminal offence is required only if it is the only effective solution to the identified problem.

candidates.case_name: WAT TING CHEONG PAUL v. PROSPER JET LTD t/a LA KAFFA
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Eugene Yim in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court assesses the inherent probability and logic of competing witness accounts when determining factual disputes, finding an account more credible if the alternative version is improbable or illogical given the circumstances.

principles.principle_label: Causation in negligence - common sense approach
principles.paraphrase_en: The question of legal causation in negligence must be determined by applying common sense to the facts of each particular case, rather than relying solely on abstract logical or scientific theories of causation.

principles.principle_label: Timeliness of complaint as credibility factor
principles.paraphrase_en: The timing of a plaintiff's complaint and subsequent actions following an incident is relevant to assessing credibility, and a reasonable explanation for any delay may be accepted by the court.
A plaintiff's account may be strengthened by corroborating evidence such as contemporaneous photographs and early complaints to professionals, which support the version of events.

principles.principle_label: Liability determination based on factual findings
principles.paraphrase_en: Once the court makes factual findings on credibility and the sequence of events, it may proceed to determine liability for negligence based on those findings.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the common sense approach to causation in negligence established in Stapley v Gypsum Mines Ltd.

candidates.case_name: WONG YUN WA, administratrix of the estate of CHAU CHUK KAN v. SURPLUS LINK LTD trading as CHOI FOOK DELIGHT BANQUET AND CHOI FOOK LUXURY BANQUET
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Lawrence KF Ng in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For an injury to be considered 'caused by accident' under the relevant legislation, the claimant must prove that a distinct event or series of events caused the injury. The injury itself cannot be considered the accident; there must be an external causative event separate from the injury.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for accident causation
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal burden rests on the claimant to demonstrate, on a balance of probabilities, that a specific event or series of events was the cause of the personal injury suffered.

principles.principle_label: Rejection of internal failure as accident
principles.paraphrase_en: An unexpected internal physiological failure, such as a cardiac arrest or stroke, does not in itself constitute an 'accident' for the purposes of compensation legislation, absent proof of an external causative event.

When assessing whether work conditions (like high temperature or physical exertion) triggered an injury such as a stroke, the court must examine medical evidence to determine if those factors caused or substantially contributed to the onset of the injury.

relationships.target_case_name: LKK Trans Ltd v Wong Hoi Chung
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the interpretation of section 5(1) of the relevant ordinance.

relationships.target_case_name: Faulds (likely referring to Chief Adjudication Officer v Faulds)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles established in Faulds regarding the definition of 'personal injury caused by accident'.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary of State for Work and Pensions v Scullion
relationships.description: The judgment notes that the UK Court of Appeal applied the Faulds principle in this case.

relationships.target_case_name: Chiu Kwai Yuk v Ascent Exhibition Design (Hong Kong) Limited & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment finds the observations in this case helpful when considering the cause of a stroke.

relationships.target_case_name: Stewart v Wilsons and Clyde Coal Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment notes that older UK authorities like this one were rejected in later cases as treating 'accident' too broadly.

candidates.case_name: KO PUI YEE v. JONES LANG LASALLE MANAGEMENT SERVICES LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Winnie Tsui in Open Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When an appellate court identifies a factual issue that was not properly determined at trial, the appropriate course is to remit the matter to the tribunal for that specific issue to be properly decided, rather than making the factual finding itself.

principles.principle_label: Adjudicator's duty to advise parties equally
principles.paraphrase_en: An adjudicator must ensure that both parties are equally aware of procedural requirements, such as the need to adduce expert evidence. A failure to advise one party while advising the other on such matters can constitute an error.
In a negligence claim, the claimant bears the legal burden of proving the three essential elements: duty of care, breach of that duty, and causation of loss.
A factual ruling made by an adjudicator is not valid if it is based on an error, such as a failure to properly apply the burden of proof or to consider the need for evidence.

candidates.case_name: POOSAALA RAM PRASAD v. THE HONGKONG AND SHANGHAI BANKING CORPORATION LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: When considering an application to strike out a claim, the court must assume all facts pleaded by the plaintiff to be true for the purpose of that application.
Questions that depend on the resolution of disputed facts or the assessment of evidence reliability are matters to be determined at trial, not at an interlocutory stage.
The reliability or unreliability of expert evidence is an issue to be evaluated and determined during the trial, not at pre-trial interlocutory stages.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG MA YU KANG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Out-of-court statements made by one accused person are generally not admissible as evidence against another co-accused, unless the accused relying on those statements invokes a specific exception.

For a murder conviction, the jury must be directed that they can only find the necessary intention if they are sure that death or really serious bodily harm was a virtual certainty (barring unforeseen intervention) as a result of the defendant's actions, and that the defendant appreciated that fact.

principles.principle_label: Honest belief defence for indecent assault
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may have a defence to aiding and abetting an indecent assault if they held an honest but mistaken belief that the act was not indecent (e.g., because it was consensual or performed for a non-sexual purpose), provided the belief is genuinely held.

Where an assault is inherently indecent, the offender's purpose or motive is irrelevant to establishing the offence; evidence of indecent purpose is only relevant where the circumstances of the assault are merely capable of being indecent.

For the offence of indecent assault, it is neither possible nor practicable to set a fixed sentencing tariff due to the wide variety of circumstances in which the offence can occur, and comparing cases for sentencing purposes is seldom useful.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Lee Kwan Kong & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for a potential exception to the general rule that an accused's out-of-court statements are inadmissible against a co-accused.

The judgment cites this case to explain the distinction between assaults that are inherently indecent and those merely capable of being indecent, noting that the offender's purpose is irrelevant for the former.
The judgment cites this case as confirming that indecent assault remains a crime of basic intent and that R v Court did not change this position.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that no fixed sentencing tariff exists for indecent assault and that comparing cases is seldom useful.

candidates.case_name: CHINA CANDY HOLDINGS LTD v. HLB HODGSON IMPEY CHENG LTD
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Damage to listing status as asset
principles.paraphrase_en: A company's listing status constitutes a valuable asset (a chose in action), and exposure to a risk of delisting causes an immediate diminution in its value, which is actionable damage.

A purely contingent liability does not constitute actionable damage until the contingency occurs, whereas a 'damaged asset' situation involves immediate damage when a possible future liability renders an asset less valuable.
A legal principle established in a case concerning one type of loss (e.g., trading losses) is not applicable to a claim involving a fundamentally different type of loss or damage.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Sephton that a purely contingent liability does not constitute damage until the contingency occurs.

relationships.target_case_name: Wardley Australia Ltd v State of Western Australia
relationships.description: The judgment cites Wardley Australia Ltd as the authority applied by the House of Lords in the Sephton case regarding contingent liabilities.

relationships.target_case_name: Shore v Sedgwick Financial Services Ltd & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites Shore as an example of the 'damaged asset' situation where actual damage occurs when an asset is rendered less valuable.
The judgment applies the holding from this case that a company's listing status is a valuable asset as a chose in action.

relationships.target_case_name: China Shanshui Cement Group Ltd v Mi Jingtian
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that the possibility of delisting constitutes damage to a company's listing status asset.

candidates.case_name: CHAU KA HUNG v. CHUNG CHUN HON
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge Harold Leong in Court

principles.principle_label: Breach of traffic regulation and duty of care
principles.paraphrase_en: A breach of a traffic regulation, such as improper entry into a yellow box, does not by itself establish a breach of a common law duty of care that gives rise to an actionable right.

principles.principle_label: Duty of care not required for contributory negligence
principles.paraphrase_en: It is not necessary to establish a breach of a duty of care to find liability for contributory negligence.
A plaintiff must prove on the balance of probabilities that a claimed injury was caused by the accident in question.

Where an expert opinion on injury is based entirely on the plaintiff's subjective complaints, the court's assessment of the plaintiff's credibility regarding the onset of symptoms is central to determining whether causation is proven.

relationships.target_case_name: Phillips v Britannia Hygienic Laundry Co.
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a breach of a traffic regulation does not itself create a common law duty of care.

relationships.target_case_name: Lai Por v Tsang Chiu Hon and Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported case alongside Phillips for the same principle regarding breach of regulation and duty of care.

candidates.case_name: HE ZHIJUAN v. HONG YIP SERVICE COMPANY LTD
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Phillis Loh (by Paper Disposal)

candidates.case_name: WILMES, FRANZ JOSEF v. QUEEN MARY HOSPITAL
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Andrew Li in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Test for reasonable prospect of success on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: For an appeal to have a reasonable prospect of success, its prospects must be more than merely fanciful but need not be shown to be probable; it is insufficient for the appeal to be merely arguable and not fanciful.

principles.principle_label: High hurdle for appealing case management decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellant appealing a case management decision faces a very high hurdle and must demonstrate that the judge went clearly wrong, made orders that clearly involved an injustice or prevented the trial court from carrying out its task, erred in principle, or made an irrational order.

principles.principle_label: Requirements for 'interests of justice' ground for appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: For the court to find there is 'some other reason in the interests of justice' why an appeal should be heard, the appeal typically must involve a question of general principle, be a question decided for the first time, or raise a question of importance where a Court of Appeal decision would be to the public advantage.

principles.principle_label: Statutory test for leave to appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: Under section 63A(2)(a) of the District Court Ordinance, an appellant must demonstrate the appeal has a reasonable prospect of success to obtain leave to appeal.

relationships.description: The judgment cites and applies the principles stated in §59/2A/4 of the Hong Kong Civil Procedure 2022 regarding the test for a reasonable prospect of success on appeal.

candidates.case_name: YINGGAO RESOURCES LTD AND ANOTHER v. THE HONGKONG AND SHANGHAI BANKING CORPORATION LTD
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC and Hon Barma JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Remoteness of damage - Hadley v Baxendale application
principles.paraphrase_en: For damages to be recoverable for breach of contract, they must arise naturally from the breach (first limb of Hadley v Baxendale) or be within the parties' contemplation at the time of contracting based on special knowledge (second limb). The crucial question is whether the loss was within the defendant's contemplation at the time of contracting based on available information.

A defendant cannot be held to have assumed responsibility for a plaintiff's losses arising from special circumstances or agreements if there is no plea or evidence that the defendant had knowledge of those special circumstances or agreements.
The objective of an award of damages for breach of contract is to place the innocent party, so far as money can achieve, in the same position as if the contract had been performed.

principles.principle_label: Extension of time for appeal - prejudice
principles.paraphrase_en: While prejudice to a would-be respondent is a ground for refusing an extension of time to appeal, the absence of such prejudice is not, by itself, a ground for granting an extension.

principles.principle_label: Extension of time for appeal - solicitor's error
principles.paraphrase_en: A solicitor's conduct in forming a wrong view on the applicable rule for the time for appeal may be inexcusable, particularly if no explanation is provided for how that wrong view was formed.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the two-limb test from Hadley v Baxendale to determine whether the claimed damages for breach of contract were too remote.

relationships.target_case_name: Richly Bright International Ltd v De Monsa Investments Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment follows the Court of Final Appeal's explanation that both limbs of Hadley v Baxendale express a single principle of liability for contemplated damages.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Hong Kong & Yaumati Ferry Co Ltd & ANOR
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding when a solicitor's error in calculating the appeal time limit is inexcusable.

relationships.target_case_name: Galoo Ltd and Others v Bright Grahame Murray (a firm) and Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that causation does not always need to be determined at trial if facts are sufficiently clear.

relationships.target_case_name: Mulvenna v Royal Bank of Scotland Plc
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case alongside Galoo regarding when causation can be determined without a full trial.

relationships.target_case_name: National Australia Bank Ltd v Nemur Varity Pty Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment notes this Australian case was cited by the appellant to argue that the first limb of Hadley v Baxendale does not preclude recovery of damages from long-term transactions.

candidates.case_name: SEAN LEONARD v. HONG KONG HOSPITAL AUTHORITY AND OTHERS
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge Harold Leong in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The general principle is that when a party discontinues an action, that party should pay the costs of the other parties involved.

principles.principle_label: Rule for stay until costs paid
principles.paraphrase_en: The rule concerning 'Stay of subsequent action until costs paid' serves a dual purpose: it establishes the principle that the discontinuing party pays costs, and it empowers the court to stay further proceedings until those costs are paid.

principles.principle_label: Proper rule for costs order on discontinuance
principles.paraphrase_en: While leave for discontinuance is granted under one rule, the general principle governing the costs order in such circumstances is found in a different, specific rule.
The court will apply the general principle that costs should follow the event (i.e., be awarded to the successful party) unless there is a good reason to depart from it.

relationships.target_case_name: Paul Y - ITC Construction Ltd v Kin Shing Co Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment affirms the application of a principle previously established in this case.

candidates.case_name: SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION v. WONG WAI KWONG DAVID AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Barma JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Director's duty of care and skill
principles.paraphrase_en: A director has a duty to exercise reasonable care, skill, and diligence, which includes taking steps to understand the nature and purpose of transactions and documents they sign, rather than signing blank documents or documents without checking their contents.

principles.principle_label: Director's duty to act in company's best interests
principles.paraphrase_en: A director has a fiduciary duty to act in the company's best interests, which requires applying their mind to assess whether specific transactions serve those interests.

A sufficient causal connection for holding a director liable for compensation is established when breaches of both the duty of care and skill and the fiduciary duty to act in the company's best interests are found.

A loss is readily ascertainable for the purpose of a compensation order when it involves a specific sum of company funds that were misapplied through the wrongful activities of directors, as demonstrated by unchallenged evidence tracing the funds.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Libertarian for the principle that a director's duty of care and skill is indistinguishable from a common law duty of care, with the fiduciary relationship merely providing the context.

candidates.case_name: CHING KWONG FAT v. FUNG DIN CHUNG RICKIE AND ANOTHER
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge H. Au-Yeung in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for contributory negligence
principles.paraphrase_en: The defendant bears the burden of proving that the plaintiff was contributorily negligent; the court must not be satisfied that the plaintiff failed to take reasonable care for their own safety.

When assessing damages for pain, suffering, and loss of amenities (PSLA), the court should compare the nature and severity of the plaintiff's injuries with those in comparable authorities to determine an appropriate award.

principles.principle_label: Treatment of pre-existing conditions in damages
principles.paraphrase_en: Medical evidence establishing a pre-existing degenerative condition is relevant to the assessment of damages, particularly when such a condition is identified through diagnostic imaging and not disputed by opposing medical experts.

relationships.description: The court compares the plaintiff's injuries to those in authorities cited by both counsel to determine the appropriate level of damages for PSLA.

candidates.case_name: KWAN KWOK KI v. WONG TUNG HUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A witness's credibility and reliability are assessed based on the inherent probability of their testimony, the presence or absence of material inconsistencies, their demeanor in giving evidence, and the overall cogency of their account.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof on factual assertions
principles.paraphrase_en: A party making a factual assertion (such as a claim that a mortgage application ratio was abnormally high) bears the burden of adducing evidence to support that assertion; the court cannot make a finding based on unsupported submissions.
The evidence of a witness must be considered as a whole, and prior dealings with a party do not automatically render a witness non-independent or dishonest absent a demonstrated motive to lie.

principles.principle_label: Adverse inference not necessary with positive findings
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the court makes positive findings on the credibility of a claimant's evidence and finds the defendant's evidence improbable, it is not necessary to draw an adverse inference to resolve the issue.

candidates.case_name: CHAN KAM CHEUNG v. RONNIE K W CHOI & KEVIN K F LO t/a CHOI, LO & CO Certified Public Accountants (Practising) AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Le Pichon in Chambers

candidates.case_name: NG WAI TAO v. SIU PATRICK CHUN WAI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Alan Kwong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A driver is negligent if they drive at high speed, attempt to cut into another lane without observing road conditions, drive against the prescribed direction, and approach another vehicle without giving sufficient warning.

The 'but for' test is the starting point for establishing causation in negligence. The claimant must show that, but for the defendant's negligence, the injury would not have occurred. The defendant's negligence need not be the sole or last cause.

For a defendant to be liable, their negligence must be a substantial contributing cause of the plaintiff's injury on the balance of probabilities. Pre-existing conditions are not a bar if they are not a substantial cause of the post-incident condition.

principles.principle_label: No contributory negligence if response reasonable
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no contributory negligence if the plaintiff's reaction to the defendant's negligence (such as braking abruptly to avoid a collision) was reasonable and cannot be criticized.
The court is not bound by sick leave certificates issued to a claimant. They are merely one piece of evidence that must be evaluated in the context of all available evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Tam Fu Yip Fip v Sincere Engineering & Trading Company Limited
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that sick leave certificates are not binding and must be evaluated alongside all evidence.
The present judgment distinguishes this case on its facts, as it involved a claimant who was found to have concocted and exaggerated injuries.

candidates.case_name: CHENG SIU MING v. CHAN YUNG MAN
judges: Before: Master Louise Chan in Chambers (paper disposal)

candidates.case_name: MOHAMMAD, RAHEES KHAN v. MOK, SIU FAI KENNEDY
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Simon Wong in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in civil injury claims
principles.paraphrase_en: In a civil claim for personal injury, the claimant bears the burden of proving, on the balance of probabilities, that their injury was caused by the defendant's actions.
The court may assess a witness's overall credibility and reliability based on their evidence and demeanor under cross-examination.

principles.principle_label: Inference from failure to produce evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a party fails to produce a document that they would reasonably be expected to produce if it existed and supported their case, the court may infer, on the balance of probabilities, that the document does not exist and the alleged event did not occur.

A claimant can only recover damages for loss of earnings or earning capacity if they first establish, on the balance of probabilities, that they suffered a relevant injury caused by the defendant's wrongful act.

candidates.case_name: YEUNG LAI PING v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An employer's duty of care requires the conduct of a reasonable and prudent employer who takes positive thought for worker safety based on what they know or ought to know. The employer must keep reasonably abreast of developing knowledge and apply it, and is held to a higher standard if they possess greater than average knowledge of risks.

The establishment of legal causation is ultimately a matter for the trial judge to determine based on the evidence, not a matter for medical experts to decide. The judge is entitled to draw reasonable and logical inferences from expert and factual evidence to find whether a breach of duty caused the injury.

Where an injury is found to be truly indivisible, meaning there is no rational basis for an objective apportionment of causative responsibility, the court should not apportion causation for the purpose of quantifying damages. This differs from cases where an injury worsens over time due to exposures for which the defendant is only partially responsible.

For a claim to proceed, the Amended Statement of Claim must plead the material facts upon which liability is founded, such that the defendant is made aware of the factual allegations. The specific legal basis of liability (e.g., alternative grounds) is less critical than the pleading of the underlying material facts.

It is generally unfair and contrary to principle to allow a defendant to raise an unpleaded case of contributory negligence on appeal, especially when the allegations are fact-sensitive and the appellate court does not have all relevant facts before it.

relationships.target_case_name: Ocean Tramping Co Ltd v Lee Kin-kai
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that causation is a matter for the judge, not for medical experts.
The judgment applies the test from this case for determining whether an injury is single and indivisible, based on the existence of a rational basis for apportionment.

relationships.target_case_name: Flywin Co Ltd v Strong & Associates Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case to reject the raising of an unpleaded defence of contributory negligence on appeal.

candidates.case_name: THE JOINT AND SEVERAL LIQUIDATORS OF DAYS IMPEX LTD (IN LIQUIDATION) v. MAHESH NANIK DAYARAM
judges: Before: Hon Harris J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Section 48(1) of the relevant ordinance can apply to claims brought under section 275.
Section 275 grants the court a broad discretionary power to order payment of any sum it considers appropriate, which may be more or less than the original debt amount.
Orders under insolvency provisions are motivated by the policy objective of increasing funds available for distribution to creditors, which aligns with the overall purpose of the insolvency regime.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Russell LJ's holding in Cyona Distributors regarding the compensatory nature of damages and debt remedies.

candidates.case_name: INVEST GAIN LTD v. NOVEL GOOD LTD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The interpretation of contractual clauses requiring written notice of a warranty claim depends entirely on the specific wording of each individual clause; there is no single governing principle beyond examining the particular language used.

The primary approach to interpreting a contractual clause is to consider the natural and ordinary meaning of its language as the best guide, before considering commercial implications or rules about construing ambiguous clauses narrowly.

If linguistic, contextual, and purposive analysis does not clearly resolve an ambiguity in an exclusion clause, the court may prefer the narrower construction, but this is not a presumption or a rule allowing strained meanings; commercial parties are free to allocate risks as they choose.
A contractual clause requiring notice of a claim by a specified deadline can operate as a time bar and exclusion clause, excluding liability if notice is not given in compliance with its terms.

principles.principle_label: Sufficiency of notice for warranty claims
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether a bald notice of a claim (without detailed particulars) is sufficient to satisfy a contractual notification provision depends on the specific wording and context of the clause, including any related provisions that may affect its operation.

relationships.target_case_name: ROK plc (in Administration) v S Harrison Group Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that clauses requiring written notice of warranty claims are common in share purchase agreements and take many forms.

relationships.target_case_name: RWE Nukem Limited v AEA Technology plc
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that every notification clause must be interpreted based on its own individual wording.

The judgment cites this case for the principle that each notice clause must be construed according to its own terms and discusses its analysis of a bald notice being sufficient under a specific clause.

relationships.target_case_name: Nobahar-Cookson v The Hut Group Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies principles from this case regarding the interpretation of exclusion clauses, including using natural meaning as the starting point and preferring narrower constructions only if ambiguity remains after full analysis.

candidates.case_name: YOICHI KUGA v. SARONIC HOLDINGS LTD
judges: Before: Master Elizabeth Cheung in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: When enforcing a cross-undertaking in damages, the normal principles of remoteness apply. The loss must be of a type that was reasonably foreseeable by the party giving the undertaking at the time the order was made, though the specific extent need not be foreseen unless special knowledge existed.

principles.principle_label: Scope of leave to enforce undertaking
principles.paraphrase_en: A court's decision to grant leave to enforce a cross-undertaking is not confined to whether a specific defendant should be granted leave, but involves the broader discretionary question of whether the court should permit enforcement of the undertaking at all.
For a party to be liable under a cross-undertaking, the injunction or order obtained must be an effective cause of the loss suffered by the applicant seeking to enforce the undertaking.

Legal expenses incurred to determine whether proposed actions comply with an undertaking (governed by Hong Kong law) are not recoverable as enforcement costs if they relate to foreign law advice on the permissibility of those actions under that foreign system, and may instead be characterized as ordinary business operating expenses.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles stated in Gee on Commercial Injunctions regarding remoteness of damage in the context of enforcing cross-undertakings.

candidates.case_name: KAMALJIT KAUR v. FAIRWOOD FAST FOOD LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge James Kwan in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Test for conditional leave to defend
principles.paraphrase_en: The test for granting conditional leave to defend is whether the defence or counterclaim raised can be considered shadowy or lacking in substance.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of trial witness statements in interlocutory applications
principles.paraphrase_en: Witness statements filed for the main trial are generally inadmissible for use in an interlocutory application for interim payment.

principles.principle_label: Accident arising out of employment requirement
principles.paraphrase_en: For a work injury claim, the accident must arise out of the employment, not merely occur in the course of employment; an attack on an employee as a person in the street, not specially targeted due to employment circumstances, does not satisfy this requirement.

principles.principle_label: Presumption and evidence to the contrary in employment injury cases
principles.paraphrase_en: If there is evidence that the accident did not arise out of or in the course of employment, the statutory presumption does not apply, and the applicant bears the ordinary burden of proving their case.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in disputed factual applications
principles.paraphrase_en: Where facts are disputed in an application and cannot be resolved without a trial, the applicant must prove their case on the evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Asparouh Ianev Dimitrov v Dominic Tak Ming Lau
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case for the principle that the test for conditional leave to defend is whether the defence is shadowy.

relationships.target_case_name: Hollywood Palace Co Ltd v Trans-Global Hong Kong Investment Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case indirectly as the authority for the shadowy defence test referenced in Dimitrov.
The present judgment cites this Court of Appeal case where a claim was dismissed for failure to prove personal injury by accident arising out of and in the course of employment.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWAN HAU CHI, VANESSA
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a criminal charge alleges multiple particulars of breach of duty of care, the jury must be directed on the degree of unanimity required for each particular. The jury does not need to find every particular proved, but must be unanimous on which specific particular(s) they find proved.

When prosecution relies heavily on expert opinion evidence that uses evaluative language (like 'competent', 'reasonable', or 'reckless') describing the defendant's conduct, the judge must give a strong warning to the jury to guard against the risk of the expert's opinion supplanting the jury's independent evaluation of the facts.

In determining whether a reasonably competent professional would have foreseen a serious and obvious risk of death, the issue of foreseeability must be assessed at the time of the alleged breach of duty, not by considering multiple breaches collectively after the fact.

When CCTV evidence with limited coverage is admitted, the judge should give appropriate directions about its limitations and on the drawing of inferences. The footage is for the jury to evaluate like any other evidence.

principles.principle_label: Jury direction on contradictory expert evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: When expert evidence on a medical issue is presented by the prosecution, and contradictory expert evidence exists, the judge must properly direct the jury on the contradictory evidence rather than presenting only one expert's conclusion.

candidates.case_name: SANDHU HARPREET SINGH (formerly known as HARPREET-SINGH) v. LOU SAI TONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Alan Kwong in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in civil cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The plaintiff bears the burden of proving their case on the balance of probabilities, and if the cause of an incident remains in doubt, the plaintiff fails to discharge that burden.

The court is not bound by the mere issuance of sick leave certificates; they are only one piece of evidence that must be evaluated in the context of all available evidence, and the court must form its own opinion on the sick leave granted based on the totality of the evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites The Popi M regarding the principle that if the cause of an incident remains in doubt on the balance of probabilities, the plaintiff fails to discharge the burden of proof.

relationships.target_case_name: Tam Fu Yip Fip v Sincere Engineering & Trading Company Limited
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Tam Fu Yip Fip that sick leave certificates are not binding and must be evaluated alongside all evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Rai Surya Prakash v Pacific Crown Security Services Ltd & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites Rai Surya Prakash to support the principle that the court must form its own opinion on sick leave based on the totality of evidence.

candidates.case_name: ACTIN TRADING LTD v. THE INCORPORATED OWNERS OF CHIU TAT FACTORY BUILDING AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Tracy Chan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The plaintiff must prove both factual causation (that the defendant's act caused the damage) and legal causation (that the damage was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the defendant's act).

The standard of care required is what is reasonable in all the circumstances, not a standard that exceeds what can reasonably be expected. Actions suggested with hindsight do not establish a breach of the duty of care.

The plaintiff bears the burden of proving both that damage occurred and the extent of that damage with sufficient evidence. The court cannot rely on reports alone if the underlying evidence (like photographs) does not clearly show the alleged damage.
Damages claimed must be reasonably foreseeable to the defendant. A blanket percentage surcharge applied to all items is not recoverable if it is a type of loss not foreseeable by the defendant.

principles.principle_label: Proof of breach of statutory duty
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for breach of statutory duty requires the plaintiff to prove, on the evidence, that the specific statutory provision was breached in relation to the alleged defect or incident.

candidates.case_name: KWOK CARMAN v. CHENG SAI WAH
judges: Before: Master J. Chow in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must determine whether proposed expert evidence would assist the trial judge in resolving a factual dispute. If it would, the evidence is admissible in principle, but the court must then weigh the application in a second-stage exercise before granting leave to adduce it.

A plaintiff's failure to follow prescribed medical treatment may be found to be intentional, rather than stemming from the subject accident, if the evidence shows the omissions were deliberate and related to unrelated personal circumstances.
In the absence of reasons to deviate, the usual costs order applies, requiring the unsuccessful party to pay the costs of the successful party.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ISLAM JAVED IQBAL
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a defendant to be found guilty of murder or manslaughter, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant's actions caused the fatal injury. If the fatal injury might have been caused by an incident for which the defendant bears no responsibility, the defendant must be acquitted.
If the defendant was acting in self-defence during the incident that caused the fatal injury, this provides a complete defence to charges of murder and manslaughter, resulting in acquittal.
The prosecution bears the burden of disproving self-defence. If the jury finds the defendant might have been acting in self-defence during the relevant incident, the defendant is not liable.

Where multiple incidents occur, the jury must analyze causation separately for each. Liability arises only if the fatal injury was caused by an incident for which the defendant bears criminal responsibility, excluding incidents where the defendant acted in self-defence.

candidates.case_name: HUANG WEIHONG v. TAO KEE ENGINEERING COMPANY LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Leung J in Chambers

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SO CHUN MAN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peony Wong

principles.principle_label: Duty to check blind spots before moving vehicle
principles.paraphrase_en: A driver has a duty to take reasonable steps to check blind spots, including by turning their head to look through windows, before moving a large vehicle, especially in areas with pedestrians, buildings, and bus stops.

principles.principle_label: Deliberate omission as breach of duty
principles.paraphrase_en: A driver's deliberate decision not to check a critical blind spot before moving, rather than a momentary lapse of attention, constitutes a flagrant breach of the duty of care owed to pedestrians.

The prosecution must prove causation between dangerous driving and death. The defence of novus actus interveniens (a new intervening act breaking the chain of causation) may be raised, but its applicability in Hong Kong criminal law is not settled.

Even if an intervening act occurs, causation may still be established if the fatal collision was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the initial dangerous driving in the circumstances in which it did occur.

relationships.description: The judgment notes there is no Hong Kong authority applying the Girdler test on novus actus interveniens in criminal law and questions its applicability.
The judgment cites this case where the Court of Appeal found it unnecessary to decide whether to adopt the Girdler test, as the facts were distinguishable.

candidates.case_name: CHAN PAK YUEN v. LAM MAN KIT
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Leung in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A report from a treating doctor is admissible to prove factual matters including that treatment was given, the nature of that treatment, and the diagnosis and treatment plan communicated to the patient. These are considered matters of fact, even though they involve professional expertise. However, opinions expressed in such reports beyond these factual communications are not admissible as expert evidence.

In personal injury actions, a party may warn the opposing party about the risk of incurring incidental costs if they adduce evidence, such as expert reports, without the required leave or in contravention of a court order.

candidates.case_name: LAW SIN HA v. CONFIDENTIAL MATERIALS DESTRUCTION SERVICE LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Charles Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: While surveillance footage generally has limited value in assessing the genuineness of psychiatric impairments, it may be relevant where it captures specific behaviors that contradict the claimant's reported symptoms or medical records.

principles.principle_label: Causation for compensation under Employees' Compensation Ordinance
principles.paraphrase_en: For an injured employee to receive compensation for temporary incapacity, it is a precondition that the incapacity results from the injury. The statutory presumption relates to the period of necessary absence, not the cause, so the employee must still establish causation.

principles.principle_label: Treatment compliance as evidence of genuine symptoms
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant's consistent engagement with prescribed treatments (such as medication, therapy, and social work follow-ups) over an extended period can support the genuineness of their symptoms and is not merely an act to advance a claim.

principles.principle_label: Temporal and symptomatic connection for psychiatric injury
principles.paraphrase_en: The development of psychiatric symptoms shortly after an accident, where the symptoms are directly connected to the accident (e.g., nightmares, flashbacks, avoidance of the scene), can support a finding that the injury resulted from the accident, especially when there is no pre-existing condition.

Discretionary bonuses or incentives may be considered part of an employee's earnings under the Employees' Compensation Ordinance if they are of a recurring nature, as evidenced by patterns in salary records, and are linked to the employer's performance and the employee's work.

relationships.target_case_name: Ale Thak Prasad v Tsang Chin Keung & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that symptoms of PTSD are largely subjective and treating doctors often accept the patient's reported symptoms without verification.

relationships.target_case_name: Tse Lai Sing v Tung Wah Group of Hospitals
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case alongside Ale Thak Prasad regarding the subjective nature of PTSD symptoms and medical reliance on patient reports.

relationships.target_case_name: Lai Yuen Hing v Lo Chi Hung & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case, as referenced in Hong Kong Civil Procedure, for the principle that an injured employee must establish causation between injury and incapacity for compensation.

relationships.target_case_name: LKK Trans Ltd v Wong Hoi Chung
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in a comparative discussion about the statutory presumption relating to the period of absence under the Employees' Compensation Ordinance.

candidates.case_name: B K AMRIT v. G-CLADDS LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Marlene Ng J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Appellate review of case management decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not lightly interfere with a case management decision made by a master or judge, especially concerning expert evidence arrangements, unless there are good reasons to do so.

principles.principle_label: Oral expert evidence as case management decision
principles.paraphrase_en: The decision on whether to require oral expert evidence at trial is a pre-eminently case-sensitive case management matter, and limited reliance can be placed on other case authorities as each case depends on its specific context.

principles.principle_label: Test for allowing oral expert evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The guiding principle for allowing oral expert evidence at trial is whether it is necessary in the interests of justice for the proper and just resolution of the case.

Causation in a personal injury case is a matter for the judge to determine using a common-sense approach and applying the civil standard of proof (balance of probabilities) to the medical evidence; it is not a matter for medical experts to decide, as they may apply a different standard.

relationships.target_case_name: Chau Chin To Chadow v Wing Fung Financial Group Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment refers to this case as containing relevant legal principles on expert evidence, which were noted by Master Roy Yu.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheung Oi Yan Ruby v Wong Hoi Sum
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Appeal authority for the observation that resolving expert disagreements based on joint reports alone can be difficult, highlighting the potential need for oral evidence.
The judgment cites this case as an example where oral expert evidence was deemed necessary because the experts disagreed on a key issue central to determining liability.

relationships.target_case_name: Lee Kin-kai, a patient by his father and next friend Li Wah v Ocean Tramping Co Ltd t/a Ocean Tramping Workshop
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that causation is for the judge to determine using a common-sense approach and the balance of probabilities standard.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Shek Ho v Shiu Ho-chi & ors
relationships.description: The judge notes having held similar views on the legal approach to causation in this earlier case.

candidates.case_name: TAIPING TRUSTEES LTD v. BOSC INTERNATIONAL COMPANY LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of expert evidence on professional standards
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence is necessary to assist the court in determining whether a professional party has discharged its duties, as the court will be hesitant to find a breach of duty without evidence on the standards within the relevant profession.

principles.principle_label: Appellate intervention in case management decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will only intervene in a case management decision from a lower court if the decision was based on an error of principle or was clearly wrong, representing a very high threshold for appeal.

principles.principle_label: De novo hearing for appeals from masters to judges
principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal from a case management decision of a master to a judge in chambers is treated as a de novo hearing, where the judge re-hears the application afresh.

principles.principle_label: Limitation on expert evidence for factual inferences
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence should not be admitted if the question can be determined based on factual evidence and the inferences drawn from it, and where expert evidence would not be helpful.

principles.principle_label: Framing of expert questions to avoid ultimate issues
principles.paraphrase_en: Questions posed to experts should generally avoid asking them to answer the ultimate question to be decided by the court, unless it is necessary and appropriate in the specific circumstances of the case.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Kar Gee Mimi v Severn Villa Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles stated by Kwan JA regarding the high threshold for appealing case management decisions.

relationships.target_case_name: Peace Mark (Holdings) Ltd v Chau Cham Wong Patrick
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that expert questions should generally avoid answering the ultimate issue for the court.

relationships.target_case_name: Formosa Taffeta Co Ltd v Banque Indosuez
relationships.description: The judgment cites Stone J's comments on the framing of expert questions to avoid ultimate issues.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG YEE SHAN AND OTHERS v. LEUNG KIN WING
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge James Kwan in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Elements of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from it.

principles.principle_label: Two situations for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust can arise in two situations: first, where there is an express agreement, arrangement, or understanding between the parties about beneficial ownership, based on evidence of express discussions; second, where there is no such express agreement but the court infers a common intention from the parties' conduct.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of resulting trust from financial contribution
principles.paraphrase_en: A resulting trust arises where a person pays wholly or in part for property vested in another's or joint names, creating a presumption they did not intend a gift; the property is held on trust for them in shares proportionate to their contributions. This presumption is rebuttable.

principles.principle_label: Applicability of trusts to HOS flats
principles.paraphrase_en: In principle, Home Ownership Scheme flats can be subject to constructive or resulting trusts in favour of contributors to the purchase price, which serves the public interest by allowing low-income persons to benefit from contributions.

relationships.target_case_name: Primecredit Ltd v Yeung Chun Pang Barry
relationships.description: The judgment applies the two situations for common intention constructive trust identified in Primecredit.
The judgment applies the principles on resulting trusts, specifically situation A where payment for property creates a presumption of trust.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheuk Shu Yin v Yip Po Wan and another
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Hoffmann's observation that HOS flats can be subject to constructive or resulting trusts.

candidates.case_name: SIT LAI YUK v. TSE HAU TANG
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge H. Au-Yeung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a common intention constructive trust to arise, there must be a shared intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial ownership, and the claimant must have acted to their detriment in reliance on that common intention, making it unconscionable for the owner to deny the claimant's interest.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for trust claims
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a property is registered in the name of the defendant, the legal burden of proof rests on the plaintiff to establish, on the balance of probabilities, that the defendant holds the property on trust for the plaintiff.

relationships.target_case_name: Luo Xing Juan v. Estate of Hui Shui See
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the authoritative statement of law on common intention constructive trusts from the cited case.

candidates.case_name: WONG LEI AH v. LEE JIM WING
judges: Before: Hon Lam VP, Cheung and Chu JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Intention to possess from payment of rent
principles.paraphrase_en: A squatter's willingness to pay rent to the owner indicates a lack of the necessary intention to possess the land for the purpose of establishing adverse possession, as it demonstrates the squatter views their possession as deriving from the owner's lawful title.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of payments to intention to possess
principles.paraphrase_en: Payments made by a squatter, such as for building renovation, improvement funds, or maintenance, are relevant factors to be considered in the overall assessment of whether the requisite intention to possess has been established.

principles.principle_label: Timing of payments in adverse possession
principles.paraphrase_en: The timing of a squatter's payments (e.g., made many years after initial occupation) is a relevant circumstance when evaluating the squatter's intention to possess the land.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tak Yue v Kung Kwok Wai David
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that a squatter's willingness to pay rent indicates a lack of intention to possess.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM TAK CHI (譚得志)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ and Mr Justice Lam PJ

principles.principle_label: Legal certainty requirement for criminal offences
principles.paraphrase_en: A criminal offence must be defined with sufficient clarity to satisfy the constitutional requirement that restrictions on rights and freedoms must be 'prescribed by law' under Article 39 of the Basic Law.

The court must determine whether the offences under sections 9 and 10 of the Crimes Ordinance require proof of an intention to incite violence or public disorder, or whether they are constitutionally valid without such a requirement.

A question arises as to whether offences under sections 9 and 10 of the Crimes Ordinance are indictable offences that must be tried in the Court of First Instance by a judge and jury under the Magistrates Ordinance.

principles.principle_label: Proportionality and constitutionality of speech offences
principles.paraphrase_en: If a speech-related offence does not require proof of intent to incite violence or disorder, it may be challenged as a disproportionate restriction on freedom of speech or as failing to meet the requirement of being prescribed by law.

relationships.description: The judgment references this case for its summary of the approach to determining when legal certainty satisfies the 'prescribed by law' requirement.

candidates.case_name: WONG CHEE SING (OR WONG CHE SING) (by his next friend WONG TAK KWAN LILIAN) v. HUNG WAH (孔华) TRADING AS TAK HING LUNG MEDICINE CO. AND ANOTHER
judges: Before : Hon Kwan VP, Cheung and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a claim for adverse possession, a claimant must demonstrate both factual possession of the land and an intention to possess it.
Factual possession requires the alleged possessor to have dealt with the land as an occupying owner would be expected to, and to have excluded others from doing so.

principles.principle_label: Intention to possess vs. intention to own
principles.paraphrase_en: The requisite mental element for adverse possession is an intention to possess the land, not an intention to own it. A mistaken belief about one's legal title (e.g., believing oneself to be a purchaser or tenant) does not preclude a claim if the factual elements are met.

The failure of a squatter to pay rates, government rent, or deal with building orders is not, by itself, conclusive evidence against a finding of factual possession. The court must consider what the squatter actually did, not what an owner might have done.

principles.principle_label: Mistaken claim no bar to adverse possession
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no legal principle that prevents a person who mistakenly believes they are a lawful tenant or purchaser from successfully claiming title by adverse possession, provided they establish the necessary factual possession and intention to possess.

relationships.target_case_name: J A Pye (Oxford) Ltd v Graham
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority for the clarification that the required intention for adverse possession is an intention to possess, not an intention to own.
The judgment applies the test from this case for determining factual possession, namely whether the possessor dealt with the land as an occupying owner would.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheung Kwong Yuen v Sun Hui Fang
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle from this case that a mistaken belief about one's legal status does not bar a claim for adverse possession.

relationships.target_case_name: Poon Chi Hang v Lai Ho Sun
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this case that the focus should be on what the squatter actually did, not on what they failed to do (like paying outgoings).

candidates.case_name: CHAN CHUN NI v. CHAN NGAI KEUNG
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for beneficial interest
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal title to property determines the beneficial interest unless proven otherwise. The party claiming a different beneficial arrangement bears the burden of proof.

A common intention constructive trust arises from a shared understanding between the property owner and claimant about beneficial interests, coupled with the claimant's detrimental reliance on that understanding, making it unconscionable for the owner to depart from it.

When a person voluntarily pays for property vested in another's name or joint names, the law presumes they did not intend a gift. The property is held on resulting trust for the payer, proportionate to their contribution, unless rebutted by evidence of a contrary intention.

principles.principle_label: Mortgage contribution timing for resulting trust
principles.paraphrase_en: For resulting trust purposes, mortgage funds used at purchase are considered a contribution by the person obligated to repay the loan at that time. Subsequent mortgage payments by someone not originally obligated do not count as a purchase contribution.

relationships.target_case_name: Luo Xing Juan v Estate of Hui Shui See
relationships.description: The judgment cites Ribeiro PJ's analysis at §38 regarding the requirements for establishing a common intention constructive trust.
The judgment references this case in discussing the limitation that subsequent mortgage payments do not constitute a purchase contribution for resulting trust analysis.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG YIU MAN v. CHU KUEN
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kenneth Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.

A common intention agreement or arrangement for a constructive trust must be based on evidence of express discussions between the parties, even if imperfectly remembered or imprecise in terms. It typically arises prior to acquisition or, exceptionally, later.

When determining claims for a common intention constructive trust or resulting trust, the court should adopt a holistic approach rather than a piecemeal analysis of numerous factual issues, to avoid losing sight of the broader context.
The parties' initial intentions regarding beneficial ownership of property may change over time, such as where one party finances a substantial improvement that significantly alters the property.

candidates.case_name: THE CHINESE CULTURAL STUDIES ASSOCIATION formerly known as THE CHINESE CULTURAL STUDIES ASSOCIATION LTD v. CHEUNG LAI NGAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge C. K. Siu in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff may only apply for summary judgment under Order 14 rule 1 of the Rules of the District Court if the defendant has first given notice of intention to defend the action.

candidates.case_name: PANG KIT PING, the administratrix of the estate of CHAN YUK HO, deceased AND ANOTHER v. CHAN CHIN BONG DOMINIC
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Grace Chow in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The rule against granting declaratory relief in default judgments is a rule of practice, not law, and yields to the court's duty to do full justice to the plaintiff. Where a declaration is the primary relief sought, it may be necessary to grant it, but its scope must not exceed what the plaintiff is entitled to and what justice requires.

A constructive trust based on common intention requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding shared beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.

A common intention constructive trust can arise in two situations: first, where there is an agreement, arrangement, or understanding between the parties about beneficial ownership reached before acquisition (or exceptionally later), based on evidence of express discussions; second, where the court infers or imputes a common intention from the parties' conduct.

relationships.target_case_name: Primecredit Ltd v Yeung Chun Pang Barry
relationships.description: The judgment applies the analysis from Primecredit Ltd regarding the two situations in which a common intention constructive trust may arise.

candidates.case_name: CHAU KWAN LAM v. CHAU KA YEE CARIE
judges: Before: Hon Lisa Wong J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The legal owner of a property is presumed to also be the beneficial owner. A party claiming a beneficial interest contrary to the legal title bears the burden of proving that interest on the balance of probabilities.

principles.principle_label: Common intention constructive trust - fundamental principle
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of common intention constructive trust is underpinned by the fundamental equitable principle of preventing unconscionable conduct. The court must assess all elements of the doctrine holistically.
For a common intention constructive trust, the relevant intention is that which is actually and mutually held by the parties. It is not sufficient for one party to have a unilateral intention.
In determining whether a common intention exists, the court considers express discussions or agreements between the parties, or direct contributions to the purchase price.
An imperfectly remembered or imprecise agreement between parties is not an automatic barrier to the court finding that an express agreement, arrangement, or understanding existed.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Neuberger's dissenting view on the need for compelling evidence to infer a change in beneficial shares after acquisition.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that the court must look at all elements of the doctrine in the round, focusing on preventing unconscionable conduct.

relationships.target_case_name: Luo Xing Juan v Estate of Hui Shui See
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that the intention relevant for a constructive trust is the intention commonly held by the parties.

relationships.target_case_name: Paragon Finance Plc v DB Thakerar & Co
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that a common intention is required for a constructive trust.
The judgment applies the principles from this case for ascertaining the common intention of parties in a property dispute.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM TAK CHI (譚得志)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Chan NPJ

principles.principle_label: Transfer of pre-NSL sedition offences to District Court
principles.paraphrase_en: Offences under Crimes Ordinance section 10(1)(b) alleged to have been committed before the National Security Law came into effect could validly be transferred to the District Court for trial under Magistrates Ordinance section 88(1)(b).

principles.principle_label: NSL effect on trial mode for sedition offences
principles.paraphrase_en: Sedition offences under Parts I and II of the Crimes Ordinance qualify as 'offences endangering national security'. Therefore, NSL Article 41(3), which requires such offences to be tried on indictment, changed the mode of trial for Crimes Ordinance section 10(1)(b) from summary-only to indictable.

principles.principle_label: Statutory displacement of common law sedition
principles.paraphrase_en: The statutory sedition offences in Hong Kong have displaced the common law offence of sedition. The legislature intended the ordinance to replace the common law, including its requirement to prove an intention to incite violence or disorder.

principles.principle_label: No implied intent to incite violence in statutory sedition
principles.paraphrase_en: An intention to incite persons to violence is not an essential element of the statutory sedition offences under Crimes Ordinance section 9(1). The provision lists alternative forms of seditious intention, and incitement to violence in paragraph (f) is only one of them.

principles.principle_label: Principle of legality inapplicable to CO ss 9 & 10
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of legality, which protects fundamental rights from unintended abrogation by general or ambiguous statutory words, does not apply to the detailed and specific provisions of Crimes Ordinance sections 9 and 10. These provisions were consciously intended to constrain seditious speech and are not the sort of 'general words' the principle was designed to address.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the holding that sedition offences under the Crimes Ordinance qualify as offences endangering national security.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Ng Hau Yi Sidney
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Appeal Committee's explanation that sedition offences are offences endangering national security.

relationships.target_case_name: Fei Yi Ming and Lee Tsung Ying v R
relationships.description: The judgment follows the Full Court's conclusion in this case that incitement to violence is not an essential element of the statutory sedition offence.
The judgment applies the principle from this case, as endorsed in Fei Yi Ming, that incitement to violence is not an essential element of the statutory sedition offence.

relationships.target_case_name: Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago v Vijay Maharaj
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes this Privy Council decision, noting its comments on the principle of legality were obiter and made in a different constitutional context.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department, Ex p Simms
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Hoffmann's explanation of the principle of legality from this case to clarify its scope and limits.

candidates.case_name: SO CHUI WAH v. QU XUANBAI
judges: Coram: Deputy High Court Judge H. Au-Yeung in Court
