relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles established in HKSAR v Lee Yau Wing regarding the focus on culpability and the limited weight of good character in traffic offense cases.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YEUNG MING KIT
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A sentencing court has discretion to impose a reduced sentence when an offense is committed due to severe financial pressure or compelling circumstances.
When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offenses, the court must consider the overall total sentence and may order parts to run concurrently to avoid a crushing aggregate term.
When offenses occur at different locations, on different dates, and involve different victims, consecutive sentences should generally be imposed as the starting point.

relationships.target_case_name: The Queen v Yau Kwok Tung
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to establish that a 4-year sentence is at the top end of the tariff for robberies where weapons were not displayed.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that sentencing courts may reduce sentences for offenses committed under grave financial hardship or compelling circumstances.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TONG PAK-FAI, SAMMY
judges: Before: HH Judge A. J. Woodcock

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MAN CHI KWONG
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.principle_label: Sentencing principles for dangerous driving causing harm
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for offenses of dangerous driving causing grievous bodily harm or death, the court must accommodate the principles of retribution, deterrence, and denunciation to maintain public confidence in the sentencing system.

In dangerous driving causing grievous bodily harm cases, the defendant's culpability should be the dominant component in sentencing, with the outcome of the offense being an important but secondary consideration.
When the focus is on the culpability of the defendant's driving in dangerous driving offenses, a clear record and general good character carry little weight in sentencing.
The dominant sentencing principle for dangerous driving causing harm offenses is general deterrence, with principles of retribution and denunciation also being relevant.
The court must consider what constitutes a just and fair final sentence in light of the totality principle, even when multiple offenses arise from the same incident.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles of retribution, deterrence, and denunciation established in Lee Yau Wing for dangerous driving causing harm offenses.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Chan Chi Chiu Louis
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Chan Chi Chiu Louis that culpability is the dominant sentencing component in dangerous driving causing grievous bodily harm cases.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG CHI CHING CANDY v. YEUNG HON SING
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Chu JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The defence of qualified privilege is defeated by malice if the defendant knew or would have known the defamatory statements were false, indicating publication was not for a proper purpose.

Courts should be very slow to infer that a defendant was actuated by improper motives to defeat privilege, unless satisfied the defendant did not believe the statement was true or was indifferent to its truth.

Irrelevant defamatory matter incorporated in a privileged statement is a factor to be considered in deciding whether malice can be inferred, based on whether the defendant did not believe it true or knew it was irrelevant.
To find malice defeating qualified privilege, a subjective test dependent on the defendant's state of mind and intention at the time of publication must be applied.
There is a presumption of judicial integrity and impartiality; cogent evidence is required to rebut it, and allegations to the contrary should not be made lightly.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG PUI FUN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits an offence under s.18(1)(b) of the Import and Export Ordinance (Cap. 60) when they attempt to export goods that are not accurately declared in the export manifest.

A person commits an offence under s.7(1) and (3) of the Protection of Endangered Species of Animals and Plants Ordinance (Cap. 586) when they attempt to export specimens of Appendix I species without the required CITES documentation.
Section 159G of the Crimes Ordinance (Cap. 200) provides the legal basis for charging a person with an attempt to commit an offence under other ordinances.
The export of specimens of Appendix I species under the Protection of Endangered Species of Animals and Plants Ordinance requires valid documentation issued under the CITES convention.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. PUN KAM HUNG
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing guideline for s17I IO employment offenses
principles.paraphrase_en: For a basic offense under section 17I of the Immigration Ordinance involving a single employee, where the employer had full knowledge or was reckless about the illegal status, with no aggravated features and employment on a casual basis, an immediate three-month sentence after trial is appropriate.

principles.principle_label: Aggravating factor: employment for cheap labor
principles.paraphrase_en: Employing a person who cannot be lawfully employed in order to obtain cheap labor constitutes an aggravating feature of the offense, even if the primary motive is financial gain rather than direct exploitation of the worker.
When assessing whether a sentence enhancement is excessive, the percentage increase must be considered in the context of the overall totality of the sentence and the starting point, not in isolation.
A more severe sentence is justified for an employer who employs several people who cannot be lawfully employed, compared to the sentence for employing a single such person.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO KA PO
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.principle_label: Sentencing framework for dangerous driving causing death/GBH
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for the offense of dangerous driving causing death or grievous bodily harm, the court must conduct a two-stage assessment: first, evaluating the objective dangerousness of the driving, and second, evaluating the moral culpability of the driver.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Law Siu Kin
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and applies the two-stage sentencing framework established by the Court of Appeal in this case.

candidates.case_name: NATURAL DAIRY (NZ) HOLDINGS LTD (IN PROVISIONAL LIQUIDATION) v. CHEN KEEN (alias JACK CHEN) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Allegations of fraud or conspiracy must be pleaded with the highest degree of specificity and particularity, and cannot be left to be inferred from the general facts.

principles.principle_label: Henderson v Henderson abuse of process in interlocutory hearings
principles.paraphrase_en: The principles preventing the re-litigation of issues (Henderson v Henderson) apply to interlocutory hearings as well as final trials. A party should generally advance all reasonably available arguments at the first opportunity in interlocutory applications concerning similar relief, unless there is a significant change in circumstances.

A 'rolled-up plea' that alleges a person 'knew or ought reasonably to have known' is treated as a single allegation of constructive knowledge, not as alternative allegations of actual and constructive knowledge. Where dishonesty requires a plea of actual knowledge, such a rolled-up plea may be defective.

principles.principle_label: Striking out dishonest assistance based on pleading wording
principles.paraphrase_en: The inclusion of the wording 'ought reasonably to have known' in a plea of dishonest assistance is not, by itself, a sufficient basis to strike out the entire claim. The court may instead order the amendment or particularization of the pleading to clarify whether 'blind-eye' knowledge is alleged.

The intention requirement for unlawful means conspiracy is not satisfied by mere carelessness ('should have known'), but can be satisfied by recklessness, where a defendant knows his acts would likely injure the plaintiff and consciously disregards that outcome.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority for the proposition that reckless imprudence can call into question a person's honesty for the purpose of dishonest assistance.

relationships.target_case_name: Top Point Ltd v K&L Gates (A Firm)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that a 'rolled-up plea' of knowledge is treated as a single allegation of constructive knowledge.
The judgment cites this case, read with Top Point Ltd, to establish that carelessness is insufficient for the intention element in unlawful means conspiracy.

relationships.target_case_name: E D & F Man Capital Markets v Come Harvest Holdings Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English authority to support the view that recklessness can satisfy the intention requirement for unlawful means conspiracy if harm was a known necessary consequence.
The judgment refers to this case in the context of discussing the benefit or effect of a prior injunction decision.

candidates.case_name: SANTA MONICA EQUITY INC. v. CHRISTELLE FILA NZOLOLO
judges: Before: Hon Anthony Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Committal for forgery in civil proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may entertain an application for committal based on alleged forgery of a document adduced in related civil proceedings, such as an application to set aside a statutory demand.

Where a respondent has been properly served with all relevant documents pursuant to a court order for substituted service but chooses not to participate, the court may order the hearing to proceed in their absence.

principles.principle_label: Evidential burden for setting aside statutory demand
principles.paraphrase_en: A party applying to set aside a statutory demand based on an alleged debt owed to them must adduce evidence in support of that debt; a mere assertion or a single document of questionable authenticity may be insufficient.
A party may commission a report from handwriting experts to examine the authenticity of a document and its signature when its genuineness is in doubt, and such a report may be admissible as evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WRIGHT JAMES MICHAEL
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: An action is unreasonable and may constitute an offense if it is taken in circumstances where a dangerous outcome is foreseeable, particularly when the action is directed towards a target without adequate safety measures to prevent harm to areas frequented by people.
A conviction requires that the charge be proved beyond a reasonable doubt.

candidates.case_name: 張侃 V. 尚品滙(香港)國際貿易有限公司
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Gary C C Lam in Court (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A representation binding a company can be made by a person with actual authority to manage the company's business generally or in relation to the specific contract matter. In principle, an agent can make such a representation about their own authority, though this would be very rare in practice.

The purpose of section 117 of the Companies Ordinance is to balance protecting outsiders who deal with the company in good faith against protecting the company from directors acting outside their authority. The legislative intent limits its application to 'directors' in the plural.
The receipt of a loan by a company can be characterized as a ministerial act, meaning it is a routine administrative action rather than an exercise of substantive authority.

Whether an agent's knowledge is attributed to the principal depends on the context of the claim. The agent's knowledge is not attributed to the principal when the principal sues the agent for loss from the agent's fraud. However, the agent's knowledge is attributed to the principal when a third party sues the principal.

relationships.target_case_name: Moulin Global Eyecare Trading Ltd v CIR
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from Moulin Global regarding when an agent's knowledge is attributed to the principal in claims by third parties.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FUNG KA WAI ROGER
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge A N Tse Ching in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court may revise a sentence if it finds the original sentence to be manifestly inadequate and wrong in principle.
Where multiple defendants are involved in the same incident, their culpability may be assessed as identical, warranting equal sentencing treatment.
The period of driving disqualification imposed should reflect both the seriousness of the offence and the offender's previous driving record.
Driving while avoidably distracted, such as by using a mobile phone (especially hand-held), constitutes an aggravating factor in sentencing for driving offences.
The commission of other offences at the same time, such as driving while disqualified or without insurance, is an aggravating factor in sentencing.

relationships.description: The judgment notes that the principles from this case were considered in a subsequent Hong Kong appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Poon Wing Kay
relationships.description: The judgment references this case as an application of the Cooksley principles to a case involving dangerous driving and racing.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Lau Sin Ting
relationships.description: Counsel invited the court to take reference from this case for determining a sentencing starting point for dangerous driving causing death.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG KWOK LEE AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YOUNG KING CHEUNG JANUARY
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TONG KIN WAI
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K K Leung

principles.principle_label: Sentencing principles for dangerous driving causing death
principles.paraphrase_en: For the offence of dangerous driving causing death, the primary sentencing objectives are general deterrence, retribution, and denunciation, due to the serious consequences of the offense. The defendant's culpability is the central factor in determining the sentence.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Poon Wing Kay and Another
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the sentencing principles established in this case, particularly regarding the approach to sentencing for dangerous driving causing death.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Liu Kwok Chun
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority supporting the sentencing principles for dangerous driving causing death.
The judgment references this case as containing relevant holdings on the matter.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Lam Siu Tong
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Appeal case for its holdings on sentencing.

candidates.case_name: BRETON JEAN v. HK BELLAWINGS.JET LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Patrick Siu in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a statement to be defamatory, it must meet a threshold of seriousness that excludes trivial claims. The imputation must tend to injure a person's reputation in their business, employment, trade, profession, calling, or office.
The meaning conveyed by allegedly defamatory words is determined from the perspective of an ordinary, reasonable, fair-minded reader, guided by established principles.

An occasion is protected by qualified privilege where the person making the communication has a legal, social, or moral duty or interest to make it to the recipient, and the recipient has a corresponding duty or interest to receive it. There is no closed list of such occasions.

Whether an occasion is privileged is determined by applying the general principle to all the circumstances, viewed with contemporary eyes. Current social conditions inform whether the public interest requires protection of the communication in the absence of malice.

principles.principle_label: Malice to defeat qualified privilege - sequential test
principles.paraphrase_en: To determine if malice defeats a defence of qualified privilege, two sequential questions must be answered: first, whether the occasion was privileged and for what purpose (a question of law); second, whether the defendant's actual purpose in publishing was that privileged purpose (a question of fact).

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for guidance on how to determine the meaning conveyed by words to an ordinary, reasonable reader in a defamation claim.

relationships.target_case_name: Leung Chi Ching Candy v Yeung Hon Sing
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reminder from this case that a subjective test based on the defendant's state of mind and intention at the time of publication is required to find malice that defeats qualified privilege.

candidates.case_name: SAMANTHA JANE BRADLEY v. SIR ELLY KADOORIE & SONS LIMITED (FOR AND ON BEHALF OF ITSELF, ITS CURRENT AND FORMER OFFICERS, EMPLOYEES AND AGENTS, INCLUDING ITS LEGAL REPRESENTATIVES, MESSRS SIMMONS & SIMMONS)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Lam PJ, Mr Justice Bokhary NPJ, Mr Justice Chan NPJ and Lord Neuberger of Abbotsbury NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A court has jurisdiction to grant a freestanding injunction to restrain harassment, even if the applicant lacks a personal cause of action, where it is just and convenient to do so to uphold the administration of justice or prevent abuse of process.

A statutory authority (or public authority) can apply for an injunction to prevent conduct that interferes with its performance of statutory functions, and the court may grant it where just and convenient, even without the authority having a personal cause of action.

principles.principle_label: Employer's duty to prevent workplace harassment
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer's common law duty to provide a safe workplace extends to taking reasonable steps to prevent reasonably foreseeable harassment of an employee by third parties, including fellow employees or customers, analogous to the duty to prevent physical assault.

principles.principle_label: Injunction in aid of Attorney General's duty
principles.paraphrase_en: An injunction may be granted on the application of the Attorney General (or Secretary for Justice) to ensure obedience to the law, even though the Attorney General lacks a personal cause of action, based on the public duty to enforce the law.

principles.principle_label: Distinction between negligence and contract claims for harassment
principles.paraphrase_en: For a negligence claim based on workplace harassment, the employee must prove actionable psychiatric injury, whereas for a breach of contract claim, damage need not be proven as contractual breaches are actionable per se.

relationships.target_case_name: Broadmoor Special Hospital Authority v Robinson
relationships.description: The judgment adopts the principle from Broadmoor that a statutory authority can seek an injunction to prevent interference with its statutory duties.

relationships.target_case_name: Abbasi v Newcastle upon Tyne Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Supreme Court's reasoning in Abbasi regarding the Broadmoor jurisdiction and its limitations on third-party standing.
The judgment cites Rollins as endorsing Lord Woolf MR's statement of principle in Broadmoor regarding injunctions for public authorities.
The judgment cites Walsh as an example where employers were held responsible for failing to prevent foreseeable criminal assaults on employees.

candidates.case_name: LAW TING PONG SECONDARY SCHOOL v. CHEN WAI WAH
judges: Before: Hon Yuen, Chu and Barma JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a party fails to lodge a statement of objection to a costs statement as directed by the court, the court may proceed to summarily assess costs based on the filed statement, adopting a broad brush approach.
The Court of Appeal has jurisdiction to vary a costs order nisi made in earlier proceedings upon a proper application.

relationships.target_case_name: LAW TING PONG SECONDARY SCHOOL v. CHEN WAI WAH
relationships.description: The present judgment references its own earlier substantive judgment from July 2021 that allowed the claimant's application to vary the costs order.

candidates.case_name: LAW TING PONG SECONDARY SCHOOL v. CHEN WAI WAH
judges: Before: Hon Lam VP, Yuen and Chu JJA in Court

candidates.case_name: LANTAU INTERNATIONAL SCHOOL LTD v. SECRETARY FOR EDUCATION
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A public authority's policy must not be applied so rigidly that it prevents the proper consideration of individual circumstances, thereby unlawfully fettering the decision-maker's discretion.
An authority is entitled to operate a general policy to govern its approach to applications, provided it still considers the individual merits of each case before applying that policy.

principles.principle_label: Distinction between policy and its application
principles.paraphrase_en: A challenge to the lawfulness of a policy itself is distinct from a challenge to its application; a policy may be lawful in principle but become unlawful if implemented too rigidly in practice.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Collymore that a general policy is permissible but must not be implemented so rigidly as to prevent proper individual consideration.
The judgment applies the principle from Adath Yisroel that a policy must not be so rigid as to amount to an unlawful fetter on discretion.

candidates.case_name: NG YAN KIT ALFRED AND ANOTHER v. EVER HONEST INDUSTRIES LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Lok J in Court

principles.principle_label: Test for penalty clause vs liquidated damages
principles.paraphrase_en: A clause will be considered a penalty if the stipulated sum is extravagant and unconscionable compared to the greatest conceivable loss from the breach, or if the breach is merely non-payment of money and the stipulated sum exceeds the amount owed.

principles.principle_label: No duty to mitigate for contractual payment in lieu
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a contract provides for payment in lieu of notice upon termination, and the employer lawfully exercises that option, the employee is contractually entitled to the payment and is under no duty to mitigate their loss.

principles.principle_label: Legitimate interest test for penalty clauses
principles.paraphrase_en: The true test for whether a clause is an unenforceable penalty is whether it imposes a detriment on the contract-breaker that is out of all proportion to any legitimate interest of the innocent party in enforcing the primary obligation.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of legitimate interest in employment contracts
principles.paraphrase_en: In assessing whether a payment-in-lieu clause in an employment contract is a penalty, the court must consider whether the employer has a legitimate interest in enforcing performance, such as avoiding disruption or difficulty in finding replacement staff.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where a clause providing for payment of one year's salary upon wrongful termination was held not to be a penalty.
The judgment cites this Court of Appeal case concerning a penalty clause in an employment contract and its analysis of legitimate interest.

candidates.case_name: P Y J v. N K S
judges: Coram : District Judge S. Lo in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: There is no fixed list of factors a court must consider when making schooling decisions for a child; each case is decided on its own facts, with the weight of each factor varying accordingly.
Greater consideration and weight should be given to the genuine, well-researched view of the child's primary carer regarding schooling, provided it is not motivated by malice.

relationships.description: The judgment references A v B as an example where factors like the child's cultural background and language foundation were considered in choosing a local school.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KANTHIMA MALEEMAO
judges: Before: HH Judge Yiu

principles.paraphrase_en: When DNA evidence is presented as a mixture from multiple individuals, the statistical likelihood ratio must be evaluated to determine the strength of support for a defendant being a contributor, acknowledging that such evidence can be very strong but not conclusive on its own.

The legal understanding of DNA evidence must account for the scientific possibility of secondary transfer, meaning DNA could be deposited on an item through indirect contact rather than direct handling by the defendant.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this Australian High Court decision as influential authority for the legal principle that the possibility of secondary DNA transfer cannot be ruled out.

candidates.case_name: LAW TING PONG SECONDARY SCHOOL v. CHEN WAI WAH
judges: Before: Hon Lam VP, Yuen and Chu JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A contract is interpreted by ascertaining the meaning it would convey to a reasonable person possessing all the background knowledge reasonably available to the parties at the time of contracting.

The test for determining if a clause is an unenforceable penalty is whether it imposes a detriment on the contract-breaker that is out of all proportion to the innocent party's legitimate interest in enforcing the primary contractual obligation.
The penalty doctrine is only engaged where there is a breach of contract; it does not apply to clauses that impose a primary obligation to pay a sum of money.

Whether a stipulated sum is a genuine pre-estimate of loss (liquidated damages) or a penalty is a question of construction, to be decided based on the terms and inherent circumstances of the contract as at the time it was made, not at the time of breach.
Where multiple documents are provided together as part of an offer, they may collectively constitute the contractual terms if the offer requires acceptance of all documents.

relationships.target_case_name: Investors Compensation Scheme Ltd v West Bromwich Building Society
relationships.description: The judgment applies the basic principle of contractual interpretation from this case.

relationships.target_case_name: Jumbo King Ltd v Faithful Properties Ltd & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the overriding objective in construction of contracts.

relationships.target_case_name: Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v New Garage and Motor Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle from this case on determining liquidated damages versus penalties as a question of construction.

relationships.target_case_name: Ip Ming Kin v Wong Siu Lan
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to highlight that the court is not confined to the contract's terms when assessing a penalty clause.

relationships.target_case_name: Cavendish Square Holding BV v Makdessi
relationships.description: The judgment applies the modern, refined test for penalty clauses established in this UK Supreme Court case.

relationships.target_case_name: Day Morris Associates v Voyce & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a contractual acceptance must be a final and unqualified assent to the offer's terms.

candidates.case_name: LN, by his next friend A, LM v. LK, The Executor to the Estate of LC, Deceased
judges: Coram : Deputy District Judge Jacqueline Lee in chambers (Not open to public)

principles.principle_label: Objective standard for reasonable financial provision
principles.paraphrase_en: The determination of what constitutes reasonable financial provision under the Ordinance is an objective assessment of what the claimant should reasonably receive, not an assessment of whether the deceased acted reasonably.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for claimed expenses
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for a specific expense as part of reasonable financial provision must be supported by objective evidence, such as medical reports or fee quotations; a claimant's unsupported assertion is insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of deceased's lifestyle and expectations
principles.paraphrase_en: The deceased's established frugal lifestyle and the expectations they set for the claimant's standard of living (such as education at government-aided schools) are relevant factors in assessing what constitutes reasonable maintenance.

principles.principle_label: Best interests of a minor claimant
principles.paraphrase_en: Proposals for the maintenance and care of a minor claimant must be shown to be in the minor's best interests, and speculative or unimplemented plans may be given little weight.

relationships.target_case_name: Ilott v The Blue Cross and others
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Ilott that reasonable financial provision is determined by an objective standard for the claimant.

candidates.case_name: H&M HENNES & MAURITZ GBC AB v. ZOETOP BUSINESS CO LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Lok J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish joint liability as a joint tortfeasor, three criteria must be satisfied, as established by the leading authority on the subject.

relationships.target_case_name: Sea Shepherd UK v Fish & Fish Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment treats this UK Supreme Court case as the leading authority on the legal test for joint tortfeasorship.

candidates.case_name: CHINNEY SHUN CHEONG BUILDING SERVICES ENGINEERING LTD (formerly known as CHINNEY E & M (MAINTENANCE) LTD) v. LO KWAI FAN
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A court has jurisdiction to provide further directions or vary an existing order for the sale of a property when there has been a material change in circumstances, particularly when the original order contains a liberty to apply clause.

A court may vary the minimum sale price specified in an order if presented with reliable evidence, such as a consistent valuation methodology, demonstrating a material change in the property's market value since the original order was made.

relationships.target_case_name: Lam Chok Lai v The Official Receiver and Trustee in Bankruptcy of Lam Chok Wai
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Lam Chok Lai that the court has jurisdiction to give further directions for a sale due to changed circumstances under a liberty to apply clause.

candidates.case_name: LI CHIU WAH JOSEPH v. HONG KONG SOCIETY OF NOTARIES
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Tang NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: To determine if a contractual provision is a penalty, the court must assess whether it is a secondary obligation imposing a detriment on the contract-breaker that is grossly disproportionate to the innocent party's legitimate interest in enforcing the primary obligation.

The inquiry into whether a sum represents a genuine pre-estimate of loss is subsumed within the broader question of whether the sum is so extravagant and disproportionate to the legitimate purpose it serves.

principles.principle_label: Restriction on new arguments on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: A party cannot normally advance a new and different case in the Court of Final Appeal from that argued in the court below, absent a proper basis for doing so.

relationships.description: The Court of Appeal applied the principles from Law Ting Pong to determine that the late charge was not a penalty.
The judgment references the traditional test for penalties established in Dunlop.
The judgment references the approach to penalties adopted in Cavendish and followed in Law Ting Pong.

candidates.case_name: BRIGHT SHIPPING LTD v. CHANGHONG GROUP (HK) LTD
judges: Before: Hon Anthony Chan J in Chambers

relationships.target_case_name: Fortenova Grupa v LLC Shushary Holding
relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Fortenova Grupa on the basis that the redemption in that case was indisputably unlawful, unlike the present situation.

relationships.target_case_name: Celestial Aviation Services Ltd v Unicredit Bank AG
relationships.description: The judgment cites Celestial Aviation Services as a related authority on the topic of payments involving sanctioned entities.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM KWAI SANG
judges: Before: HH Judge Stanley Chan

principles.principle_label: Elements of unlawful sexual intercourse with girl under 13
principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits an offence under section 123 of the Crimes Ordinance if they have sexual intercourse with a girl who is under the age of 13, regardless of the victim's consent.
A defendant who pleads guilty to a charge is properly convicted on that charge without the need for a full trial on the merits.

principles.principle_label: Court's power to call for victim impact report
principles.paraphrase_en: In sentencing for sexual offences involving a young victim, the court has the discretion to adjourn the case and call for a victim impact assessment report to inform the sentencing decision.

candidates.case_name: LAM SHUI KING v. POLLUTION & PROTECTION SERVICES LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Coram: Master Louise Chan in Chambers (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: The burden of proving that a plaintiff had constructive knowledge of a material fact rests on the party asserting it, and the test for such knowledge is objective.

The knowledge required to start a limitation period is a state of mind with a sufficient degree of certainty to justify taking preliminary steps towards a claim, such as seeking advice. It can include a reasonably firm belief that warrants investigation, not absolute certainty.
When assessing whether a plaintiff had the requisite knowledge, the court must consider the plaintiff's intelligence in understanding the information available to them.
A plaintiff is expected to make reasonable investigations based on information available and cannot expect a defendant to provide every detail to absolve them of this duty.

When deciding on an extension of time, the court considers the prejudice to a defendant from losing a limitation defence. The court may take an overall view of the claim's prospects of success, but cannot make a final determination on the merits at the interlocutory stage.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Nash regarding the nature of knowledge required to start the limitation period.

candidates.case_name: NLC v. YMF
judges: Before: Hon Yuen JA and B Chu J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court will not make a joint custody order if the parents are so antagonistic that they would be unable to agree on most matters, as such an order would be unworkable.
The party seeking to vary an existing custody order bears the burden of demonstrating why such a variation is necessary.
The court must take into account the views of a child, particularly as they grow older, when making any important decisions concerning the child's welfare.

A child's relationship with a parent's new family is more relevant to access arrangements than to decisions concerning custody matters, such as education. The custody order does not determine the time spent with the child, which is governed by the access order.

principles.principle_label: Appellate interference with findings of fact
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not normally interfere with the findings of fact made by a first instance judge who has seen and heard the witnesses give live evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites the authority of PD in support of the proposition that a sole custody order does not prevent the non-custodial parent from inputting views or challenging decisions.

candidates.case_name: LI CHIU WAH JOSEPH v. HONG KONG SOCIETY OF NOTARIES
judges: Before: Hon Yuen, Barma and Au JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To determine if a contractual provision is an unenforceable penalty, the court must assess whether it is a secondary obligation that imposes a detriment on the party in breach which is grossly disproportionate to any legitimate interest the innocent party has in securing performance of the primary obligation. The innocent party's legitimate interest does not extend to punishment, but is limited to performance or a suitable alternative, typically compensation.

principles.principle_label: Court of Appeal's discretion on new points
principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Appeal possesses the discretion to consider new legal arguments raised on appeal, but it is a firmly established principle that it should not permit new points that are fact-sensitive or that would have affected the presentation of evidence or conduct of the trial below. This restriction applies not only when new evidence is required, but also when allowing the new point would necessitate considering counter-arguments that could have altered the trial's course.

relationships.target_case_name: Cavendish Square Holding BV v Talal El Makdessi
relationships.description: The judgment adopts and applies the 'true test' for identifying penalty clauses as formulated in the Cavendish Square Holding case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU HO KEI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing range for conspiracy to defraud
principles.paraphrase_en: In a case involving a company fraudulently claiming payment for non-existent 'ghost workers' over a sustained period, a sentencing range of 30 to 36 months' imprisonment can be correct.

principles.principle_label: Rejection of mitigation based on lack of skilled workers
principles.paraphrase_en: A mitigation claim that an offence was committed due to an inability to find skilled workers can be rejected if the evidence shows the individuals involved did not possess special skills qualifying them for the job.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing range established in Cheung Suet Ting for a similar fraud involving 'ghost workers'.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Li Wai Man
relationships.description: The judgment notes this case was cited by the defence in mitigation to argue for a lower sentence when an offence is committed by following instructions rather than greed.

candidates.case_name: CKFG v. WHHK
judges: Coram : Her Honour Judge Grace Chan in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Primary consideration in child welfare cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The sole and overriding legal principle in cases concerning children is the determination of what is in their best interests.

principles.principle_label: Status of Payne factors in relocation
principles.paraphrase_en: The factors outlined in Payne v Payne are not part of the legal test or applicable principles for deciding relocation applications; they may serve only as a potentially useful guide within a broader multi-factorial balancing exercise.

A court must give real and meaningful effect to the wishes of a child, especially when the child is mature, thoughtful, and expresses those wishes rationally and with emotional understanding; merely acknowledging and then discounting them constitutes an error.
Little or no weight should be placed on a child's expressed preference if, on the balance of probabilities, that preference does not constitute an informed decision.

principles.principle_label: Weight on Social Welfare Officer report
principles.paraphrase_en: Great weight should be accorded to the opinion and recommendation of a Social Welfare Officer when their investigation into an application is found to be all-round and objective.

relationships.description: The judgment clarifies that the Payne factors are not part of the legal test but may be used only as a non-binding guide within a wider balancing exercise.

candidates.case_name: PYK v. CCLA
judges: Before Coram: Deputy Judge R. Chow (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: Where a party fails to provide full and frank disclosure of their assets in financial proceedings, the court may draw an adverse inference regarding their financial ability.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. AN KAM FUNG
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI KAM FUNG (賴錦鋒)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ and Mr Justice Chan NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A trial judge has a duty to put an alternative verdict before the jury if there is evidence upon which a reasonable jury might return that alternative verdict, even if not raised by the parties or inconsistent with the defence case, to prevent over-conviction, under-conviction, or wrongful acquittal.

The threshold requirement for directing a jury on an alternative verdict is low: the evidence supporting the alternative must not be so incredible, tenuous, or uncertain that no reasonable jury could accept it. This low threshold is consistent with the prosecution's burden to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt.

The way the defence presents its case on the facts is highly relevant in determining whether sufficient evidence exists for an obvious alternative verdict. If a factual alternative does not arise from how the defence deals with and presents the evidence, this will usually be decisive.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the principle established in Ho Hoi Shing regarding a judge's duty to direct on alternative verdicts.
The present judgment applies the principle and cautionary note from Chau Yui Ming regarding the approach to alternative verdicts.
The judgment cites Ho Hoi Shing for the proposition that the threshold for directing on an alternative verdict is a low one.

relationships.target_case_name: Various trafficking cases cited by applicant
relationships.description: The Court distinguishes various trafficking cases cited by the applicant on their facts, finding they do not support the applicant's argument.

candidates.case_name: MAKHLOUFI ALI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Hon Au and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Judicial review scope in non-refoulement cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The Court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is limited; it will only intervene if there are errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality in the Board's decision, and does not serve as an additional appellate avenue.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of factual findings by Board
principles.paraphrase_en: Findings of fact made by the Torture Claims Appeal Board, including assessments of credibility and the severity of alleged harm, are to be respected if they are reasonably open to the Board based on the evidence before it.

principles.principle_label: Burden to demonstrate public law error
principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant challenging a non-refoulement decision must demonstrate a reasonably arguable public law error in the Board's decision to succeed in judicial review.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the legal principles established in Nupur Mst regarding the Court's approach to appeals in non-refoulement cases.
The judgment applies the principles from Re Md Shohel Sheak concerning the proper role of the Court in non-refoulement appeals.
The judgment applies the legal framework set out in Re Limbu Birkhaman for assessing non-refoulement appeals.

candidates.case_name: CHAN YAU LING v. HOUSING APPEAL TRIBUNAL AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A decision-maker entrusted with discretion may adopt a guiding policy but must not apply it rigidly to the point of fettering that discretion. The policy must allow for exceptions and consideration of the specific circumstances of each case.
A tribunal must give proper consideration to the particular circumstances of a case and not allow a general policy to automatically determine the outcome without such consideration.
Where a tribunal holds a hearing on a matter, it is unlikely that it considered itself to have no discretion in the outcome, as that would render the hearing redundant.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KONG TSZ CHUNG RYAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for ketamine trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: The sentencing starting point for trafficking in ketamine is determined primarily by the quantity of the drug, with reference to established precedents for specific weight ranges.

principles.principle_label: Aggravating factors in drug trafficking sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's role beyond that of a mere courier, such as involvement in repackaging, delivery, and arranging storage premises, constitutes aggravating factors that warrant an upward adjustment from the quantity-based starting point.
A one-third discount from the sentencing starting point is applied for a timely guilty plea.

When trafficking involves multiple types of dangerous drugs, a small quantity of one drug may be disregarded for sentencing purposes if it is negligible compared to a large quantity of another primary drug.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing enhancement for repeat trafficking offence
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's previous conviction for a similar trafficking offence is an aggravating factor that warrants an enhancement to the sentence beyond the quantity-based starting point.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing precedent from this case to determine a starting point for trafficking 39kg of ketamine.
The present judgment applies the sentencing precedent from this case to determine a starting point for trafficking 122kg of ketamine.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Lee Hin Long Owen
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing precedent from this case to determine a starting point for trafficking approximately 4.2kg of ketamine.

candidates.case_name: LN, by his next friend A, LM v. LK, The Executor to the Estate of LC, Deceased
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge Jacqueline Lee (Paper disposal)

candidates.case_name: CLC v. OIE
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Thelma Kwan in Chambers (not open to public)

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. TONG WAI HUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Macrae VP and A Pang J in Court

principles.principle_label: Joint enterprise liability for riot/unlawful assembly
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of joint enterprise applies to the offences of unlawful assembly and riot under sections 18 and 19 of the Public Order Ordinance. A party to a joint enterprise is criminally liable for acts done in furtherance of the common purpose, even if not physically present at the scene.

A fundamental principle of criminal law is that a secondary party (accessory) is guilty of the same offence as the principal offender. This principle applies to parties liable under the doctrine of joint enterprise for unlawful assembly and riot.

principles.principle_label: Legislative intent to maintain public order
principles.paraphrase_en: When construing whether joint enterprise liability applies to unlawful assembly and riot offences, the court must adopt an interpretation that gives full recognition to the public interest in maintaining public order and ensuring all perpetrators are caught, while avoiding over-charging innocent persons.

principles.principle_label: Primary offence provisions vs. liability principles
principles.paraphrase_en: Sections 18 and 19 of the Public Order Ordinance define the offences of unlawful assembly and riot with principal offenders in mind. The requirements for principal offenders at the scene do not displace or concern the liability of accessories or parties to a joint enterprise who may not be present.

principles.principle_label: Consistent treatment of accessories and joint enterprise parties
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no logical or policy justification for treating traditional accessories and parties to a joint enterprise differently under sections 18 and 19. Both are parties to the offences and should be held liable as such.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Jogee for the fundamental principle that a secondary party is guilty of the same offence as the principal.
The judgment applies the principle from Sze Kwan Lung that a party to a joint enterprise is criminally liable for acts done in furtherance of the joint enterprise, including for riot offences.
The judgment cites Stewart for the proposition that a party to a joint enterprise participates in the criminal act of another.
The judgment cites Chan Kam Shing, which itself quoted from R v Stewart, regarding participation in a joint enterprise.

The judgment distinguishes the principles in Leung Tin Kei as dealing with the liability of principal offenders at the scene, not with the liability of accessories or joint enterprise parties who may be absent.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG KONG FAI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.principle_label: Knowledge requirement for handling stolen goods
principles.paraphrase_en: For the offense of handling stolen goods, the defendant's belief that the goods might be stolen, without taking steps to confirm or ascertain their origin, can be sufficient to establish the necessary knowledge or suspicion.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing uplift for repeated dishonesty offender
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant with a particularly bad record of previous convictions for dishonesty offenses is to be treated as a repeated offender, warranting an increase in the starting point of the sentence.
When adjusting a sentence, particularly when increasing it for an offender's bad record, the court must consider the overall totality of the sentence to ensure it is just and proportionate.

relationships.target_case_name: Unspecified (Appeal Committee of the Court of Final Appeal)
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the Appeal Committee's determination that a specific rule does not permit reduction of a sentence term below a minimum threshold.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SAFDER MAJAD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Bernard Chung in Court

principles.principle_label: Elements of reckless arson endangering life
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of arson being reckless as to whether life would be endangered under sections 60(2), (3) and 63(1) of the Crimes Ordinance (Cap. 200) requires proof that the defendant, without lawful excuse, destroyed or damaged property by fire, intending or being reckless as to such destruction or damage, and was reckless as to whether the life of another would be endangered.

principles.principle_label: Admission of facts as basis for conviction
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's plea of guilty and admission of the Summary of Facts establishes the factual basis for the conviction, including the circumstances, actions, and mental state described in the charge.

principles.principle_label: Recklessness as to endangerment of life
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence is established where the defendant's act of setting a fire in a residential building containing other occupants demonstrates recklessness as to whether the life of another would be endangered, which can be inferred from the setting and the foreseeable consequences.

judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is limited to examining the decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality; it does not involve reassessing the factual merits of the claim or substituting its own view of the evidence.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary decision-makers responsible for assessing evidence, making factual findings, and determining matters such as risk of harm, state protection, and internal relocation viability in non-refoulement claims.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The present judgment cites Nupur Mst to support the proposition that judicial review intervention is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

principles.principle_label: Status of social welfare officer recommendations
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must give due consideration to reports from social welfare officers but is not bound to follow their recommendations; such recommendations do not constitute the court's decision.

principles.principle_label: Parental right to consultation on major matters
principles.paraphrase_en: A parent, regardless of custodial status, retains the right to be informed and consulted about major decisions concerning their child's upbringing, such as education, and may apply to the court to resolve disagreements.

principles.principle_label: Child's best interests as paramount consideration
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's primary consideration in custody matters is the child's best interests, and all evidence must be evaluated holistically to determine what arrangement serves those interests.

principles.principle_label: General costs practice in children cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In applications concerning children, the usual order is for no order as to costs, as the objective is to determine the best arrangement for the child, not to designate a winning or losing party.

relationships.target_case_name: Naziya Aslam v Ali (Custody and Removal)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that the court is not bound by social welfare officer recommendations.
The judgment cites this case to reinforce the status of social welfare officer recommendations as non-binding on the court.
The judgment applies the general costs practice established in this case to the present children proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: London Borough of Sutton v Davis (Costs) No.2
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to clarify that the 'no costs' approach in children cases is a general practice, not a strict principle.

candidates.case_name: NG HON LAM EDGAR v. THE HONG KONG HOUSING AUTHORITY

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish unlawful discrimination, a complainant must first show they were treated differently from a person in a comparable situation based on a prohibited ground, such as sexual orientation. Only after this is shown does the court consider if the differential treatment is justified.
cited_statutes: Articles 22(1) and 1(1) of the Hong Kong Bill of Rights

principles.principle_label: Court's role in analogous situation analysis
principles.paraphrase_en: It is a question of law for the court, not a public authority, to decide whether two persons or groups are in a relevantly similar or analogous situation for a discrimination claim, considering the specific context of the differential treatment.
principles.paragraph_span: [] (2) (The suggestion that due or significant weight...)

principles.principle_label: Unified test for constitutionality and discrimination
principles.paraphrase_en: The same test is used to determine if differential treatment is unlawful as is used to determine if incursions into constitutionally protected rights are lawful. In a discrimination context, this is called the 'justification' test.
The first step in justifying differential treatment is to ask whether it pursues a legitimate aim.

principles.principle_label: Margin of appreciation in social policy
principles.paraphrase_en: In matters of general economic or social strategy, national authorities enjoy a wide margin of appreciation. However, this wide margin does not justify adopting measures that violate the prohibition of discrimination or fail the proportionality requirement.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the reasoning from QT regarding the analysis of analogous situations and the unified test for constitutionality and discrimination.
The court declines to defer judgment pending the appeal in the Infinger case, distinguishing the procedural circumstances and prior delays.

principles.paraphrase_en: The offense of fraud under section 16A of the Theft Ordinance requires proof that the defendant, by deceit and with intent to defraud, induced another person to act, resulting in benefit to the defendant or prejudice (or substantial risk of prejudice) to that other person.

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's admission to a Summary of Facts prepared by the prosecution constitutes formal acceptance of those facts for the purpose of sentencing.
The court must read and consider mitigation letters submitted on behalf of the defendant, which may demonstrate genuine remorse, rehabilitation, and family support.
While not excusing the offence, a defendant's stated motive (such as financial desperation) may be noted as part of the factual narrative admitted by the defendant.

principles.paraphrase_en: The court is bound to follow the established legal principle regarding what constitutes a penalty clause in a contract.

principles.principle_label: No obligation prior to definitive agreement
principles.paraphrase_en: Where parties are negotiating towards a loan, there is no binding obligation on either party to enter into the loan agreement until definitive loan documents are executed.

principles.principle_label: Implied term for best endeavours not established
principles.paraphrase_en: An argument for implying a term requiring parties to use their best endeavours to complete a transaction, based on business efficacy, was raised but not substantively developed.

An argument hinting at the application of the prevention principle—that a party should not deliberately prevent the occurrence of a condition precedent—was referenced but not fully developed in submissions.

principles.paraphrase_en: A contractual sum payable upon breach will be considered liquidated damages rather than a penalty if the amount is not extravagant, unconscionable, or out of all proportion to the legitimate interest protected by the clause.

The difficulty in quantifying loss of goodwill must be assessed in the specific commercial context of the business, as the impact of a closure differs substantially between types of premises, such as a high-end shopping mall versus a standalone franchised outlet.

Where a party abandons a claim at trial, the normal rule is that they should pay the costs associated with that claim, but the reduction may be limited if the issues overlap substantially with those that were argued.

The court may consider the manner in which a defence was conducted, and may criticize it as profligate and disproportionate if numerous arguments are advanced but later abandoned, wasting time on irrelevant issues.

principles.principle_label: Strict liability for unlawful sexual intercourse with girl under 13
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of unlawful sexual intercourse with a girl under the age of 13 is one of strict liability, meaning the prosecution does not need to prove the defendant knew or believed the victim was under 13.

principles.principle_label: Age of victim as essential element
principles.paraphrase_en: For a charge of unlawful sexual intercourse under section 123, the prosecution must prove the victim was a girl under the age of 13 at the time of the offence.
The offence of indecent conduct towards a child under the age of 16 requires proof that the defendant engaged in indecent conduct with or towards the child.

principles.paraphrase_en: The act of holding someone's neck, resulting in documented tenderness, can constitute the actus reus of common assault.

Substantial delay between the offence and trial may be raised as a factor potentially prejudicial to a defendant, requiring the court to consider the timeline of events including surrender, charging, and adjournments.

candidates.case_name: WMW v. CYCH
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge J. Chow in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The meaning of a clause in a maintenance agreement concerning a child's education is determined by its ordinary meaning, focusing on the act of enrollment rather than physical attendance.

The court may disregard submissions regarding the parties' negotiation history or how they arrived at specific financial figures in an agreement when such background is not relevant to the legal interpretation of the clause.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU KA CHUN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: The offence under section 37D(1)(a) of the Immigration Ordinance requires the prosecution to prove that the defendant assisted the passage within Hong Kong of persons who were unauthorized entrants.

The actus reus of assisting the passage of unauthorized entrants can be established by conduct such as meeting them after they land, communicating with co-conspirators, and helping them with their luggage into a vehicle intended for their transport.

Individuals can be jointly charged for an offence where their actions, conducted in concert, collectively constitute the elements of the crime, such as when two drivers coordinate to meet and transport unauthorized entrants.

candidates.case_name: POON OI YIN FLORENCE v. WONG KWAN AND OTHERS

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish adverse possession of land without legal title, a claimant must demonstrate both factual possession and the intention to possess (animus possidendi) throughout the relevant limitation period.

principles.principle_label: Limitation Ordinance application to equitable interests
principles.paraphrase_en: The Limitation Ordinance applies to equitable interests in land, including interests in proceeds of sale held on trust, in the same manner as it applies to legal estates, with time running similarly.
When land is held on trust, the trustee's legal estate is not extinguished by limitation periods until every beneficial owner's right of action to recover the land has been time-barred.

principles.principle_label: Trustee's right to sue for beneficiaries
principles.paraphrase_en: Trustees may bring an action to recover land on behalf of beneficiaries with unbarred rights of action, even if the trustees' own right of action would otherwise be time-barred.

principles.principle_label: State of mind vs objective acts in adverse possession
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant's subjective belief (such as considering themselves a licensee) does not necessarily prevent a finding of adverse possession if their objective acts demonstrate unequivocal possession and assertion of right.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the two-part test from Powell v McFarlane requiring factual possession and intention to possess for establishing adverse possession.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tak Yue v Kung Kwok Wai & Another (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Hong Kong case as additional authority supporting the adverse possession principles established in Powell v McFarlane.
The judgment applies the reasoning from Ho Hang Wan that objective acts of possession can override a claimant's subjective licensee mentality.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. IVANCHENKO DMYTRO
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Bernard Chung

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for dangerous drugs trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: The court established a baseline custodial sentence of three years imprisonment for each count of trafficking in dangerous drugs.
A one-third discount from the starting sentence is applied in recognition of a timely guilty plea.
When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offences, the court must consider the overall total sentence to ensure it is not excessive.
For related but distinct offences, the court may order parts of the sentences for subsequent charges to run consecutively to the sentence for the primary charge.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YUNG KI CHEUNG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of burglary under section 11(1)(b) of the Theft Ordinance is established when a person enters part of a building as a trespasser and steals property therein.
A person can be a trespasser for burglary by entering part of a building that is closed to the public, even if the building's internal operations (like machines) are still functioning.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YU KIN HONG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of totality must be considered when assessing sentence, but the presence of other aggravating features can still justify enhancing a sentence even if the base sentence is already lengthy and just.

candidates.case_name: THE JOINT AND SEVERAL TRUSTEES OF THE PROPERTY OF THE BANKRUPT v. LEUNG YUET MEI
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kent Yee in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must determine objectively whether an order for sale is beneficial to all co-owners; the dissent or assertions of opposing co-owners are not determinative.
The court will generally make an order for sale on a co-owner's application unless refusal would better serve all co-owners or the order would cause very great hardship to one co-owner.

relationships.description: The judgment notes a divergence from this English authority's approach that creditors' interests ordinarily prevail over hardship to the bankrupt's family.
The judgment notes a divergence from this English authority's approach regarding the weight given to creditors' interests versus family hardship.
The judgment proceeds on the basis of this authority's approach, which declined to follow the English line of authorities.

relationships.target_case_name: Shanghai Commercial Bank Limited v Lee Yau Tak & Chan Wan Ying
relationships.description: The judgment acknowledges this Court of Appeal decision which noted the conflicting lines of authority but did not resolve them.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI JINTIAN
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.principle_label: Sentencing factors for young offenders in sexual cases
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing young offenders for sexual offenses, the court may consider leniency if the offense involved mutual agreement between parties of similar age, occurred in private, lacked threats or inducements, and involved responsible safety measures that minimized health risks.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles articulated by the Court of Appeal regarding leniency for young offenders in certain sexual offense circumstances.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MA CHUN MAN
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Pang and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: NSL and local law convergence principle
principles.paraphrase_en: The National Security Law is intended to converge and be complementary with Hong Kong's local laws. Where the NSL does not provide specific rules on an issue, local legal principles apply.

For sentencing under Article 21 of the NSL, which distinguishes cases of 'serious nature' from 'minor nature', the classification is not defined in the NSL itself. Therefore, Hong Kong courts must apply local sentencing principles to make this determination.
The most fundamental consideration in sentencing is generally the gravamen (seriousness) of the offence itself.

principles.principle_label: Rule of law principle under NSL
principles.paraphrase_en: Offences endangering national security must be dealt with in adherence to the principle of the rule of law. Conviction and punishment must only be for acts that constitute offences under the law.

principles.principle_label: Mitigation for prior convictions and remorse
principles.paraphrase_en: A prior conviction does not itself aggravate the offence unless it shows the defendant to be a persistent offender. It merely removes the mitigating benefit of good character. Similarly, a failure to show remorse or make restitution merely means an absence of mitigating factors; it does not positively aggravate the sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Ho Yung Yin regarding how prior convictions and lack of remorse affect sentencing mitigation.

candidates.case_name: MASON GROUP HOLDINGS LTD formerly known as MASON FINANCIAL HOLDINGS LTD AND ANOTHER v. TAM JOSEPH WING ON AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge KC Chan in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The established economic tort of lawful means conspiracy is carefully defined to avoid imposing liability on legitimate business activities. Liability requires the deliberate infliction of injury on the plaintiff in the absence of a just cause or legitimate interest, as distinct from merely advancing one's own interests by lawful means where damage to another is a foreseeable but not intended consequence.

For a conspiracy, it is not necessary to prove when or where the initial agreement was made, nor that every conspirator performed every overt act. The act must be done pursuant to the conspiracy, and parties must be sufficiently aware of the surrounding circumstances and share the same object to be said to be acting in concert at the time of the act complained of.

principles.principle_label: Inference of overt acts in furtherance of conspiracy
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether pleaded overt acts were done in furtherance of a conspiratorial agreement can be a matter of inference to be drawn from the pleaded facts. The court will not strike out a claim if it is not impossible to draw such an inference from those facts.

In assessing whether a conspiracy is adequately pleaded, the court may be asked to consider the timing and connections between various events and transactions, take a holistic view, and draw threads together to see if an inference of a conspiratorial agreement is possible.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from this case regarding the careful definition of economic torts, particularly lawful means conspiracy.

relationships.target_case_name: Metro Holdings Ltd v Periwin Development Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that overt acts in furtherance of a conspiracy can be a matter of inference from pleaded facts.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI CHEE YING AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Toh , Hon D’Almada Remedios and Hon Alex Lee JJ in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a continuing offence, the statutory limitation period for prosecution does not begin to run until the period of the continuing offence has ended. A prosecution may be initiated on any day during the commission of the offence or, subject to time limits, after it ends.

A charge of statutory conspiracy, being a continuing offence, is subject to the same rule: the limitation period commences only upon the cessation of the conspiracy, not from the date of the initial agreement.

principles.principle_label: Commencement of proceedings by laying information
principles.paraphrase_en: Criminal proceedings for an indictable offence are generally commenced by laying a complaint or information before a magistrate. The information is considered 'laid' when it is received at the office of the clerk to the justices by an authorised staff member.

principles.principle_label: Warrant or summons as court process
principles.paraphrase_en: Once an information is laid, the issuance of a warrant or summons is a matter for the court, not the prosecution. These are merely processes to bring the accused before the magistrate and are not required for proceedings to be considered instituted.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the CFA's principle that the limitation period for a continuing offence starts only after the offence period ends.
The present judgment follows the Court of Appeal's reasoning that for a conspiracy charge, the limitation period runs from the cessation of the conspiracy.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Manchester Stipendiary Magistrate, Ex parte Hill
relationships.description: The judgment applies the House of Lords' holding on when an information is considered laid for the purpose of commencing proceedings.
The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's endorsement and adoption of the Ex parte Hill principle.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Kennet Justices, ex parte Humphrey and another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in support of the proposition that instituting proceedings does not require the accused to be brought before a court.
The judgment cites this case for the principle that a warrant or summons is merely a process to bring the accused before the court.

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for a trafficking offence involving more than one type of dangerous drug, the court should adopt a combined approach to determine the appropriate starting point.
There are three recognized tests to cross-check whether a sentence for drug trafficking is manifestly inadequate: the absurdity test, the ratio test, and the conversion test.
The court must select the most appropriate cross-check test based on the circumstances of the case to determine the notional sentencing starting point.
When determining the final sentence within a guideline band, the court must assess the defendant's specific role in the offence.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing methodology and cross-check tests established in Chan Yuk Leong for cases involving multiple types of dangerous drugs.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for armed robbery with weapon
principles.paraphrase_en: For an ordinary case of armed robbery where a dangerous weapon like a knife is displayed to the victim, the normal appropriate starting point for sentencing is 5 years' imprisonment.

principles.principle_label: Adjustment of starting point for weapon type and use
principles.paraphrase_en: The sentencing starting point for a robbery-related offence can be adjusted based on the nature of the weapon involved, its capability to cause harm, and the manner of its use against a victim.
A defendant who pleads guilty at the earliest opportunity is entitled to a full one-third discount on the sentence, whereas a plea entered after a trial date is set only merits a 25% discount.

When sentencing for multiple offences arising from the same course of events, the court must apply the totality principle. Sentences may run consecutively to reflect distinct, independent criminal acts, or concurrently where the criminality of one offence has already been factored into the sentence for another.

principles.principle_label: Equal culpability in conspiracy despite different roles
principles.paraphrase_en: In a conspiracy, participants can be found equally culpable despite having different functional roles, provided their involvement was pivotal to the execution of the common plan.

candidates.case_name: CHAN WAI KEUNG LAWRENCE v. AU CHI MAN ALBERT AND OTHERS

principles.paraphrase_en: The established economic torts are narrowly defined to avoid interfering with legitimate business activities or imposing liability beyond what is justified in principle.

For lawful means conspiracy, liability depends on whether the defendant acted without just cause or legitimate interest, as opposed to merely exercising a right to advance their own interests through lawful means, even if damage to the plaintiff was foreseeable.

Conspiracies are inherently concealed; it is not necessary to prove when or where the initial agreement was made, that every conspirator performed every overt act, or that all conspirators joined at the same time, provided the acts were done pursuant to the conspiracy and the parties shared the same object.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: Conspiracies are inherently concealed; it is not necessary to prove when or where the initial agreement was made, that every conspirator performed every overt act, or that all conspirators joined at the same time, provided the acts were done pursuant to the conspiracy and the parties shared the same object.

principles.principle_label: Property sale price and mortgage discharge
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no legal principle or conveyancing practice requiring the purchase price of a property to be sufficient to discharge an existing mortgage; a vendor may sell at a loss and cover any mortgage shortfall with their own funds.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from this case regarding the careful definition of economic torts and the distinction in lawful means conspiracy.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HU MINGJIE

principles.paraphrase_en: Statements or documents created by an alleged co-conspirator can be admitted as evidence against another accused charged with the same conspiracy, provided they were made in furtherance of the common purpose and there is other reasonable evidence of the accused's participation. They are not admissible to prove the truth of any assertions contained within them.
Save for exceptional cases, the sentence for trafficking in a dangerous drug should not normally exceed 35 years' imprisonment, as per revised sentencing guidelines.
Applicants or appellants sentenced on or after 18 September 2018 should benefit from revised sentencing guidelines established in a prior leading case.

principles.principle_label: Aggravating factor of recent similar conviction
principles.paraphrase_en: A previous criminal conviction for the same type of offence, particularly one committed shortly before the current offence, is a serious aggravating factor that must be taken into account when applying the totality principle in sentencing.
A sentence for a current offence can be enhanced to reflect a previous conviction for a related offence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Ahern regarding the co-conspirators' rule for the admissibility of evidence.
The judgment follows the sentencing guideline from Sum Ka Wa which sets a normal maximum sentence for drug trafficking.
The judgment applies the principle from Lam Man Tak regarding who is entitled to benefit from revised sentencing guidelines.
The judgment cites Huang Ruifang (No 3) as the source of the revised sentencing guidelines for drug trafficking.
