principles.principle_label: Mens rea timing for agreement-based offences
principles.paraphrase_en: The mental state required for an offence is assessed based on the defendant's state of mind at the time the agreement was formed.

principles.principle_label: Inference of knowledge for drug importation
principles.paraphrase_en: A court cannot infer that a defendant was a knowing party to drug importation or was assisting such importation unless the admitted facts provide sufficient material to support such an inference.
The general desire to foster rehabilitation for young offenders should be considered in sentencing, even when specific statutory provisions for young offenders do not apply.

principles.principle_label: Mitigating value of youth for serious offences
principles.paraphrase_en: The defendant's young age may carry little significance in mitigating sentence when the offences committed are extremely serious.

relationships.description: The court applies the sentencing principle and approach established in this case.
The court applies the sentencing principle and approach established in this case.
The court follows the Court of Appeal's holding from this case regarding the inference of knowledge in drug importation offences.

candidates.case_name: TAIHEI DENGYO KAISHA, LTD v. BOK JOENG HONG KONG CO LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Master Keith Lam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A default judgment creates an estoppel only for issues that were necessarily and with complete precision decided by that judgment. The scope of what is conclusively determined must be scrutinized with extreme particularity.

A default judgment establishing liability does not automatically determine the quantum of damages or equitable compensation. The court must still conduct a proper assessment of the loss for which the defendant is responsible.

principles.principle_label: Liability of non-direct recipients in fraud
principles.paraphrase_en: A non-direct recipient (not a first-layer recipient) of fraudulently obtained funds may, in principle, be held liable for the entire defrauded sum, not merely the amount they personally received.

principles.principle_label: Distinguishing liability based on recipient role
principles.paraphrase_en: The inference that a recipient was a party to a fraud is more readily drawn against a direct recipient of the majority of the defrauded sum than against a more remote recipient.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Ozer Properties that a default judgment for damages to be assessed does not necessarily determine specific underlying issues like tenancy status.

relationships.target_case_name: Natures Mark HK Ltd v Hongkong Honxintai Trading Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an authority where a non-direct recipient was held liable for the entire defrauded sum in an email fraud case.

relationships.target_case_name: 800 Columbia Project Company LLC v Xiang Yang Logistics Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as a precedent allowing damages against a non-direct recipient for the full loss in a fraud case.
The judgment notes that the cited passage in Natures Mark does not state a principle different from that set out by Mance J in Grupo Torras.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHO YIN TAK

principles.paraphrase_en: Direct comparison of sentences in other individual cases is not meaningful because each case depends on its own specific facts and circumstances.
Sentencing guidelines from established cases can serve as a benchmark, and a sentence that aligns with this benchmark is not necessarily wrong in principle or manifestly excessive.
For offences not requiring a test of 'exceptional circumstances', the court must consider all circumstances of the offence and the defendant when deciding whether to impose a suspended sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principle for suspended sentences as stated in Secretary for Justice v Wade.

candidates.case_name: KOO MING KOWN v. THE BAPTIST CONVENTION OF HONG KONG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge MK Liu in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal from a Master's decision is conducted as a fresh hearing, where the judge considers the applications anew as if they were brought for the first time.

principles.principle_label: Test for joinder of new party
principles.paraphrase_en: The test for joining a new party under Order 15 rule 6 is whether there is a genuine claim and a proper question to be tried between the plaintiff and the intended defendant, which is necessary or just and convenient to resolve. While the applicant need not show the merits of their case, joinder will be refused if the intended defendant shows it is plain and obvious the plaintiff has no case against them.

Factual findings from a judgment in earlier proceedings are inadmissible as evidence in subsequent proceedings, unless the party is bound by them through issue estoppel or res judicata. The rationale is that each court must form its own opinion based on the evidence before it.

The Hollington principle does not render inadmissible the underlying evidence referred to in an earlier judgment, such as the contents of documents or witness testimony. Statements of the factual evidence before the previous court are not excluded.

For a misrepresentation claim, the plaintiff must have acted on the representation in a manner induced by it. If the plaintiff acts in a contradictory way (such as investigating the truth of the representation), this may undermine the claim that they relied on it.

relationships.target_case_name: Hollington v F. Hewthorn & Co. Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the established Hollington principle that prior judicial findings are inadmissible in subsequent proceedings unless binding through estoppel.

relationships.target_case_name: Capital Century Textile Company Limited v Li Dianxiao & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the formulation of the Hollington principle and its exception regarding underlying evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Jin Jiang Investment Limited & Anor v 深圳市威廉金融控股有限公司 & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to reinforce the Hollington principle and its exception for factual evidence presented in prior proceedings.

principles.paraphrase_en: Wilful blindness, which equates to knowledge in law, is established only when a defendant suspects a fact, realizes its high probability, but deliberately refrains from obtaining confirmation specifically to be able to deny knowledge later. It requires an intention to cheat the administration of justice and is not merely suspicion or deliberate disregard of suspicious circumstances.
Suspicion alone does not equate to knowledge for establishing criminal liability. A wilful blindness direction should only be given when the issue is properly warranted by the evidence.
Acts and declarations by a co-conspirator made in furtherance of the conspiracy can be used as evidence to prove both the existence of the agreement and the participation of each alleged conspirator.

principles.principle_label: Independent evidence requirement for co-conspirator rule
principles.paraphrase_en: The co-conspirator's rule requires independent evidence of the conspiracy before acts and declarations of co-conspirators can be utilized against other defendants, though a specific direction to the jury on this requirement may not be fatal if there is an abundance of such independent evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Harjani to note that the doctrine of wilful blindness has been subject to academic and judicial criticism.
The judgment applies the principle from Lee Wing Yan that wilful blindness directions are fraught with difficulties and require careful employment.
The judgment applies the warning from Law Wai Choi about the danger of treating deliberate disregard of suspicious circumstances as an automatic alternative to direct evidence of knowledge.
The judgment cites Ahern v The Queen for the principle that acts and declarations by co-conspirators in furtherance of the conspiracy can prove both agreement and participation.

principles.paraphrase_en: The appropriate sentencing discount for a guilty plea depends on the stage at which the plea is indicated. An indication made after trial dates are fixed but before trial commencement typically warrants a discount in the range of 20% to 25% from the starting point.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing discount range established in Ngo Van Nam to determine the appropriate reduction for a guilty plea entered at a specific procedural stage.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG KIN CHUNG

principles.principle_label: NSL convergence principle with local laws
principles.paraphrase_en: The National Security Law is intended to operate in conjunction with Hong Kong's local laws, seeking convergence, compatibility, and complementarity. Local laws continue to apply normally unless expressly or by necessary implication displaced by the NSL, with NSL provisions prevailing in case of inconsistency.

principles.principle_label: Application of conspiracy provisions to NSL offences
principles.paraphrase_en: Through the convergence principle, section 159A of the Crimes Ordinance applies to make it an offence to conspire to incite others to commit crimes under the National Security Law, even though conspiracy is not expressly mentioned in the relevant NSL provisions.

The convergence principle applies to the interpretation of the NSL's sentencing provisions, meaning local sentencing laws and principles are intended to apply alongside relevant NSL provisions within the NSL framework.

Section 159C of the Crimes Ordinance was enacted to align penalties for conspiracy offences with those for substantive offences, ensuring sentences are subject to maximum terms and proportionate to offender culpability.

principles.principle_label: Section 159C operation within NSL sentencing framework
principles.paraphrase_en: The correct application of the convergence principle requires section 159C to operate within the sentencing framework established by NSL21 to achieve convergence, compatibility, and complementarity between the two sets of provisions.

relationships.description: The judgment follows the Court of Final Appeal's interpretation of the NSL's legislative intent regarding convergence with local laws.
The court notes that lower courts in this case failed to thoroughly consider the convergence principle regarding section 159C's operation within NSL sentencing frameworks.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Ng Gordon Ching-hang & 46 Others
relationships.description: The judgment questions the lower court's view that NSL sentencing ranges in section 159C cases are merely referential rather than mandatory.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for drug trafficking conspiracy
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining the notional sentence after trial for drug trafficking conspiracy, the court adopts a combined approach and assesses fairness by applying the absurdity test, the conversion test, and the ratio test.

A defendant's role in a drug trafficking syndicate must be assessed based on available evidence; where material is insufficient to show they were not relatively low in the conspiracy hierarchy, this may be a mitigating factor in sentencing.

For a sentencing enhancement to apply based on a co-defendant's status (such as being a minor), the court must be satisfied the defendant had actual knowledge of that status; mere association is insufficient if knowledge cannot be safely inferred.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: For a sentencing enhancement to apply based on a co-defendant's status (such as being a minor), the court must be satisfied the defendant had actual knowledge of that status; mere association is insufficient if knowledge cannot be safely inferred.

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for trafficking in multiple types of dangerous drugs, the court should adopt a combined approach applying the absurdity test, conversion test, and ratio test, while considering aggravating features such as a wider market and the combined harmful effects on users.

principles.principle_label: Primary tariff reference for mixed drug cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In cases involving trafficking in multiple drugs, the sentencing court should primarily refer to the sentencing tariff for the most prominent drug type involved.

The conversion test may be applied to convert quantities of different drugs into a notional quantity of the primary drug for sentencing purposes, based on evidence such as cash rewards found and drugs in possession.
For trafficking in methamphetamine ('Ice'), where the narcotic content is between 300 to 600 grammes, the appropriate sentencing range is 15 to 20 years' imprisonment.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Islam S M Majharul
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the combined sentencing approach and tests established in this Court of Appeal case for multi-drug trafficking.
The judgment applies the sentencing tariff for methamphetamine ('Ice') trafficking established by this Court of Appeal decision.

principles.principle_label: Inference from failure to call witness
principles.paraphrase_en: An irresistible inference can be drawn against a party who, being in the best position to call a witness to contradict an opposing party's evidence, fails to do so. The court may infer that the witness's evidence would have been unfavorable to that party.

principles.principle_label: Representation of belief or intention as fact
principles.paraphrase_en: A representation concerning a person's present belief or intention can constitute a statement of fact. If the representor did not actually hold that belief or intention at the time of making the statement, the representation is false.

The tort of conspiracy has carefully defined elements to avoid encroaching on legitimate business activities and to prevent imposing liability beyond what is justified in principle. A claim for conspiracy must specifically plead these required elements.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Edgington that a statement of intention or belief can be a representation of fact.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Wai Keung Lawrence v Au Chi Man Albert & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that four specific elements must be pleaded to sustain a claim in conspiracy.

principles.paraphrase_en: The particulars of a conspiracy charge should specify the unlawful agreement the prosecution seeks to prove, not merely the substantive criminal offense intended. This focuses the trial on the specific agreement alleged.
The statutory test for allowing a criminal appeal is whether the conviction is 'unsafe or unsatisfactory'. The two terms are disjunctive, but case law does not establish they bear different meanings.

principles.principle_label: Lurking doubt as a route to unsafe verdict
principles.paraphrase_en: The 'lurking doubt' principle is one recognized route for the Court of Appeal to determine if a conviction is unsafe. It is not a separate test but a vivid expression of the statutory 'unsafe or unsatisfactory' standard.

The application of the 'lurking doubt' principle requires reasoned analysis, not a subjective judicial hunch. It should not be used to allow the appeal court to simply substitute its own view of guilt for the jury's verdict.

principles.principle_label: Miscarriage of justice and unsafe conviction
principles.paraphrase_en: If a conviction is found to be unsafe, the court cannot conclude that no miscarriage of justice has occurred. A miscarriage requires errors or irregularities capable of having a very substantial impact on the trial.

relationships.target_case_name: Stafford v Director of Public Prosecutions
relationships.description: The judgment cites Stafford for the summary of the appellate test, noting the 'lurking doubt' principle is a way of expressing the 'unsafe or unsatisfactory' standard.
The judgment cites Pope to illustrate the English Court of Appeal's effort to limit the scope of the 'lurking doubt' principle.
The judgment cites McIlkenny for the observation that the terms 'unsafe or unsatisfactory' are disjunctive but not held to bear different meanings.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HUI KWAI CHUNG, DICK AND ANOTHER

principles.principle_label: Conspiracy to traffic dangerous drugs - elements
principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits the offence of conspiracy to traffic in a dangerous drug when they agree with another person to unlawfully traffic in a dangerous drug, such as cocaine, even if the specific date of the agreement is unknown but falls within a defined period.
A statement made by a defendant upon arrest and under caution is admissible as evidence against them, and can include explanations about their possession of drugs and knowledge of the circumstances.
Subsequent video-recorded interviews where a defendant admits to the details of a criminal agreement and their role in it are admissible as evidence of the conspiracy.

principles.principle_label: Agreed Summary of Facts as evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: An Agreed Summary of Facts presented at the time of a guilty plea forms the factual basis for sentencing and is binding on the defendants.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUA SHIRLEY MASIGLA AND OTHERS

principles.principle_label: Burden and standard of proof direction
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge may give a general direction on the burden and standard of proof at the start of the summing-up, in line with specimen directions, and later refer to specific evidence while reminding the jury of those overarching principles.
The correct approach to a defendant's out-of-court statements, as established in Liberato v R and applied in Sze Kwan Lung v HKSAR, should be followed when directing a jury.

Specimen Direction 39, concerning confessions possibly induced by oppression, does not apply where there is no reasonable basis for the jury to conclude that oppression was or might have been present.

A judge has discretion to grant a sentencing discount for a defendant's assistance to the authorities, but granting a further discount for participation in a rehabilitation program may be wrong in principle based on subsequent authority.

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for conspiracy to traffic in an unspecified quantity of dangerous drugs, the court must establish a starting point based on reasonable inferences and assumptions drawn from the case facts, and any ambiguity in those inferences should generally benefit the defendant.

principles.principle_label: Global approach to sentencing multiple charges
principles.paraphrase_en: In cases involving multiple drug trafficking charges, it is appropriate to adopt a global sentencing approach by aggregating the quantities of drugs from all charges to determine a single overall starting point.

When applying a global sentencing approach for multiple offenses, the court must consider the principle of totality to ensure the final sentence is proportionate to the offender's overall criminality.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing approach established in Tsang Ka Wing for conspiracy cases involving unspecified drug quantities.

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may admit the contents of a mobile phone as evidence after hearing submissions on its admissibility.
The prosecution may rely on the testimony of a co-conspirator who has pleaded guilty and testifies as a tainted witness to establish the acts of another defendant.

principles.principle_label: Use of expert evidence for digital forensics
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence can be used to establish the provenance of digital images, such as linking a photo to a specific mobile phone device.
Telegram messages between identified user accounts can be relied upon by the prosecution to prove communication between conspirators and to support inferences about their actions and intentions.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG HO LAM AND ANOTHER

principles.paraphrase_en: The court has the discretion to order that sentences for multiple charges be served concurrently rather than consecutively, resulting in a single total sentence.

principles.paraphrase_en: For evidence of a co-conspirator's acts or declarations to be admissible against another accused under the co-conspirator's rule, there must first be independent evidence admissible against that accused which sufficiently links them to the conspiracy.

The co-conspirator's rule allows evidence of acts and declarations made by one conspirator in furtherance of the conspiracy to be used as evidence to prove the nature and extent of the conspiracy and the participation of other conspirators.

Out-of-court statements or documents may be admissible not for their truth, but where the coincidence between their contents and other proven facts is so exact that it reasonably supports an inference that the statements are true or relevant to a common plan.

principles.principle_label: Jury's role on inference from evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: Where evidence is relevant to an issue such as the existence of a conspiracy, it is for the jury to decide whether to draw the necessary inferences from that evidence.
The prejudicial effect of evidence can be mitigated by clear judicial directions that confine the jury's use of the evidence to a specific, permissible purpose.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: The prejudicial effect of evidence can be mitigated by clear judicial directions that confine the jury's use of the evidence to a specific, permissible purpose.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Oei Hengky Wiryo that coincidence between out-of-court material and other evidence can support an inference of truth.
The judgment follows the Court of Final Appeal's ruling in Vivien Fan regarding the requirement for independent foundation evidence for the co-conspirator's rule.
The judgment cites Ahern v The Queen for the established principle of the co-conspirator's rule, noting its accuracy is not in doubt.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NAWAZ SARFRAZ AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon M Poon JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Co-conspirator rule - evidence of common purpose
principles.paraphrase_en: For the co-conspirator rule to apply, there must be some independent evidence, beyond the contested statement or document itself, indicating that the individuals shared a common unlawful purpose. This evidence does not need to prove the specific details of the agreement, but must show an agreement for some unlawful activity.
Before the co-conspirator rule can be invoked to admit evidence, the foundational evidence relied upon must itself be independently admissible.

principles.principle_label: Temporal proximity for inference of relevance
principles.paraphrase_en: Close timing between communications can support an inference that they are related and relevant to the same subject matter or ongoing activity.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Ahern regarding the requirement for independent evidence of a common unlawful purpose for the co-conspirator rule.
The judgment cites R v Smart to support a broad interpretation of what constitutes evidence of an agreement for the co-conspirator rule.

candidates.case_name: SUNNI INTERNATIONAL LTD (IN LIQUIDATION) v. KAO WAI HO FRANCIS AND OTHERS

principles.paraphrase_en: For a serious allegation like unlawful means conspiracy, the court must find the inference of concerted action and shared unlawful intent to be compelling and plainly established based on the primary facts.

To establish unlawful means conspiracy, the claimant must prove the defendant was sufficiently aware of the surrounding circumstances and shared the same unlawful object (intention to injure) with other conspirators.
A party may, for practical reasons, abandon part of its claim for relief even when it is entitled to that relief in principle, thereby limiting the scope of the judgment.

candidates.case_name: TSUI PING SUM AND ANOTHER v. CHAN KA LOK AND OTHERS

principles.principle_label: Strike out application sole issue determination
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining a strike out application, the court may focus on a single, central issue to decide the application, such as whether a party has properly pleaded a required element of a claim like knowledge in dishonest assistance.

A claim for misrepresentation can be based on fraud if the maker knew the representation was false, or on negligence if the maker was merely careless. The statutory misrepresentation claim involves a shift in the burden of proof.

A constructive trust may be imposed as a remedy for unjust enrichment where the defendant is enriched at the claimant's expense, but the claimant must show the case falls within a recognized category or a principled extension thereof.

relationships.target_case_name: Dingway case / Golden Gate International Investment Co Ltd

principles.principle_label: Risk of economic loss in fraud
principles.paraphrase_en: A victim of fraud can suffer not only actual economic loss but also be put at risk of further economic loss by acting on false representations, which they would not have done had they known the truth.

Participants in a joint criminal enterprise generally share equal culpability for the crime, irrespective of their specific roles, because the crime could not have been completed without each participant's contribution.
In sentencing for crimes committed as part of a joint enterprise, courts generally do not apportion blame or adjust sentences to any marked degree between participants based on differing roles.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Muhammad Rizwan and Ors
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that sentencing courts generally do not apportion blame between participants in a joint enterprise.

candidates.case_name: SINGH BALJIT v. FORWARD & CO., LAWYERS

principles.paraphrase_en: It is an abuse of the court's process for a plaintiff to attempt to re-litigate a claim that has already been dismissed by the court in a previous action.

principles.principle_label: Striking out frivolous or vexatious claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A statement of claim may be struck out if it discloses no reasonable cause of action, or if it is scandalous, frivolous, or vexatious.
Claims arising from the termination of employment should properly be dealt with by the Labour Tribunal, not the Court of First Instance.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YUNG CHI HANG

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining culpability in drug trafficking cases, the court assesses the defendant's specific role (such as storekeeper or courier) and the level of trust implied by the quantity of drugs handled, even if evidence of a senior hierarchical position is lacking.

A defendant's prior criminal record does not automatically constitute an aggravating sentencing feature, particularly when the current conviction is for a type of offence not previously committed, absent other aggravating circumstances.

principles.principle_label: Evidential standard for prior drug quantities
principles.paraphrase_en: The court requires sufficient evidence to be sure of the quantity of drugs involved in prior trafficking occasions; where a defendant's account is imprecise, the court will not rely on direct arithmetic extrapolation from vague units (like 'bags') to determine those quantities.

principles.principle_label: Consecutive sentence for conspiracy plus substantive offences
principles.paraphrase_en: A sentence for a conspiracy offence should generally be consecutive to sentences for the substantive offences committed pursuant to that conspiracy, as it represents additional culpability, subject to the totality principle.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Ngai Yiu Ching that a conspiracy offence warrants a consecutive sentence due to it representing culpability additional to the substantive trafficking charges.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM CHEUK HANG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Chan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Active participation in a deception scheme, such as by adopting a false identity and conveying specific false information to a victim, can support the reasonable inference that the participant was briefed by and acted in concert with the principal orchestrator of the fraud.

A defendant's actions and statements, such as mentioning lawyers in the context of a demand for money, can demonstrate their awareness that the victim is acting under a specific false belief, which is a key element in establishing fraudulent intent.

candidates.case_name: DINGWAY INVESTMENT LTD (Provisional Liquidators Appointed) v. CHINA CITY CONSTRUCTION & DEVELOPMENT CO.,(HONG KONG) LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Barma JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Striking out standard for defective pleadings
principles.paraphrase_en: A pleading will not be struck out merely because it contains deficiencies, if those deficiencies can be cured by requesting further and better particulars, especially when trial is not imminent.

While allegations of serious wrongdoing like conspiracy or dishonest assistance require careful pleading, the court may find the level of precision sufficient if the core allegations are clear and deficiencies are remediable by particulars.

Liability for dishonest assistance requires a dishonest state of mind, which can consist of either knowledge that one cannot honestly participate in the transaction, or suspicion combined with a conscious decision not to make inquiries.
An allegation of material non-disclosure will fail if it is unrealistic to expect a party to have pieced together information from various incomplete strands to make the alleged disclosure.

relationships.target_case_name: Royal Brunei Airlines Sdn Bhd v Tan
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from Royal Brunei Airlines regarding the mental element required for dishonest assistance.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WU SHING HONG
judges: Before: DHCJ A Wong

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing a young offender, the court should consider the general desire to foster rehabilitation, even if the specific statutory provision for sentencing in the absence of a conviction record does not apply.

principles.principle_label: Limits of mitigation for serious offences
principles.paraphrase_en: The mitigating weight of a defendant's youth and clean record can be significantly reduced or carry little significance when the offence committed is serious.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM TSZ KIN, KENT AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge must direct the jury about obvious alternative verdicts that are supported by the evidence. Whether an alternative verdict arises depends on how the defence case is presented; if the defence does not raise a factual basis for the alternative, this is usually decisive.
If a defendant raises the defence of duress, the judge must direct the jury that if they find the defendant's account of duress is true or might be true, they must acquit the defendant.

The prosecution may prove the existence of a criminal conspiracy by asking the jury to infer its existence from the evidence presented, such as CCTV footage showing defendants purchasing equipment relevant to the crime.

principles.principle_label: No alternative verdict where defence is duress
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no basis for a judge to put an alternative verdict to the jury when the defendant's sole defence is that they committed the offence but under duress.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle from Chau Yui Ming regarding a judge's duty to direct on alternative verdicts.

candidates.case_name: HOI TIN TONG COMPANY LTD v. CHOY KWOK KEUNG
judges: Before : Hon Chu VP, Cheung and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for justification defence
principles.paraphrase_en: In a defamation case, the defendant bears the legal burden of establishing that the defamatory statement's sting is substantially true.

A defence of justification must be properly raised in the pleadings; a court may consider whether a finding is a permissible variation or development of what was pleaded, rather than a new, separate, and distinct matter.
For a claim of conspiracy to injure to succeed, there must be evidence of an agreement or understanding between the parties to injure the plaintiff.
A reporter engaging in investigative journalism to expose alleged malpractices, without an agreement to injure, does not constitute a conspiracy.
A defendant may not be held liable for the publication of words in materials for which they are not the original publisher, but may be responsible for words in publications they authored.

relationships.description: The judgment references this case in the context of whether a finding at trial constitutes a permissible variation of pleadings.
The judgment cites this case for the principle that the 'greater includes the lesser' in pleading matters.

relationships.target_case_name: Hung Yuen Chan Robert v. Hongkong Standard Newspaper
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Arab Bank Ltd v. Ross as referenced in this Hong Kong case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. RICO PAUL JASON LOPEZ
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for conspiracy offenses, the court must consider that the conspiracy was ongoing and would have continued until the participants were arrested, making it more serious than a single transaction.
A conspiracy involving multiple transactions is significantly more serious than a single act of trafficking.

relationships.description: The court bears in mind the sentencing approach stated by the Court of Appeal in this case.
The court applies the principle as modified by the Court of Appeal in this case.
The court takes into account the sentencing principles specified in this case.
The court takes into account the sentencing principles specified in this case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ENRIQUEZ ANNA MAE JUSTIMBASTE
judges: Before: Hon Macrae Acting CJHC, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person's mere presence in the vicinity of an object, even with knowledge of its existence, does not constitute possession. Possession requires actual physical custody or control over the object, coupled with knowledge and an intention to exercise that control.
Mere presence at a location where a crime is being committed is insufficient, by itself, to establish complicity in that crime or membership in a conspiracy to commit it.

relationships.description: The judgment references a relevant specimen direction for jury instructions on the concept of possession.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWUN CHI MING
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.principle_label: Global approach to sentencing for multiple drug offenses
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple charges of trafficking in the same dangerous drug, it is appropriate and sensible to adopt a global approach by aggregating the quantities of drugs involved across all counts to determine the overall starting point.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for conspiracy to traffic unspecified drug quantity
principles.paraphrase_en: In sentencing for a conspiracy to traffic in an unspecified quantity of dangerous drugs, the court must make its best effort to establish a starting point based on inferences and assumptions properly drawn from the case's specific facts, and where such inferences are not clear-cut, they should lean in the defendant's favor.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of aggravating and mitigating factors
principles.paraphrase_en: As part of the structured sentencing approach, the court must consider the presence of any aggravating factors or exceptional mitigating circumstances.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Tsang Ka Wing regarding how to determine a sentencing starting point for conspiracy to traffic in an unspecified quantity of dangerous drugs.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HON CHUN YAM
judges: Before: DHCJ Woodcock

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: The starting point for sentencing in drug trafficking cases is normally determined by established sentencing tariffs and the quantity of dangerous drugs involved.
A guilty plea entered at the earliest opportunity constitutes a mitigating factor in sentencing, absent other exceptional mitigating or aggravating circumstances.

The offence of manufacturing dangerous drugs is more serious than trafficking alone, as those involved in manufacturing play a more significant role than mere couriers or storekeepers, and the scale of operation typically extends beyond the quantity seized at arrest.
Sentences for manufacturing dangerous drugs are generally far heavier than those for trafficking offences.
When imposing sentences for multiple offences, the court must consider the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU HON YEE
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.paraphrase_en: The sentencing principle for drug offences is that sentences for manufacturing dangerous drugs are more severe than those for trafficking the same drugs.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Wong Chun Ping and Others as an authority supporting the sentencing principle that manufacturing offences attract heavier sentences than trafficking.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Cheung Wai Kwong and Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites R v Cheung Wai Kwong and Another as an authority supporting the sentencing principle that manufacturing offences attract heavier sentences than trafficking.
The judgment cites HKSAR v Ip Yuet Ho as an authority supporting the sentencing principle that manufacturing offences attract heavier sentences than trafficking.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOI HOI MING
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Bruce SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The starting point for bail decisions is that a court must order an accused person to be admitted to bail, as established by statute and reflecting the common law and constitutional right.
The statutory bail regime is underpinned by the fundamental principle that a person charged with a criminal offence is presumed innocent until proven guilty.
The general right to bail is subject to specific statutory exceptions and conditions set out in the relevant ordinance.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Article 87 of the Basic Law as the constitutional foundation for the statutory bail principle.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE YUEN-FOOK AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and Wong J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge has a duty to provide adequate reasons for their decisions, with the required level of detail depending on the specific context and circumstances of the case.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of evidence by judge alone
principles.paraphrase_en: When a professional judge sitting alone assesses evidence, it can generally be assumed they are aware of the legal elements of the offence, and their reasons for accepting or rejecting evidence should be directed towards those elements.
When assessing evidence, it is usually preferable for a judge to begin by identifying the specific elements of the offence that are in dispute.

relationships.target_case_name: Oriental Daily Publisher Ltd v Commissioner for Television and Entertainment Licensing Authority
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that what constitutes adequate reasons depends on the context and circumstances.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Kong Wai Chun and Others
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that the necessary detail in a judge's reasons depends entirely on the nature of the evidence and issues.
The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the preferable approach for a judge to take when assessing evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHALIQ SALMAN (aka KHALIA SALMAN)
judges: Before: Hon Zervos Acting CJHC and Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may be entitled to a sentencing discount for providing assistance to the authorities, including being prepared to give evidence against co-conspirators, even if the prosecution ultimately does not proceed against those individuals based on that evidence.

principles.principle_label: Jury's role in assessing witness credibility
principles.paraphrase_en: Issues concerning the credibility or reliability of a witness's account, including that of a defendant offering assistance, are matters for a jury to resolve, not for the court to pre-judge in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SHE SUET YEE AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for ketamine trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: For trafficking in 2,000 grams of ketamine, the sentencing starting point should be at least 18 years' imprisonment; for 3,000 grams, at least 20 years. For quantities exceeding 3,000 grams, the starting point may exceed 20 years, but the sentence increase should not be directly proportional to the drug quantity increase.

principles.principle_label: Hierarchy and role in drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's position in the hierarchy of a drug trafficking operation is a relevant sentencing factor. A defendant who recruits others, instructs them on operational matters (such as renting premises), and determines their remuneration occupies a higher level of responsibility.

principles.principle_label: Consecutive sentences for separate drug batches
principles.paraphrase_en: When offenses involve wholly separate and different batches of drugs stored at different locations, the principle of imposing consecutive sentences is appropriate, subject to the overall totality of the sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing guidelines and starting points for ketamine trafficking established in Chan Ka Yiu.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MAU KA PO
judges: Before: Hon S T Poon J

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining an appropriate sentence in drug cases, the court may fairly base its calculation on the quantities of drugs seized, applying any inferences or assumptions in a manner that favors the defendant.

When imposing a sentence consecutive to an existing term, the court must consider the overall gravity of the offence and apply the totality principle to ensure the total sentence is just and proportionate.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG FUNG
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Zervos JA and Barnes J in Court

principles.principle_label: Provocation as partial defence to murder
principles.paraphrase_en: Provocation is a partial defence that reduces murder to manslaughter. The prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the killing was not provoked. If the jury concludes the killing might have been provoked, they must return a verdict of manslaughter, not murder.

The existence of murderous intent (intent to kill or cause really serious bodily harm) is an essential element of murder that must be decided by the jury upon consideration of all evidence, even if the defence does not actively contest it.

principles.principle_label: Judge's duty to direct on alternative verdicts
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge has a general duty to direct the jury on an obvious alternative verdict to ensure an accused is not over-convicted or under-convicted, irrespective of whether the parties request such a direction.

principles.principle_label: Limits on duty to direct on alternative verdicts
principles.paraphrase_en: The duty to direct on an alternative verdict depends on how the defence case was conducted and whether there is sufficient evidence to support an obvious alternative. A direction may be omitted if it would confuse the jury, particularly when the defence is solely focused on a different, specific alternative.

relationships.description: The court distinguishes the application of legal principles on alternative verdicts from Ho Hoi Shing based on the different way the defence case was run in the present matter.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSANG CHEUNG-YAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and Anthea Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A participant in a joint enterprise can be held liable for murder if the victim's death results from an act carried out within the scope of that enterprise, even if the participant's own specific act did not directly cause the death.

In a joint enterprise, different participants can be convicted of different offences (e.g., murder vs. manslaughter) based on the jury's findings regarding their individual mental states (intent to kill or cause GBH vs. a lesser intent), even if they participated in the same underlying criminal act.

principles.principle_label: Murder by disposal of presumed corpse
principles.paraphrase_en: If a person, intending to kill or cause grievous bodily harm, renders another unconscious, mistakenly believes them to be dead, and then disposes of the body in a manner that causes death, they can be guilty of murder if the initial act and the disposal are part of one continuous course of conduct.

If no joint enterprise is established, each defendant's liability for murder must be assessed individually based on their own conduct and whether they personally had the intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm, and whether that conduct caused the death.

The offence of assault occasioning actual bodily harm is committed when there is an assault (use or threat of violence without consent) that results in actual bodily harm, which is a lower threshold than causing grievous bodily harm with intent.

relationships.description: The judgment references Osland v R as an example where different verdicts were reached for co-accused in a joint enterprise murder case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG KIE-CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Anthea Pang J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM CHIU LAU
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J

candidates.case_name: PARAMJIT SINGH v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Hon Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A non-refoulement claim cannot be based on a fear that the applicant has subjectively created or imagined without objective, reasonable grounds to believe there is a substantial risk to life.
For a non-refoulement claim to succeed, the applicant must genuinely and reasonably believe they would face a substantial risk to their life if returned.

The principle of non-refoulement, which prohibits returning a person to a place where they would face persecution, is derived from Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG SHUN LAM
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIANG YAOQIANG (梁耀強)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Tang NPJ and Mr Justice French NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Final Appeal will not interfere with the Court of Appeal's exercise of discretion to order a retrial unless there is a serious error of law, principle, or approach, such as failing to consider a relevant factor or considering an irrelevant one.

principles.principle_label: Considerations for retrial - interests of justice
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering whether to order a retrial, the interests of justice include the accused's interests, the public interest in finality, the avoidance of undue delay and oppression, and the public interest in seeing those guilty of serious crimes brought to justice.

principles.principle_label: Attribution of counsel's fault to defendant
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether fault or tactical decisions by defence counsel should be attributed to the defendant depends on the facts of each case; attribution is not automatic, particularly when there is no evidence the defendant was party to the decision.

Under section 4 of the Homicide Ordinance, where there is evidence of provocation causing loss of self-control, the question of whether the provocation was enough to make a reasonable person 'do as he did' must be left to the jury, who must consider everything done and said.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing discount for guilty plea to manslaughter
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who has consistently offered to plead guilty to manslaughter and has never sought complete acquittal should receive a full one-third sentencing discount.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HOSSAIN MD IMRAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.paraphrase_en: A conviction for murder requires proof beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant unlawfully killed another person with malice aforethought, which includes an intention to kill or cause grievous bodily harm.

The offence of child cruelty under section 27 of the Offences Against the Person Ordinance is established when a person wilfully assaults, ill-treats, neglects, abandons, or exposes a child in a manner likely to cause unnecessary suffering or injury to health.
A guilty verdict in a criminal trial for serious offences like murder and child cruelty must be unanimous among all jurors.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG CHUN MAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and A Pang JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Extended joint enterprise murder mens rea
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal question presented is whether, for a secondary party to be guilty of murder under extended joint criminal enterprise principles, it is sufficient that they foresaw another participant might intentionally cause really serious injury, or whether it is necessary that they foresaw another participant might kill (cause death) with intent to kill or cause really serious injury.

principles.principle_label: Use of co-defendant's guilty plea as evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A legal question is raised regarding whether a jury is entitled to rely on a defendant's own guilty plea to a lesser offence as an admission against interest regarding a material fact in issue in the trial.

principles.principle_label: Use of accomplice's guilty plea as evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A legal question is raised regarding whether a prior guilty plea by an accomplice who testifies for the prosecution can be used by the jury as evidence supporting the prosecution case against a defendant.
Evidence that prosecution witnesses (accomplices) had pleaded guilty to manslaughter in the same case and were awaiting sentence was properly before the jury and was not a matter in dispute.

candidates.case_name: PARAMJIT SINGH v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.principle_label: Role of court in non-refoulement judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's function in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is not to conduct a fresh assessment of the claim but to examine the Board's decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary decision-makers responsible for assessing evidence and making factual findings regarding risk of harm, state protection, and internal relocation in non-refoulement claims.
A court may intervene in a non-refoulement decision through judicial review only upon identifying errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality in the decision-making process.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the limited grounds upon which a court may intervene in non-refoulement decisions through judicial review.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSANG CHEUNG YAN (曾祥欣)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Stock NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant appealing on grounds of inconsistent verdicts bears the burden of demonstrating both that the verdicts are factually inconsistent and that the inconsistency is so severe that an appellate court cannot allow the challenged verdict to stand.

principles.principle_label: Joint enterprise with differing mens rea
principles.paraphrase_en: A jury is entitled to return different verdicts for co-defendants in a joint enterprise if it finds that, while they participated in a common plan, their individual intentions (mens rea) differed.

candidates.case_name: ABOGADO EDNA BARRIENTOS v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge Bruno Chan :

principles.paraphrase_en: A party challenging an administrative decision must clearly and properly identify specific errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality in that decision to establish a reasonably arguable basis for challenge.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for factual credibility
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate body's adverse credibility finding, made after a thorough analysis of oral evidence and with the benefit of hearing the witness, is entitled to significant deference and is not lightly to be disturbed absent identified legal error.

principles.principle_label: Duty of rigorous examination in non-refoulement
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must subject decisions rejecting non-refoulement claims (including torture and Bill of Rights risks) to rigorous examination and anxious scrutiny.
Bare assertions made for the first time in legal proceedings, which were not raised before the original tribunal, require corroborative evidence and will not alone found a successful challenge.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOW CHING YIN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, McWalters and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof in criminal cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must prove the defendant's guilt beyond a reasonable doubt. If, after considering all evidence, a reasonable doubt exists as to the defendant's guilt, the prosecution has failed to meet its burden and the defendant must be acquitted.

principles.principle_label: Manslaughter by unlawful and dangerous act
principles.paraphrase_en: Manslaughter can be established by proving the defendant committed an unlawful and dangerous act. An act is considered dangerous if all sober and reasonable people would recognize it as such.

The court will generally defer to the reasonable professional judgment of experienced defence counsel regarding the selection of available defences, unless presented with evidence that such judgment was unreasonable or improper.

The mere presence or association of multiple individuals in a group does not establish a conspiracy to commit a crime. Individual responsibility must be proven separately, and some group members may be prosecution witnesses rather than co-conspirators.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN CHI SHING
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J

principles.paraphrase_en: The offense of attempted murder requires proof of an act done with the specific intent to kill, going beyond mere preparation toward the commission of murder.
A guilty plea entered at the earliest opportunity in committal proceedings is a significant mitigating factor to be considered in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. VALENCIA BERNIE MACATANGAY
judges: Before: Hon Tam J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI KAN YAU
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Lies as evidence of murderous intent
principles.paraphrase_en: Subsequent lies told by a defendant do not necessarily demonstrate an intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm; they may simply indicate a desire to distance oneself from a death for which one is responsible.
Lies told by a defendant do not inherently tend to show that the defendant was not acting under provocation.

principles.principle_label: Proper direction on lies in murder cases
principles.paraphrase_en: A jury should be directed that lies can support a prosecution case of murder only if the jury is sure the lies were told to conceal the fact of murder, rather than merely to conceal a connection with the death.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Taylor regarding the proper jury direction on the significance of lies in a murder case.
The judgment cites Richens in relation to the issue of lies and provocation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI KAN YAU
judges: Before: Hon Yau J

principles.paraphrase_en: The actus reus of murder is established when the defendant's unlawful act causes the death of the victim, as demonstrated by the fatal wound being the direct cause of death.
The mental element for murder requires the prosecution to prove the defendant had the intention to kill or cause grievous bodily harm at the time of the act.
Confessions made by a defendant under caution in video-recorded interviews and during re-enactments are admissible as evidence against them, provided proper procedures are followed.

principles.principle_label: Evidence - expert opinion on victim's state
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence from a pathologist and toxicologist regarding the victim's likely state of consciousness at the time of the attack, based on post-mortem findings and toxicology reports, is admissible and relevant to the circumstances of the killing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN MAN SUM IVAN
judges: Before: Hon Wong J

principles.paraphrase_en: When a jury returns a verdict of manslaughter based on provocation rather than murder, the sentencing judge must proceed on that factual basis and assess culpability accordingly.

Sentencing for manslaughter requires consideration of the whole circumstances, including the facts revealed by evidence, the background and circumstances leading to the incident, the defendant's personal background, and any mitigation.

principles.principle_label: Irrelevance of prior convictions on retrial
principles.paraphrase_en: Previous convictions from trials that were subsequently overturned on appeal have no bearing on the assessment of the defendant's culpability in the current proceeding.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWAN TAT YEE
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, D Pang and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A jury must be directed to disregard a confession if they find it was or may have been obtained involuntarily, under oppression, or in circumstances likely to render it unreliable, even if they believe the confession is true.

In a murder trial, the prosecution bears the burden of proving the killing was not a result of provocation. The defendant is guilty of murder only if the jury is sure of the requisite intent and concludes the killing was not, and may not have been, due to provocation.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof and defence evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt. A jury must be directed that even if they do not positively believe the defence evidence, they cannot find against the accused on an issue if that defence evidence leaves them in reasonable doubt about it.

When evidence of a prosecution witness conflicts with that of a defence witness, a judge must direct the jury that preferring the prosecution evidence does not, by itself, conclude the issue of guilt. The jury must still consider whether the prosecution has proved its case beyond reasonable doubt.

A jury must be directed that a defendant's decision not to give evidence should not be held against them, and they must decide the case based on whether the prosecution evidence proves guilt beyond reasonable doubt, while considering the defence evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from Mushtaq regarding the necessity of directing a jury to disregard confessions obtained in oppressive or unreliable circumstances.

The judgment applies the principle from Liberato that a jury must be directed that preferring prosecution evidence over defence evidence does not automatically satisfy the burden of proof beyond reasonable doubt.

candidates.case_name: KHAN MOHIB ALI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Hon Chow JA and H. Au-Yeung J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court must apply the principle of irrationality when reviewing the decisions of administrative bodies such as the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board.

principles.principle_label: Procedural fairness duty to give reasons
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of procedural fairness requires administrative bodies like the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board to provide reasons justifying their decisions.

The assessment of risk of persecution in non-refoulement claims must be conducted with reference to the non-refoulement principle established in Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees.

candidates.case_name: ALI KHAN SAJID alias SAJID ALI KHAN alias RASHID ALI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.principle_label: Time limit for judicial review leave applications
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for leave to apply for judicial review must be made within three months from the date of the decision being challenged, as stipulated by statute and court rules.
cited_statutes: Rules of the High Court O.53 r.4(1)

principles.principle_label: Court's role in non-refoulement judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: In a judicial review of a non-refoulement claim, the court's function is not to reassess the claim de novo but to scrutinize the Board's decision for legal error, procedural unfairness, or irrationality, as the primary fact-finding role rests with the Director and the Board.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of evidence by the Board
principles.paraphrase_en: The Torture Claims Appeal Board, as a primary decision-maker, has the authority to assess the credibility and reliability of an applicant's evidence, including identifying discrepancies and changes in the claim's narrative.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case to support the principle that judicial review intervention is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality in non-refoulement decisions.

candidates.case_name: REYES MILAGROS LAGUA v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is limited to examining the decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality; it does not involve reassessing the factual merits of the claim or substituting its own view of the evidence.

The primary responsibility for assessing non-refoulement claims, including evaluating evidence, making factual findings on risk, state protection, and internal relocation, lies with the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board, not the court.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the proposition that judicial review intervention is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

candidates.case_name: MALIK MUHAMMAD SARFRAZ v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of a non-refoulement claim is not to reassess the claim anew. The primary fact-finders are the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board. The court will only intervene for errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality in the Board's decision.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof and plausibility assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: While the claimant bears the burden of proving a well-founded fear of risk, if no adverse credibility finding is made against the claimant, the Board must proceed to analyze the claim on all applicable grounds on the basis that it is plausible. This guideline is not a rigid rule of law and its application depends on the circumstances.

principles.principle_label: Principle of objectivity in credibility assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: A decision-maker must not engage in speculative arguments based on their own theories about how a claimant or others should have acted or how events should have unfolded. The assessment must focus on what is objectively or reasonably believable, based on reliable sources of information.

When assessing whether refoulement would breach a person's right to a fair trial, the decision-maker must consider whether failures in the destination country's criminal justice system are so flagrant that they amount to a fundamental breach nullifying that right.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that judicial review intervention is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.
The Board applied the reasoning from this Court of Appeal decision when assessing whether failures in Pakistan's criminal justice system nullify the right to a fair trial.
The judgment states the Court of Appeal in Hidayah accepted the approach that a plausible claim must be assessed on all applicable grounds if no adverse credibility finding is made.

candidates.case_name: KHAN MOHIB ALI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is limited to examining the decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality; it does not involve reassessing the factual merits of the claim or substituting its own view of the evidence.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary bodies responsible for assessing evidence, making factual findings, and determining non-refoulement claims, including evaluating risk of harm, state protection, and internal relocation viability.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the limited grounds on which a court may intervene in a non-refoulement decision via judicial review.

candidates.case_name: PANAGODA LIYANAGE SURANGA DULIP PERERA v. DIRECTOR OF IMMIGRATION
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: A subsequent non-refoulement claim requires the applicant to prove both a significant change in circumstances since the previous claim was finally determined and that this change, combined with previously submitted material, gives the new claim a realistic prospect of success.
The fact that an applicant has established a family in Hong Kong is not a relevant factor for consideration in a non-refoulement claim.
Matters that have been finally determined by a previous decision of the Torture Claims Appeal Board cannot be re-litigated in a subsequent claim.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for subsequent claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The Court must apply anxious scrutiny when reviewing an immigration officer's decision on a subsequent non-refoulement claim, examining whether the officer correctly stated the law, identified issues, and analyzed evidence under the relevant statutory provision.
Immigration officers must observe a very high standard of fairness in non-refoulement proceedings, including advising applicants on relevant law and procedure.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSIM SUM-KIT, ADA
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for diminished responsibility
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal burden of proving the partial defence of diminished responsibility rests on the defendant, and this imposition is constitutionally valid and sound in principle.

principles.principle_label: Diminished responsibility as an exception, not an element
principles.paraphrase_en: The defence of diminished responsibility does not require the defendant to disprove an element of the offence of murder, but to establish a special exception or excuse. The prosecution must still prove all elements of murder beyond reasonable doubt.

principles.principle_label: Test for justifying a reverse legal burden
principles.paraphrase_en: To determine if a reverse onus provision is justified, the court considers whether it encroaches on the presumption of innocence and, if so, whether that modification is proportionate. A key factor is whether the burden relates to an essential element of the offence or to a special defence or exception.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of innocence and special defences
principles.paraphrase_en: The presumption of innocence under Article 6(2) of the ECHR is directed at the offence charged. Imposing a legal burden on a defendant to prove a special defence like diminished responsibility is less objectionable as it does not alter the core ingredients of the offence that the prosecution must prove.

relationships.description: The judgment follows the analysis in R v Foye, holding it highly instructive on the validity of placing a legal burden on the defendant to prove defences like insanity or diminished responsibility.
The judgment follows R v Wilcocks in rejecting constitutional challenges to the legal burden of proof for diminished responsibility.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that upholds the legal burden on a defendant to prove insanity, analogously supporting the burden for diminished responsibility.
The judgment applies the test from this case to assess whether a reverse burden of proof is justified within reasonable limits.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Director of Public Prosecutions, Ex p Kebilene
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Hope's comments from this case regarding factors to balance when assessing reverse onus provisions.

relationships.target_case_name: The People (Director of Public Prosecutions) v Heffernan
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that raising diminished responsibility does not negate any element of the prosecution's case for murder.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI KAN YAU
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHAW KIM SUN
judges: Before: DHCJ Keith

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG FUNG (王豐)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ and Mr Justice Bokhary NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: For a murder conviction, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant was not acting under provocation. The defense succeeds if there is a reasonable possibility that the defendant lost self-control due to an unforgivable provocation, even if that scenario is not proven to have occurred.

The jury does not need to find that provocation definitely occurred, but only that it might have occurred. If the evidence raises a reasonable possibility of provocation, the prosecution must exclude that possibility to secure a murder conviction.

The overall circumstances of the killing, including the scene's condition, the nature of injuries, and the method of infliction, must be considered when determining whether provocation might have occurred. Frenzied or uncontrolled violence may support the possibility of loss of self-control.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIMBU MACHINDRA
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and A Pang JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When directing a jury on the mental element for a crime of specific intent like murder, judges should generally avoid elaborating on the meaning of intent. A modified direction from Woollin may be used where necessary, instructing the jury that if they are sure the defendant foresaw death or serious harm as a virtual certainty from their voluntary act, they may find the necessary intent.

principles.principle_label: When a Nedrick/Woollin direction is not required
principles.paraphrase_en: A Nedrick or Woollin direction is not called for in every murder case. It is not required where the defendant's primary desire or motive was clearly to cause injury to the victim, and the sole question for the jury is whether they intended to cause really serious bodily harm.

In a case where the defendant's intoxication is relevant, the jury should be directed that if they believe the defendant was so drunk as to be incapable of forming the intent to kill or cause really serious bodily harm, they must acquit of murder. However, a drunken intent is still an intent if the jury is sure it was formed.

relationships.description: The judgment discusses the so-called Nedrick direction on inferring intent, which was subsequently modified.
The judgment adopts the modification from Woollin, notably substituting 'find' for 'infer' in the direction on intent.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SO HON TO, STEPHEN
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG KIE CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The term 'substantial' in the context of diminished responsibility is an ordinary English word that imports a question of degree for the jury to determine. It does not mean 'more than minimal' or 'more than trivial'; a merely trivial impairment is insufficient to establish the defence.

Ordinarily, a trial judge is not required to provide a detailed legal definition of the word 'substantial' to the jury, as it is an ordinary word they can understand. A direction should only be given if there is a risk the jury has misunderstood its import, in which case they should be told that a more than merely trivial impairment is insufficient.

Where the evidence only shows some 'partial impairment' of mental responsibility, a judge is not obliged to direct the jury that, as a matter of law, such partial impairment may be capable of supporting a defence of diminished responsibility.

principles.principle_label: Withdrawal of murder charge - rebutting diminished responsibility
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge has the power to withdraw a charge of murder from the jury at the close of evidence if there is no rational or proper evidential basis for the prosecution to rebut the defence of diminished responsibility. This is an application of the principle that juries may only convict on evidence capable of supporting a conviction.

principles.principle_label: Primacy of jury on diminished responsibility
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether an accused's mental responsibility was substantially impaired is ultimately a question for the jury to determine. The trial judge must exercise great caution and respect the jury's role as the primary decision-maker on this issue.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows Golds in holding that 'substantial' is an ordinary English word and that judges should not ordinarily define it for the jury.
The judgment applies the reasoning from David Squelch, which itself applied Golds, regarding when a direction on the meaning of 'substantial' is required.
The judgment cites Brennan for the principle that a murder charge may be withdrawn if there is no rational basis to rebut defence expert evidence on diminished responsibility.
The judgment cites Khan (Dawood) as authority supporting a judge's power to withdraw a murder charge from the jury in relation to a contested diminished responsibility defence.
The judgment cites Yeung Kuen Chi by analogy to explain why courts avoid over-explaining simple words like 'substantial' to juries.

candidates.case_name: IQBAL HASSAN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge (Non-refoulement Claims) Teresa Wu :

principles.paraphrase_en: A non-refoulement claim based on persecution requires the claimant to demonstrate a well-founded fear of persecution for a reason protected under law. The decision-maker must assess whether there is a real chance or real risk of harm amounting to persecution.

A decision-maker may reject a non-refoulement claim in its entirety if satisfied that the claim has been fabricated. The assessment can be based on findings that supporting documents are wholly fabricated or materially altered and do not support the claimant's account.
A tribunal or board fulfills its duty by giving substantive consideration to the core elements of an applicant's case, as evidenced in its written decision.

candidates.case_name: FERNANDEZ MARICEL BOLOAVIO v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE AND ANOTHER
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is not to reassess the claim de novo or make fresh factual findings, but to scrutinize the decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary decision-makers responsible for assessing evidence, making factual findings, and determining risks, state protection, and internal relocation viability in non-refoulement claims.
The applicant bears the burden of proving their claims for non-refoulement protection, and the Board must assess whether this burden has been discharged based on the applicable legal principles.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the limited grounds on which a court will intervene in a non-refoulement decision during judicial review.

candidates.case_name: CHARANJEET SINGH v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung in Court

principles.principle_label: Extension of time factors for judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering an application to extend time for filing a judicial review application, the court must evaluate four factors: the length of the delay, the reasons for the delay, the merits of the intended application, and any prejudice to the respondent if the extension is granted.

relationships.target_case_name: AW v. Director of Immigration and William Lam
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the four-factor test established in AW for assessing applications to extend time for judicial review.
The judgment references the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority.
