candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWAN TAT YEE (關達儀)
judges: Before : Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Gleeson NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A Liberato direction, which instructs a jury they must acquit if they believe the defence evidence or think it may be true, is only appropriate when the evidence in question is genuinely exculpatory. It should not be given for evidence that is merely supportive of the defence case but not conclusive.

When evidence conflicts, a judge must direct the jury that simply preferring prosecution evidence over defence evidence does not, by itself, discharge the prosecution's burden of proof. The prosecution must prove its case beyond reasonable doubt based on evidence the jury accepts, regardless of whether they reject the defence evidence.

Competing expert opinion evidence is neither true nor false in an absolute sense; it is opinion. Such evidence may be supportive of a party's case but is not conclusive. A jury should not be directed that they must accept a particular verdict if they think an expert opinion may be correct.

The purpose of a Liberato direction is to prevent the jury from confusing their evaluation of the credibility of evidence (who to believe) with the resolution of the legal issue of whether the prosecution has proved its case beyond reasonable doubt.

principles.principle_label: When a Liberato direction is required
principles.paraphrase_en: A Liberato direction is not called for in every case involving conflicting evidence. It is only required where, if the jury believes or thinks the defence evidence might be true, they would be obliged to acquit the defendant of the charged offence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies and clarifies the principles from Liberato regarding when a specific jury direction is necessary concerning defence evidence.
The judgment cites Johnson v Western Australia to note a potential shortcoming in using a template Liberato direction and to endorse a preferable formulation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE WAI MAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, M Poon and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A complete judicial direction on diminished responsibility must be given in accordance with the Specimen Directions in Jury Trials. Failure to provide the full direction, particularly regarding the effect of voluntary consumption of substances on the defense, constitutes a misdirection.

An offender who rejects a plea offer and proceeds to trial relying on a defense that, if successful, would result in a complete acquittal, is not normally entitled to the full one-third sentencing discount. Instead, a lesser discount should be applied.

relationships.description: The court finds this older judgment of very little assistance for the present sentencing considerations.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN KWAI SANG
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.principle_label: Provocation as partial defense to murder
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who kills another may be convicted of manslaughter rather than murder if they acted under provocation, meaning they lost self-control due to a sudden and temporary loss of self-control caused by things said or done.
For provocation to be left to the jury, there must be evidence that the defendant was provoked to lose self-control and that a reasonable person might have reacted similarly.
Provocation can arise from a cumulative series of acts or a course of conduct over time, culminating in a final triggering incident that causes the defendant to snap.
It is for the jury to decide, based on all the evidence, whether the defendant was provoked and whether a reasonable person might have been so provoked.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAW WAI MAN
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offenses arising from related incidents, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate, considering the entire course of conduct rather than simply adding individual sentences.
A timely guilty plea warrants a reduction in the overall sentence imposed, reflecting the utilitarian value of saving court time and resources, and demonstrating remorse.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ARIF AQIB AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Yau J

principles.paraphrase_en: A person can be convicted of murder under common law as part of a joint enterprise if they participate in an attack with others, knowing that the attackers are armed with lethal weapons like knives and intend to use them, even if they do not personally inflict the fatal injuries.

principles.principle_label: Accessorial liability for wounding with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: A person can be convicted of wounding with intent under section 17(a) of the Offences Against the Person Ordinance as part of a joint criminal enterprise if they assist or guide attackers to the scene, knowing the attackers are armed with weapons and intend to cause grievous bodily harm.

principles.principle_label: Common law murder and statutory punishment
principles.paraphrase_en: Murder is an offence under common law, but its punishment is provided by statute, specifically section 2 of the Offences Against the Person Ordinance.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHIU CHUNG YUEN
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG MAN KONG
judges: Before: Hon Johnny Chan J

principles.principle_label: Murder - intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm
principles.paraphrase_en: A conviction for murder requires proof that the defendant, at the time of the act, intended either to kill the victim or to cause them grievous bodily harm.

Evidence of premeditation, such as purchasing a weapon and changing clothes to avoid recognition, is relevant to establishing the defendant's state of mind and intent prior to the commission of the offence.
The history of the relationship between the defendant and the victim, including a deterioration from friendship to hostility, can provide context for the defendant's motive and actions.

candidates.case_name: MUZAFFAR BILAL v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge Michael WONG :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is limited to examining the decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality. It does not involve reassessing the factual merits of the claim, as that is the function of the primary decision-makers.

The obligation for a decision-maker to consider internal relocation as an alternative only arises if the applicant has first established that they qualify as a refugee under the Refugee Convention by demonstrating a real risk of persecution in their home area.

principles.principle_label: Leave to apply for judicial review standard
principles.paraphrase_en: For the court to grant leave to apply for judicial review, the applicant must demonstrate a reasonably arguable claim with realistic prospects of success.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the proposition that judicial review intervention is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that internal relocation only needs consideration if the applicant first qualifies as a refugee.
The judgment applies this authority to reinforce that internal relocation is only relevant if a real risk of persecution in the home area is established.

relationships.target_case_name: Po Fun Chan v Winnie Cheung
relationships.description: The judgment follows the Court of Final Appeal's standard that leave for judicial review requires a reasonably arguable claim with realistic prospects.

candidates.case_name: MIA SHAHIN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Order by Hon Fung J :

principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant for judicial review must clearly and properly identify specific grounds for relief, such as errors of law, irrationality, or procedural unfairness, in their application documents; failure to do so may result in dismissal of the application.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for torture claims
principles.paraphrase_en: Decisions of immigration authorities on torture and human rights claims must be examined by the court with rigorous examination and anxious scrutiny to ensure no error of law, procedural unfairness, or failure to apply high standards of fairness.

The Director of Immigration must assess torture claims under multiple applicable grounds including risk of torture under immigration law, and risks to absolute rights under the Bill of Rights such as right to life and freedom from torture or inhuman treatment.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM HO NAM (譚浩南)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ and Mr Justice Lam PJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A trial judge must not misdirect a jury by creating the impression that they must accept the defendant's evidence as true in order to find a defense like provocation established. The jury should be directed to consider all evidence, including the defendant's account, even if they do not fully believe it.

For a ground of appeal based on alleged misdirection to succeed, it must be reasonably arguable that the directions created a real danger of the jury misunderstanding the legal test. Mere repetition of arguments rejected by the Court of Appeal is insufficient unless it demonstrates the lower court's analysis was arguably wrong.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principles from Liberato regarding proper jury directions when evaluating defense evidence.

candidates.case_name: NG YUI TONG v. CHAN CHI KEUNG
judges: Before : Hon Chu VP and Cheung JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Pleading requirements for malice in defamation
principles.paraphrase_en: A general allegation that statements were 'maliciously published' is insufficient to properly plead express malice under the specific rules of court governing defamation claims.

relationships.target_case_name: Omar, Zarina v Chow Yee Ping
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Omar regarding insufficient pleading of malice in defamation cases.

candidates.case_name: THE INCORPORATED OWNERS OF SHATIN NEW TOWN AND OTHERS v. CHEUNG KWOK KEUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Andrew Li in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Qualified privilege for common interest communications
principles.paraphrase_en: Communications made for the genuine purpose of sharing information in which the recipients have a common interest (such as building management affairs among owners) are made on a privileged occasion, attracting qualified privilege.

principles.principle_label: Defeat of qualified privilege by malice
principles.paraphrase_en: Qualified privilege is defeated if the defendant did not have an honest belief in the truth of the communication, was recklessly indifferent to its truth or falsity, or was motivated by a desire to injure the plaintiff.

A defendant may be found to be recklessly indifferent to the truth or falsity of a defamatory allegation if, in circumstances where community standards recognize a moral obligation to verify serious allegations, they publish without any idea whether the allegation is true or false.

The statutory defence under section 29A of the Buildings Management Ordinance adds nothing to the common law defences of qualified privilege and honest comment, and it only applies where a defendant acts in good faith and in a reasonable manner.

principles.principle_label: Discretion to grant injunction in defamation
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may exercise its discretion to grant an injunction in a defamation case where there is evidence of the defendant's hostility, intransigence, and a tendency to make casual accusations against the plaintiff.

relationships.target_case_name: Law Kin Man Freeman v Chan Kin Hung
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case regarding reckless indifference to truth in the context of a moral obligation to verify serious allegations.
The judgment cites the standard for reckless indifference to truth as discussed in the authority of Horrocks v Lowe.
The judgment applies the holding from this case on the relationship between the BMO section 29A defence and the common law defences of qualified privilege and honest comment.

relationships.target_case_name: Au Yee Ming Ivan v Ng Fei Tip
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where the court exercised its discretion to grant an injunction in a defamation action concerning building management.
The judgment applies the test from Bonnard v Perryman regarding the grant of interim injunctions in defamation cases.

candidates.case_name: TSUI WAI YIP v. LAM MO CHIU
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Timon Shum in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To determine the natural and ordinary meaning of a statement, one must read the statement in its full context within the document, not in isolation, to avoid taking it out of context.
For the defence of fair comment, the commentator must indicate, either explicitly or implicitly, the range of facts upon which the comment is based.
The defence of fair comment can be defeated if the claimant establishes that the comment was made with malice.

Statements about a person's state of mind or motives, which cannot be directly verified by another person but only established by inference, are likely to be classified as comment rather than statements of fact.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Joseph v Spiller regarding the requirement to indicate the factual basis for a comment to establish the defence of fair comment.
The judgment cites Branson v Bower for the principle that statements about a claimant's unverifiable state of mind are likely to be considered comment.
The judgment cites this case as an example where a defence of truth can succeed despite minor inaccuracies if the main charge is substantially true.

candidates.case_name: YIP WAI TAK VIVIAN v. LEE KA WO ESMOND AND ANOTHER
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge Rebecca Lee in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: In determining whether words are defamatory, the court should not require evidence of the actual effects of the words on readers, as this type of evidence is inadmissible for that purpose.
When assessing whether words are defamatory, they must be considered as a whole, taking into account their context and the surrounding circumstances.
Judgment can be entered against defendants based on the admissions or positions they have taken in their pleaded defence.

The test for qualified privilege involves two questions: first, whether the occasion was privileged (a question of law for the judge), and second, whether the defendant's purpose in publishing was consistent with that privilege (a question of fact).
A plaintiff must specifically plead particulars of malice to overcome a defence of qualified privilege, and the court cannot find malice by inference alone without positive evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Hung Yuen Chan Robert v Hong Kong Standard Newspapers Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle established in this case that evidence of actual effects of words is inadmissible when determining if they are defamatory.

relationships.target_case_name: Multi-Winner Investment Ltd. v Lau Ming Yee
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that defamatory words must be considered as a whole with their context.

relationships.target_case_name: Poon Hau Kei v Hsin Chong Construction Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that judgment can be entered based on what defendants have pleaded in their defence.

relationships.target_case_name: Town Planning Board v Society of Protection of the Harbour Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Final Appeal case, though the specific application is not detailed in the provided paragraphs.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG CHI CHING CANDY v. YEUNG HON SING
judges: Before: His Honour Judge MK Liu in Chambers (Open to Public) (Remote Hearing)

principles.principle_label: Scope of remission to arbitral tribunal
principles.paraphrase_en: When a court remits an arbitral award back to the tribunal for a specific purpose, the tribunal's authority is limited to that particular issue and does not extend to a general reassessment of the entire matter.

principles.principle_label: Arbitrators' duty to demonstrate consideration of issues
principles.paraphrase_en: Arbitrators must clearly demonstrate in their award that they have considered and determined specific issues raised by the parties, such as whether expenses were unrecoverable or reimbursable.

The scope of a remission order from an appellate court is determined primarily by the language of the order itself, and in the absence of limiting words, it may encompass the entire issue as originally formulated.

principles.principle_label: Limits on fresh evidence in remitted arbitral proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: When an award is remitted to arbitrators for a narrow, specific issue, parties are not entitled to lead fresh evidence on that remitted issue even if the remission order contains no express prohibition against new evidence.
The precise scope of a remitter should be determined by reference to the appellate court's judgment and the pleadings that were before that court at the time of the appeal.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the Privy Council's holding in Sans Souci regarding the narrow scope of remitted issues and restrictions on fresh evidence in arbitral proceedings.
The judgment follows the Court of Appeal's approach in determining the scope of a remitter by reference to the appellate judgment and contemporaneous pleadings.
The judgment references the Ladd v Marshall test as the standard the Court of Appeal would apply when considering the admissibility of new evidence on appeal.

candidates.case_name: THAPA KAMALA v. TONG MING-KAY (PC4374) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A civil claim alleging malicious prosecution cannot be used to collaterally attack final rulings made in the criminal trial, including rulings on whether there is a case to answer and findings of fact in the verdict.

principles.principle_label: Two-part test for reasonable and probable cause
principles.paraphrase_en: The existence of reasonable and probable cause for a prosecution involves both a subjective test (whether the prosecutor honestly believed there was a case fit to be tried) and an objective test (whether the circumstances would lead an ordinary prudent person to conclude the accused was probably guilty).

principles.principle_label: Objective test standard - prima facie case
principles.paraphrase_en: The objective aspect of reasonable and probable cause is satisfied if the evidence is sufficient to make out a prima facie case warranting a charge, meaning there is adequate material to place before a tribunal, not that it would necessarily secure a conviction.
A prosecutor's duty does not extend to investigating whether there is a probable defence, testing the full strength of the defence, or verifying the truth of every assertion made by a suspect.

principles.principle_label: Expert report insufficient for setting law in motion
principles.paraphrase_en: Merely providing an expert report that is later found insufficient to support a conviction does not, by itself, constitute the act of setting the law in motion for the purpose of a malicious prosecution claim.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the authority that rulings in a criminal trial are final determinations and bind subsequent civil proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Howarth v Chief Constable of Gwent
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles summarized in Howarth regarding the two-part test for reasonable and probable cause in malicious prosecution.
The judgment cites Glinski as the source of the principles on reasonable and probable cause, including the limitation of a prosecutor's duty.

relationships.target_case_name: Coudrat v Commissioners of Her Majesty’s Revenue and Customs
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Coudrat that a prosecutor is not required to investigate the truth of every assertion by a suspect.
The judgment cites the Galbraith test for no case to answer as being similar to the test for reasonable and probable cause to prosecute.

relationships.target_case_name: Chin Kam Chiu v FTI Consulting, Inc & ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that providing an insufficient expert report does not alone amount to setting the law in motion.

candidates.case_name: TSUI WAI YIP v. LAM MO CHIU
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Timon Shum in Chambers (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: A judicial decision handed down after a statement was published cannot be used to justify or interpret the defamatory nature of that statement at the time it was made.
When a judge follows an existing case authority, the party challenging that decision must demonstrate either that the authority was wrongly decided or that the judge erred in applying it.
The court cannot engage in guesswork or draw conclusions from photographic backgrounds without proper evidence regarding the location where events occurred.

relationships.target_case_name: Multi-Winner Investment Ltd v Lau Ming Yee
relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the legal principles established in Multi-Winner Investment Ltd v Lau Ming Yee.

candidates.case_name: PANG YU HIM v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Ko in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The mental state required for the tort of misfeasance in public office is subjective. It can be established in three ways: (1) targeted malice (specific intent to injure), (2) untargeted malice (knowledge that injury would follow in the ordinary course), or (3) reckless indifference to whether injury would result.

Proof of material damage is a necessary element of the tort of misfeasance in public office. The long-standing common law requirement for special damage should not be disturbed without compelling reasons.

Exemplary damages may be awarded for misfeasance in public office where compensatory damages are insufficient to mark the court's disapproval and deter future misconduct, but the general policy of law does not encourage such awards where no material damage exists to compensate.

principles.principle_label: Heads of damage for malicious prosecution
principles.paraphrase_en: In an action for malicious prosecution, damages may be claimed under three heads: damage to the person, damage to property, and damage to reputation, as established in common law.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Ribeiro JA's observation in Tang Nin Mun regarding the subjective mental state requirement for misfeasance in public office.
The judgment applies the House of Lords' ruling in Three Rivers on the subjective mental state variants for establishing misfeasance in public office.
The judgment follows Watkins in confirming that material damage is a necessary ingredient of the tort of misfeasance in public office.
The judgment distinguishes Kuddus, noting that while it established exemplary damages could be awarded for misfeasance, Watkins later clarified the requirement for material damage.
The judgment cites the ancient authority of Savile v Roberts for the three heads of damage available in malicious prosecution claims.

candidates.case_name: LAM CHUNG PING AND OTHERS v. CHAN YUET KUEN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Kenneth KY Lam in Court

principles.principle_label: Malice requirement for slander of title
principles.paraphrase_en: For the tort of slander of title to be established, the defendant's publication must have been made with malice.

principles.principle_label: Slander of title as malicious falsehood
principles.paraphrase_en: The cause of action for slander of title falls under the broader legal category of the tort of malicious falsehood.

principles.principle_label: Elements of malice in malicious falsehood
principles.paraphrase_en: Malice in the context of malicious falsehood is established if the defendant knew the published statement was false, was reckless as to its truth, or published it for an improper purpose unrelated to protecting a legitimate interest.

relationships.target_case_name: Hong Kong Wing On Travel Service Ltd v Hong Thai Citizens Travel Services Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the legal principles for malicious falsehood established in this cited case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. RAHMAN MD. RAIHAN
judges: Before: Hon Toh J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offence of criminal intimidation under section 24(a)(i) of the Crimes Ordinance, the prosecution must prove that the accused threatened another person with injury, with intent to cause alarm to that person or to cause that person to do or omit to do any act.

principles.principle_label: Elements of assault occasioning actual bodily harm
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offence of assault occasioning actual bodily harm under section 39 of the Offences against the Person Ordinance, the prosecution must prove an assault that resulted in actual bodily harm to the victim.

candidates.case_name: LI SAU MUI v. MAN WAI CHEONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Joseph Vaughan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A statement is defamatory if it tends to discredit a person, lower their estimation in the eyes of others, cause them to be shunned, or expose them to hatred or ridicule. This includes statements alleging dishonesty, untruthfulness, or unfitness for office.
The meaning of a potentially defamatory statement must be determined by reading it in the context of the entire document or communication in which it appears, not in isolation.
A defendant in a defamation case can succeed on the defence of justification if they prove that the substance of the defamatory statement is true.

A defendant can rely on the defence of honest comment (fair comment) for a defamatory statement if it is an expression of opinion on a matter of public interest, based on true facts, and made without malice.

principles.principle_label: Privilege assessed at time of statement
principles.paraphrase_en: The question of whether a statement is protected by privilege is to be judged based on the circumstances known to the defendant at the time the statement was made, not on information that subsequently comes to light.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this English case for guidance on how to determine the meaning conveyed by words to an ordinary, reasonable reader.

candidates.case_name: PET LAND AND ANOTHER v. CHOW PO CHU AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Simon Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for libel or malicious falsehood fails if the plaintiff cannot prove, on the balance of probabilities, that the defendant published the allegedly defamatory statement.

A defendant acts with malice in publishing a defamatory statement if they know the statement is not true or that the factual basis for it is uncertain, but nevertheless publishes it as an established fact.

An occasion is protected by qualified privilege where the person making the communication has a legal, social, or moral duty or interest in making it to the recipient, and the recipient has a corresponding duty or interest in receiving it. The categories are not closed and are assessed based on current social conditions.

principles.principle_label: Qualified privilege - application to consumer complaints
principles.paraphrase_en: A family member of a customer has a sufficient interest to communicate concerns about a business's service (such as a shop owner's attitude) to other potential customers or members of the local community, who have a corresponding interest in receiving such information, creating an occasion of qualified privilege.

principles.principle_label: Fair comment defence - requirement of factual basis
principles.paraphrase_en: The defence of fair comment fails if the defendant's statement is not based on facts which are true or protected by privilege, or if the defendant does not explicitly or implicitly indicate the facts on which the comment is based.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG CHI CHING CANDY v. YEUNG HON SING
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Ko in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Communications between the Incorporated Owners or Management Committee and the owners concerning the management and administration of the estate are capable of being protected by qualified privilege.

principles.principle_label: Loss of qualified privilege through improper purpose
principles.paraphrase_en: The defence of qualified privilege is defeated if the defendant publishes the material for an improper purpose, such as venting personal spite, rather than for the purpose consistent with the duty or interest on which the privilege is based.

principles.principle_label: Certification and display of meeting minutes
principles.paraphrase_en: Under the Building Management Ordinance, the secretary of the management committee must keep minutes of general meetings, which must be certified by the chairperson as a true record and displayed in a prominent place within 28 days of the meeting.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. GURUNG NIRAJ
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An action constitutes more than mere preparation for entering premises as a trespasser when it involves physically testing or manipulating a security mechanism (like a digital door lock) with the intent to gain unauthorized entry, especially when external barriers are not secured.
A defendant's own admission can provide sufficient evidence to establish the specific intent to steal required for the offense of burglary.

candidates.case_name: LAM WAI IP v. THE SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Yuen JA and G Lam JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a claim of malicious prosecution, the plaintiff must properly identify who the prosecutor is. The claim can be brought against the Secretary for Justice as the prosecuting authority, not just against the police officers who conducted the investigation.

principles.principle_label: Malicious prosecution - effect of claimant's guilt
principles.paraphrase_en: If it is found as a fact that the claimant was actually guilty of the offence charged, this can negate the absence of reasonable and probable cause in a malicious prosecution claim, even if the prosecutor did not believe the claimant was guilty at the time of prosecution.

principles.principle_label: Malicious prosecution - reasonable and probable cause assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: The decision to prosecute has reasonable and probable cause when there is clear evidence supporting the charges, such as police testimony about finding illegal substances on the defendant's person during a search.

principles.principle_label: Malicious prosecution - malice and honest belief
principles.paraphrase_en: An investigating officer's honest belief in the guilt of the claimant does not automatically negate the existence of malice in a malicious prosecution claim, particularly if the prosecution case was tainted by serious illegality.

principles.principle_label: Malicious prosecution - factual basis for claim
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for malicious prosecution based on alleged evidence fabrication requires the plaintiff to establish that the evidence was indeed fabricated, not merely that there are inconsistencies or alternative explanations for the evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment references Martin v Watson as establishing principles regarding who can be considered a prosecutor for malicious prosecution claims.

relationships.target_case_name: Ministry of Justice (Sued as Home Office) v Scott
relationships.description: Cited as an example applying the principle from Martin v Watson where prison officers giving false evidence could be considered to have procured prosecution.

relationships.target_case_name: The Commissioner of the Police of the Metropolis v Copeland
relationships.description: Cited as authority that bad faith of investigating officers can be relevant to establishing malice in malicious prosecution claims.

relationships.target_case_name: Rees v Commissioner of Police for the Metropolis
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Rees that honest belief in guilt does not negate malice when the prosecution is tainted by serious illegality.

candidates.case_name: WILMES FRANZ JOSEF v. CORONER, WEST KOWLOON COURT
judges: Before: Hon Barma and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Pleading requirements for misfeasance in public office
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for the tort of misfeasance in public office must plead all necessary elements of the tort with sufficient particularity to disclose a reasonable cause of action; a court is not obliged to treat a defective tort claim as a different type of application.

principles.principle_label: Nature of application under Coroners Ordinance s.20
principles.paraphrase_en: An application under section 20 of the Coroners Ordinance is a distinct proceeding, generally commenced by originating summons, where the Court of First Instance exercises a discretion to consider de novo whether an inquest ought to be held, without requiring proof of fault or error by the coroner.

principles.principle_label: Judicial discretion to strike out defective pleadings
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge has discretion to strike out a statement of claim and the action if the pleading fails to disclose a reasonable cause of action, and is not required to permit amendments or re-characterize the action into a different type of application if inappropriate in the circumstances.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v William Ng Esq, a Coroner
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that a section 20 application does not require proof of coroner fault or judicial review error categories.

candidates.case_name: THAPA KAMALA v. TONG MING KAY AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Appeal's power to grant leave to amend pleadings should generally be exercised only in connection with its appellate jurisdiction, considering whether the amendment addresses the real questions in controversy and serves a useful purpose for the determination of the appeal.
For a claim of malicious prosecution to succeed, the damages allegedly suffered by the plaintiff must have been caused by, or been within the knowledge or foreseeability of, the defendant.

principles.principle_label: Effect of biased judicial ruling on reasonable cause
principles.paraphrase_en: A judicial finding of apparent bias against the presiding magistrate may be relevant to the issue of whether there was reasonable and probable cause for a prosecution, particularly where the magistrate's prior rulings were relied upon to establish such cause.
A proposed amendment to pleadings should be assessed on whether it can cure the identified defects in the existing pleadings; if it cannot remedy the defects, the amendment serves no useful purpose.

relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes the Galbraith Ruling's effect on the malicious prosecution claim, noting it cannot stand due to the subsequent finding of apparent bias against the Deputy Magistrate.

candidates.case_name: CHIN KAM CHIU v. FTI CONSULTING, INC AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: There is no legal requirement for an expert witness in legal proceedings to hold specific professional qualifications such as being a certified public accountant; a claim cannot be founded merely on the expert's lack of a particular certification.

For a claim of malicious prosecution to succeed, the plaintiff must plead and prove that the defendant initiated or set the law in motion regarding the prosecution, that the prosecution was without reasonable and probable cause, and that the defendant was actuated by malice.

principles.principle_label: Expert report insufficient for prosecution initiation
principles.paraphrase_en: Merely providing an expert report that is later found insufficient to secure a conviction does not, by itself, constitute initiating a prosecution or 'setting the law in motion' for the purpose of a malicious prosecution claim.

In a malicious prosecution claim, the statement of claim must plead with sufficient particularity the facts from which malice is to be inferred; a failure to meet this pleading standard cannot be remedied by submissions alone.
Where a claim is fundamentally defective in law and no viable amendment can cure its deficiencies, the court is entitled to strike it out as being wholly and indisputably bad.

candidates.case_name: LAI CHEE YING v. TA KUNG PAO (HONG KONG) LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The modern judicial trend is to exercise discretion to order jury trials only rarely, reflecting a gradual development in practice.

Whether the proviso in section 33A(1) of the High Court Ordinance applies depends on the unique circumstances of each case, considering if the dispute turns on a broad picture rather than minute details.

relationships.target_case_name: International Social Service (Hong Kong Branch) v Vision First Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the proposition that there is a modern trend of rarely exercising discretion to order jury trials.
The judgment applies the reasoning from this case regarding how to determine if the s.33A(1) proviso applies based on whether issues depend on a broad picture or small details.

candidates.case_name: KHAN ASAF NAWAZ v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE on behalf of COMMISSIONER OF POLICE
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Ko in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To succeed in an action for malicious prosecution, the claimant must prove four elements: that the defendant initiated a criminal prosecution against them; that the prosecution ended in the claimant's favour; that the prosecution was without reasonable and probable cause; and that it was motivated by malice. The burden of proof for all elements rests on the claimant.

For liability under joint criminal enterprise, it is not a legal requirement that the accused be physically present at the scene of the crime. Liability can arise from an arrangement or understanding between parties, and presence together merely strengthens the case for such liability.

principles.principle_label: Reasonable and probable cause assessment timing
principles.paraphrase_en: The existence of reasonable and probable cause for a prosecution is assessed based on the evidence available at the time of charging. The absence of further incriminating evidence after the charge is laid is irrelevant to this assessment.

An honest belief in the guilt of the accused does not automatically negate the existence of malice in a malicious prosecution claim. Malice can be established if the prosecution was conducted with an improper motive or through knowingly wrongful means.

The police may still be considered as prosecuting an offence for the purpose of tort liability even after transferring the case to an independent prosecutor, as they retain a duty to inform the prosecutor of relevant matters that subsequently come to light.

relationships.description: The judgment cites McHugh J's statement from Osland regarding liability in joint enterprise not invariably requiring presence at the scene.
The judgment cites the High Court of Australia's reasoning in McAuliffe regarding liability for acts done in pursuit of a common purpose.
The judgment applies the principle from this CFA case, where a murder conviction was upheld despite the appellant not being present during the attack.

relationships.target_case_name: Rees v Commissioner of Police for the Metropolis
relationships.description: The judgment cites Rees for the principle that honest belief in guilt does not negate malice if the prosecution is tainted by serious wrongdoing.

relationships.target_case_name: Clifford v Chief Constable of Hertfordshire Constabulary
relationships.description: The judgment cites Clifford via a textbook for the principle on police continuing duty after transferring a prosecution.

candidates.case_name: PAC FUNG FEATHER CO LTD v. THE INCORPORATED OWNERS OF HOI LUEN INDUSTRIAL CENTRE AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Leung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A defense of justification in defamation succeeds if the defendant demonstrates that the substance or 'sting' of the published allegation is substantially true, even if specific examples or details within the publication are not proven exactly as stated.

When determining the meaning of allegedly defamatory material, the court should consider the overall impression the material makes on a reasonable person, having regard to all the circumstances, and then check that impression against detailed textual arguments.

A defendant is entitled to justify a defamatory publication by proving the truth of a 'common sting' or general imputation derived from the publication as a whole, provided such a common theme exists.

A justification defense to a general charge should not necessarily fail merely because a specific example cited in the publication is more serious than what is proved at trial, if the unproven particular does not cause material additional injury to the claimant's reputation.

A defense of qualified privilege is available for communications made on a privileged occasion, provided the content of the communication does not exceed the reasonable limits of that privilege in the circumstances.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Cruise by implying its facts were different, as it involved an attempt to justify an imputation (hypocrisy) by reference to an unconnected allegation (Church membership).

candidates.case_name: PANG SIU WING alias PANG WING v. CHUNGSHAN COMMERCIAL ASSOCIATION, HONG KONG
judges: Before: Hon Lok J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The malice of an individual director or agent can be attributed to a corporation by applying ordinary principles of agency, where the individual's state of mind is considered the state of mind of the corporation.
To establish dishonesty or improper motive, there must be a sufficient evidential basis; mere suspicion or the late discovery of information does not prove prior knowledge or wilful falsehood.

A defence of justification for defamation fails if the plaintiff is found not to have acted dishonestly or in breach of relevant rules, as the underlying factual basis for the defamatory statement is untrue.

relationships.target_case_name: Tam Heung Man v The Incorporated Owners of Lung Poon Court
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Tam Heung Man that the malice of an individual (like a chairman) can be attributed to a corporation using ordinary agency principles.

candidates.case_name: COOMBER, PETER JOHN v. WONG KING WAH
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Jonathan Wong in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Judicial officers are immune from civil liability for damages when performing their judicial duties, even if they make errors of fact or law.

candidates.case_name: THAPA KAMALA v. HO LAI MING (Deputy Special Magistrate at Eastern Magistrates’ Courts)
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge H. Au-Yeung (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: When considering a strike-out application based on no reasonable cause of action, the court must assume the facts alleged in the statement of claim are true and cannot consider evidence, addressing the matter solely on the pleadings.

If a pleading is defective only due to a lack of particulars to which the opposing party is entitled, the correct approach is to order the provision of those particulars rather than striking out the pleading.

Questions of law can be determined in a strike-out application provided the underlying facts are certain, though courts may sometimes decline to decide difficult questions of law in such applications.

Whether a judicial officer enjoys immunity from suit depends on the effect and operation of the relevant statutory provisions; if those provisions allow an action to be brought, an argument based on judicial independence cannot succeed.

relationships.target_case_name: Park Young Sook v Sharon Melloy
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that Article 85 of the Basic Law is amplified by section 71 of the District Court Ordinance.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. K.D.D.H.
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

candidates.case_name: CHINA CONCRETE COMPANY LTD v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The court possesses jurisdiction to grant a declaration that certain conduct is not criminal, either regarding actual/threatened proceedings or future conduct, but this jurisdiction should only be exercised in truly exceptional circumstances.

A defendant in properly instituted criminal proceedings should not generally be permitted to seek a civil declaration to pre-empt or 'hive-off' the construction of a criminal statute from the criminal court, as this constitutes an impermissible intrusion into the criminal court's domain.

An allegation of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient particularity; particulars of facts that are merely consistent with honesty are insufficient, and the defendant must know the primary facts from which dishonesty is to be inferred.

principles.principle_label: Private law right enforcement involving public law
principles.paraphrase_en: A litigant possessing a private law right may enforce it through an ordinary action, even if the proceedings incidentally involve examination of a public law act or decision.

A plea of specific intent to injure requires facts from which such intent can be inferred; it is insufficient if the pleaded facts do not negate a genuine and honest belief in the lawfulness of the defendant's position.

relationships.target_case_name: Imperial Tobacco Ltd v Attorney General
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle from Imperial Tobacco that it is generally impermissible for a civil court to grant a declaration on criminality after criminal proceedings have commenced.

relationships.target_case_name: Roy v Kensington and Chelsea and Westminster Family Practitioner Committee
relationships.description: The judgment cites Roy for the principle that a private law right can be enforced by ordinary action even if it involves incidental examination of a public law decision.

candidates.case_name: HO KWAN YIU v. DENNIS KWOK WING HANG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may grant a stay of civil proceedings based on the relevance of related criminal convictions and sentences, the application of section 64 of the Evidence Ordinance, and general case management considerations.
In a trial before a jury, the court must exercise particular care regarding the type of evidence that is permitted to be placed before them.

candidates.case_name: LI SAU MUI v. MAN WAI CHEONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Joseph Vaughan in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: An order for indemnity costs requires the presence of special or unusual features in the case that justify a departure from the standard basis of taxation.

relationships.target_case_name: Town Planning Board v Society for the Protection of the Harbour Ltd (No 2)
relationships.description: The present judgment treats this case as the leading authority establishing the correct approach for awarding indemnity costs.

relationships.target_case_name: Commissioner of Inland Revenue v Poon Cho Ming John (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the summary of principles for indemnity costs from this case, as cited within it.

candidates.case_name: THE INCORPORATED OWNERS OF ALLWAY GARDENS v. LAM YUEN PUN
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge Harold Leong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court determines whether words are defamatory by applying the test of how a reasonable reader would understand them, considering the natural and ordinary meaning of the words in context, without requiring proof of the publisher's intention.

The term 'internally decided' or 'pre-determined' in the context of a tender process is defamatory as it implies a lack of due process, non-transparency, and potentially corrupt or sinister conspiracy.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for justification defense
principles.paraphrase_en: In a defamation case where the defense of justification is raised, the defendant bears the burden of proving the substantial truth of the defamatory statements.

The court will reject a justification defense when allegations are groundless, lack supporting evidence, have reasonable explanations, or are based on coincidence and circumstantial matters without evidence of sinister motive.

relationships.target_case_name: Oriental Daily Publisher Ltd. v Ming Pao Holdings Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case regarding the defendant's burden to prove truth or substantial truth in a justification defense to defamation.

candidates.case_name: LO CHI LIK ERIC v. YUEN CHI HO CHRIS
judges: Before : Hon Cheung and Chu JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant in a defamation case can succeed on the defense of justification by proving the substantial truth of the defamatory statement.

A defamatory statement can carry one of three levels of meaning: that the plaintiff actually committed a serious act, that there are reasonable grounds to suspect they committed it, or that there are grounds to investigate whether they are responsible. A defendant cannot justify a statement by merely proving grounds for suspicion.
When a single publication contains multiple separate and distinct defamatory imputations, each imputation is separately actionable.

principles.principle_label: Justification based on failure to acknowledge
principles.paraphrase_en: A defamatory statement questioning a person's professionalism or academic integrity for failing to acknowledge the source of an idea or contribution can be justified, even if the resulting work is not considered plagiarized due to different data.

candidates.case_name: CHIU KEI LEUNG v. CHUI DEON YAU HAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Jonathan Wong in Chambers (open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The Rules of the District Court draw a clear distinction between discontinuance of an entire action and withdrawal of a specific claim within an action.

principles.principle_label: Court's power to strike out on withdrawal application
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has the power to strike out a particular claim upon the claimant's application to withdraw that specific claim.

principles.principle_label: Timing of application as relevant consideration
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may consider the timing of an application, such as when it is made after all evidence has been given and the court could already assess the merits of the case.

relationships.target_case_name: Albright & Wilson v SB Chemicals Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment references Aldous J's observation that previous cases on leave to discontinue did not establish a clear principle as they were fact-specific.

candidates.case_name: PANG SIU WING alias PANG WING v. CHUNGSHAN COMMERCIAL ASSOCIATION, HONG KONG
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and G Lam JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court should exercise caution and require the clearest grounds before overturning a trial judge's finding that exonerates a party of dishonesty or want of probity.

The defence of qualified privilege for a defamatory publication can be defeated if the manner and extent of the publication (e.g., in multiple newspapers on consecutive days) is excessive and disproportionate, particularly where the association's own rules indicate disclosure of reasons for an expulsion is unnecessary.

A defendant in a defamation action cannot rely on a defence of justification for meanings they have put forward (Lucas-Box meanings) if those meanings do not correspond to the defamatory meanings alleged by the plaintiff and found by the court.

relationships.target_case_name: Central Bank of Ecuador & Ors v Conticorp SA & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that an appeal court should be very cautious in overturning a trial judge's finding exonerating a party of dishonesty.
The judgment cites this case as authority for the proposition that a finding exonerating a party of dishonesty should not be displaced on appeal except on the clearest grounds.

candidates.case_name: WELL BORN REAL ESTATE MANAGEMENT LTD v. LEE TAK LUN ANDREW (Also known as Andrew Lee)
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Harold Leong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining the meaning of a statement, the relevant context is what a reasonable person, considering all circumstances, would understand to be the context.
The court must determine the ordinary and natural meaning of allegedly defamatory words by examining them within their proper context.
A defence of qualified privilege is defeated if the defendant did not hold a subjective honest belief in the truth of the published statement, which constitutes malice.
For a defence of qualified privilege to succeed, the defendant must have subjectively held an honest belief in the truth of the statement at the time of publication.
The defence of qualified privilege requires that the defendant's motive in publishing the statement was to further a legitimate common interest, not to knowingly publish untruths.

candidates.case_name: WOON THEAN MUN v. HONG KONG AIRLINES LTD
judges: Before: Hon Marlene Ng J in Court

principles.principle_label: New points on appeal - fairness principle
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court should generally refuse to entertain a new legal point not raised in the first instance proceedings, based on the foundational imperative of fairness to prevent unfair surprise to the respondent.

New points on appeal that are fact-sensitive or would have affected the course of evidence or conduct of the case at the hearing below should not be allowed, even if they do not strictly necessitate further evidence.

The Flywin principle restricting new points on appeal, though originating in civil trial contexts, is a principle of general application that also applies to appeals against tribunal awards or determinations on points of law.

It is generally not open for an appellant on appeal against a tribunal determination to take a point that was not pleaded in the proceedings below, even though tribunal hearings are conducted informally.
On appeal, the court should not allow the appeal merely on grounds of misdirection or improper admission/rejection of evidence unless substantial wrong or miscarriage of justice has been occasioned.

relationships.target_case_name: Flywin Co Ltd v Strong & Associates Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Flywin doctrine as a discretionary principle preventing new points on appeal based on fairness considerations.
The judgment cites this case to support that the Flywin principle is one of general application beyond civil trials.

relationships.target_case_name: Nice & Well Ltd v Fu Mee Yuk Shirley
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority that points not pleaded below are generally not open to an appellant on appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheng Loon Yin v Secretary for Justice & anor
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in discussing the limitations on raising new points on appeal.
The judgment references the Ladd v Marshall requirements as relevant to the admission of fresh evidence on appeal.

candidates.case_name: 莊如芬 v. 黃志輝 AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Aidan Tam in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Test for permanent injunction in libel
principles.paraphrase_en: The court will grant a permanent injunction in a defamation case if there is a reasonable basis to believe the defendant will publish the defamatory material again in the future.

relationships.description: The judgment references the 12th edition of this authoritative text to support the stated legal test for granting permanent injunctions.

candidates.case_name: CHAN KWOK KUEN v. LAI YAT HO AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: There is no automatic rule that costs must be awarded on an indemnity basis when an ex parte order is discharged due to material non-disclosure. An indemnity basis is typically appropriate only if the non-disclosure was deliberate or seriously culpable.

A factual assertion made in support of a legal application must be supported by evidence from a person with direct knowledge of that fact. An affirmation from a solicitor, without evidence from the client, may be insufficient to establish the client's state of knowledge.

relationships.description: The judgment cites the commentary in Hong Kong Civil Procedure 2026 regarding the principles for awarding costs on an indemnity basis following the discharge of an ex parte order.

candidates.case_name: COOMBER, PETER JOHN v. MR. PANG CHUNG PING
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Jonathan Wong in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The court has the power to strike out a statement of claim and dismiss an action where the claim is frivolous, vexatious, or discloses no reasonable cause of action, particularly when it is part of a pattern of unmeritorious litigation.

principles.principle_label: Misfeasance in public office as cause of action
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff may rely on the tort of misfeasance in public office as a cause of action against members and staff of the judiciary whom the plaintiff alleges to have committed wrongful acts in their official capacity.

Where a plaintiff has previously commenced multiple actions against different parties arising from the same underlying events, and those prior actions have been dismissed, the court may view subsequent related actions as an abuse of process.

relationships.target_case_name: Coomber, Peter John v. Mr. Wong King Wah
relationships.description: The present judgment references the prior related action DCCJ 1956/2022, which was dismissed by the same judge.

relationships.target_case_name: Coomber, Peter John v. Ms. Lau Yin Ha Eva & Ms. Cheung Man Ying Sandy
relationships.description: The present judgment references the prior related action DCCJ 3416/2022, which was also dismissed by the same judge.

candidates.case_name: HO KWAN YIU v. DENNIS KWOK WING HANG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Jury trial unsuitability for Reynolds privilege
principles.paraphrase_en: Cases where a defendant relies on Reynolds privilege or involve mixed law and fact are particularly unsuitable for jury trial due to the complex division of functions between judge and jury and the need for specific factual findings.

principles.principle_label: Broad picture vs small details test
principles.paraphrase_en: The court should consider whether the dispute will be determined by examining a broad overall picture rather than by analyzing small detailed points.

principles.principle_label: Defamation issues best determined by jury
principles.paraphrase_en: The core issues in a defamation trial are best decided by ordinary citizens who can apply their life experience to the case.

candidates.case_name: CONVOY COLLATERAL LTD v. CHO KWAI CHEE (also known as CHO KWAI CHEE ROY) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: HONG KONG FAR INFRARED RAYS ASSOCIATION LTD v. LAM HONG NAM AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Court

candidates.case_name: SHENZHEN HONEYCOMB SYSTEM CO LTD v. HCT TECHNOLOGIES (HONG KONG) CO LTD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Indemnity costs may be ordered against a party who pursues an appeal that lacks reasonable prospects of success, particularly where the appeal appears to be a delaying tactic against the enforcement of an arbitral award.

A court may refuse to stay the enforcement of an arbitral award pending an appeal if it finds the appeal has no reasonable prospect of success and if enforcement would be prejudiced by delay, especially where the award creditor is a foreign entity.
A procedural challenge to the authority of a party to institute enforcement proceedings for an arbitral award can be dismissed with costs if the challenge lacks merit.

relationships.description: The judgment references the prior decisions of Mimmie Chan J and Anthony Chan J in the same proceedings without expressing disagreement.

candidates.case_name: GERMAN POOL GROUP CO LTD AND OTHERS v. DYSON TECHNOLOGY LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kent Yee in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A court order requiring disclosure must precisely identify the specific documents or categories of documents to be disclosed. Failure to do so may expose the person making the disclosure list to liability for swearing a false affidavit, even if they acted honestly.
The assessment of compensation payable under a cross-undertaking in damages is determined by the proper construction of the terms of that specific undertaking.

principles.principle_label: Undertaking as sole basis for damages claim
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for damages arising from the grant of interlocutory relief can only be made if, and to the extent that, it is permitted by the terms of the undertaking given to the court.
The object of a cross-undertaking in damages is to compensate the party affected by wrongly granted interlocutory relief, not to punish the party who obtained the relief.
Exemplary damages are only available in a few confined categories established at common law, such as cases involving oppressive, arbitrary, or unconstitutional action by government servants.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that disclosure orders must be precise to avoid liability for a false affidavit.

relationships.target_case_name: Al-Rawas v Pegasus Energy Ltd and Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that compensation under a cross-undertaking depends on the construction of its terms.
The judgment follows the House of Lords' categorization that confines exemplary damages to specific categories, including oppressive government action.

The judgment distinguishes the present facts from this case, rejecting the argument that mass publication of an injunction order constitutes a similar misuse falling within an exemplary damages category.

candidates.case_name: NGAI WAI YEUNG v. LAU YUN FUNG BERTHA
judges: Before: Master Matthew Ho in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Test for striking out defamation claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A defamation claim should only be struck out if it is plain and obvious that the words complained of are incapable of bearing any defamatory meaning. The application is decided on the pleadings alone, assuming the pleaded facts are true, and the claim should not be struck out unless its legal basis is unarguable or almost incontestably bad.

A statement is defamatory if it tends to discredit the claimant, lower them in the estimation of others, cause them to be shunned or avoided, or expose them to hatred, contempt or ridicule. This includes statements that impute unfitness or lack of ability to a person in any office or profession.

For malicious falsehood, the plaintiff must prove that the complained words are false and were made with malice. Falsity is determined by what the words would convey to an ordinary reader. If a person says something they know to be untrue, that constitutes malice in itself.

Statements that arguably attack a plaintiff's efficiency or professional ability prima facie establish a defamatory meaning. An imputation of inefficiency or incompetence in a person's profession or calling is usually defamatory, even if it does not necessarily lower them in general estimation.

principles.principle_label: Striking out where facts are contentious
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the legal validity of a cause of action depends on disputed facts, and those facts are taken in favour of the party sought to be struck out, an order to strike out should not be made. The claim should proceed to trial if factual issues are contentious.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Wing Ho v Chong Lai Wah
relationships.description: The judgment applies the test from this case that defamation claims should only be struck out if it is plain and obvious the words are incapable of bearing a defamatory meaning.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that a claim should not be struck out unless its legal basis is unarguable or almost incontestably bad.

relationships.target_case_name: Suen Wah Fai v Cis Global Opportunities Fund SPC
relationships.description: The judgment applies the approach from this case that disputed facts should be taken in favour of the party sought to be struck out.

relationships.target_case_name: Fan Chak Wah Edmond v Lam Wai Keung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that falsity in malicious falsehood is determined by what the words would convey to an ordinary reader.

relationships.target_case_name: Golden Field Glass Works Co Ltd v Yeung Chun Keung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that statements attacking professional efficiency prima facie establish defamatory meaning.

relationships.target_case_name: Sim Hok Gwan v Tin Tin Yat Po Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that imputations of professional inefficiency are usually defamatory.

candidates.case_name: STEPHEN KRUGER v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Hon S T Poon J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Police officers may lawfully act without an arrest or search warrant if they believe they are authorized by a specific statutory provision, such as section 17C of the Immigration Ordinance.

candidates.case_name: CHAN SHUNG FAI v. CHAN KAM WAH
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Kenneth K. H. Lee in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may find a witness credible and reliable based on candid and coherent testimony, limited cross-examination on key facts, and the opposing counsel's restraint from attacking the witness's credibility in submissions.

principles.principle_label: Effect of belated apology on damages
principles.paraphrase_en: An apology issued at a very late stage of proceedings, particularly when it appears to be a tactical move rather than genuine, may be considered practically worthless and not a valid basis for reducing an award of damages.

A defendant's own witness statement admitting a clear connection between the publication of defamatory material and a specific event (like an election) can be used to establish the context and purpose of the publication.

A defendant's explanation for their motive, raised for the first time shortly before trial and absent from their original witness statement, may be rejected by the court if it contradicts earlier evidence.

principles.principle_label: Use of comparable cases for quantum
principles.paraphrase_en: In assessing damages for defamation, the court may consider schedules of comparable cases categorized by the nature of the publication (e.g., media, non-media, internet) to determine an appropriate award.

relationships.target_case_name: Hung Yuen Chan Robert v Sing Tao Ltd & Anor
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that a very late apology may be worthless and not reduce damages.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Iu Seng v Ko Choi Hin & Ors
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case as a comparable authority for assessing damages in a defamation claim involving individuals with public standing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE WAI MAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, M Poon JA and A Pang JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant cannot rely on the effects of voluntarily consumed alcohol to establish the defence of diminished responsibility, as the law holds individuals responsible for the effects of alcohol they choose to drink.

The existence of some voluntary intoxication does not automatically remove the defence of diminished responsibility; the jury must consider the broader evidence and whether the defendant's condition overrode their control.
The jury must take a broad common-sense view of all the evidence to determine whether alcohol consumption was voluntary or compelled by the defendant's condition.

relationships.description: The judgment aligns with the House of Lords' view that voluntary intoxication should not completely exclude the defence of diminished responsibility.
The judgment applies the principle from Wood that the law on voluntary intoxication and diminished responsibility is not absolute or 'black and white'.
The judgment applies the ratio from Stewart, clarifying that voluntary intoxication does not automatically negate the defence of diminished responsibility.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SIU WAI CHEONG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and A Pang J in Court

principles.principle_label: Provocation requires causal link to loss of control
principles.paraphrase_en: For the defense of provocation to arise, the jury must find that the alleged provoking conduct actually caused the defendant to lose self-control.
When assessing whether an omission in jury directions on provocation constitutes an error, the court considers whether the omitted words could have caused conceivable prejudice to the defense.

principles.principle_label: Intoxication and specific intent for murder
principles.paraphrase_en: The jury must decide whether the defendant had the specific intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm at the time of the act, even if intoxicated; if intoxication prevented formation of that intent, the defendant is not guilty of murder.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG KIE CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI CHEUNG CHOI
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Lord Hoffmann NPJ

principles.principle_label: Hearsay nature of statements to experts
principles.paraphrase_en: Statements made by a defendant to an expert witness are admissible only as evidence of the factual basis for the expert's opinion, not as evidence of the truth of those statements.
In Hong Kong, the judiciary cannot create new exceptions to the hearsay rule; this is a matter for the legislature.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of expert opinion with disputed foundation
principles.paraphrase_en: In a jury trial, where a defence like diminished responsibility is raised, it is rarely appropriate for a judge to rule an expert opinion inadmissible merely because its factual basis is disputed, as this would improperly withdraw the defence from the jury's consideration.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for diminished responsibility
principles.paraphrase_en: The burden of proving the partial defence of diminished responsibility lies on the defendant and must be discharged by admissible evidence.

principles.principle_label: Medical history not a hearsay exception
principles.paraphrase_en: Medical history or background information about an accused's mental condition does not fall within any common law exception to the hearsay rule.

relationships.description: The judgment affirms the Court of Appeal's holding in Kissel regarding the hearsay nature of statements to experts and the exclusion of one expert's opinion within another's report.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Wai Man & Others v HKSAR
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Bokhary PJ's caution against the judiciary creating new hearsay exceptions.
The judgment applies the same stance taken by Ribeiro PJ that the legislature, not the judiciary, creates hearsay exceptions.

relationships.target_case_name: Myers v Director of Public Prosecutions
relationships.description: The judgment cites the majority principle from Myers regarding the judicial role in relation to hearsay.
The judgment notes that the House of Lords in Blastland followed the principle from Myers regarding hearsay.
The judgment references that Kissel cited Ramsay v Watson for the principle that a defendant's statements to an expert are not evidence of their truth.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE MAN LOK
judges: Before: Hon Kwok J in court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may impose a Hospital Order under section 45 of the Mental Health Ordinance as the most suitable disposal method after considering the nature of the offence, the defendant's character and background, psychiatric evidence, and other available sentencing options.

Where psychiatric evidence establishes that a defendant was suffering from a severe mental illness at the time of the offence, and that the illness substantially impaired their mental responsibility, rational judgment, and self-control, this is a significant mitigating factor in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIU KA KI
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM WAI MAN STEPHANIE
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing, courts should primarily compare the present case to past authorities involving factually similar circumstances, such as the killing of one's own child, and may find cases with dissimilar circumstances less helpful.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of factual similarity in sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: A key factor in determining the relevance of a past sentencing authority is the similarity of the method and circumstances of the offense, such as a premeditated plan involving suicide, administering sedatives, and causing death by carbon monoxide poisoning.

relationships.description: The present judgment finds the circumstances of the killing in Lee Fung Yee to be similar to the present case for the purpose of comparative sentencing analysis.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YANG LEYING
judges: Before: Hon Kwok J in court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may impose a hospital order under section 45 of the Mental Health Ordinance when, after considering the nature of the offense, the defendant's character and background, psychiatric evidence, and other available sentencing options, it determines this to be the most suitable disposal method.

Psychiatric evidence regarding a defendant's mental state, insight into illness, compliance with treatment, and need for supervised care is a relevant factor in determining the appropriate sentencing disposition.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG PO YAN
judges: Coram: Hon Pang JA, M Poon JA and Andrew Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for insanity defense
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal burden of proving the defense of insanity rests on the defendant, based on the presumption that every person is sane and responsible for their crimes.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of innocence and prosecution burden
principles.paraphrase_en: In criminal cases, the general burden of proof remains on the prosecution due to the presumption of innocence protected by the Basic Law and the Hong Kong Bill of Rights Ordinance.
The presumption of sanity applies to both the general issue of guilt and any special issues concerning the defendant's mental state.

principles.principle_label: Reverse burden and presumption of innocence
principles.paraphrase_en: Imposing a reverse legal burden of proof on the defendant regarding a defense may infringe upon the defendant's right to the presumption of innocence.
The prosecution is not limited to medical evidence to prove a defendant's mental state; it may rely on witness testimony, recorded interviews, and other relevant evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for its discussion on the burden of proof for the defenses of insanity and diminished responsibility.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI CHEUNG CHOI
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, McWalters and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Evidential threshold for suicide pact defence
principles.paraphrase_en: For the partial defence of suicide pact to be left to the jury, there must be sufficient evidence that the deceased had a settled intention of dying and was a willing party to a common agreement to die together. Speculative possibility is insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of psychiatric evidence based on unproven history
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert psychiatric opinion based on a patient's history may be admissible even if the factual foundation of that history is not independently proven, though its weight may be affected. Exclusion is not mandatory in such circumstances.

principles.principle_label: Non-hearsay use of statements to experts
principles.paraphrase_en: Statements made to a psychiatrist are admissible as non-hearsay when their relevance lies solely in the fact they were said (to explain the basis of the expert's opinion), not in proving the truth of the facts asserted within them.

relationships.description: The judgment references this Australian ruling in the context of arguments about the admissibility of an accused's statements to psychiatrists.
The court disagrees that the principle from this case on non-hearsay evidence has the broad application argued by counsel, confining it to its specific context.
The judgment applies the two categories of admissibility for expert evidence based on patient history established in this Australian authority.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG MAN KONG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An expert witness is permitted to give opinion evidence on matters within their specialized field, whereas ordinary witnesses are generally restricted to testifying about facts they directly observed or heard.
The jury is not obliged to accept an expert witness's opinion, especially if the factual basis for that opinion conflicts with the jury's own findings of fact.
