Factors indicating a planned, determined, and persistent attack, followed by further violence, can support a finding that the defendant did not lose self-control.
Leave to appeal must be refused if the proposed ground of appeal is not reasonably arguable.
An applicant has the right to renew a refused leave application to the Court of Appeal, but the court may order loss of time spent in custody if the renewal is unjustified.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG MAN YI MANDY
judges: Before: Hon Yeung VP, D Pang JA and M Poon J in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for insanity defence
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal burden of proof (persuasive burden) is placed on the defendant who raises the defence of insanity. This is an exception to the general principle that the prosecution bears the burden of proving guilt.
Everyone is presumed in law to be sane and to possess sufficient mental faculties to be held criminally responsible for their actions.

principles.principle_label: Proportionality of reverse onus for insanity
principles.paraphrase_en: Imposing a persuasive burden on a defendant to prove the defence of insanity is a reasonable and proportionate restriction on the right to be presumed innocent.
cited_statutes: Article 11(1) of the Hong Kong Bill of Rights Ordinance

principles.principle_label: General burden of proof in criminal cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The fundamental principle in criminal prosecutions is that the burden of proving the defendant's guilt rests on the prosecution.

relationships.description: The judgment affirms the principle from Woolmington that the prosecution bears the general burden of proof in criminal cases.
The judgment applies the exception established in M’Naghten’s case, which places the burden of proof for insanity on the defendant.
The judgment follows the Hong Kong precedent set in Lam Kwong Choi, which imposed a persuasive burden on a defendant raising insanity.
The judgment cites Chaulk to support the conclusion that reversing the onus for insanity is a reasonable restriction on the presumption of innocence.
The judgment cites Heffernan, which concurred with analyses that the reverse burden for insanity satisfies the proportionality test.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM KWOK-WING
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BUI VAN-CUONG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and Anthea Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For the defense of provocation to arise, there must be provoking conduct that caused the defendant to lose self-control. If no such provocation exists, the defendant is guilty of murder.

When assessing provocation, the jury must consider whether a person of ordinary self-control would have reacted as the accused did, taking into account the accused's entire factual situation including history, experiences, background, and personal attributes.
The law of provocation should not indirectly incorporate the concept of diminished responsibility, as these are distinct legal defenses with different requirements.

relationships.description: The judgment references Lord Simon's approach in Camplin regarding consideration of the 'entire factual situation' when assessing provocation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWOK WAI YIN
judges: Before: Hon Wong J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHIU LAI HA
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

principles.paraphrase_en: A hospital order under section 45 of the Mental Health Ordinance may be the most suitable sentence for manslaughter when psychiatric evidence supports it and considering the offender's circumstances, character, and available sentencing options.

When sentencing for manslaughter, the court must consider all relevant circumstances including the nature of the offence, the defendant's character and antecedents, personal mitigation, psychiatric evidence, and alternative sentencing methods.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI CHEUNG CHOI (李長再)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ and Mr Justice Lam PJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge is only required to leave the defence of suicide pact to the jury if there is a sufficient evidential basis to support it.
The established legal principles concerning alternative verdicts govern when a judge must direct a jury on an alternative defence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies established principles concerning alternative verdicts to determine if a suicide pact defence should have been left to the jury.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO SAI YU
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant charged with murder may be convicted of manslaughter if they plead guilty to manslaughter on the basis of diminished responsibility and the prosecution accepts the plea.

For a homicide conviction, the prosecution must prove that the defendant's unlawful acts caused the victim's death, even if a pre-existing medical condition made the victim more vulnerable to the fatal injuries.
A defendant's statement made under police caution, where they admit to the act and provide a motive, is admissible as evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FUNG CHUN KWAI
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG MEI TAK
judges: Before: Hon Toh J

principles.paraphrase_en: A jury's verdict of manslaughter on the grounds of diminished responsibility is accepted as the factual and legal basis for sentencing, shifting the focus from murder to the defendant's impaired mental responsibility.

The court may consider psychiatric and psychological reports to understand the defendant's background and character, including the absence of a stereotypical criminal disposition, as part of the sentencing exercise.

Expert assessments of a high risk of violent recidivism, based on factors like unpredictable violent outbursts, lack of warning signs, and inability to manage anger, are relevant considerations for sentencing and risk management.
Evidence that a defendant retains a definite ability to exert self-control when choosing to do so is a pertinent factor for the court to evaluate in the sentencing context.

In sentencing for a violent offence, the court treats the quick escalation of grievances into extreme violence, coupled with limited remorse and a lack of empathy towards the victim, as indicators of high risk and seriousness.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIU SIU TJIN
judges: Before: Hon Toh J

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may approve the prosecution's acceptance of a plea to manslaughter instead of murder when psychiatric reports establish a basis of diminished responsibility under the relevant ordinance.

A long-standing diagnosis of paranoid schizophrenia, characterized by symptoms such as suspiciousness and referential delusions, can form the factual and medical foundation for a finding of diminished responsibility.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO PO KWONG
judges: Before: Hon Yau J

principles.principle_label: Murder requires proof of specific intent
principles.paraphrase_en: To secure a murder conviction, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant unlawfully killed the victim with the specific intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm.

principles.principle_label: Diminished responsibility as a partial defence
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may raise the defence of diminished responsibility, arguing that their mental responsibility for the killing was substantially impaired by an abnormality of mind, which if accepted, reduces murder to manslaughter.
A defendant may raise the defence of provocation, arguing that they were provoked to lose self-control, which if accepted, reduces murder to manslaughter.
It is for the jury to determine, based on all the evidence, whether the prosecution has proven the requisite intent for murder.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI CHEUNG CHOI
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Pang and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Time limit for CFA leave application
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for leave to appeal to the Court of Final Appeal must be filed within 28 days from the date of the Court of Appeal's decision.

An application for certification of a point of law by the Court of Appeal should be made immediately after its judgment is delivered, as delay may hinder compliance with the time limit for applying for leave to appeal to the Court of Final Appeal.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAW WAN TAK
judges: Before: DHCJ McWalters

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BUI VAN-CUONG
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Stock NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing provocation, the jury must first determine if the defendant was provoked, then evaluate the gravity of that provocation from the defendant's perspective considering all circumstances and any personal characteristics affecting its impact, and finally decide whether an ordinary person with normal self-control would have reacted as the defendant did.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWAN TAT YEE
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, D Pang and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A Liberato direction to the jury is required only when the case turns on choosing between the credibility of the prosecution's account and the defendant's account.
The Liberato direction does not apply to situations involving inconsistent statements by a defense witness or conflicting evidence presented by the defense itself.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the established scope of the Liberato direction.

relationships.target_case_name: Law Chung Ki & Another v HKSAR
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the established scope of the Liberato direction.
The judgment cites this case as authority for the established scope of the Liberato direction.
The judgment cites this case as authority for the established scope of the Liberato direction.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG YAN KIN
judges: Before: Hon Campbell‑Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: If evidence is relevant and admissible, and the jury is entitled to consider it and assign appropriate weight, it is generally artificial to withhold the document itself from the jury unless specific prejudicial content is identified.

principles.principle_label: Judicial discretion to exclude prejudicial evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The court retains discretion to exclude a document from the jury if it contains actually prejudicial material that outweighs its probative value.
The burden lies with the party seeking to exclude a document to provide evidence or submissions demonstrating specific prejudice.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEN XUEPING
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

principles.paraphrase_en: A plea of guilty to manslaughter based on the defence of diminished responsibility under the Homicide Ordinance can be accepted by the prosecution in satisfaction of a murder charge.

For the defence of diminished responsibility to apply, the defendant must have been suffering from an abnormality of mind (such as a depressive disorder) arising from an underlying condition, which substantially impaired their mental responsibility for the acts leading to the killing.

When sentencing for manslaughter on grounds of diminished responsibility, the court should consider calling for psychiatric and other expert reports to assess the defendant's mental state and appropriate sentencing options.

A hospital order under the relevant ordinance is a recommended sentencing option where psychiatric reports indicate it is appropriate for a defendant convicted of an offence linked to mental disorder.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ISLAM JAVED IQBAL
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Appeal retains discretion to decide whether an application for leave to appeal against conviction or sentence should continue or abate following the death of the applicant/appellant, and must consider all relevant factors in exercising that discretion.

principles.principle_label: Factors for continuing appeal after death
principles.paraphrase_en: When exercising discretion regarding an appeal after an appellant's death, the court should consider factors set out in relevant authorities such as Cheng Chee Tock Theodore and Smith.
The court must ensure that any continued appeal proceeds on a proper adversarial basis, with proper legal representation and argument of the issues.
The willingness of another party to be substituted as appellant is relevant if the court considers substitution necessary for the appeal to continue.
The court may consider the availability of legal aid to ensure appropriate legal representation for a continued appeal after an appellant's death.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the approach established in Tang Tik Hung regarding appeals after an appellant's death.
The judgment applies factors set out in Cheng Chee Tock Theodore when exercising discretion on continuing appeals after death.
The judgment applies factors from Smith as helpful guidance for exercising discretion on appeals after death.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WAHAJ FYAZ
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and Anthea Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A jury may only draw an inference against a defendant if it is the only reasonable inference that can be drawn from the proven facts. If there is a reasonable inference in the defendant's favor as well as an adverse one, the adverse inference must not be drawn.
A party may not cross-examine their own witness unless the court finds the witness is hostile, meaning the witness is not desirous of telling the truth at the instance of the party calling them.

If a witness shows themselves to be decidedly adverse, the trial judge has a discretion at common law to allow them to be cross-examined, and has discretion over the mode of examination to best serve the purposes of justice.

principles.principle_label: Prohibition on knowingly calling hostile witness for inadmissible statement
principles.paraphrase_en: It is wrong in principle for the prosecution to knowingly call a witness who is hostile for the purpose of putting before the jury a prior statement that is itself inadmissible to prove facts against the accused.

The test for applying the proviso to dismiss an appeal despite an error is whether a reasonable and properly instructed jury, acting on the evidence properly before it, would inevitably have convicted.

relationships.description: The judgment references statements of principle from this case concerning inferential reasoning in criminal trials.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that it is wrong to knowingly call a hostile witness to put an inadmissible prior statement before the jury.
The judgment follows the holding from this case regarding the judge's discretion to allow cross-examination of a decidedly adverse witness.
The judgment distinguishes the present case from Henderson, which involved controversial expert evidence as the sole determining factor of guilt.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI WUN YIN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae Acting CJHC, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Prosecuting counsel has a duty to conduct cross-examination responsibly and properly, and it is contrary to fundamental fairness to contest a matter known to be true or supported by substantive evidence.
A jury must be directed not to assume a defendant's knowledge or participation in a conspiracy based solely on their spousal relationship with a co-conspirator.
A defendant cannot be found guilty of conspiracy simply because they were lazy or negligent in their duties as a company director; specific knowledge and participation must be proven.
Cross-examination is a crucial part of the adversarial process, allowing parties to test the veracity and reliability of evidence and to assess witness credibility.

relationships.description: The judgment references Lyttle for the proposition that counsel's wide discretion in cross-examination is constrained by a duty to act responsibly and properly.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG NGOI YEE MARGARET (吳靄儀) (D3)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Lord Neuberger of Abbotsbury NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of proportionality operates at the constitutional level in Hong Kong to determine the permissible limits of restrictions on rights guaranteed by the Basic Law and the Hong Kong Bill of Rights.

principles.principle_label: Judicial acts not subject to constitutional review
principles.paraphrase_en: Judicial acts of the HKSAR courts are not subject to constitutional review for inconsistency with entrenched rights, as the courts exercise independent judicial power under the Basic Law.
The first stage in a constitutional challenge is to determine whether the impugned measure engages a constitutionally protected right; if it does not, the challenge fails at this stage.
The outcome of a proportionality analysis depends on the specific measure and context, with challenges to different rules relying on the same protected right potentially leading to different results.

relationships.target_case_name: Kwok Wing Hang v Chief Executive in Council
relationships.description: The present judgment notes that the Court of Final Appeal in Kwok Wing Hang disagreed with the Court of Appeal's proportionality assessment and held certain offences to be proportionate and valid.

relationships.target_case_name: Chiang Lily v Secretary for Justice
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Chiang Lily that certain powers vested by the Basic Law are not constitutionally reviewable.

relationships.target_case_name: Tam Sze Leung v Commissioner of Police
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from Tam Sze Leung that a constitutional right is not engaged if the impugned act is not attributable to a public authority.

relationships.target_case_name: Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Ltd v New World Development Co Ltd & Others
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where a right was not engaged because the body in question was not a 'court'.

relationships.target_case_name: Medical Council of Hong Kong v Helen Chan
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where a practice was held not to infringe the right to an independent and impartial tribunal.

relationships.target_case_name: Kong Yunming v Director of Social Welfare
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example of a successful rule challenge on proportionality grounds where a policy change was found to lack a legitimate aim.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LO LAI CHAU
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge is only required to give a self-defence direction to the jury if there is a proper evidential foundation for it in the case. The absence of any suggestion that the defendant was acting in self-defence means no such direction is necessary.

principles.principle_label: Jury direction on guilty plea to lesser offence
principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant has pleaded guilty to a lesser offence like manslaughter, but is on trial for a greater offence like murder, the judge must carefully consider what, if anything, to tell the jury about that plea, based on the evidential foundation.

A precedent concerning jury directions based on a single piece of circumstantial evidence is distinguishable where the factual circumstances are entirely different, such as when the defence at trial provided an alternative innocent explanation for the conduct in question.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Nguyen Anh Nga because it involved different circumstances where the defendant's reaction was the sole circumstantial evidence and the defence offered an innocent explanation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LO LAI CHAU
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge is not required to leave the issue of self-defense to the jury when the defense case at trial was not presented on that basis and there is no evidence to support it.

principles.principle_label: Disclosure of guilty plea to jury
principles.paraphrase_en: Mentioning a guilty plea to manslaughter to the jury during closing submissions is a tactical decision by the defense, not a consequence of a judicial ruling on the availability of a self-defense plea.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from a Privy Council case that a self-defense direction is not required if the issue was not raised at trial and lacks evidential support.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN MAN SUM IVAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, McWalters JA and Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When evidence exists upon which a jury could find provocation, counsel for either the Crown or the defendant has a duty to bring it to the judge's attention before the summing-up. The judge must then consider if the evidence justifies leaving the issue of provocation to the jury, and if so, is statutorily required to do so.

principles.principle_label: Threshold for leaving provocation to jury
principles.paraphrase_en: The judge must first determine if there is any evidence from which a reasonable jury could conclude the defendant was actually provoked to lose self-control. If no such evidence exists, the judge should not leave provocation to the jury. If such evidence exists, the issue must be left for the jury's consideration.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of evidence for loss of self-control
principles.paraphrase_en: The judge's responsibility in assessing whether to leave provocation to the jury is to examine all the evidence, taking it at its most favourable to the defendant, and ask whether the jury might conclude he had lost self-control. The defendant's own account is not conclusive; the judge must consider the totality of the evidence.

principles.principle_label: Judge's role on provoking conduct evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: If there is evidence on which a jury could find there was provoking conduct, the judge's role is limited to assessing the factual possibility of such conduct. It is not for the judge to determine whether the accused was in fact provoked by that conduct; that is a matter for the jury.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the Privy Council's reasoning on the judge's limited role when evidence of provoking conduct exists.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM HO NAM
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and M Poon JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant gives evidence, the jury must be directed that they can act on the defendant's evidence even if they only consider it might be true, not only if they find it to be true. The failure to give this direction creates a risk the jury might wrongly think they must accept the defendant's evidence as true to find a defence established.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of jury directions as a whole
principles.paraphrase_en: When evaluating whether a required direction (like the Liberato direction) was properly given, the summing-up must be viewed in its entirety to determine if the essential message was effectively conveyed to the jury.
A jury can only consider and act on a defendant's admissions made in a video-recorded interview if they are satisfied both that the admissions were made voluntarily and that they are true.

Hong Kong trial judges retain a broad discretion and a wide margin of assessment in deciding whether to give a good character or propensity direction to the jury, and are not bound by an absolute obligation to do so as in some other jurisdictions.

For the partial defence of provocation, which reduces murder to manslaughter, the prosecution bears the burden of proving beyond reasonable doubt that the killing was not a result of provocation. The defendant does not have to establish that it was.

relationships.description: The judgment references the principle from Jim Fai v HKSAR that a simple comparison of a defendant's accounts without proper direction may offend established legal principles.
Cited alongside Jim Fai v HKSAR for the principle that comparing a defendant's accounts without more may be problematic.
Referred to for the principle that the adequacy of a jury direction depends on whether it contains all required elements, not necessarily its specific wording.
Cited for the holding that Hong Kong judges have broad discretion regarding character directions and are not bound by the English Vye obligation.
The judgment notes that the obligation imposed on English judges by Vye to give good character directions does not apply to Hong Kong courts.
The judgment indicates the Court of Final Appeal in Pang Hiu San approved the common law position as stated in R v Mushtaq.

candidates.case_name: MA CHUN MAN v. COMMISSIONER OF CORRECTIONAL SERVICES
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Prisoners do not have a legal right or entitlement to remission; the Commissioner of Correctional Services has discretion to grant remission based on a prisoner's industry and good conduct.

principles.principle_label: NS Test as evaluative risk assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: The National Security Test for early release is a holistic, predictive, and evaluative exercise based on all relevant factors concerning the national security risk posed by a prisoner's release; it does not involve a legal burden of proof or a presumption against release.

A distinction exists between a measure that constitutes a penalty and a measure concerning the execution or enforcement of a penalty; changes to early release provisions generally relate to enforcement rather than the imposition of a new penalty.
The concept of a 'penalty' under the ICCPR and Hong Kong Bill of Rights is autonomous, requiring courts to assess the substance of a measure beyond its formal classification.

The imposition of supervision orders and recall to prison under the Post-release Supervision of Prisoners Ordinance does not constitute a heavier penalty under ICCPR Article 15 or Bill of Rights Article 12(1), as supervision is not motivated by punitive considerations.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the holding that supervision orders and recall under the PRSPO do not constitute a heavier penalty under relevant human rights provisions.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tak Wai v Commissioner of Correctional Services
relationships.description: The judgment cites the case for its description of CSD's past practice regarding the printing of computed release dates on prisoner identity tags.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Leung Hiu Yeung & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Final Appeal case as an example where remission was a factor taken into account in sentencing appeals.
The judgment cites this European Court of Human Rights case for the distinction between a measure constituting a penalty and one concerning the execution of a penalty.
The judgment cites this European Court of Human Rights case alongside Del Río Prada for the same principle distinguishing penalty from enforcement.

candidates.case_name: YUEN TSZ CHUN (as the administrator of the estate of CHAN CHOI LIN (陳彩蓮), deceased) v. LO CHI KEUNG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Court

principles.principle_label: Interpretation of s.8A(2) Intestates' Estates Ordinance
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the value of beneficial interests acquired by a surviving spouse under foreign intestacy law exceeds HKD 1,000,000, the reference to that 'net sum' in section 4(4) is omitted, and the distribution of the residuary estate proceeds directly under the provisions of section 4(4)(a) and (b).
The legislative intent behind the spousal accounting provisions in the Intestates' Estates Ordinance is to be ascertained and applied when interpreting the relevant sections.

principles.principle_label: Plain meaning rule of statutory interpretation
principles.paraphrase_en: The primary approach to interpreting a statutory provision is to give its words their plain and natural meaning, unless this contradicts the clear legislative intent.

relationships.description: The judgment cites In re Collens for the principle that succession to immovable property is governed by the lex situs (law of the place where the property is situated).

candidates.case_name: SUCCESS LANE DEVELOPMENT LTD v. IFC DEVELOPMENT (SUITE) LTD AS AGENT FOR IFC DEVELOPMENT LTD
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Alan Kwong in Chambers (Open to the Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A corporation may have standing to bring a claim for harassment in its own capacity, not because it can experience emotional distress, but because it may incur expenses to address harassment affecting its employees.
Courts will not decide hypothetical or academic questions that have no practical effect on the parties' rights or interests.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's reasoning that a corporation may bring a harassment claim based on costs incurred to address employee harassment.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that courts should not adjudicate academic controversies without practical utility.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI KAN YAU
judges: Before: Hon Macrae Acting CJHC, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence of an accused's conduct after an offence does not lose its probative value merely because it is equally consistent with multiple inferences or alternative explanations. Such evidence can still be submitted for the jury's consideration, provided a proper caution is given regarding its use.
It is generally unnecessary for a judge to give a jury any special direction on how to approach circumstantial evidence.
A decision or course of action taken by defence counsel at trial, which represents a reasonable forensic choice available to them, will not typically found a basis for appeal.

An appellate court will not engage in speculation about whether a different forensic strategy by trial counsel (such as seeking an advance ruling) would have produced a different outcome, as such arguments are based on multiple layers of hypothetical scenarios.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this Canadian case for the proposition that post-offence conduct evidence may have no probative value if equally explained by consciousness of guilt for multiple offences where culpability for one is admitted.
The judgment cites this subsequent Canadian Supreme Court case which explained the circumstances warranting a specific direction to a jury regarding post-offence conduct.
The judgment applies the principle from this case, reinforced by Bokhary NPJ, that special jury directions on circumstantial evidence are normally unnecessary.
The judgment applies the reasoning from this Australian case to reject an argument based on counsel's failure to seek a preliminary ruling, dismissing it as speculative.

candidates.case_name: PANG LOK SZE v. DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC PROSECUTIONS
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's power to review prosecutorial decisions is extremely limited and applies only in rare circumstances where there is evidence of bad faith or unconstitutional conduct by the Department of Justice.
Article 63 of the Basic Law protects the independence of the Department of Justice in controlling criminal prosecutions, shielding it from judicial interference.
A prosecutorial decision may be subject to challenge only if there is evidence it was made under political instruction or in bad faith, rendering it unconstitutional.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the Court of Appeal's approval in Re Leung Lai Fun of the principles established in RV v Director of Immigration.
The judgment applies the principle from RV v Director of Immigration regarding the protection of prosecutorial independence under Article 63 of the Basic Law.

candidates.case_name: SIR ELLY KADOORIE & SONS LTD (FOR AND ON BEHALF OF ITSELF, ITS CURRENT AND FORMER OFFICERS, EMPLOYEES AND AGENTS, INCLUDING ITS LEGAL REPRESENTATIVES, MESSRS SIMMONS & SIMMONS) v. SAMANTHA JANE BRADLEY
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge H. Au-Yeung in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Order 14A suitability - legal vs factual issues
principles.paraphrase_en: The Order 14A procedure for determining questions of law or document construction is inappropriate when factual issues are interwoven with the legal issues, as the court cannot decide legal questions based on assumed or hypothetical facts and would need to hear evidence to resolve factual disputes.

principles.principle_label: Order 14A scope - final determination of claims/issues
principles.paraphrase_en: Order 14A can be invoked not only to finally determine the entire cause or matter, but also to finally determine any specific claim or issue within it, subject to the court's discretion.

The question of law or document construction to be determined under Order 14A must be stated in clear, careful, and precise terms without difficulty, obscurity, or ambiguity regarding what is to be decided.
The court's discretion under Order 14A is wide, but the procedure is not intended for the determination of trivial matters, even if the formal requirements for its use are satisfied.

A challenge to a plaintiff's locus standi (standing to sue) must be raised at the outset of proceedings or when it comes to the attention of the court or defense, and must be decided promptly; it is inappropriate to raise it by way of defense and allow the action to proceed without deciding the standing issue.

relationships.target_case_name: Allen v Gulf Oil Refining Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Allen v Gulf Oil Refining Ltd as authority for the requirement that questions for determination under Order 14A must be formulated clearly and precisely.

relationships.target_case_name: Shell Hong Kong Ltd v Yeung Wai Man Kiu Yip Co Ltd & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that the Order 14A procedure should not be used for trivial matters, despite the court's wide discretion.

relationships.target_case_name: 佛山市宏達發展公司清算組 v East Legend Investment Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this Court of Appeal case regarding the proper timing and necessity for deciding challenges to a plaintiff's locus standi.

relationships.target_case_name: Korso Finance Establishment Anstalt v. John Wedge
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported Court of Appeal case as an example where specific factors were relevant to exercising discretion to allow a question to be determined under Order 14A.

candidates.case_name: SIR ELLY KADOORIE & SONS LTD (FOR AND ON BEHALF OF ITSELF, ITS CURRENT AND FORMER OFFICERS, EMPLOYEES AND AGENTS, INCLUDING ITS LEGAL REPRESENTATIVES, MESSRS SIMMONS & SIMMONS) v. SAMANTHA JANE BRADLEY
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court should only strike out a claim as a remedy of last resort, particularly when serious allegations require proper adjudication.
An employer owes a common law duty to provide a safe working environment, which includes protecting employees from abuse, ill-treatment, or harassment by others.
To establish the tort of harassment, it is sufficient to prove recklessness as to whether the victim would suffer injury; specific intent to cause injury is not required.
The court has jurisdiction to grant a free-standing injunction even in the absence of an existing cause of action against the person to be enjoined.
Equity provides flexible, discretionary injunctive relief, focusing on substance over form, with no rigid limiting rules apart from justice and convenience.

relationships.target_case_name: Wolverhampton City Council v London Gypsies and Travellers
relationships.description: The judgment references this UK Supreme Court case as a leading appellate decision on the availability of free-standing injunctions.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tai Wai David v The Hong Kong SAR Government
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the arguable existence of a tort of harassment, preventing strike-out at an interlocutory stage.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that an injunction is not always conditional on the existence of a cause of action.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. W.N.M.
judges: Before: HH Judge Stanley Chan

principles.paraphrase_en: A parent commits the offense of wilful ill-treatment or neglect of a child when they fail to provide adequate and proper medical treatment over a period, in a manner likely to cause the child unnecessary suffering or injury to health.
A conviction requires the prosecution to prove its case against the defendant beyond a reasonable doubt.
A court may find a witness not credible or reliable based on an assessment of their evidence, and such a finding can be supported by specific points from the testimony.

principles.principle_label: Fact-finding based on totality of evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A court's factual findings, including those relating to the elements of an offense, must be made by considering the totality of the evidence as a whole.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. L.L.K. AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: District Judge Clement Lee

principles.principle_label: Elements of ill-treatment or neglect of a child
principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits an offence under section 27 of the Offences against the Person Ordinance if they are over 16, have custody, charge, or care of a child under 16, and willfully assault, ill-treat, or neglect the child in a manner likely to cause unnecessary suffering or injury to health.

The legal duty under section 27 arises when a person over 16 has the custody, charge, or care of a child, which can be established during a specific period when the child is residing with and under the control of the defendants.

The offence requires willful conduct, which can be established by a pattern of assault, ill-treatment, or neglect, including verbal abuse and differential treatment, that is likely to cause the child unnecessary suffering or injury to health.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. IP CHUN YIN JERRY AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.paraphrase_en: When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offenses, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is just and proportionate, considering any overlapping factual circumstances between the offenses.
Where the factual considerations for separate charges overlap, the court may order part of the sentences to be served concurrently to achieve a fair overall sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. IU WAI CHEUNG
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court

relationships.description: The court mentions this case as an example of domestic context manslaughter sentencing but finds comparisons unhelpful.
The court cites this case as part of a group of domestic manslaughter cases but declines to draw comparisons.
The court references this case among others but explicitly states comparisons with other cases are not helpful.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Chan Man Sum Ivan
relationships.description: The court notes this domestic manslaughter case but finds comparisons to other cases unhelpful for sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BU SHUIQIANG
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

principles.principle_label: Sentencing discount for guilty plea after trial
principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant pleads guilty after contesting the case at trial (e.g., by raising defenses like self-defense or lack of intent), the sentencing discount for the guilty plea should be less than the full one-third normally given for an early plea.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for manslaughter by provocation
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must determine an appropriate starting point for a sentence of imprisonment for manslaughter by reason of provocation based on all the circumstances of the specific case.

principles.principle_label: Limited utility of comparative sentencing cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In sentencing for manslaughter, previous cases are of limited assistance due to the infinitely variable ways the offense can be committed, and reference should primarily be made to matters of general principle.

relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and rehearses the principle from Leung Yuk Ping regarding a reduced sentencing discount for a guilty plea entered after contesting the case.

relationships.target_case_name: Sze Kwan Lung & Ors v HKSAR
relationships.description: The judgment references this case as containing a definitive statement of principle on sentencing discounts, which was cited by counsel.
The judgment notes this case as one where the principle from Sze Kwan Lung was subsequently applied by the Court of Appeal.
The judgment notes this case as one where the principle from Sze Kwan Lung was subsequently applied by the Court of Appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Wong Kam Shing, Jackie
relationships.description: The judgment notes this case as one where the principle from Sze Kwan Lung was subsequently applied by the Court of Appeal.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAW LAM YING
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.principle_label: Applicability of legal principles across similar offences
principles.paraphrase_en: A legal principle established for one offence under the Offences against the Person Ordinance can be equally applicable to another offence under the same Ordinance, specifically from section 17 to section 19.
cited_statutes: Offences against the Person Ordinance s.17
Offences against the Person Ordinance s.19

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may consider evidence of impulsive action and provocation when assessing the defendant's intent and the circumstances of the offence.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing distinction based on context and deterrence
principles.paraphrase_en: Sentencing for wounding offences should distinguish between spontaneous incidents among acquaintances and confrontations with strangers in public, with the latter requiring a stronger element of public deterrence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. XIAO CHUNQIANG
judges: Before: Hon Pang, M Poon and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The right to a fair trial does not require a perfect trial; the court will assess claims of unfairness by taking a practical view of all the circumstances.
The video-recorded interview (VRI) itself is the evidence, while transcripts and translations are merely aids to assist the jury in understanding the VRI.

principles.principle_label: Jury's use of aids during deliberation
principles.paraphrase_en: During deliberations, the jury is entitled to review both the transcript and the video-recorded interview (VRI) if they wish to do so.
The jury must consider video-recorded interviews and re-enactments as a whole and must not analyze isolated segments out of context.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIU SUN KONG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Pang and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge must direct the jury on an alternative verdict only if the evidence supporting it is not so incredible, tenuous, or uncertain that no reasonable jury could accept it. This threshold is low and aligns with the prosecution's burden to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of defence case to alternative verdict
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining if sufficient evidence exists for an obvious alternative verdict, the way the defence case is factually presented is relevant. If a factual alternative does not arise from how the defence has dealt with the evidence, this will usually be decisive.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows Ho Hoi Shing in holding that the threshold for directing on alternative verdicts is a low one.
The judgment applies the principle from Chau Yui Ming regarding the relevance of the defence's factual presentation to the availability of an alternative verdict.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIMBU SANGAM
judges: Before: HH Judge Kathie Cheung

principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits the offence of wounding with intent under section 17(a) of the Offences against the Person Ordinance when they unlawfully and maliciously wound another person with the intent to cause grievous bodily harm.

principles.principle_label: Admission of facts in guilty plea
principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant pleads guilty, the court accepts the Amended Summary of Facts admitted by the defendant as the factual basis for sentencing.

principles.principle_label: Mitigation based on provocation and personal circumstances
principles.paraphrase_en: In sentencing for a violent offence, the court may consider mitigating factors including alleged provocation (such as racial slurs), the defendant's personal and family circumstances, stress, and lack of prior convictions.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LU KWOK LEUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

principles.principle_label: Conviction on plea and agreed facts
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant can be properly convicted based on their own guilty plea and agreement to an amended summary of facts presented by the prosecution.

The offense of wounding under section 19 of the Offences against the Person Ordinance is established by proving the act of inflicting injury, with the resulting harm constituting a wound or grievous bodily harm.
A statement made by a defendant under police caution, which includes an admission of wrongdoing and an explanation of the circumstances, is part of the admissible evidence in the case.
A medical report detailing the nature, extent, and healing process of injuries is relevant evidence for assessing the seriousness of a wounding offense.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO KA HO
judges: Before: HH Judge A. J. Woodcock

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who enters a guilty plea is entitled to a one-third reduction in the sentence that would otherwise be imposed.
In sentencing, the court must assess the relative weight of mitigating factors presented, and a guilty plea may be the only factor carrying substantial weight in certain cases.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG TUNG MO
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: The courts have a duty to denounce the use of violence to resolve domestic or interpersonal problems, even when those problems arise from stress or emotional outbursts within private home environments.
Whether an attack was premeditated or resulted from a sudden loss of self-control is a relevant factor for the court to consider in its assessment of the offense.
The court may consider the likelihood of re-offending, including changes in the offender's personal circumstances that might reduce future risk, as a relevant sentencing factor.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ZHOU FENG
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offence under section 17(a) of the Offences against the Person Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant unlawfully and maliciously wounded another person with the specific intent to cause that person grievous bodily harm.
The actus reus of the offence is satisfied by the act of wounding, which in this context is established by chopping the victim's head with a chopper, causing bleeding and a skull fracture.

principles.principle_label: Malice and intent for grievous bodily harm
principles.paraphrase_en: The mental element (mens rea) requires proof that the defendant acted maliciously and with the specific intent to cause grievous bodily harm, which can be inferred from the use of a dangerous weapon (a chopper) to attack a vulnerable part of the body (the head).

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MCENTEGART, MARTIN CHRISTIAN
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ALI KHURRAM
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court

candidates.case_name: L, T v. M, MD
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Sharon D. Melloy in Chambers (Not open to public). Paper disposal

principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to appeal requires an arguable case with a reasonable chance of success. An appellate court will not interfere with the exercise of a lower court's discretion unless it was clearly incorrect, or the judge was wrong on the law or a legal principle.
A party's reprehensible conduct during proceedings is a relevant factor for the court to consider when determining liability for costs.
There is a general rule that a party in a position to pay their own costs should do so, and a court will not lightly order another party to pay them in full absent good reason.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NGAI HON KWONG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Pang JA and A Pang J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person breaches their duty of care in gross negligence manslaughter by performing a dangerous act (like igniting a naked flame) before eliminating a known risk of harm, and that breach must cause death.
Whether an act constitutes gross negligence is a question of fact for the jury to determine based on the evidence presented.

principles.principle_label: Judge's duty to leave alternative verdict
principles.paraphrase_en: The trial judge must leave an alternative verdict (like manslaughter) to the jury if there is evidence capable of establishing each element of that offence, regardless of whether the jury might ultimately accept that evidence.

When an appellate court substitutes a conviction (e.g., from murder to manslaughter), it must independently assess the evidence and the defendant's culpability to determine the appropriate sentence for the substituted offence, not sentence based on a specific factual permutation not left to the jury.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. PANG YUK TING
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.paraphrase_en: A person can be guilty of manslaughter if they unlawfully kill another, even if they did not intend death or grievous bodily harm, provided the killing resulted from an unlawful and dangerous act.
For unlawful act manslaughter, the prosecution must prove that the defendant's unlawful act caused the victim's death. The act need not be directed at the victim but must be a substantial cause.
A guilty plea involves the defendant admitting not only the legal elements of the offence but also the summary of facts presented by the prosecution, which forms the factual basis for sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG HING LOK
judges: Before: Hon Tam J in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for manslaughter in Hong Kong
principles.paraphrase_en: Hong Kong courts will not automatically adopt sentencing ranges from other jurisdictions (like the UK) for manslaughter, particularly in cases involving unlawful and dangerous acts like a single blow. The court must consider the local context, including Hong Kong's crowded and tense environment, and the need for deterrence.
Manslaughter can be established where an unlawful and dangerous act (such as a blow) causes death, even if the death was an unintended consequence. The circumstances of such offences vary widely.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the sentencing principle from Lo Bing Sun that UK sentencing guidelines are not automatically applicable in Hong Kong for manslaughter.
The present judgment cites Tsang Siu Ching as another example of a case involving unintentional killing (manslaughter).

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG CHAK-MING
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee J

principles.principle_label: Elements of unlawful and dangerous act manslaughter
principles.paraphrase_en: For a conviction of unlawful and dangerous act manslaughter, the prosecution must prove the defendant committed an unlawful and dangerous act that caused death, with the base offence here being common assault.
If the defendant was acting in lawful self-defence at the material time, this provides a complete defence to a charge of manslaughter.

principles.principle_label: Objective test for dangerousness in manslaughter
principles.paraphrase_en: For an act to constitute unlawful and dangerous act manslaughter, it must be 'dangerous' in all the circumstances, which is an objective assessment for the jury.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI WANG PONG, FRANKLIN
judges: Before: Hon Kwok J in court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant is found unfit to stand trial, a jury may still determine whether the defendant committed the acts charged, under the special procedures of the Criminal Procedure Ordinance.

principles.principle_label: Gross negligence manslaughter - duty of care
principles.paraphrase_en: A doctor owes a duty of care to a patient when administering medical treatment, and a breach of that duty through gross negligence can found a charge of manslaughter.

principles.principle_label: Gross negligence - standard of care breach
principles.paraphrase_en: Gross negligence can be established by evidence of multiple failures in the medical procedure that fall below accepted standards, such as failing to check patient history, monitor vital signs, and provide necessary safety equipment.
For manslaughter by gross negligence, the prosecution must prove that the defendant's negligent acts caused the death of the victim.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MAN TAT YAN
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's old age and poor health condition are relevant mitigating factors that the court must consider when determining sentence.

principles.principle_label: Aggravation for lack of medical training
principles.paraphrase_en: The absence of prior medical training before engaging in illegal medical practice is an aggravating factor that can warrant a more severe sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWAN HAU CHI, VANESSA
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for gross negligence manslaughter
principles.paraphrase_en: Sentences for gross negligence manslaughter vary widely based on the specific facts of each case, and direct comparison with previous cases is unhelpful because each case is fact-sensitive.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing based on magnitude of conduct
principles.paraphrase_en: The appropriate sentence is determined by assessing the overall magnitude of the defendant's wrongful actions and failures to act.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN KWONG YIU
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J

candidates.case_name: JASPAL SINGH v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is not to reassess the claim anew but to scrutinize the Board's decision for errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

principles.principle_label: Legal assistance standard in non-refoulement procedures
principles.paraphrase_en: The high standard of fairness requires adequate legal assistance for non-refoulement claimants, but does not mandate legal representation throughout the entire procedure.

principles.principle_label: Fugitive from justice vs persecution victim
principles.paraphrase_en: A person fleeing punishment for an offence (fugitive from justice) is not entitled to non-refoulement protection, unlike a person fleeing persecution who is a victim of injustice.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in non-refoulement claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The claimant bears the burden to establish, even to a low standard, that they face a threat of harm reaching the required severity for a non-refoulement claim.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that judicial review intervention is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.
The judgment follows this Court of Appeal decision regarding the scope of required legal assistance in non-refoulement procedures.
The judgment follows this Court of Appeal decision regarding the scope of required legal assistance in non-refoulement procedures.
The judgment follows this Court of Appeal decision regarding the scope of required legal assistance in non-refoulement procedures.
The judgment follows this Court of Appeal decision regarding the scope of required legal assistance in non-refoulement procedures.
The judgment follows this Court of Appeal decision regarding the scope of required legal assistance in non-refoulement procedures.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOW HEUNG WING, STEPHEN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and A Pang JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Application of Donoghue v Stevenson principles
principles.paraphrase_en: The established negligence principles from Donoghue v Stevenson apply to cases involving a duty to take reasonable care for the safety of others.
The assessment of whether negligence is gross depends on the overall appalling nature of the failures, with some matters potentially being so significant that others pale in comparison.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the established negligence principles from Donoghue v Stevenson to the facts of the case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN HO MING
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Motor manslaughter is a more serious offense than causing death by dangerous driving and is reserved for particularly serious cases. The offender's culpability is the primary factor in determining the seriousness of the offense.
Multiple deaths and the fact that a vehicle was carrying passengers are significant aggravating features in motor manslaughter cases.

principles.principle_label: Starting point for gross negligence manslaughter
principles.paraphrase_en: The starting point for sentence in gross negligence manslaughter cases must be the initial consideration in sentencing.
It is wrong in principle for a court to disregard the principle of general deterrence when sentencing for serious offenses.
When a final sentence is reached after applying a specific discount (such as one-third), the original starting point can be inferred by reversing that discount calculation.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Law Siu Kuen
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that motor manslaughter is more serious than causing death by dangerous driving and reserved for particularly serious cases.
The judgment cites this recent decision of the Court of Appeal in its sentencing analysis.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that the starting point is the initial consideration for sentence in gross negligence manslaughter cases.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU YUN KEUNG
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. VILLANUEVA JILL NAOMI GARCIA
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee J

principles.paraphrase_en: When considering sentencing precedents, the court must assess their helpfulness to the present case based on the specific circumstances and distinguishing factors.

principles.principle_label: Mens rea for preventing lawful burial
principles.paraphrase_en: For the common law offense of preventing lawful burial, the defendant's intention is a relevant consideration; absence of intent to prevent burial may be a mitigating factor, even if the conduct had that result.

principles.principle_label: Mitigation for young and unsophisticated offenders
principles.paraphrase_en: Youth, lack of sophistication, and limited coping skills of an offender are relevant personal circumstances that may be considered in mitigation, particularly where they contribute to poor decision-making.

A sentencing court should not fetter the discretion of the Immigration Department or direct how it should handle an offender's immigration status, though it may express a non-binding hope for sympathetic consideration.

relationships.description: The court finds this sentencing case not very helpful for the present purpose due to differing circumstances.
The court finds this sentencing case not very helpful for the present purpose due to differing circumstances.
The court accepts the defendant shares similar mitigating characteristics of youth and lack of sophistication as the offender in this cited case.

candidates.case_name: EXXON LTD AND ANOTHER v. ZENG CHENG ENGINEERING COMPANY LTD
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge G. Chow in Chambers (open to public)

principles.principle_label: No general right to dispose of uncollected goods
principles.paraphrase_en: At common law, a bailee does not have a general right to dispose of goods that the bailor has refused or is unable to collect.

An involuntary bailee who performs an act that would normally constitute conversion is only liable if they acted without reasonable care. Acts done in good faith with the objective of returning goods to the owner are considered reasonable.
Placing or leaving a chattel on another's land without consent constitutes trespass. The landowner is entitled to remove the offending chattel, provided they take reasonable care in doing so.

principles.principle_label: Recovery of removal costs in trespass
principles.paraphrase_en: In cases of trespass to land, the costs of removing offending articles or illegal structures are recoverable as damages from the trespasser.

relationships.description: The judgment references this case for the proposition that a bailee might imply consent to a sale by giving warning, but notes the difficulties with this approach.

relationships.target_case_name: Desir Anthony C v Knight Frank (Services) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this local case regarding the modified liability standard for involuntary bailees acting in good faith.
The judgment follows the principle established in this English Court of Appeal decision that a landowner may remove trespassing chattels with reasonable care.

candidates.case_name: FINE GOLD DEVELOPMENTS LTD v. PERSONS WHOSE PROPERTIES ARE LEFT UNCOLLECTED AT FACTORY C, 1ST FLOOR, KWAI BO INDUSTRIAL BUILDING, NO.40 WONG CHUK HANG ROAD, HONG KONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Gekko Lan in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Placing or leaving personal property on another person's land without their consent constitutes trespass, and the landowner is entitled to remove the offending items provided they take reasonable care in doing so.

An involuntary bailee who, in good faith, performs an act that would normally amount to conversion (like disposing of goods) is only liable if they acted without reasonable care. Acts conscientiously performed with the objective of returning goods to the owner are considered reasonable.

principles.principle_label: No general right to dispose of uncollected goods
principles.paraphrase_en: At common law, there is no general right for a bailee to dispose of goods that the bailor has refused or is unable to collect. Silence in response to a warning about disposal cannot generally be taken as implied consent to a sale.

relationships.description: The judgment adopts the principle from this English case that leaving chattels without consent is trespass, entitling the landowner to remove them with reasonable care.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Cho Fai v Lam Kit Heung
relationships.description: The judgment notes that the principle from Jones v Gospel & White was applied in this local case concerning an involuntary bailee.
The judgment references this case for the proposition that a bailee might imply consent to a sale by warning the bailor, but notes the difficulties with this approach.

relationships.target_case_name: Desir Anthony C v Knight Frank (Services) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the modified liability standard from this local case, which holds that an involuntary bailee is only liable for conversion if they lacked reasonable care.

candidates.case_name: WOO TAK YAN v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE / DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Winnie Tsui in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A tribunal has an obligation to investigate any matter it considers relevant to a claim under its statutory powers, which may affect the exercise of its discretion to enter judgment.
A tribunal may decline to exercise its discretion to enter judgment against a party when relevant matters require further inquiry, even if there are queries about a party's assertions.
A legal authority relied upon by a party may be distinguished and not followed if the factual circumstances of the present case are materially different.
Whether a defendant's actions (such as removal of belongings) were justified is a matter to be determined at trial, not suitable for summary disposal.

relationships.description: The deputy adjudicator distinguished the authority relied on by the claimant based on the factual circumstances of the present case.

judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

candidates.case_name: AMIN NURUL v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE

candidates.case_name: STANDARD CHARTERED BANK (HONG KONG) LTD v. LOK SHEK HUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Teresa Wu in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A person who signs a legal document is generally bound by its terms, even if they did not read or understand the details before signing.

principles.principle_label: No general equity jurisdiction for improvident bargains
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no general equitable jurisdiction to set aside transactions merely due to inequality of bargaining power or because a bargain proves to be improvident or harsh for one party.

principles.principle_label: Limited scope of unconscionability under UCO
principles.paraphrase_en: The Unconscionable Contracts Ordinance (UCO) targets unconscionable contracts, not all unconscionable conduct, and assessment is confined to the contract's terms at the time of making, specifically in consumer dealings.

principles.principle_label: Guidance from foreign jurisprudence on unconscionability
principles.paraphrase_en: Foreign jurisprudence (e.g., Australian law) on similar statutory concepts like 'unconscionable conduct' may provide guidance in interpreting the UCO, but its broader scope must be distinguished from the Hong Kong ordinance's narrower focus.

relationships.target_case_name: Bank of China (Hong Kong) Ltd v Fung Chin Kan & Another
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle established in this case that a signatory is bound by a document's terms even if not read.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that unconscionability is not a general remedy for adjusting harsh contracts between competent parties.

relationships.target_case_name: Chang Pui Yin v Bank of Singapore Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the approach from this case that Australian jurisprudence on unconscionable conduct can offer guidance for interpreting the UCO.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MOK SHU PIU
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Woodcock in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A court can only accept an unequivocal guilty plea. A plea becomes equivocal if the defendant attaches a qualification at the time of pleading that, if true, could negate guilt. The determination of equivocality is made at the time the plea is entered.

principles.principle_label: Discretion to reverse plea after conviction
principles.paraphrase_en: After conviction but before sentence, a court has a discretion to allow a defendant to reverse an unequivocal guilty plea. This discretion must be exercised very sparingly and only in clear cases, with the overriding consideration being the interests of justice.

principles.principle_label: Post-conviction qualification does not create equivocality
principles.paraphrase_en: If a defendant makes a qualification that might show innocence only after conviction, this does not retrospectively render an originally unequivocal plea equivocal.

principles.principle_label: Material indicating innocence as a consideration
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering the reversal of a guilty plea, the court may consider whether materials exist that could indicate the defendant's innocence or persuade a jury of innocence.
A defendant seeking to reverse a guilty plea may argue that the plea was not an informed and deliberate decision, for instance due to a lack of access to potentially exculpatory evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Chan Chi Ho Lincoln
relationships.description: The present judgment refers to this authority for the principles governing the discretion to allow reversal of a guilty plea after conviction.

candidates.case_name: WOO TAK YAN v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Alan Kwong in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not interfere with a lower tribunal's factual findings if they were based on a careful consideration of the available evidence and the tribunal correctly identified the issues in dispute.

The test for apparent bias is whether a fair-minded and informed observer, having considered all relevant circumstances, would conclude there was a real possibility or real danger that the tribunal was biased.
A tribunal must apply the 'reasonable apprehension' test to determine if there is apparent bias, and adverse findings or comments alone do not satisfy this test.

relationships.target_case_name: GM-SA aka DG, M-S v DDPJ (Recusal)
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and applies the established 'reasonable apprehension' test for apparent bias as articulated in that case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN HO
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, McWalters and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When evaluating whether delay in bringing a case to trial causes unfairness, the court must consider the case's nature and complexity, the evidence involved, potential issues, and any other sensitive or relevant factors.

principles.principle_label: Waiver of right to be present at trial
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether an accused person has waived their right to be present at their trial is a factual question to be determined based on all the circumstances of the case.
A court must exercise its discretion judicially, in accordance with the relevant law and legal principles.

The decision to start or continue a trial in the absence of a defendant must be made with great care and circumspection, guided by applicable principles and the fundamental objective of ensuring a fair trial.

candidates.case_name: LINE POWER LTD v. CHEN JINHUI
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Maria Yuen in Chambers

candidates.case_name: MD KAMRUL ISLAM ALIAS MD HARUN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD/ NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.principle_label: Judicial review standard for factual findings
principles.paraphrase_en: The court will not interfere with a tribunal's factual findings unless they demonstrate an error of law, procedural unfairness, or are so unreasonable that no reasonable tribunal could have reached them.
Administrative decisions can only be challenged as Wednesbury unreasonable if they are so irrational that no reasonable decision-maker could have arrived at the same conclusion.

relationships.target_case_name: Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd v Wednesbury Corporation
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the Wednesbury unreasonableness standard to assess the rationality of the Board's decision.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HARPAL, SINGH
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Woodcock in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court must be aware of the dangers of mistaken identification and cannot rely solely on CCTV footage for definitive identification if the footage is unclear, shows the subject wearing a mask, or shows clothing different from that worn by the defendant when arrested.

On a rehearing in a magistracy appeal, the appellate court must examine the magistrate's reasoning and findings for error or flaw, and will only uphold the conviction if satisfied the magistrate's conclusion was justified, the analysis was sound and based on proper evidence, and the conviction is neither unsafe nor unsatisfactory.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. AGBANYIM EMEKA PRINCE
judges: Before: Hon Zervos, M Poon JJA and Andrew Chan J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To succeed on an appeal based on incompetent legal representation, an applicant must establish that the representation was flagrantly incompetent.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as establishing the requirement that legal representation must be flagrantly incompetent to ground an appeal.
The judgment cites this case alongside Chong Ching-yuen for the same principle regarding the standard for incompetent legal representation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YIU CHI MING
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, McWalters & Zervos JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Even after a judge rules a confession admissible following a voir dire, the accused may still challenge its reliability and voluntariness before the jury. The jury must be directed to disregard a confession if they find it was or may have been obtained through oppression or in circumstances likely to render it unreliable.

A jury must be directed that if they conclude a confession was or may have been obtained by oppression or in circumstances likely to render it unreliable, they must disregard it, regardless of whether they believe it to be true. This prevents the jury from relying on a confession they find was improperly obtained.
A confession is only admissible if it is voluntary, meaning it was not obtained by fear of prejudice, hope of advantage held out by a person in authority, or by oppression.

principles.principle_label: Jury's role in assessing confession reliability
principles.paraphrase_en: The jury's task involves two key questions: first, whether they are sure the admissions are true; and second, whether those admissions were or may have been obtained as a result of oppression or something likely to render them unreliable. If the second question is answered affirmatively, the admissions must be disregarded.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Ibrahim for the classic common law test that a confession must be voluntary and not obtained by fear of prejudice or hope of advantage.
Cited as authority adding 'oppression' to the Ibrahim test for the voluntariness of a confession.
Cited for the principle that the Police and Criminal Evidence Act 1984 is a codifying act to be interpreted by its natural meaning, not influenced by prior law.
The judgment applies the key principles from Mushtaq, requiring a jury direction to disregard confessions obtained by oppression or in unreliable circumstances.

relationships.target_case_name: Benjamin and Ganga v The State of Trinidad and Tobago
relationships.description: The judgment follows this Privy Council case, which rejected an earlier authority and aligned English law with Hong Kong law on the need for a Mushtaq direction even for alleged fabricated confessions.
The judgment notes this earlier Privy Council authority was rejected in Benjamin and Ganga regarding the need for a Mushtaq direction for alleged fabricated confessions.

candidates.case_name: RE GURUNG CHETBAHADUR
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge To in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Extension of time factors for appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: When deciding whether to extend the time limit for appealing a refusal of leave to apply for judicial review, the court considers four key factors: the length of the delay, the reasons for the delay, the merits of the intended appeal, and any prejudice to the respondent if the extension is granted.

principles.principle_label: Obligation to notify court of address change
principles.paraphrase_en: A litigant has a legal obligation to inform the court of any change of address, including changes resulting from being taken into custody, and must bear the consequences of failing to do so, such as not receiving court notices.

principles.principle_label: Limited role of court in non-refoulement judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: In a judicial review of a non-refoulement claim, the court's role is not to reassess the claim or evidence anew. Its function is limited to scrutinizing the decision of the primary decision-makers (the Director and the Board) for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

relationships.target_case_name: Jagg Singh and Torture Claims Appeal Board/non-Refoulement Claims Petition Office
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the four-factor test established in Jagg Singh for considering an extension of time to appeal.
The judgment applies the principle from Karamjit Singh that a litigant must inform the court of an address change and bears the risk of not doing so.

candidates.case_name: HA CHE WAI v. MEDICAL SUPERINTENDENT OF THE PAMELA YOUDE NETHERSOLE EASTERN PSYCHIATRIC OBSERVATION UNIT AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.principle_label: Common law supplementation of statutory procedures
principles.paraphrase_en: The common law will intervene to supplement statutory procedures to ensure fairness, unless doing so would frustrate the purpose of the statute.

principles.principle_label: Detention orders restrict constitutional and common law liberty
principles.paraphrase_en: Orders for detention under the Mental Health Ordinance restrict both constitutional rights under the Bill of Rights and common law rights to liberty, engaging requirements of procedural fairness.
The determination of whether section 68A of the Mental Health Ordinance applies to a particular class of persons is a question of statutory interpretation.

principles.principle_label: Conditional discharge order restricts personal liberty
principles.paraphrase_en: A conditional discharge order made under section 42B of the Mental Health Ordinance prima facie restricts a person's personal liberty, though such restriction may be justified by the proportionality test.

principles.principle_label: Purpose of mental health review tribunals
principles.paraphrase_en: Mental health review tribunals are designed to ensure that a patient's rights are practical and effective, and their procedures should be user-friendly to enable direct communication.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the holding that a conditional discharge order under MHO s.42B prima facie restricts personal liberty.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tze Yam v Commissioner of Police (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that there may be a distinction between different types of liberty restrictions.

relationships.target_case_name: S, T, P v London Borough of Brent
