relationships.description: The judgment cites this English case as an example of the common law supplementing statutory procedures to ensure fairness.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MA KA KIN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Pang JA and Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Lurking doubt as aspect of unsafe conviction
principles.paraphrase_en: The concept of 'lurking doubt' is not a separate legal test but rather forms part of the statutory assessment of whether a conviction is unsafe or unsatisfactory.

An appellate court may invoke 'lurking doubt' only with great caution, requiring a substantial remaining doubt about the propriety of the conviction, leading to a distinct feeling of unease or a real danger of injustice.

relationships.target_case_name: Kwong Kin Hung v The Queen
relationships.description: The judgment follows the Privy Council's clarification that 'lurking doubt' is not an alternative test but part of the 'unsafe and unsatisfactory' assessment.
The judgment references Widgery LJ's guidance on lurking doubt from this case as having been repeatedly applied.

relationships.target_case_name: Stafford v Director of Public Prosecutions
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Kilbrandon's summary of the test to be applied by appellate courts regarding lurking doubt.
The judgment applies this Court's definition that lurking doubt means a substantial remaining doubt about the conviction's propriety.

candidates.case_name: NNAJIOFOR OGECHI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.principle_label: Court's role in non-refoulement judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: In judicial review of non-refoulement claims, the court's function is not to reassess the factual merits of the claim de novo. The court's role is limited to scrutinizing the Board's decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality, as the primary fact-finding responsibility lies with the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board.

A court may refuse an application to withdraw legal proceedings if there is insufficient evidence that the withdrawal was made voluntarily and with a full understanding of the consequences, particularly where the withdrawal would expose the applicant to a claimed risk of serious harm.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The present judgment cites Nupur Mst to support the established principle that judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is limited to examining errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM HOI KIT
judges: Before: Sir Brian Keith, Deputy Judge of the High Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A confession statement is admissible in evidence only if the prosecution proves beyond reasonable doubt that it was made voluntarily, without being induced by any improper pressure or threat.

When assessing whether a confession was made voluntarily, the court may consider the defendant's overall circumstances, demeanor, and body language during the interview, as well as the contextual realities they would have faced at the time.

While previous convictions may be admissible when the defendant's evidence attacks a police officer's character, they are not necessarily determinative on the issue of the voluntariness of a confession.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BAGUMA GEORGE
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Leave to appeal out of time criteria
principles.paraphrase_en: When deciding whether to grant leave to appeal out of time, the court must consider three factors: the length of the delay, whether the applicant has provided a reasonable explanation for the delay, and whether sufficient grounds of appeal are demonstrated.
The applicant must demonstrate substantial grounds to justify the delay in seeking leave to appeal out of time.

principles.principle_label: Court's duty to review grounds for arguability
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has the discretion to examine the proposed grounds of appeal to ensure that refusing leave does not prevent a substantial and plainly arguable ground from being heard.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Wong Kai Kong & Anor
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the three-factor test established in R v Wong Kai Kong for considering leave to appeal out of time.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. WOO TAK YAN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Chase Pun in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSUI CHI WAI
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A confession is considered voluntary if it is made after a lawful arrest and caution, where the defendant has been informed of their rights and is not acting under oppression or assault from the arresting officers.

Police officers executing their duty are required to identify themselves, conduct a lawful search based on reasonable suspicion, and then arrest and caution the suspect. Minor injuries sustained during a lawful arrest due to the defendant's resistance do not render the arrest unlawful or oppressive.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of officer credibility and duty
principles.paraphrase_en: The court assesses the credibility of police officers' actions by determining whether they acted within the scope of their duty to restrain a resisting suspect, rather than engaging in an assault. Minor harm resulting from necessary restraint is permissible.

candidates.case_name: 林建安 v. 大埔警區刑事調查隊第十二隊譚啟軒督察及另一人
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A judicial review application can be brought to challenge a police decision to apply for forfeiture of bail money and the resulting court order.

principles.principle_label: Proper parties in judicial review against police
principles.paraphrase_en: Police officers in charge of an investigation can be named as respondents in a judicial review application, with representation provided by the Department of Justice.
A court may determine a judicial review application on paper without an oral hearing after considering all submitted materials, including initial responses and replies from parties.

principles.principle_label: Irrelevance of underlying investigation to forfeiture
principles.paraphrase_en: The background facts of the police investigation that led to the grant of bail are not relevant to determining the legality of the subsequent forfeiture of bail money.

principles.principle_label: Bail condition breach as forfeiture basis
principles.paraphrase_en: Failure to comply with bail reporting conditions, such as failing to report as required, provides grounds for police to apply for forfeiture of bail money deposited with them.

candidates.case_name: SAM FAI CONSTRUCTION CO LTD v. YAT MING CONSTRUCTION & ENGINEERING LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BALAKRISHNAN MAHENDRAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court

principles.principle_label: Discretion to proceed with trial in absentia
principles.paraphrase_en: The court possesses a discretion to begin or continue a criminal trial in the absence of a defendant. This discretion applies even where the trial has not yet commenced, and its exercise is guided by considerations of practical justice, including the interests of co-defendants, witnesses, and the efficient administration of justice.

principles.principle_label: Irrelevance of offence seriousness to in absentia discretion
principles.paraphrase_en: The seriousness of the offence charged is not a relevant factor for the court to consider when deciding whether to exercise its discretion to proceed with a trial in the defendant's absence.

relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle from R v Jones that the seriousness of the offence is not a factor in deciding whether to proceed in the defendant's absence.
The judgment applies the same principle established in Singh Gursewak regarding the irrelevance of offence seriousness to the discretion to proceed in absentia.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YIM SHEK WO
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP and Pang and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When directing a jury on the reliability of a defendant's admissions, the judge must instruct them to first consider whether the admissions were or might have been obtained by improper police conduct, before deciding if they are true.

principles.principle_label: Leave to appeal out of time - factors
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering an application for leave to appeal out of time, the court must consider: (1) the length of the delay; (2) whether there is a reasonable explanation for the delay; and (3) whether sufficient grounds of appeal are shown. The court may examine the grounds to ensure it is not refusing a substantial and arguable appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Wong Kai Kong & Anor
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the three-factor test from R v Wong Kai Kong & Anor for determining whether to grant leave to appeal out of time.

candidates.case_name: RE LIN YIFAN
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kent Yee in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish ordinary residence for bankruptcy jurisdiction under section 4(1)(c)(i), the petitioner does not need to show the debtor was resident throughout the entire three-year period, nor that the debtor intended to remain permanently. It requires residence that is not casual or uncertain, but conducted in the ordinary course of the debtor's life.

principles.principle_label: Place of residence for bankruptcy jurisdiction
principles.paraphrase_en: The jurisdictional requirement under section 4(1)(c)(i) can be satisfied by the debtor having a place of residence in Hong Kong at some point during the relevant period, even if it is not the debtor's primary home, such as being able to stay at a relative's residence.

relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the interpretation from Re Cao Zhong regarding the requirements for establishing ordinary residence under bankruptcy jurisdiction.
The present judgment applies the principle from Re Ku Chu Keung that a place of residence for bankruptcy jurisdiction can include accommodation at a relative's home.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. AGBANYIM EMEKA PRINCE
judges: Before: Hon Macrae Acting CJHC in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish trafficking in a dangerous drug, the prosecution must prove the defendant had possession of the drugs and knowledge of their nature. Possession can be established by evidence of access to the premises where drugs are found and control over the items.
Facts formally admitted under the Criminal Procedure Ordinance become evidence in the case without further proof and can be used to establish elements of the offence.
A video recorded interview is admissible as evidence if its admissibility is not challenged at trial, particularly when conducted with appropriate interpreter assistance.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TANG YIU CHEONG, JACKIE
judges: Before: Hon Macrae Acting CJHC in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant does not possess a right to select their judge and cannot reasonably expect to be notified of a judicial change.
The test for apparent bias requires the court to examine all relevant circumstances and determine whether a fair-minded and informed observer would conclude there was a real possibility of bias.
Alleged prior police misconduct is not automatically relevant to determining whether a plea entered before a magistrate many months later was voluntary.
Pressure from family members must be sufficient to actually render a plea involuntary; a judge is entitled to find such pressure insufficient as a matter of fact and common sense.

relationships.target_case_name: Falcon Private Bank Ltd v Borry Bernard Edouard Charles Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Falcon Private Bank that judges should generally continue with a case where no reasonable grounds for apprehending bias exist.
The judgment applies the test for apparent bias as formulated in Md Emran Hossain.
The judgment cites O’Neill to support the proposition that the test for apparent bias is the same in civil and criminal cases.

candidates.case_name: CHINA FORESTRY HOLDINGS CO LTD (in official liquidation) v. TOP WISDOM OVERSEAS HOLDINGS LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A fiduciary who receives a benefit in breach of their duty may hold that benefit on trust for their principal, and this principle is not necessarily confined only to cases involving bribes or secret commissions.

Where an agent or fiduciary makes an unauthorized profit by exploiting an opportunity arising from their position, equity may impose a constructive trust requiring them to account for that profit to their principal.
A proprietary claim based on an alleged constructive trust should not be struck out unless it is obviously unsustainable and has no real prospect of success.

relationships.target_case_name: FHR European Ventures LLP v Cedar Capital Partners LLC
relationships.description: The present judgment follows FHR's broader discussion of the equitable rule regarding proprietary remedies for fiduciary breaches, rejecting the argument that it is confined to bribes/secret commissions.

relationships.target_case_name: Sinclair Investments (UK) Ltd v Versailles Trade Finance Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment questions whether Sinclair remains fully sound authority, noting an arguable inconsistency between its formulation of the principle and that in FHR.
The judgment cites this case as an example where a fiduciary (a barrister) was held to have acquired discounted bills for his client, illustrating the application of the equitable rule.
Cited as a Privy Council authority where a mayor buying land for himself was held to be buying for the city, supporting the principle that unauthorized fiduciary profits are held for the principal.

candidates.case_name: TIAN HONGMEI v. ZHANG WEI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Chu VP and Cheung JA

principles.paraphrase_en: Courts should not adopt a piecemeal approach to litigation, where claims are brought in separate proceedings that should properly be tried together. This is against established legal principles.

relationships.target_case_name: Li Cheong v Lee Kwai Tai
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Li Cheong v Lee Kwai Tai that a piecemeal approach to litigation is wrong.

relationships.target_case_name: Choy Po Chun v Au Wing Lun
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Choy Po Chun v Au Wing Lun that a piecemeal approach to litigation is wrong.

candidates.case_name: TSUI KOON WAH v. IP MEI HO t/a MICHELLE IP & CO, SOLICITORS
judges: Before: Hon Marlene Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will only interfere with a lower court's costs order if the decision is wrong in principle or plainly wrong. The mere fact that the appellate court might have exercised the discretion differently is insufficient grounds for intervention.

principles.principle_label: Appellate interference with case management decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court should be slow to interfere with case management decisions, which are discretionary. The appellant faces a very high hurdle and must show the judge went clearly wrong, causing injustice, erred in principle, or made an irrational order.

An appellate court cannot interfere with a lower court's factual inference merely because other plausible inferences could be drawn from the material. Interference is only permitted if the decision exceeds the generous ambit of reasonable disagreement and is plainly wrong.

The distinction between nominal damages and small damages does not depend on the amount awarded. A token sum for nominal damages is different from a similarly small sum awarded as compensation for a trifling but proven loss.

principles.principle_label: Bailment liability and proof of loss
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant can establish liability for loss under the law of bailment by proving the bailee's breach of duty, but must also provide sufficient evidence to prove the quantum of the loss. Failure to sufficiently prove the value of the lost item limits the recoverable damages.

relationships.target_case_name: Ho Shu Kwong v Chiang Chun Yuan
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that an appellate court can only interfere with a costs order if it is wrong in principle or plainly wrong.

relationships.target_case_name: Kaifull Investments Ltd v Commissioner of Inland Revenue
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case on when an appellate court can interfere with a lower court's factual inferences.

relationships.target_case_name: Mimi Kar Kee Wong Hung v Severn Villa Ltd & ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from this Court of Appeal case regarding the high threshold for appealing case management decisions.
The judgment cites this case for the principles governing the award of costs, noting they are applicable in the Small Claims Tribunal.

relationships.target_case_name: Roache v News Group Newspaper Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principles governing when an appellate court should intervene in a costs discretion.

relationships.target_case_name: Choy Yee Chun v Bond Star Development Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case alongside Roache for the principles on appellate intervention in costs discretion.

candidates.case_name: ZPMC OFFSHORE SERVICE CO LTD (Formerly known as SHANGHAI ZHENHUA HEAVY INDUSTRIES SHIPPING CO LTD) v. PHILIP JEFFREY ADKINS AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Harris J in Court

principles.principle_label: Statutory exception to Foss v Harbottle rule
principles.paraphrase_en: Sections 728-730 of the Companies Ordinance (Cap. 622) create a statutory exception to the rule in Foss v Harbottle, allowing members to seek remedies for conduct affecting the company under specified circumstances.

A member's right to commence an action under section 729 is limited to situations where their interests 'have been, are or would be affected' in their capacity as a member, not merely because a wrong has been done to the company.

principles.principle_label: Relationship between member and company actions
principles.paraphrase_en: If a company is willing and able to take action to remedy a wrong done to it, a member's interests are unlikely to be impacted in a way that engages section 729, avoiding conflict and duplication of proceedings.

Division 3 of Part 14 of the Companies Ordinance provides remedies for members regarding others' conduct in relation to companies, including injunctions, declarations that contracts are void or voidable, and damages.

relationships.description: The judgment explains that Division 3 of Part 14 of the Companies Ordinance creates a statutory exception to the rule in Foss v Harbottle.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG CHIU YUN
judges: Before: Hon Pang, M Poon and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Prohibition on credibility use of delayed complaint
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution is prohibited from using a defendant's delay in making a complaint to attack the defendant's credibility, just as it is prohibited from using such delay as evidence of guilt.

principles.principle_label: Judge's duty to direct on prohibited use of delay
principles.paraphrase_en: A trial judge has a duty to provide full jury directions regarding the principle prohibiting the use of a defendant's delayed complaint for assessing credibility or guilt, and not merely to clarify the defense's position.

principles.principle_label: Need for expert evidence on absence of forensic traces
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge cannot assert that there are many reasons why contact may not leave DNA or fingerprint traces in the absence of supporting expert evidence, particularly when the admitted facts show a large quantity of a defendant's personal items lacked his biological traces.

Whether the absence of forensic traces (like DNA or fingerprints) is significant must be assessed based on the unique circumstances of each case, and a similar judicial comment may not be wrong in all situations.

relationships.target_case_name: Lam Sze Nga and Lam Sze Nga (No 2)
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Lam Sze Nga that prohibits using a defendant's delay in complaint for credibility attacks (the Credibility Use) as much as for proving guilt.

relationships.target_case_name: Hoang Gia Huan and Manan Abdul
relationships.description: The judgment cites Hoang Gia Huan and Manan Abdul as the latest authority on the issue of assessing the significance of absent forensic traces, noting outcomes depend on case-specific facts.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v.CHAN CHU LEUNG (陳柱良)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Gleeson NPJ

principles.principle_label: Right of silence and impeachment of credibility
principles.paraphrase_en: An accused person's exercise of the right of silence cannot be used to challenge their credibility. Cross-examination or comment that seeks to do so is impermissible.

principles.principle_label: Scope of right of silence regarding complaints
principles.paraphrase_en: The right of silence does not create a free-standing 'right of no-complaint' unrelated to the exercise of that right. The absence of a complaint about police impropriety may, in certain contexts, be a permissible subject of cross-examination if it does not touch upon the core right of silence.

Where the voluntariness of a confession is disputed, cross-examination is permitted on an accused's failure to raise a complaint about alleged police misconduct earlier, as this goes to the credibility of that complaint in the specific context of determining admissibility.

principles.principle_label: Limitation on inroads to right of silence
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle permitting cross-examination on late complaints in the context of challenging a confession's voluntariness does not provide a basis for general inroads into the right of silence to challenge late complaints of police misconduct for broader purposes.

principles.principle_label: Right of silence post-arrest and caution
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle protecting an accused's right of silence after arrest and caution, regarding a failure to complain of police misconduct to persons not in authority, should not be diluted. Situations where no caution was given or the person was not under arrest are distinguishable.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Lam Sze Nga for the approach that maintains the accused's right of silence while permitting specific cross-examination on late complaints in the context of disputed confessions.
The judgment affirms the principle from Lee Fuk Hing that the right of silence precludes using an accused's silence to impeach their credibility.
The judgment notes that Ata Asaf applied the prohibition from Lee Fuk Hing on using silence to impeach credibility.
The judgment cites the formulation from Petty & Maiden to distinguish matters concerning the offence and participants from other evidence.
The judgment distinguishes Parkes on the basis that the defendant there was not under arrest or cautioned, unlike the present case.
The judgment distinguishes Chan Kung Ling as a case where there was no caution, questioning, or indication that the defendant was told he need not answer.

candidates.case_name: KOK WAI LEUNG v. HO TIN BUILDING MATERIAL LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Leung in Court

candidates.case_name: CHAN MING KIT v. LUK KWOK KEUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Alfred Cheng in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: In personal injury litigation, the court does not automatically accept the contents of a treating doctor's medical report at face value, as it cannot be assumed the doctor has critically assessed the genuineness and reliability of the patient's complaints.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of lost income for self-employed
principles.paraphrase_en: For a self-employed person, such as a law firm partner, a claim for loss of income during sick leave requires evidence that their share of profits actually decreased due to the incident, as work can be delegated to others.

A party who puts forward false evidence on a central issue, thereby wasting judicial resources and pursuing a hopeless defence, can be penalized with an order for indemnity costs as it constitutes an abuse of court process.

Where a plaintiff recovers damages within the jurisdiction of the Small Claims Tribunal but should have known from the outset that this was the likely outcome, and has failed to provide sufficient evidence to justify a higher claim, costs may be awarded on a scale commensurate with that Tribunal.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Shui Ming v Yeung Kam Shing
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the precedent from this case to assess the appropriate level of damages for pain, suffering, and loss of amenity.

relationships.target_case_name: Yuen Ka Ho v Wong Chin Man & Ors
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the precedent from this case to assess the appropriate level of damages for pain, suffering, and loss of amenity.

candidates.case_name: ZHANG QIANG v. CISCO SYSTEMS (HK) LTD
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The determination of whether a legally binding contract has been formed is based on an objective assessment of the parties' exchanges, not on their subjective intentions. Agreement on material terms (consensus ad idem) is required.

At common law, for a contract to be void due to unilateral mistake, the non-mistaken party must have actual knowledge of the other party's mistake regarding a material term. Constructive knowledge alone is insufficient for the common law principle.

Equity provides a separate, flexible jurisdiction to grant relief (such as rendering a contract voidable) in cases of unilateral mistake where the non-mistaken party has constructive knowledge and there is evidence of unconscionable conduct or sharp practice.
The legal concept of severance does not apply in the context of a contract affected by unilateral mistake.

When assessing a witness's credibility, the court must consider the inherent probability of their evidence, its consistency with undisputed evidence and contemporaneous documents/conduct, and its internal consistency. Demeanor is relevant but undue weight should not be placed on it.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Final Appeal's explanation of the objective test for determining contract formation.
The judgment cites Lord Phillips' explanation of the objective approach to contract formation and the qualification regarding consensus ad idem.

relationships.target_case_name: Ching Chi Sau v Yip Woon Yin Judy
relationships.description: The judgment accepts a submission based on this case regarding the approach to witness credibility.

relationships.target_case_name: Chwee Kin Keong v Digilandmall.com Pte Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Singapore case as an example of a jurisdiction drawing a distinction between actual knowledge for voidness and a wider equitable jurisdiction for voidability.

candidates.case_name: YAO GUOLIANG AND ANOTHER v. FOREVER WINNER INTERNTIONAL LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.principle_label: Nature of shares as legal choses in action
principles.paraphrase_en: Shares in a company constitute legal choses in action, not chattels. Share certificates are physical chattels that evidence ownership, but the rights and obligations of shareholders are governed by the company's articles of association and the authorized terms of issue.

principles.principle_label: Transfer of legal title to chattels
principles.paraphrase_en: Transferring legal title to chattels requires an act equivalent to delivery to the transferee, coupled with the transferor's intention to transfer ownership. The requisite intention is usually clear in a sale contract, but for a gift, it must be proven, potentially aided by evidential presumptions depending on the relationship between the parties.
Whether a breach is technical is assessed by reference to the nature of the obligation breached and the consequences of that breach.

The seriousness of alleged contempt and the utility of contempt proceedings are fact-dependent. The significance of any particular factor varies according to the specific context and facts of the case.

relationships.target_case_name: Navigator Equities Limited, Vladimir Anatolevich Chernukhin v Oleg Vladimirovich Deripaska
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority for the principle that determining if a breach is technical involves examining the obligation's nature and the breach's consequences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM MING LOK
judges: Coram: Magistrate Daniel Tang Siu-hung

principles.paraphrase_en: A guilty plea is invalid and a nullity if the defendant lost the capacity to make a voluntary and deliberate choice, such as when induced by strong advice from counsel.
The court has discretion to allow a defendant to reverse a guilty plea, with the paramount consideration being whether a miscarriage of justice would otherwise occur.

principles.principle_label: Miscarriage of justice from induced plea
principles.paraphrase_en: A miscarriage of justice arises if the court does not allow a plea change where evidence shows the defendant was induced to plead guilty and thereby lost voluntary choice.

relationships.description: The judgment cites R v Lennard in the context of discussing reasonable excuse for failing to provide a specimen.
The judgment cites DPP v Eddowes regarding the approach to determining whether an excuse for failing to provide a specimen is reasonable.
The judgment references DPP v Beech for the principle that assessing a 'reasonable excuse' is an objective exercise considering the statute's purpose and evidence of intoxication.

candidates.case_name: SILVER UNIVERSE INVESTMENTS LTD v. CHINA TIMES SECURITIES LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Lam VP and Yuen JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for mortgage relationship termination
principles.paraphrase_en: A mortgagor seeking damages without having repaid the loan bears the burden of proving there was a specific point in time when the mortgagor-mortgagee relationship was effectively terminated through a clear election.
Interim payment should not be granted when there exists a triable dispute regarding the adequacy of an alleged acceptance of repudiation.

principles.principle_label: Material distinction in unauthorized transfer cases
principles.paraphrase_en: A case involving an unauthorized and unlawful transfer of a loan from its inception can be materially distinguished from cases where such initial illegality is not established.

relationships.target_case_name: Thanakharn Kasikorn Thai Chamkat (Mahachon) v Akai Holdings Ltd (No 2)
relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Akai on the basis that the transfer of the loan in Akai was unauthorized and unlawful from the beginning, which is a material distinction from the present case.

candidates.case_name: ZQA AND OTHERS v. SCC AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung and Au JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish civil contempt for breaching a court order, three elements must be proven: first, the order's meaning and requirements must be construed; second, it must be shown the person failed to comply with the order as construed; and third, the failure to comply must have been accompanied by the requisite mental state for punishable contempt.

A breach of a court order constitutes contempt if the act or omission was done consciously, voluntarily, and not casually, accidentally, or unintentionally. The fact that the breach was committed in reliance on legal advice that turned out to be wrong does not negate liability, though it may serve in mitigation.

principles.principle_label: Reliance on legal advice not a defence
principles.paraphrase_en: Reliance on legal advice is not a defence to a charge of civil contempt for breaching a court order. Even if the breach was done reasonably and with due care based on a belief, from legal advice, that it was not a breach, liability for contempt is still established.
Where the terms of a court order are plain and clear, a failure to comply with those terms can found a contempt, provided the necessary mental element is present.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the three-stage test for establishing civil contempt as set out in this case.

relationships.target_case_name: Effiscient Ltd v Edward Eugene Lehman
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from this case regarding the elements required to establish punishable contempt.

relationships.target_case_name: Heatons Transport (St Helens) Ltd v Transport and General Workers’ Union
relationships.description: The judgment cites this House of Lords case as having approved the relevant principle on contempt.
The judgment cites an extract from Millett J in this case as encapsulating the relevant principle on contempt.
The judgment notes that the Court of Final Appeal did not disapprove of this case in relation to the issue of intention in contempt.

candidates.case_name: LIU RUISHAN by WU WEIXIA her next friend v. FU YICK FAI
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Jonathan Wong in Court

principles.principle_label: Recovery of medical expenses from wrongdoer
principles.paraphrase_en: Medical expenses incurred for treating a plaintiff are recoverable from the wrongdoer, and recovered amounts should be repaid to the Hospital Authority to avoid the government subsidizing the wrongdoer's insurer.

principles.principle_label: Recovery of expenses for necessary services
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff can recover out-of-pocket expenses for necessary services freely given by a friend, without altering the friendly character of those services, provided the services were necessary.
A plaintiff is entitled to recover all expenses reasonably incurred for medical treatment resulting from injuries, including the reasonable choice of private or overseas hospitals.

principles.principle_label: Disallowance of fund manager costs for modest future loss
principles.paraphrase_en: Fund manager costs for managing an award for future loss are not necessary or allowable when the future loss amount is not particularly large and the future period is not particularly long.

relationships.description: The judgment cites the case for the principle on recovering expenses for necessary services freely provided by a friend.

relationships.target_case_name: Lai Pui Ling v Ho Chi Keung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this case to disallow a claim for fund manager costs due to the modest size and duration of the future loss award.

candidates.case_name: LAU CHUNG v. HUI KENG YEE AND ANOTHER
judges: Coram: Hon H. Au-Yeung J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may order the provisional striking out of a claim if the plaintiff fails to attend a pre-trial review, provided proper notice of the order and its consequences is served on the plaintiff.

A party who successfully establishes proprietary or promissory estoppel is entitled to a declaration securing the benefit of the equitable right, which may include a life interest in property or monetary compensation representing its value.
An equitable interest arising from expenditure on another's land is not invalid merely because the specific interest to be secured was not expressly defined.

In resolving a case, the rigid classification of an estoppel as either promissory or proprietary is often unhelpful; the focus should be on granting appropriate relief rather than doctrinal categorization.
An act performed under demand or to avoid argument, and which is not a voluntary relinquishment of a known right, cannot be taken as an agreement to permanently surrender that right.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheung Lai Mui v Cheung Wai Shing & Others
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority on principles related to estoppel.

relationships.target_case_name: Luo Xing Juan v. Estate of Hui Shui See
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that the court's aim is to grant relief securing the benefit of the established equitable right.
The judgment references the principle from this case that equity from expenditure need not fail for lack of express indication of the interest.
The judgment applies the reasoning from this case that distinctions between types of estoppel are often not critical to resolving the dispute.
The judgment cites this decision from the same court.

candidates.case_name: N v. SECRETARY FOR SECURITY AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Article 4 of the Hong Kong Bill of Rights imposes a positive duty on the government to provide practical and effective protection to victims of forced labour. The government has a wide discretion in how to fulfill this duty, but it includes a procedural obligation to investigate when aware of credible suspicions of such abuses.

The Victim Identification Screening (VIS) mechanism is an investigative, non-adjudicatory process used by law enforcement to identify potential trafficking victims and offer assistance. It does not determine legal rights, and there is no legal right to be granted formal victim status through this process.
The duty to investigate under BOR4 for forced labour arises separately and distinctly from any duty to investigate trafficking in persons, even if the factual contexts overlap.

Whether existing government measures provide practical and effective protection against rights violations is a highly fact-sensitive issue. The court's role is to resolve the specific dispute before it based on the evidence presented, not to consider general or hypothetical shortcomings of the system.

To establish that enacting a bespoke criminal offence is necessary to fulfill the government's protective duty, an applicant must demonstrate a causal link between the absence of such an offence and a specific failure to provide practical and effective protection in their case.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows ZN (CFA) in affirming the government's positive duty under BOR4 to protect against forced labour and its wide margin of discretion in doing so.
The judgment applies the principle from ZN (CFA) that the government's framework for combating forced labour is an acceptable method to comply with its BOR4 duties.
The judgment cites the first-instance decision in CB(CFI) where the court previously found a causal link between a BOR4 investigative breach and the lack of a bespoke offence.
The judgment follows the Court of Appeal's ruling in CB(CA), which held that the correct approach on causal connection was not applied in the lower court's decision.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. AU CHUN FOO
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. AU CHUN FOO
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BHUIYAN JASHIM UDDIN
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge Wong Sze-lai, Lily

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TUNG PAK FAI
judges: Before: Hon Toh J

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for wounding with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: There are no formal sentencing guidelines for the offence of wounding with intent under section 17; each case must be assessed individually based on its specific facts and the defendant's personal circumstances.

principles.principle_label: Seriousness and mens rea for wounding with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: Wounding with intent is a very serious offence carrying a maximum sentence of life imprisonment. Its gravity is underscored by the principle that the mens rea required is sufficient for murder had the victim died.

While the usual sentencing range for wounding with intent is 3 to 12 years' imprisonment, the court must assess the offender's culpability to determine the appropriate sentence within or outside that range.
An offender's continued use of a weapon and resistance after being physically restrained is an aggravating factor in sentencing for wounding.
When imposing sentences for multiple offences arising from a single incident, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate.

relationships.description: The present judgment finds this Court of Appeal case helpful for sentencing in a wounding with intent case.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that the mens rea for wounding with intent is sufficient for murder had death occurred.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. AU YEUNG KA WAI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge E Yip

principles.paraphrase_en: A person can be liable for an offense committed by others if they participate in a joint criminal enterprise with a common purpose, even if their specific role is limited to watching or guarding.

principles.principle_label: Actus reus through presence and assistance
principles.paraphrase_en: The actus reus of an offense can be established by being present at the scene and performing a role that assists or encourages the principal offenders, such as guarding the door during an assault.
Mens rea for participation in a joint enterprise can be inferred from prior meetings and discussions where the plan to commit the offense was formulated.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUI SIU LUNG
judges: Before: District Judge Clement Lee

principles.paraphrase_en: The maximum sentence for wounding with intent is life imprisonment, with a usual sentencing range of 3 to 12 years depending on the facts. The gravity of the offense is determined primarily by the offender's intent to cause really serious bodily harm, not necessarily the actual injuries inflicted.

In determining the gravity of a wounding with intent offense, the primary factor is the assailant's intent to cause really serious bodily harm. The extent to which that intent was realized in the actual injuries is of secondary significance.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that intent to cause serious harm is the primary factor in assessing offense gravity for wounding with intent.
The court invites parties to consider this case as relevant authority on the issue of prosecution delay in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. THE CHEUNG FUNG
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of wounding contrary to section 19 of the Offences against the Person Ordinance requires proof that the defendant unlawfully and maliciously wounded another person.

principles.principle_label: Elements of wounding with intent under s.17
principles.paraphrase_en: The more serious offence of wounding with intent contrary to section 17 of the Offences against the Person Ordinance requires proof of wounding with specific intent to cause grievous bodily harm.

principles.principle_label: Elements of assaulting police officer under s.36(b)
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of assaulting a police officer contrary to section 36(b) of the Offences against the Person Ordinance requires proof of an assault on an officer who was at the time acting in the due execution of their duty.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. THAPA MANISH AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: HH Judge E Yip

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN MING LEONG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peter Hui

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SHAKEEL AWAIS
judges: Before: HH Judge Casewell

principles.principle_label: Elements of wounding with intent to resist apprehension
principles.paraphrase_en: For a conviction under section 17(a) of the Offences against the Person Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant unlawfully and maliciously wounded another person with the specific intent to resist or prevent their own lawful apprehension or detention.

The actus reus of wounding is established by proving the defendant inflicted an injury that breaks the continuity of the skin, as evidenced by cuts or lacerations requiring medical treatment such as stitches.
A defendant's admission of the elements of the offence during a video-recorded interview can provide evidence supporting a guilty plea and the factual basis for sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KO ALEXANDER MING-CHE
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge KH Cheang in court

principles.paraphrase_en: When multiple offenses arise from the same incident or course of conduct, the court may order sentences for those offenses to run consecutively, but must consider the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is not excessive.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Chan Ka Wah (陳嘉華)
relationships.description: The present judgment references the sentencing approach in Chan Ka Wah where consecutive sentences were imposed for burglary and wounding charges arising from the same incident.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHAN ILTAF
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Ivy Chui in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for sustained assault on vulnerable victim
principles.paraphrase_en: A sustained assault on a vulnerable family member within the same household, involving multiple weapons and creating a substantial risk of grave injury, is an aggravating factor in sentencing.
The use of sharp implements directed at the head of a victim is an aggravating factor as it carries an obvious and substantial risk of causing serious harm.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TUNG PAK FAI
judges: Before: Hon M Poon JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing factors for wounding with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for wounding with intent, the court must consider relevant factors established in precedent, including the level of persistence shown by the offender during the incident.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing factors outlined in Chan Chun Tat to assess whether the starting point for the sentence was correct.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG KA CHEUNG
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J

principles.principle_label: Sentencing factors for wounding with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for the offence of wounding with intent, courts should consider a non-exhaustive list of factors to assess the gravity of the offence, as there is no formal sentencing guideline and past cases are highly fact-specific.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the non-exhaustive list of sentencing factors provided in Chan Chun Tat for assessing the gravity of a wounding with intent offence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG CHEUNG HEI
judges: Before: Recorder Martin Hui, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offences, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is proportionate to the defendant's total culpability.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Lau Yun Leung
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the Court of Appeal's holding on the application of the totality principle in sentencing for multiple offences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM CHUN YUNG
judges: Before: HH Judge K Lo

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG WING SUM
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.principle_label: Elements of s.17(a) wounding with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offence under section 17(a) of the Offences against the Person Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant unlawfully and maliciously wounded the victim with the specific intent to cause grievous bodily harm.

principles.principle_label: Admission of guilt as proof of elements
principles.paraphrase_en: A guilty plea constitutes a formal admission by the defendant of all the essential legal elements of the charged offence as set out in the particulars.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. GURUNG MOHIT
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Jason Wan in Court

principles.principle_label: Elements of s.17(a) wounding with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish an offence under section 17(a) of the Offences Against the Person Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant unlawfully and maliciously wounded another person with the specific intent to cause grievous bodily harm.

principles.principle_label: Admission of facts and guilty plea
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's guilty plea and admission of the summary of facts presented by the prosecution is sufficient for the court to enter a conviction on the charge.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHO KING-CHUN
judges: Before: HH Judge A. J. Woodcock

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAI WAN HOI
judges: Before: Hon Anthea Pang JA

principles.paraphrase_en: When imposing consecutive or partially consecutive sentences for multiple offenses, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is proportionate to the defendant's overall criminality and culpability.
A standard one-third discount from the starting sentence should be applied for a timely guilty plea.
Voluntary restitution made by the defendant can warrant a further, discrete reduction in the sentence beyond the guilty plea discount.
The court may order sentences for distinct offenses to run consecutively or partially consecutively to reflect the seriousness of multiple criminal acts.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YUEN PAK HUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

principles.principle_label: Conviction on plea and agreed facts
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant can be properly convicted of an offence based on their guilty plea and acceptance of an amended summary of facts.
The offence of wounding with intent under section 17(a) requires proof of a wounding act done with the specific intent to cause grievous bodily harm.
A defendant's admission made under police caution, including an explanation for their actions, constitutes admissible evidence against them.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHAN FAIZAN ALI
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of wounding with intent under section 17(a) of the Offences against the Persons Ordinance requires proof that the defendant unlawfully and maliciously wounded another person with the specific intent to cause grievous bodily harm.

The offence of possession of a dangerous drug is established when a person is found to have physical custody or control of a substance classified as a dangerous drug, regardless of the quantity, for personal consumption or otherwise.
A defendant's confirmation of guilty pleas and admission of a Summary of Facts before the Court of First Instance constitutes a formal acceptance of the factual basis for sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG MUK LEUNG
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. L. Z. X. AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Tam J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A starting point of 5 years' imprisonment is appropriate for the offence of rape, which can be enhanced for aggravating factors.
A one-third discount from the enhanced starting point is to be applied for a guilty plea.
When sentencing for multiple offences arising from the same incident, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final combined sentence is appropriate and not excessive.
For distinct offences arising from a single incident, the court may order sentences to run consecutively, subject to the totality principle.
A Victim Impact Report prepared by a clinical psychologist is a relevant consideration for the court in sentencing, particularly for assessing the psychological harm caused by the offence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ARIF AQIB
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.paraphrase_en: A participant in a joint enterprise may be convicted of a lesser offense than the principal actors if the jury finds they did not foresee or realize that a weapon would be used during the attack, even if a weapon was ultimately used by others in the enterprise.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for multiple counts from single incident
principles.paraphrase_en: When multiple offenses arise from the same factual incident, the court should take an overall view and may impose a global sentence, with concurrent sentences on each count being appropriate.

When sentencing for wounding offenses under section 19, the court must consider the gravity of the offense, including whether the attack was brutal, unprovoked, premeditated, and targeted at unarmed victims, as well as the actual consequences even if unintended by the defendant.
The sentencing judge must respect and bear in mind the jury's verdict regarding the defendant's mental state and level of culpability when determining the appropriate sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NIP CHING WAI
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When no sentencing guidelines exist for an offence, the court may consider a range of sentences established in previous cases, but must focus primarily on the specific facts and circumstances of the instant case rather than making direct comparisons with other cases.

relationships.description: The judgment references Hung Kar Chun for establishing a sentencing range of 3-12 years imprisonment for the relevant offence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUNG KAM KI
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.principle_label: Wounding with intent - unlawful and malicious act
principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits the offence of wounding with intent to cause grievous bodily harm when they unlawfully and maliciously wound another person with the specific intent to cause grievous bodily harm.
While provocation and humiliation may be considered as mitigating factors in sentencing for a violent offence, they do not justify the offender taking the law into their own hands.

principles.principle_label: Taking law into own hands not permitted
principles.paraphrase_en: An individual is not permitted to take the law into their own hands, regardless of how much they resent the treatment they received from others.

When an offender leaves the scene of an initial altercation and then returns with a weapon, this demonstrates a cooling-off period that negates any claim of immediate provocation as a complete defense.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. DA SILVA SANTOS ALEXANDRO
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge MH Tsui in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing range for wounding with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: For the offense of wounding with intent, there is no fixed sentencing guideline, and each case is decided on its specific facts. The typical imprisonment range is 3 to 12 years, but courts may impose sentences outside this range if the circumstances justify it.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites this authority to support the proposition that sentences for wounding with intent can fall outside the usual 3-12 year range depending on the circumstances.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MAK SHING HEI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peter Hui

principles.paraphrase_en: If an offender suffers from a mental illness that contributed to the commission of the offense, the court may grant a sentencing discount to reflect diminished criminal culpability.

principles.principle_label: Rehabilitation vs. punishment for young offenders
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing a young offender, the court must balance the need for punishment with the offender's best interests and prospects for rehabilitation, particularly when negative peer associations and triad involvement are present.

A court must critically assess an offender's claims of remorse and reform against the factual background, including their associations and conduct, to determine the genuineness of the mitigation and the appropriate sentence.

relationships.description: The judgment references Chui Peng as authority for the proposition that mental illness contributing to an offense may warrant a sentencing discount.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LU A TUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge So Wai Tak

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's status as a Form 8 (Conditional Stay) holder constitutes an aggravating factor in sentencing when they commit a crime in Hong Kong.
The sentencing court must apply the totality principle by assessing the overall criminality of the offenses to ensure the final combined sentence is just and proportionate.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle summarized in Singh Gursevak regarding the aggravating factor of a defendant's immigration status.
The judgment notes that the Court of Appeal in Singh Gursevak applied the authority of Ali Saif.
The judgment applies the totality principle as articulated in Wang Quanwen to assess overall criminality for a just sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YU PAK SHUN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Sham

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIU GUOSHENG
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J

principles.paraphrase_en: There is no established sentencing guideline for the offence of wounding with intent to cause grievous bodily harm, but it is recognized as a very serious offence carrying a maximum penalty of life imprisonment.

The seriousness of the offence of wounding with intent is underscored by the principle that the mental state (mens rea) required for this offence is sufficient to constitute the mental element for murder, should the victim die from the injuries inflicted.

relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and upholds the principle established in Lau Cheong regarding the mens rea for wounding with intent being equivalent to that for murder.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUI SIU LUNG
judges: Before: District Judge Clement Lee

principles.paraphrase_en: A trial judge must assess whether the probative value of a dock identification outweighs its prejudicial effect when deciding to allow it.

The absence of an identification parade and a significant time lapse between the witness's last sighting of the accused and the trial are relevant factors that may weigh against allowing dock identification.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YU KWAN MAN
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

principles.paraphrase_en: When an offender suffers from a mental illness that contributed to the offense, the court may reduce the sentence to reflect diminished criminal culpability.

principles.principle_label: Reduced weight for general deterrence in mental disorder cases
principles.paraphrase_en: While general deterrence remains a relevant sentencing consideration, it should be given less weight when sentencing an offender with a mental disorder or intellectual handicap.

principles.principle_label: Impaired mental functioning relevance to sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: Impaired mental functioning, whether temporary or permanent, is relevant to sentencing, including by reducing the moral culpability of the offending conduct, which affects the just punishment and the relevance of denunciation as an objective.

principles.principle_label: Victim forgiveness weight in wounding cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In sentencing for wounding with intent, the mitigating weight given to forgiveness by the victim decreases as the seriousness of the injury increases.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Chiu Peng that a sentencing discount may be given for diminished culpability due to mental illness.
The judgment cites the Australian Verdins case for principles on how impaired mental functioning reduces moral culpability and affects sentencing objectives.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO MING TAT
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offence of wounding contrary to section 19 of the Offences against the Person Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant unlawfully and maliciously inflicted a wound upon another person.
The actus reus of the offence is the unlawful infliction of a wound, which involves breaking the continuity of the whole skin.
The mens rea required for the offence is malice, which encompasses either an intention to cause some injury or recklessness as to whether injury is caused.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. RASKOTI AMMAR
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Alice Chan

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. GURUNG ROYAL
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant's innocent explanation for forensic evidence (fingerprints, DNA) is rejected by the jury, and that evidence is combined with other evidence placing a person of the defendant's description at the scene, it can be sufficient to establish the defendant's presence and participation in the offence.
The standard specimen directions derived from R v Turnbull are to be used in all cases where identification is in issue, guiding the jury on assessing identification evidence.

A jury's question or finding regarding the specific role or level of involvement (such as being the 'mastermind') of a co-defendant is irrelevant and does not assist another defendant in challenging their own conviction.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principles and standard specimen directions established in R v Turnbull for cases involving identification evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG CHEUK YIU
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peter Hui

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ALI ZAIN
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must assess guilt for dangerous driving based on the manner of driving itself, not on the consequences of any resulting accident.

A reasonable driver must pay attention to road circumstances but is entitled to assume pedestrians will not cross the road arbitrarily at any time; they are not required to be prepared for pedestrians crossing at free will.
Even when a driver has a green light, they are expected to proceed through a pedestrian crossing with caution.

principles.principle_label: Higher duty of care for drivers towards vulnerable road users
principles.paraphrase_en: Drivers of motor vehicles bear a heavier burden of care in situations involving pedestrians or cyclists due to the greater vulnerability of those road users.

principles.principle_label: Effect of proceeding to trial on a guilty plea to a lesser offence
principles.paraphrase_en: If a plea of guilty to a lesser alternative offence is not accepted and the trial proceeds on the more serious charge, the guilty plea to the lesser offence is deemed withdrawn, leaving only the plea of not guilty to the more serious charge.

relationships.description: The judgment adopts the principle from Lam Chi Fat that dangerous driving is assessed based on the driving manner, not the accident consequences.
The judgment applies the principle regarding a reasonable driver's expectations of pedestrian behavior from Yeung Yiu Kin.
The judgment follows the procedural rule from Wong Ka Fai regarding the withdrawal of a guilty plea to a lesser offence when trial proceeds.
The judgment notes that the principle in Wong Ka Fai was derived by citing the English case of R v Hazeltine.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ASHRAF TAHIR
judges: Before: District Judge E Lee

principles.paraphrase_en: Previous sentencing decisions have limited reference value because the circumstances of each offence and the background of each offender differ significantly from case to case.
A stated range of imprisonment (e.g., 3 to 12 years) is not a fixed sentencing tariff but a general guideline that must be applied flexibly according to the specific facts.

relationships.description: The present judgment finds this case of limited use because it involved an appeal against conviction only, not sentencing.
The present judgment applies the sentencing principle from this case that stated imprisonment ranges are not rigid tariffs and that case comparisons are of limited value.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MEHMOOD ARSHAD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Douglas Yau in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUNG WAI CHUNG
judges: Before: HH Judge Kathie Cheung

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAW CHEUK YIN
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J

principles.paraphrase_en: When multiple offences arise from the same incident but constitute distinct criminal acts, imposing completely concurrent sentences is incorrect in principle.
The court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence properly reflects the overall criminality of all offences committed.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the sentencing principle established in HKSAR v Yang Chao regarding concurrent sentences for separate offences.

candidates.case_name: GOLDJET DEVELOPMENT LTD v. RANDAL ALAN WHITECOTTON AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge To in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court assesses witness credibility by considering factors such as consistency, demeanor, corroboration, potential interest in the outcome, and the witness's general character and respect for the law.

Evidence from a witness with no stake in the litigation and no affiliation with the parties can provide valuable corroboration for the evidence of other witnesses, though its weight may depend on the witness's access to full information.

The court must exercise caution and give little weight to the evidence of a witness who is found to be smart and selective in telling the truth, unless that evidence is corroborated by other reliable evidence.

A witness's credibility is severely undermined by a blatant contradiction between their current testimony and a prior sworn statement, casting doubt on their willingness to testify truthfully according to their interests.

In assessing a late application, the court should consider the prejudice caused by distracting the other party from preparing for trial, which is a recognized form of prejudice, particularly grave if the trial has already commenced.

relationships.target_case_name: Chun Wo Building Construction Ltd v Metta Resources Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that prejudice from a late application includes distracting the other party from trial preparation.

candidates.case_name: GOLDJET DEVELOPMENT LTD v. RANDAL ALAN WHITECOTTON AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge To in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Amendment of pleadings - necessity and prejudice
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may allow amendments to pleadings if they are necessary to determine the real question in controversy between the parties, the application is made promptly, and the opposing party would not suffer prejudice as a result.

principles.principle_label: Indemnity costs - litigation conduct and sanctioned offers
principles.paraphrase_en: A party may be awarded indemnity costs based on the opposing party's litigation conduct and for failing to accept a sanctioned offer, pursuant to the Rules of the High Court.
A court may award interest on costs under the Rules of the High Court where a sanctioned offer is not accepted.

Reserved costs, if any, should be awarded to the successful party in the relevant proceedings and taxed on a party and party basis, unless the court has specific knowledge justifying a different basis.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HASAN MD MEHEDI
judges: Before: HH Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: For a conviction of possession of dangerous drugs, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant had knowing possession of the drugs.

The court may convict a defendant of trafficking in dangerous drugs if the only reasonable inference from the evidence is that the drugs were in the defendant's possession for the purpose of trafficking.
The court may reject a defendant's explanation as to how drugs came to be in their possession if it finds that explanation to be fabricated and untruthful.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BALAKRISHNAN MAHENDRAN
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J

principles.paraphrase_en: False imprisonment can be established by the unlawful detention of a person, even if the initial taking was not pre-planned, provided it involves deprivation of liberty and the use or threat of violence during the detention.

The use of violence, threats of violence (including threats of death or serious harm), and the duration of the detention are relevant factors in assessing the seriousness of an unlawful detention offence.

candidates.case_name: JOHNSON BENJAMIN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement, as established in Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol, prohibits the return of a person to a territory where they would face a risk of persecution.
cited_statutes: Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees and its 1967 Protocol

relationships.description: The present judgment references the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini regarding the applicable legal framework.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HASAN MD MEHEDI
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. AZHAR GHAFAR
judges: Before: HH Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant admits to breaching a probation order, the court may discharge that order and proceed to sentence the defendant for the original offence for which probation was granted.

When considering whether to continue probation after a breach, the court must assess whether further supervision would be beneficial, considering factors such as the defendant's compliance, insight into their behavior, and willingness to engage with rehabilitative options.

principles.principle_label: Court's limited options after probation failure
principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant has performed unsatisfactorily on probation, showing a lack of insight and refusal of rehabilitative treatment, the court's sentencing options become severely limited, often necessitating the termination of probation and imposition of a custodial or other sentence for the original offence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO LIN CHUN
judges: Before: DHCJ Woodcock

principles.paraphrase_en: A sentencing starting point of 14 years' imprisonment can be appropriate for a kidnapping offense, considering the specific ordeal suffered by the victims and the circumstances of the case.

The courageous actions of a victim in escaping and seeking help, which shortens the duration of the ordeal and leads to police involvement, can be considered as relevant facts when determining the appropriate sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites the case to reference a 15-year starting point upheld for a kidnapping involving a child snatched by armed men.

candidates.case_name: OGUZIE IKECHUKWU CHARLES v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Lisa Wong J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing future risk of harm for non-refoulement claims, decision-makers must consider whether the feared ill-treatment originates from private disputes unrelated to protected grounds under refugee law, and whether the evidence supporting the risk is reliable rather than based on hearsay.
An appellate body may find an applicant untruthful based on inconsistent and changing accounts of material facts provided during hearings, which undermines the credibility of the claim.

principles.principle_label: Procedure for adducing new evidence on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: New evidence cannot be adduced in support of appeal submissions without first obtaining leave from the court, and documents lodged without such leave will not be considered by the court.

If there has been a significant change of circumstances since a claim was finally determined, the proper procedure is for the applicant to make a subsequent claim rather than attempting to introduce new evidence on appeal without leave.

relationships.target_case_name: 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees
relationships.description: The judgment applies the non-refoulement principle under Article 33 of the Convention in assessing the risk of persecution.

candidates.case_name: OMAR JAUFER MOHAMED HARIS v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge Reuden Lai :

principles.principle_label: Leave to apply for judicial review - test
principles.paraphrase_en: For a court to grant leave to apply for judicial review, the claim must be reasonably arguable and enjoy realistic prospects of success.

principles.principle_label: Limits of judicial review on factual findings
principles.paraphrase_en: In a judicial review, a court cannot generally challenge the factual findings of a tribunal unless those findings involve errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for non-refoulement claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A court must examine a tribunal's decision on a non-refoulement claim with rigorous examination and anxious scrutiny, assessing for errors of law, procedural unfairness, or a failure to apply high standards of fairness.

relationships.target_case_name: Po Fun Chan v Winnie Cheung
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the test for granting leave to apply for judicial review established in Po Fun Chan v Winnie Cheung.

candidates.case_name: ARMAN AZAM v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Hon G Lam and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for non-refoulement decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: The court will not interfere with a Torture Claims Appeal Board decision unless it identifies an error of law, procedural unfairness, or findings that are Wednesbury unreasonable or irrational.

Non-refoulement claims are assessed with reference to the principle under Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees and its 1967 Protocol, which prohibits returning a person to a country where they would face persecution.

relationships.description: The judgment references the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority on non-refoulement matters.

candidates.case_name: MATABUENA, MARK DIOLA v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement, as established in Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol, prohibits the return of a person to a territory where they would face a risk of persecution.
A tribunal or board is entitled to reject an applicant's evidence regarding the facts of their claim if it finds that evidence to be lacking in credibility or substance.

principles.principle_label: Insufficiency of general comments without evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: General assertions or comments made by an applicant, which are not supported by any corroborating evidence, are an insufficient basis upon which to establish a claim.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority.
The present judgment cites the Court of Appeal's decision in Salim Ahmed as relevant authority.

candidates.case_name: ARMAN AZAM v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement, as established in Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol, prohibits the return of a person to a territory where they would face a risk of persecution.

A decision of an administrative board, such as the Torture Claims Appeal Board, can only be successfully challenged on judicial review if it contains an error of law, involves procedural unfairness, or is Wednesbury unreasonable/irrational.

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining a non-refoulement claim, the decision-making body must assess the applicant's risk by considering multiple factors, including past experiences, the probability of future harm, the availability of state protection, and the feasibility of internal relocation.

principles.paraphrase_en: The fact that a tribunal accepts some parts of an applicant's factual evidence (such as forming a general background to the claim) does not automatically mean the tribunal must find a real risk of harm upon return. principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing the risk of harm for a non-refoulement claim, the decision-maker must consider whether the risk is both real and foreseeable, not merely speculative. The absence of past physical harm can be a relevant factor in this assessment.

A claim for non-refoulement protection may fail if the claimant's home country has a functioning government and legal system capable of providing reasonable protection against the alleged persecutors.
If a claimant can reasonably and safely relocate within their home country to avoid the risk of harm, this may negate the need for non-refoulement protection.

principles.principle_label: Judicial review grounds for immigration decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: A challenge to an immigration decision on grounds of unreasonableness or unfairness requires the applicant to identify specific errors of law, irrationality, or procedural unfairness; bare assertions without particulars are insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for Torture Claims Appeal Board
principles.paraphrase_en: The court will examine decisions of the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board with rigorous examination and anxious scrutiny, assessing for errors of law, procedural unfairness, and whether high standards of fairness were applied.

principles.principle_label: Credibility and corroboration in torture claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The Board may reject a torture claim based on adverse credibility findings and a lack of reliable corroborative evidence, particularly where it has had the benefit of hearing the applicant's oral evidence and questioning.

Non-refoulement claims in Hong Kong encompass risks of torture under the Immigration Ordinance, and risks of violation of absolute rights under the Hong Kong Bill of Rights, including rights to life and freedom from torture or cruel treatment.

principles.paraphrase_en: When deciding whether to extend time for an applicant to appeal a refusal of leave for judicial review, the court must consider the merits of the intended appeal.
