principles.paraphrase_en: A fiduciary duty arises when the relationship between two parties creates an objective, legitimate expectation recognized by equity that the fiduciary will not use their position in a manner contrary to the principal's interests. This expectation is assessed objectively and does not depend on the subjective beliefs of either party.

Whether a senior employee or manager owes fiduciary duties to their employer depends on their specific role and functions, particularly in relation to the specific duties they were assigned to perform.

principles.principle_label: Breach of duty of fidelity test
principles.paraphrase_en: An employee breaches their duty of fidelity when their act undermines the employer's confidence in them and fundamentally damages the employment relationship at its core.

relationships.target_case_name: TBC International (HK) Ltd v Chan Kwok Sum
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that senior employees can owe fiduciary duties based on their specific assigned roles.

candidates.case_name: NORMAN GUY DONALD AND ANOTHER v. LEE MICHAEL CHAK CHUEN in his capacity as executor of the estate of LI PAK IU AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Chu VP, Yuen and Au JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Interpretation of void gifts in wills
principles.paraphrase_en: When a gift in a will clause is void from the beginning (void ab initio), there is no property created that trustees can subsequently apply for the benefit of other potential beneficiaries under the same clause.

principles.principle_label: Application of contractual severability to wills
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of severability, which is a principle of contract law, does not automatically apply to the construction of wills.

principles.principle_label: Test for invalidity affecting other objects in a will clause
principles.paraphrase_en: The test from Re Canning's Will Trusts may be applied to determine whether the invalidity of one gift or object in a will clause invalidates another object, such as a trustee's power, within the same clause.

principles.principle_label: Primary of clear language in will interpretation
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the language of a will clause is clear, extrinsic considerations such as the adequacy of provisions for other family members become of little materiality in interpretation.

relationships.target_case_name: Midland Business Management Ltd v Lo Man Kui
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes the application of the contractual severability doctrine from this case to the construction of wills.

relationships.target_case_name: Sadler v Imperial Life Assurance Co of Canada Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes the contractual principle of severability applied in this case from the principles governing will construction.
The judgment applies the test from this case to determine the effect of one void gift on other objects within the same will clause.

candidates.case_name: JOINT AND SEVERAL PROVISIONAL LIQUIDATORS OF HSIN CHONG CONSTRUCTION CO LTD (PROVISIONAL LIQUIDATORS APPOINTED) v. THE CHINESE UNIVERSITY OF HONG KONG AND OTHERS
judges: Before : Hon Cheung and Chu JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An application for directions under section 200(3) of the Companies (Winding Up and Miscellaneous Provisions) Ordinance is reserved for cases where a genuine difficulty arises in the course of the liquidation.

The requirement to segregate trust monies, as discussed in authorities like Lehman, Rayack, and MacJordan, applies in the context of companies holding trust monies that have gone into liquidation, and does not apply to a party not in liquidation.

For a trust to arise, the separate trust fund must be fully constituted prior to insolvency; a mere contractual obligation to create a trust fund is insufficient if no separate fund is actually established.

principles.principle_label: Identification vs. segregation of trust assets
principles.paraphrase_en: So long as trust assets can be clearly identified, setting them aside in a separate fund is not strictly required for a trust to be valid.

relationships.target_case_name: MacJordan Construction Ltd v. Brookmount Erostin Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites MacJordan for the principle that a trust fund must be fully constituted prior to insolvency.
The judgment cites Re Kayford for the principle that clear identification of trust assets can suffice without strict segregation.
The judgment notes that Hunter v Moss chose not to follow MacJordan on the requirement for appropriation of specific assets.

relationships.target_case_name: Yew Sang Hong Ltd v. Hong Kong Housing Authority
relationships.description: The judgment discusses this case regarding arguments about Quistclose trusts and set-off rights in retention money disputes.

candidates.case_name: CHINA CONSTRUCTION BANK (ASIA) CORPORATION LTD v. KLARENKEN ENTERPRISE LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Discharge of debt by placing funds at creditor's disposal
principles.paraphrase_en: A debtor's payment obligation is discharged when securities or funds held by the creditor are, by the debtor's instruction, placed at the creditor's disposal for settlement, regardless of whether the creditor actually applies them.

principles.principle_label: Condition for funds being at creditor's disposal
principles.paraphrase_en: For funds to be considered at a creditor's disposal, the creditor must have the practical ability to apply them. Funds subject to litigation or rival claims that prevent the creditor from accessing them are not at the creditor's disposal.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Trustor AB to illustrate the principle that funds are not at a creditor's disposal if litigation prevents their access.

candidates.case_name: THE JOINT AND SEVERAL LIQUIDATORS OF GREEN ELITE LTD (IN OFFICIAL LIQUIDATION IN THE BRITISH VIRGIN ISLANDS) v. STANDARD CHARTERED BANK (HONG KONG) LTD
judges: Before: Hon Fung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: For a court to find a director liable under section 120(1) of the BVI Business Companies Act, it must be satisfied that the director acted dishonestly. A director who holds a genuine but mistaken belief about their entitlement to deal with company assets is not dishonest.
A director can be held personally liable for a loan that appears in a company's accounts if that director alone is responsible for the transaction.

principles.principle_label: Requirements for pre-action disclosure against third parties
principles.paraphrase_en: To obtain pre-action disclosure from a third party, the applicant must show: (1) a real prospect the information may lead to locating or preserving assets subject to a proprietary claim; (2) the request for documents must be as specific as a subpoena; and (3) the court must balance the potential advantage of disclosure against the detriment to the person from whom disclosure is sought.
A party is estopped from reopening a factual issue that has been conclusively determined by a court of competent jurisdiction in prior proceedings.

principles.principle_label: No need for discovery where destination of funds is known
principles.paraphrase_en: Discovery or disclosure from a third party (such as a bank) is not necessary to realize assets if it is already known where the funds in question have gone, and the claimant can trace the funds directly to the recipients.

relationships.target_case_name: Nam Tai Property Inc v IsZo Capital LP & anr
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that dishonesty is required under section 120(1) of the BVI Business Companies Act.

candidates.case_name: Y AND ANOTHER v. GI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Security for costs in arbitration setting aside applications
principles.paraphrase_en: Order 23 rule 1 of the Rules of the High Court, which governs security for costs, applies to applications made to the court under section 81 of the Arbitration Ordinance to set aside an arbitral award.

principles.principle_label: Policy against facilitating challenges to arbitral awards
principles.paraphrase_en: The policy of Hong Kong law is to support the arbitral process and arbitral awards; therefore, the court should not make it easier or more accessible to challenge an award by disapplying the ordinary rules for security for costs.

relationships.target_case_name: P1 v D (Arbitration: Security for Costs)
relationships.description: The present judgment fully endorses and follows the reasoning in P1 v D regarding the application of security for costs rules to applications to set aside arbitral awards.

candidates.case_name: ALPHA & LEADER ASSOCIATES LTD v. VICTORY MEGA TRADING LTD
judges: Before : Hon Kwan VP and Cheung JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Duty of full and frank disclosure
principles.paraphrase_en: The duty to make full and frank disclosure applies to ex parte applications, but not to inter partes hearings where the other party has had a fair opportunity to present evidence.

For a failure to disclose to be material, it must relate to a matter that the court was required to consider in granting the relief sought. If the duty of disclosure does not arise, the alleged non-disclosure is not a ground for setting aside the order.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of evidence in interlocutory applications
principles.paraphrase_en: In an interlocutory application, the court does not make definitive findings of fact; its role is to assess whether there is a good arguable case based on the evidence presented.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of explanations for lack of evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering a party's lack of evidence to refute allegations from separate proceedings, the court should take into account any reasonable explanation provided for that lack of evidence, such as a settlement made on a no-admission basis.

relationships.description: The present judgment holds that the trial judge properly addressed the defendant's argument based on this case regarding the identity of parties to the contract.

candidates.case_name: LUI KWONG SHING trading as WAI SING ENGINEERING COMPANY v. PINE WOOD (F.E.) INTERNATIONAL LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Matthew Ho in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for contractual conditions
principles.paraphrase_en: A party alleging breach of a contractual condition must provide evidence to support that allegation; the absence of contemporaneous complaint when a breach should have been obvious can support a finding that the condition was satisfied.

The court may reject a witness's evidence as exaggerated or inconsistent when it is contradicted by documentary records and the witness's claimed attitude is at odds with their demonstrated conduct in correspondence.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for factual findings
principles.paraphrase_en: Factual disputes in civil cases are resolved on the balance of probabilities, with the court preferring one party's evidence over another's where conflicts exist.
When interpreting a signed undertaking, the court examines its actual terms to determine its legal effect, not a party's subjective belief about its purpose, unless induced by misrepresentation.

principles.principle_label: Inference from failure to call material witness
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a prima facie case is established and a party fails to call a material witness who could speak to the relevant facts without plausible explanation, the court may draw an adverse inference against that party.

candidates.case_name: SMARTEK E & M ENGINEERING (HK) LTD v. WIN WIN WAY CONSTRUCTION CO LTD
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Andrew Li in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A witness's evidence may be rejected if it contradicts undisputed contemporaneous documents, defies common sense, and is inconsistent with common industry practice.

principles.principle_label: Formation of partly written, partly oral contracts
principles.paraphrase_en: A contract may be formed partly in writing and partly orally, where an unsigned written document can form the foundational framework of the agreement, supplemented by subsequent oral discussions.
Evidence from a witness who no longer works for a party may be considered particularly convincing as they can be viewed as independent.

principles.principle_label: Accrual of cause of action for goods/services
principles.paraphrase_en: The cause of action for the supply of goods or services generally arises when the goods are delivered or services are supplied, absent special contractual terms to the contrary.

principles.principle_label: Procedural payment terms and cause of action
principles.paraphrase_en: Procedural contractual provisions for payment assessment or demands do not delay the accrual of the cause of action for supplied goods or services.

relationships.target_case_name: China Light & Power Company Ltd v See Kong Silk Ltd & Ors
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that procedural payment terms do not affect when a cause of action accrues.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG CHI KIT
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Caesar Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: When facts are capable of an innocent explanation, and the defendant chooses not to testify to provide that explanation, the court may draw a stronger inference against the defendant regarding the facts in issue.

principles.principle_label: Elements of theft - appropriation of property belonging to another
principles.paraphrase_en: For theft, the prosecution must prove the defendant appropriated property belonging to another, with the intention to permanently deprive the other of it, and did so dishonestly.

The test for dishonesty in theft is the two-fold test established in R v Ghosh, which considers both the objective standards of ordinary reasonable people and the defendant's own subjective state of mind.

principles.principle_label: Mistaken credit as property belonging to another
principles.paraphrase_en: Funds mistakenly credited into a person's account can constitute 'property belonging to another' for the purposes of theft, as the beneficial ownership remains with the entity that made the mistaken transfer.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Li Defan regarding drawing adverse inferences when a defendant chooses not to testify to provide an innocent explanation for the facts.
The judgment applies the two-fold test for dishonesty established in Ghosh to determine whether the defendant's conduct was dishonest.

candidates.case_name: LEADER SCREWS MANUFACTURING COMPANY LTD v. HUANG SHUNKUI
judges: Before: Hon Barma JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Assets subject to a post-judgment Mareva injunction remain the property of the defendant until they are taken in execution, and remain available to satisfy any costs orders in favor of the plaintiff.

principles.principle_label: Security for costs pending appeal - overriding principle
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's primary consideration in an application for security for costs pending appeal is whether it is just to grant such security based on the specific facts of the case.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the general principle from Ming Hsieh regarding asset ownership under Mareva injunctions to the context of post-judgment injunctions.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE HEUNG YEE
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The circumstances under which a trial judge must give a good character direction to the jury, and the consequences of failing to do so, are governed by the Court of Final Appeal's majority opinion in Tang Siu Man v HKSAR (No 2). The direction should address both credibility and propensity.

The test for determining dishonesty involves two stages: first, whether according to the ordinary standards of reasonable and honest people the defendant's conduct was dishonest; second, if it was, whether the defendant must have realized that reasonable and honest people would regard their conduct as dishonest.

A belief can be honestly or genuinely held even if it is not reasonably held. The reasonableness of a belief is relevant as a factor for the court to consider when determining whether the belief was actually and honestly held, but an unreasonable belief does not automatically mean it was dishonest.

The court may attach limited or no weight to records of interview where the wording used (such as neutral terms like 'took' versus 'stole') does not accurately reflect the defendant's actual state of mind at the time, particularly when the central issue is dishonest intent.

An appellate court will not interfere with a sentence imposed at trial unless it is wrong in principle or manifestly excessive. The fact that a sentence is not light does not, by itself, provide a sufficient reason for appellate intervention.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from Tang Siu Man regarding when a good character direction must be given and the consequences of its omission.
The judgment applies the two-stage test for dishonesty established in Ghosh.

candidates.case_name: BLUESUN CAPITAL LTD v. TEWOO GROUP FINANCE NO 3 LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Manzoni, SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: For the purpose of determining whether a bondholder meets a specified percentage threshold to direct trustee action, bonds that are beneficially held by or on behalf of the issuer, guarantor, or their subsidiaries are deemed not to be outstanding, even if not formally cancelled.

Whether bonds are beneficially held for an issuer is a question of fact to be determined by reference to the intention of the acquiring entity and the issuer at the time of acquisition. The party asserting beneficial ownership bears the burden of establishing that intention.

A trustee holds money received for bond payments on trust for application according to the trust deed. A bondholder meeting a specified percentage threshold of outstanding bonds is entitled to require the trustee to bring an action for recovery.

candidates.case_name: WONG TO YICK WOOD LOCK OINTMENT LTD v. IMPERIAL CHARITY HALL MEDICINES LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge MK Liu in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Goodwill in trade marks and get-up
principles.paraphrase_en: Substantial goodwill can exist in a registered trade mark, an unregistered mark, and the overall get-up or presentation of a product.
Once passing-off is established, the court is generally entitled to presume that the plaintiff has suffered some damage, unless the specific circumstances indicate otherwise.

The legal threshold for establishing a likelihood of confusion is met not by a mere possibility, but if a number of ordinary consumers would be caused to reasonably doubt or wonder whether the defendant's goods share the same origin as the plaintiff's.
In the global assessment of likelihood of confusion, the defendant's intention is a relevant factor, including whether they copied the plaintiff's mark or deliberately adopted a similar sign.

When assessing deceptive similarity, the court must consider the imperfect recollection of the average consumer, and a defendant's argument based on minute, detailed differences between marks is misconceived if it ignores this principle.

relationships.target_case_name: British Telecommunications Plc v One in a Million Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that a name can be an instrument of deception in two scenarios.

relationships.target_case_name: Tsit Wing (Hong Kong) Co Ltd v TWG Tea Co Pte Ltd (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the test for likelihood of confusion as articulated by Gummow NPJ in this case.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that the defendant's intention is a factor in the global appreciation test for confusion.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong To Yick Wood Lock Ointment Limited v Singapore Medicine Co (a firm) & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle of imperfect recollection established in this earlier case involving the same plaintiff.

candidates.case_name: CHAN SUET YAN, the person appointed to represent the estate of PANG HO TONG, deceased v. TSUI LOK HEI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Bonnie Cheng in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in adverse possession
principles.paraphrase_en: The party claiming title through adverse possession bears the legal burden of proving, with compelling evidence, both factual possession and the requisite intention to possess the land to the exclusion of all others for the statutory period.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of possession from paper title
principles.paraphrase_en: The registered owner of land is legally presumed to be in possession of that land by virtue of holding the paper title.

principles.principle_label: Requirement for clear and compelling evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A court cannot make a finding of fact based on an expert's evidence when there is no clear and compelling direct evidence from the claimant themselves to support that fact.

principles.principle_label: Evidential significance of acts of possession
principles.paraphrase_en: Acts such as cutting vegetation or picking fruit from land are not necessarily sufficient to demonstrate adverse possession, especially where the land has inherent value, and their significance depends on the context and the nature of the land.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tak Yue v Kung Kwok Wai
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle from this case that the paper title owner is deemed to be in possession of the land.

relationships.target_case_name: Luckway Properties Limited v Liu, Chi Kong & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the test for adverse possession as set out in this case, regarding the burden of proof and the elements of factual possession and intention.

relationships.target_case_name: Cadija Unna v S Don Maris Appa
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes the present case on the basis that the context of land value and use in Hong Kong is different from the circumstances in the cited Privy Council case.

candidates.case_name: CHAPMAN INTERNATIONAL MACAO COMMERCIAL OFFSHORE LTD v. HEALTH LIFE TEXTILE (HK) COMPANY LTD
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge G. Chow in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for payment to third party
principles.paraphrase_en: When a debtor pays a third party instead of the creditor, the debtor bears the legal risk. If the money does not reach the creditor, the debtor must prove the creditor authorized the third party to receive payment, held the third party out as authorized, induced the debtor to believe so, or that a relevant trade custom existed.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for factual assertions
principles.paraphrase_en: A party asserting a fact (like an agreement) must establish it on the balance of probabilities. If they fail to raise even a prima facie case, the court cannot draw an adverse inference from the opposing party's failure to call a witness to refute it.

The court assesses a witness's credibility by examining the internal consistency of their evidence and their performance under cross-examination. Minor errors or exaggerations that are not material to the core issues do not necessarily destroy a witness's credibility.

The court makes findings of fact based on the evidence and the balance of probabilities. If a party fails to provide credible or satisfactory explanations for their assertions, the court will find they have not discharged their burden of proof.

relationships.target_case_name: Sky King Machinery Engineering Ltd v China Harbor Engineering Company Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a failure to raise a prima facie case precludes drawing an adverse inference from the opponent's failure to call a witness.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Kim Hong v Chan Yan Hong & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case, though the specific principle for which it is cited is not detailed in the provided paragraphs.

candidates.case_name: KWOK CHEUK KIN v. PRESIDENT OF LEGISLATIVE COUNCIL FOR AND ON BEHALF OF THE LEGISLATIVE COUNCIL
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Lam VP and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person bringing a judicial review application must demonstrate a sufficient interest in the matter, which is a distinct requirement separate from whether the legal arguments are reasonably arguable on their merits.

principles.principle_label: Merit as factor in standing assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: While the legal merit of an applicant's contention is a relevant factor when assessing whether they have standing to bring a judicial review, it is not by itself sufficient to confer standing.

Individuals whose interests are not directly affected by an administrative decision or action generally lack the standing to challenge it, even if they are constituents of an elected representative who is affected.
In a rolled-up hearing for judicial review, a court's finding that an applicant lacks standing effectively determines that the substantive legal challenge also lacks merit.

relationships.target_case_name: 郭卓堅 v 立法會主席梁君彥
relationships.description: The present court applies the standing principles established in this prior case involving the same applicant to reject his claim of standing based on his elected legislator being impeded.

relationships.target_case_name: AXA General Insurance Ltd v HM Advocate
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Supreme Court authority for the principle that there is a correlation between the criteria for deciding if a third party should have standing and other requirements.

candidates.case_name: KWAN CHI ON v. 張偉雄 AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Gary C C Lam in Court (Open to public)

principles.principle_label: Exclusive possession requirement for adverse possession
principles.paraphrase_en: For a squatter to establish adverse possession, they must demonstrate exclusive occupation of the land. The absence of fencing is not determinative; the key question is whether the squatter took steps to exclude others, including the owner or their agents, from the land.

principles.principle_label: Test for excluding others in adverse possession
principles.paraphrase_en: The test for establishing adverse possession is not merely whether other persons entered the land, but whether the squatter took any steps to exclude others from the land.
Mere use of land for a purpose such as farming, without evidence of steps to exclude others, does not constitute exclusive possession required for adverse possession.

principles.principle_label: Limited ratio of Wong Tak Yue on intention to possess
principles.paraphrase_en: The ratio of Wong Tak Yue v Keung Kwok Wai is limited to the specific situation where a squatter's willingness to pay rent to the owner negates the requisite intention to possess. It does not create a general exception to the rule against hypothetical questions in evidence.

principles.principle_label: Physical difficulty of access not equivalent to exclusive possession
principles.paraphrase_en: The fact that land may be physically difficult to access (e.g., on a slope) does not, by itself, amount to exclusive possession for the purpose of adverse possession, especially where various parties have readily entered the land.

relationships.target_case_name: Cadija Unna v S Don Maris Appa
relationships.description: The present case is distinguished on the basis that land in Hong Kong is valuable, unlike the swampy land in Ceylon where cutting grass was held to be an act of possession.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tak Yue v Keung Kwok Wai & anor (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that a squatter's willingness to pay rent negates the intention to possess, but clarifies its ratio is limited to that specific context.

relationships.target_case_name: Pang Moon Wan v Yu Chi Foon
relationships.description: The judgment cites the judge's own previous decision to support the interpretation that the ratio of Wong Tak Yue is limited to willingness to pay rent.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Ting On v Multi-Skill Investments Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the point that the context of Wong Tak Yue is very specific and does not create a broad exception.

candidates.case_name: HING YIP HOLDINGS (HONG KONG) LTD (In Compulsory Liquidation) v. CELLMARK CHINA LTD (In Voluntary Liquidation) (formerly known as Pechiney Far East Limited) AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The party alleging misappropriation of funds bears the legal burden of proving it on the balance of probabilities. The court cannot infer misappropriation merely from an absence of documentation without sufficient factual evidence about the payments.

principles.principle_label: Standard for breach of director's fiduciary duty
principles.paraphrase_en: The fiduciary duty of a director to act bona fide in the company's best interests is tested subjectively. A director is in breach only if they did not honestly believe their act or omission was in the company's interests, even if the action involved a reasonable risk of liability such as breach of contract.

For a finding of dishonest assistance in a breach of trust or fiduciary duty, it is sufficient if the defendant entertained a 'clear suspicion' that funds were being misappropriated. It is not necessary to prove the defendant knew all the detailed facts of the breach.

When determining a defendant's subjective state of mind (such as dishonesty), the court may draw inferences from what the person knew, said, and did, including their evidence. The reasonableness of an alleged belief is often determinative of whether the court will accept it.

principles.principle_label: Pleading requirement for breach of contract claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim based on an alleged breach of contract terms (such as trust provisions in banking facilities) cannot be pursued unless it is specifically pleaded that the defendants procured or caused that breach, or assisted others in doing so.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Regentcrest that the test for a director's breach of fiduciary duty is subjective, based on the director's honest belief.
The judgment cites the discussion in Twinsectra regarding 'blind-eye knowledge,' where knowledge is imputed if a person deliberately avoids inquiry to avoid confirming a suspicion.

relationships.target_case_name: Barlow Clowes International Ltd v Eurotrust International Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Barlow Clowes that a 'clear suspicion' of misappropriation is sufficient for dishonesty in assistance claims.

relationships.target_case_name: Bilta (UK) Ltd v NatWest Markets plc
relationships.description: The judgment applies the explanatory reminders from Bilta regarding how to determine a defendant's subjective state of mind for dishonesty.

candidates.case_name: MAMMY PANCAKE COMPANY LTD v. CARLA DAY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Fung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Claims of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient detail; stating facts that could also indicate honest conduct is inadequate.
Where a party deliberately evades service, such as by returning properly sent mail to a provided address, the court may deem service to have been effectively completed.

candidates.case_name: GALLERIA (HONG KONG), LTD AND ANOTHER v. DBS BANK LTD., HONG KONG BRANCH Defendant
judges: Before: Hon Kwan Ag CJHC, Cheung JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The correct approach to assess dishonesty in a dishonest assistance claim involves a two-stage test: first, ascertain what the defendant actually knew, and second, objectively assess whether, based on that knowledge, the defendant's conduct was dishonest.

principles.principle_label: Knowing receipt barred by valid contract
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim in knowing receipt cannot succeed where the defendant's receipt of the property was made pursuant to a valid and binding contract, unless that contract has been set aside.

relationships.target_case_name: Criterion Properties v Stratford UK Properties
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Criterion that a knowing receipt claim is barred by a valid and binding contract governing the receipt.

candidates.case_name: POLYLINE DEVELOPMENT LTD v. CHING LIN CHUEN AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Manzoni SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Striking out pleadings - reasonable cause of action
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering an application to strike out a pleading for disclosing no reasonable cause of action, the court must assume the pleaded facts are true and cannot consider evidence. A pleading should not be struck out merely for lacking particulars; the proper remedy is to order particulars.

Allegations of fraud or dishonesty must be sufficiently particularized. Pleading facts consistent with honesty is insufficient. The defendant must know the primary facts from which an inference of dishonesty is to be drawn.

principles.principle_label: Test for inferring dishonesty at pleading stage
principles.paraphrase_en: At the interlocutory stage, the test for a properly pleaded allegation of dishonesty is whether, based on the pleaded primary facts, an inference of dishonesty is more likely than one of innocence or negligence. The court only determines if facts are pleaded that justify the plea; the ultimate assessment of evidence is for trial.

Where a person (A) makes a voluntary payment to another (B) or pays for property vested in B or jointly, there is a presumption A did not intend a gift. The money or property is held on resulting trust for A (or proportionately for joint purchasers). This presumption is rebuttable by the counter-presumption of advancement or direct evidence of A's intention to make a gift.

principles.principle_label: Striking out as abuse of process - test
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim can be struck out as an abuse of process or as frivolous only if it is shown to be bound to fail. The fact a claim is conjectural is not a bar; the key is whether it is demonstrably unsustainable.

relationships.target_case_name: Asgain Co Ltd v Cheng Yan Kan (No 2)
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle upheld in this Court of Appeal case regarding estoppel by convention/contractual convention.

relationships.target_case_name: Chung Pui Tong v Qian Zhen
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle applied by Mimmie Chan J regarding estoppel by convention.

relationships.target_case_name: Szeto Wing Hong v Maintown Industries Ltd & Another
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle applied by Linda Chan J regarding estoppel by convention.

candidates.case_name: TANG YUAN YI v. TOKYO INTERNATIONAL INVESTMENT LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge William Wong SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The fraud exception to summary judgment is not engaged merely because a claim arises from a background of fraud. It is only engaged if the plaintiff must allege and prove fraud against the defendant to establish or maintain its claim.

A court should not liberally infer an allegation of deliberate dishonesty where none is expressly made. An act or omission should only be considered inherently dishonest if its very nature implies dishonesty.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of all materials for fraud exception
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining if the fraud exception applies, the court must consider all relevant materials, including subsequent pleadings and affidavits in reply, not just the initial claim.

principles.principle_label: Unjust enrichment claim independent of fraud
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim in unjust enrichment against a recipient of funds can be established without the plaintiff needing to allege or prove fraud against that defendant, even if the funds originated from a scam perpetrated on the plaintiff by a third party.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the test from Universal Capital Bank to determine whether the fraud exception to summary judgment is engaged.
The judgment applies the principles established in Zimmer regarding the fraud exception in the context of an unjust enrichment claim.

relationships.target_case_name: Laerdal Medical Limited v Hong Kong Haocheng International Trade Limited
relationships.description: The present case follows the reasoning in Laerdal, where the fraud exception was held not to be engaged in a similar email fraud and unjust enrichment scenario.

relationships.target_case_name: Menfond Electronic Art & Computer Design Co., Ltd v Wong Wang Tat Victor & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites Menfond for the principle that an allegation of deliberate dishonesty should not be inferred too liberally by the court.

candidates.case_name: KWONG YI LING (suing on behalf of herself and all other shareholders in the 6th Defendant except the 1st and 2nd Defendants) v. LAU KWUN LEUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Linda Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Scope of assistance in breach of trust
principles.paraphrase_en: The concept of 'assistance' in a breach of trust is not limited to acts that occur before or at the same time as the breach. The nature of the breach determines what constitutes assistance.

relationships.description: The judgment references the textbook 'Lewin on Trust' for an explanation of the principle concerning assistance in a breach of trust.
The judgment references the textbook 'Bullen & Leake' for an alternative statement of the principle concerning assistance in a breach of trust.

candidates.case_name: THE LAW SOCIETY OF HONG KONG v. A SOLICITOR
judges: Before : Hon Yuen JA, Barma JA and Chow JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The professional disciplinary body is in a uniquely advantageous position to assess and discharge its duties in disciplinary matters.
Orders made by a solicitors' disciplinary tribunal are not primarily punitive; their purposes are to prevent repetition of the offence and to uphold the reputation of the legal profession.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Re A Solicitor regarding the non-punitive purpose of disciplinary tribunal orders.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ALI NAZAKAT
judges: Before: Hon Pang and Zervos JJA and M Poon J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence of uncharged acts may be admitted if excluding it would leave the jury with an incomplete or incomprehensible account of events, provided the court assesses whether its prejudicial effect outweighs its probative value.

When considering the admissibility of evidence, particularly regarding uncharged acts, the court must evaluate whether the risk of unfair prejudice to the defendant outweighs the evidence's value in proving facts in issue.

principles.principle_label: Jury's ability to handle prejudicial evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court may be satisfied that a jury, properly directed, would not use admitted evidence in an impermissible manner or be distracted from the core issues of the case.
For an appeal against conviction to succeed based on the admission of evidence, the appellant must demonstrate an unacceptable risk of prejudice that rendered the trial unfair.

candidates.case_name: WELLSON SILK TRADING LTD v. CHEUNG WAI YING AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A statutory derivative action is commenced by a shareholder applying for leave to bring proceedings in the company's name, which must be granted by the court before the writ is issued.

A minority shareholder may bring an action in the name of the company, despite the general rule that the company itself is the proper plaintiff, if leave is obtained from the court under the statutory derivative action procedure.
The court may grant leave to commence a statutory derivative action by consent of the parties, based on the affirmations filed in support of or in opposition to the leave application.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN KAM CHING (陳鑑清)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Lord Phillips of Worth Matravers NPJ

principles.principle_label: Definition of false instrument under s.69(a)(vii)
principles.paraphrase_en: A document is a false instrument under section 69(a)(vii) of the Crimes Ordinance if it is made in circumstances in which it was not in fact made, which includes documents that contain false representations about extraneous facts or underlying transactions, not just lies about the document's own making or alteration.

The traditional requirement in forgery law, known as the automendacity principle, is that a document must 'tell a lie about itself'—meaning the falsity must relate to the document's own making or authenticity, not merely to extraneous facts or underlying transactions.

The phrase 'in circumstances in which it was not in fact made' in forgery provisions can be interpreted broadly to include false statements about external circumstances (like an underlying transaction being a sham) that are integral to the document's purported effect, not just the physical act of creation.

principles.principle_label: Sham transaction as basis for falsity
principles.paraphrase_en: A document may be considered false if it purports to effect a transaction (like a sale) that was never intended to transfer property, rendering the transaction a sham, irrespective of the truthfulness of specific details like the stated price.

relationships.description: The judgment discusses Donnelly as a binding English authority that supported a broad interpretation of forgery provisions similar to section 69(a)(vii).
The judgment notes that Jeraj followed the binding precedent of Donnelly in applying a broad construction of forgery.
The judgment cites this reference as canvassing competing views on the scope of forgery and noting academic criticism of the broad approach in Donnelly.
The judgment references Windsor for the historical formulation of the automendacity principle in forgery law.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. RAMAN KUMAR (also known as RAMAN KUMAR)
judges: Before: HH Judge Casewell

candidates.case_name: KWOK HIU KWAN v. CONVOY GLOBAL HOLDINGS LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.principle_label: Director's duty of good faith - subjective test with objective elements
principles.paraphrase_en: A director's duty to act in good faith in the company's best interests is generally subjective, but becomes more difficult to prove if the action caused substantial detriment to the company. If there is no evidence the director actually considered the company's interests, the test becomes objective: whether an intelligent and honest director could have reasonably believed the action was in the company's interests.

The duty to exercise powers for proper purposes is assessed objectively. The court must identify the power, its proper purpose, the actual purpose for which it was exercised, and determine if that purpose was proper. The director's honest belief about acting in the company's interests is irrelevant to this assessment.

principles.principle_label: No conflict duty - strict objective test
principles.paraphrase_en: The duty to avoid conflicts of interest is objective and strictly enforced. The test is whether a reasonable person would see a real or substantial possibility of conflict. This inflexible rule applies regardless of conscious wrongdoing or whether the company actually suffered loss.

principles.principle_label: Articles void if contrary to companies legislation
principles.paraphrase_en: Any provision in a company's articles of association that conflicts with the relevant companies legislation is void. This principle is based on public policy and applies generally.

principles.principle_label: Court's reluctance to micromanage internal affairs
principles.paraphrase_en: The court will generally not intervene to micromanage issues of internal company management that fall within the remit of the board of directors or shareholders in general meeting.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as part of the line of authority establishing that articles conflicting with companies legislation are void.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Greater Beijing Region Expressways Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Hong Kong case as part of the development of the principle that articles contrary to companies legislation are void.

relationships.target_case_name: Ghossoub v Team Y&R Holdings Hong Kong Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported Court of Appeal case which references and summarizes the principle from Re Greater Beijing Region Expressways Ltd.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSE LOI FUK
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge R Wong

principles.paraphrase_en: The offense of fraud under section 16A of the Theft Ordinance requires the defendant to have dishonestly obtained a loan by using forged documents, with the intent to permanently deprive the lender of the money.
A defendant's admission to the police regarding the commission of an offense, including the motive, method, and benefit received, can constitute strong evidence supporting a conviction.
A defendant's act of absconding from justice is a relevant factor that may be considered as an aggravating circumstance during sentencing, as it demonstrates a disregard for the legal process.

In sentencing, the court may consider mitigating factors such as the defendant's personal background, family situation, employment history, and expressions of remorse, as presented in mitigation letters.
A defendant's previous convictions, particularly those for similar offenses of dishonesty, are relevant to sentencing as they reflect on the defendant's character and propensity to re-offend.

candidates.case_name: NEW SPARKLE ROLL INTERNATIONAL GROUP LTD v. ZHENG HAO JIANG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Alexander Stock SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Subjective test for director's breach of duty
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a breach of duty by directors, the company must prove the directors acted dishonestly and not in the company's bona fide interests. The test is subjective, focusing on whether the directors honestly believed their actions were in the company's interest, not whether the actions were objectively beneficial or merely negligent.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for serious allegations
principles.paraphrase_en: The standard of proof for serious allegations such as fraud or dishonesty is commensurate with the gravity of the allegations. Inferences of dishonesty are not to be drawn lightly.

principles.principle_label: Caution in summary determination of dishonesty
principles.paraphrase_en: While it is permissible in principle to resolve allegations of dishonesty on a summary basis, the authorities indicate that courts should exercise caution in doing so.

candidates.case_name: LAW YIU WAI RAY AND ANOTHER v. CHEUNG YING MAN also known as CHRISTINA HADIWIBAWA
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Manzoni SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Strike out inappropriate for privilege determination
principles.paraphrase_en: A court should not decide on a strike-out application whether a document or arrangement is covered by legal professional privilege, as such a determination requires detailed factual investigation.

principles.principle_label: Strike out inappropriate for statutory exemption scope
principles.paraphrase_en: The scope and application of a statutory exemption, particularly when its meaning is difficult to ascertain, is not suitable for determination on a strike-out application and should be decided once all facts are known at trial.

principles.principle_label: Waiver of privilege not determinable on strike out
principles.paraphrase_en: The question of whether a party has waived confidentiality or privilege through their conduct is a fact-intensive issue that cannot be properly determined on a strike-out application and must be addressed following a trial.

principles.principle_label: Breach of statutory data protection duty
principles.paraphrase_en: Disclosing personal data in breach of the statutory duties under the Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance, specifically Principle 3 of Schedule 1, can found a cause of action.
If a person discloses information they ought not to have disclosed, even if done in the course of their job, the affected party is entitled to seek remedies under the pleaded causes of action.

candidates.case_name: CHINA MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC (IN LIQUIDATION) AND OTHERS v. SAMSON TSANG TAK YUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Stable-door point as part of risk assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: The 'stable-door' point concerning whether assets have already been dissipated is not a separate legal issue but forms part of the overall assessment of the risk of dissipation of assets when considering an injunction.

principles.principle_label: Equity will not act in vain
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle that equity will not act in vain, meaning an injunction will not be granted if the defendant's assets have already been dissipated, is reflected in the consideration of the 'stable-door' point.

relationships.description: The judgment references this case as having cited the principle that equity will not act in vain in the context of the 'stable-door' point.
The judgment notes that the principle from Re Chau Cham Wong Patrick was cited with approval in this case.

relationships.target_case_name: Ras al Khaimah Investment Authority v Bestfort Development LLP
relationships.description: The judgment notes that the English Court of Appeal in this case criticized and rejected the 'stable-door' point principle.

candidates.case_name: RICH ENGINEERING LTD v. FIRE SERVICE INSTALLATION CONTRACTORS DISCIPLINARY BOARD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A disciplinary board must provide reasons for its sentencing decisions, including its choice of penalty, the determination of sentence length, decisions on concurrency or consecutiveness, and the application of the totality principle.

An appeal under Order 55 rule 3(1) is conducted as a rehearing, but the court must give proper weight to the original tribunal's decision and does not typically rehear evidence or permit re-cross-examination. The appellant must demonstrate the tribunal erred in law, departed from a principle, or misapprehended the facts.
An applicant alleging that a disciplinary board considered itself handicapped in its sentencing deliberations bears a heavy burden of proof to establish that fact.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: An applicant alleging that a disciplinary board considered itself handicapped in its sentencing deliberations bears a heavy burden of proof to establish that fact.

candidates.case_name: ZOU GEBING v. HONG KONG XINXIN INFORMATION LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge MK Liu in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A proprietary injunction must be tied to a specific asset under the defendant's control or its traceable proceeds, over which the plaintiff asserts a proprietary claim.
For liability in knowing receipt, the defendant must have actually received the property in question; merely benefiting in an abstract sense is insufficient.

The threshold for establishing the substantive merits of a claim when seeking a proprietary injunction is lower than for a Mareva injunction; the applicant need only show a serious question to be tried.

principles.principle_label: No automatic balance of convenience for trust property
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no general rule that the balance of convenience automatically favors preserving trust property once a serious issue to be tried is shown in a proprietary injunction application; each case depends on its own facts.
The court may consider whether there is a real risk that a party would be unable to obtain adequate compensation if an injunction is later found to have been wrongly granted or refused.

relationships.target_case_name: Pacific Rainbow International Inc v Shenzhen Wolverine Tech Ltd & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment references this case for the proposition that the threshold for a proprietary injunction is lower than for a Mareva injunction.

relationships.target_case_name: 李明實, 方壘 and史洪源 (suing on behalf of themselves and the other employees employed by or by the Group of Companies under和利時科技集團有限公司) (Hollysys Group Limited) and Ors v Ace Lead Profits Limited and Another
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's decision from this case to reject a general principle that balance of convenience readily favors preserving trust property.

relationships.target_case_name: Heitkamp & Thumann KG v Living Profit Trading Development Ltd & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes this case as involving email fraud and not establishing a general principle for proprietary injunctions.
The judgment distinguishes this case as concerning discovery in aid of a Mareva injunction for trust property, not a general principle for proprietary injunctions.

candidates.case_name: RE ZPMC-RED BOX ENERGY SERVICES LTD
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Court

principles.principle_label: Onus for establishing cross-claim in winding-up
principles.paraphrase_en: The company bears the burden of proving that its cross-claim against the petitioner is genuine, serious, and substantial, requiring supporting details to show it is based on substantial grounds.

The standard for assessing whether a cross-claim is sufficient to resist a winding-up petition is essentially the same as the test applied in a disputed debt petition: whether the debt is disputed in good faith and on substantial grounds.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the test for a disputed debt petition as established in Re Sinom (Hong Kong) Ltd.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG TING FUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of burglary under section 11(1)(b) of the Theft Ordinance is established when a person enters a building or part of a building as a trespasser and commits theft therein.
A defendant's guilty plea and agreement to an amended summary of facts constitutes a formal admission of the elements of the offence, leading to conviction.

candidates.case_name: LAU TUNG HOI KENT v. LAU TUNG KUEN
judges: Before: Mr. Recorder Jin Pao, SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Costs in administration proceedings - general principle
principles.paraphrase_en: In administration proceedings involving hostile litigation between a beneficiary and an executor, the starting point is that the unsuccessful party should pay the costs of the successful party.
When determining costs in administration proceedings, the court should apply the established categories set out in Re Buckton to classify the nature of the litigation.

principles.principle_label: Absence of dishonesty not decisive for costs
principles.paraphrase_en: The absence of a finding of dishonesty against a party is not, by itself, sufficient to displace the general principle that costs follow the event.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the three-category framework from Re Buckton to classify the costs issue in these administration proceedings.

candidates.case_name: CHINACHEM CHARITABLE FOUNDATION LTD v. CHAN WAI TONG CHRISTOPHER AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Yuen JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A claim alleging fraud or dishonest conduct must be struck out if it is advanced without any proper factual basis to support the allegation.

A court may strike out a statement of claim on grounds including that it discloses no reasonable cause of action, is scandalous, frivolous or vexatious, may prejudice or delay a fair trial, or is an abuse of court process.
On appeal against a striking out order, the appellate court will consider whether the matters relied on by the claimant raise any triable issues, even making assumptions in the claimant's favour.

candidates.case_name: CLARK QUANTUM KENT (formerly known as John Mohammad Clark and John Julius Tasca) v. HAI TIN LTD (formerly known as Allied Finance Asia LTd) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Barma JA and Au JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Test for leave to serve out of jurisdiction
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's approach to deciding whether to grant leave to serve process outside the jurisdiction is analogous to the test for summary judgment under Order 14 of the Rules of the High Court.

Summary judgment under Order 14 is generally not available in cases involving allegations of fraud, and the court should not investigate the merits of such allegations when determining if a good arguable case exists for service out of jurisdiction.
The court must be satisfied that the plaintiff has established a good arguable case on the merits when considering an application for leave to serve out of jurisdiction.
A plaintiff's claim must fall within one or more of the specified gateways under Order 11 of the Rules of the High Court to justify service of process outside the jurisdiction.

One available gateway for service out of jurisdiction under Order 11 is where the claim is brought to enforce, rescind, dissolve, annul or otherwise affect a contract, or to recover damages for breach of contract.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG WAI MING
judges: Before: HH Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: The starting point for sentencing can be reduced for various reasons, including when the defendant's role is that of a sneak thief, walk-in burglar, or opportunistic burglar who steals whatever is available.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Cheung To Ming that a lower sentencing starting point is appropriate for sneak thieves or opportunistic burglars.

candidates.case_name: TSE MAN YIU AND OTHERS v. TSE MAN WAI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The primary purpose of allowing amendments to pleadings is to ensure the court can determine the actual legal disputes between the parties and correct procedural errors.

Even if an application to amend pleadings is made late, it should generally be allowed if no genuine prejudice is caused, as courts aim to adjudicate on the substantive issues rather than let technical rules prevent the real claims or defenses from being heard.
A party raising the defense of illegality in civil proceedings must specifically plead it in their statement of case; it cannot be raised without proper pleading.

Where the common intention behind a property purchase under a public housing scheme is that the eligible applicant will not own or reside in the property, but instead uses their eligibility to benefit ineligible third parties, this constitutes a misuse of the scheme and does not qualify as a legitimate family resource-pooling arrangement.

relationships.target_case_name: Sino Favour Development Ltd & Anor v Kan Wai Chung & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites Fung J's holding regarding when illegality must be pleaded in civil wrongs.
The judgment cites this case for the principle that illegality must be pleaded in private civil wrongs.
The judgment cites this case indirectly via Monat Investment for the scope of when illegality must be pleaded.

candidates.case_name: THE LAW SOCIETY OF HONG KONG v. JIM YIU MING AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Au JA and Chow JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Suspension is generally the appropriate sanction when a solicitor, though not acting dishonestly, has demonstrated a failure to meet the required standards of integrity, probity, and trustworthiness. The purpose is to uphold public confidence in the profession.

principles.principle_label: Use of sentencing precedents in discipline
principles.paraphrase_en: In solicitor disciplinary proceedings, it is unhelpful to compare sentences by arguing one case is less serious than another. Each case must be decided on its unique facts, and prior cases are not binding precedents for sanction, as lapses in professional standards vary in gravity.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Bolton regarding the maintenance of public confidence as the essential issue when considering suspension for lapses in integrity, probity, and trustworthiness.

candidates.case_name: ORIENTAL TEXTILE PRODUCTS LTD v. ASIA TELEVISION HOLDINGS LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may still make a costs order against a party as a matter of principle, even if the opposing party indicates it does not seek costs, and it is for the recipient of the costs order to decide whether to enforce it.

candidates.case_name: JSP INTERNATIONAL S.R.O. v. ALACRITY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers (by Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: An allegation of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient particularity. It is insufficient to merely state facts that could be consistent with honest conduct; the specific primary facts from which dishonesty is to be inferred must be clearly set out so the defendant knows the case to meet.

A court may decline to grant a declaration that a defendant holds funds on constructive trust, even where it is prepared to declare that the plaintiff has a proprietary interest in or ownership of those funds. The two forms of relief are distinct.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites Edison Norge AS as an example where declarations of proprietary interest were granted but declarations of a constructive trust were refused.

candidates.case_name: EVERCARE HEALTH LTD v. SO LAI KAN also known as GOBBY SO AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge B. Mak in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A proprietary injunction must be connected to a specific asset, or its traceable proceeds, that is held by or under the control of the defendant and is the subject of a proprietary claim by the plaintiff.

To obtain a proprietary injunction, the plaintiff must generally provide reasonable evidence showing that the specific asset (or its traceable proceeds) exists and is held by or under the control of the defendant.

principles.principle_label: Limitation on injunction for dissipated assets
principles.paraphrase_en: If the asset that is the subject of the proprietary claim has been dissipated and is no longer traceable, a proprietary injunction will not normally be available.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN SHEK LUN
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LUI WAI LOK
judges: Before: Hon Campbell‑Moffat J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM TSZ HIM AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Dishonesty element in conspiracy to defraud
principles.paraphrase_en: For the offence of conspiracy to defraud, dishonesty is an essential element, and the agreed means must be dishonest according to the established Ghosh test.
When applying the Ghosh test for dishonesty, the judge must correctly address the two specific questions outlined in that test.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the Ghosh test to determine the essential element of dishonesty in the offence of conspiracy to defraud.

candidates.case_name: KWOK LAI SHEUNG v. MORE INSURANCE BROKER COMPANY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge B. Mak in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: CHINA MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES INC (In Liquidation) AND OTHERS v. THE BANK OF EAST ASIA, LTD
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: An allegation of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient particularity, including the primary facts from which dishonesty is to be inferred; particulars that are equally consistent with honesty are insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Bank's receipt of funds - ministerial vs beneficial capacity
principles.paraphrase_en: It is not a settled principle of law that a bank always receives funds in a purely ministerial capacity when a trustee deposits trust money into a personal account; the bank may potentially receive it beneficially, making it a complex and unsettled area unsuitable for striking out.

principles.principle_label: Fraud not essential for knowing receipt or want of authority
principles.paraphrase_en: Fraud is not an essential element of claims based on knowing receipt or want of authority, meaning a statutory limitation period applicable to actions based on fraud does not necessarily apply to such claims.

The practice of deciding preliminary points of law should generally be confined to cases where the facts are complicated and the legal issue is short and easily decided; otherwise, it may increase cost and time without true procedural economy.

principles.principle_label: Fraudulent trading vs dishonest assistance distinction
principles.paraphrase_en: Liability for fraudulent trading under statute requires that the defendant carried on the business of the company in a fraudulent manner; this is distinct from, and should not be equated with, liability for dishonestly assisting breaches of duty by directors.

relationships.target_case_name: Hing Yip Holdings (Hong Kong) Limited v Cellmark China Limited
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for its obiter analysis that fraud is not an essential element of claims based on want of authority and knowing receipt.
The judgment references this case in the context of discussing when a statutory limitation period for fraud-based actions applies.

relationships.target_case_name: George Chu v Tan Giong Seng Johnson
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that it is not in the interests of justice to relieve a party from an unless order if they consciously decided not to comply.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM SEE CHUNG STEPHEN
judges: Before: HH Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offences, the court must consider the overall criminality and ensure the total sentence is not excessive, adjusting individual sentences to achieve a just and proportionate total term.

The court determines whether sentences for separate offences should run consecutively or concurrently based on factors including whether they form part of a single criminal enterprise, the defendant's overall culpability, and the need to reflect the total criminality.

In sentencing for fraud offences, relevant aggravating factors include the number of victims, the total sum involved, commission of offences while on bail or after jumping bail, and a history of similar dishonesty offences.

The specific methods used to commit fraud—such as posting online advertisements, using one's own identity or bank account, and meeting victims in person—are factual circumstances relevant to assessing the offence's nature and the offender's culpability.

candidates.case_name: GALLERIA (HONG KONG), LTD (IN COMPULSORY LIQUIDATION) AND ANOTHER v. DBS BANK LTD., HONG KONG BRANCH
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Suspicions that do not meet the threshold for establishing blind-eye knowledge are not irrelevant to the assessment of dishonesty in dishonest assistance claims. The court must first ascertain all relevant facts, including the defendant's knowledge and beliefs, as part of the dishonesty test.

The test for dishonesty in dishonest assistance involves two stages: first, ascertaining what the defendant knew (including facts and beliefs), and second, objectively assessing whether their conduct was dishonest based on that knowledge.
Contemporaneous documents constitute highly important evidence in determining a defendant's knowledge and state of mind for claims of dishonest assistance.

For liability in fraudulent trading, mere knowledge of a number of fraudulent transactions is insufficient. The defendant must be found to be a party to carrying on the business for a fraudulent purpose.
The standard of unconscionability for knowing receipt is lower than the standard of dishonesty required for dishonest assistance, and the two should not be conflated.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YEUNG SARAH SUM YEE
judges: Before: The Honourable Madam Justice Toh in Court

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of hearsay for state of mind
principles.paraphrase_en: Statements made to a third party are not excluded by the hearsay rule when they are admitted as evidence solely to prove the state of mind of either the maker of the statement or the person to whom it was made, rather than to prove the truth of the statement's content.

A police officer is not required to immediately terminate an interview and comply with procedural safeguards (like those under Principle (d)) simply because a suspect makes an answer that appears to be an admission. The officer may make further inquiries to place the admission in a proper context and to determine the precise scope and nature of the offence before deciding there is sufficient evidence to charge.

relationships.description: The judgment suggests the Magistrate may have erred in applying this case to conclude there was no breach of Principle (d) in obtaining the second interview record.
The judgment references this case for the principle that statements to a psychiatrist can be original evidence not requiring confirmation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LO WAN
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: When an offender commits separate and distinct offences, such as theft of a credit card and subsequent use of that card, the sentences for these offences should generally be ordered to run consecutively.

Even when consecutive sentences are appropriate in principle, the court must apply the totality principle to review the aggregate sentence, ensuring it is not unduly harsh and is proportionate to the offender's overall criminality.
When applying the totality principle, the court must be careful to avoid undue harshness that could result from using the same aggravating factor more than once in the sentencing calculation.

relationships.description: The judgment notes the defence's submission that this case is comparable and invites the court to adopt similar sentencing starting points.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUCK SZE MING AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court must consider the overall culpability of the defendant across all offenses to ensure the final sentence is just and proportionate, rather than simply adding individual sentences.

Offenses committed at different times, involving different premises and different victims, constitute separate incidents, and the commission of a subsequent offense adds to the defendant's overall culpability.

relationships.description: The judgment references this case as authority for the application of the totality principle in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TANG KWOK KEUNG (also known as LI CHING KEUNG)
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peony Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A sentencing court may increase the starting point of a sentence based on specific aggravating features of the offense or the offender's circumstances.

When applying multiple sentence enhancements, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final sentence is proportionate and not excessive, which may require reducing the total enhancement period.

candidates.case_name: CHINA MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC. (IN LIQUIDATION) AND OTHERS v. SAMSON TSANG TAK YUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Inference of dissipation risk from dishonesty
principles.paraphrase_en: When the alleged dishonesty is central to the claim against a defendant, the court may infer a real risk of asset dissipation from establishing a good arguable case of that dishonesty to the required standard.

Delay in applying for a Mareva injunction may not be considered prejudicial if the applicant was unable to properly formulate a good arguable case earlier, for instance due to impediments in investigation such as lack of records or non-cooperation from relevant parties.

An ex parte without notice application for an injunction requires justification, such as urgency or a need for confidentiality. The absence of such factors constitutes a procedural irregularity that can be grounds for setting the injunction aside.

Establishing a real risk of asset dissipation depends on the specific facts of the case, and no general guidelines can be laid down. Factors considered are often not direct evidence of dissipation but relate to the nature of the defendant's assets and the circumstances of the case.

relationships.target_case_name: Lakatamia Shipping Co Ltd v Toshiko Morimoto
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Lakatamia that a good arguable case of central dishonesty can support an inference of a real risk of dissipation.
The judgment cites the textbook for its discussion on the requirement of 'solid evidence' of a risk of dissipation and the fact-specific nature of the inquiry.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MUTUNGI DORCUS
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge Kathie Cheung in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSUI KING WA
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peony Wong

principles.paraphrase_en: A one-quarter enhancement to the sentence is appropriate for offenses involving organized crime elements.
When sentencing for multiple charges involving different transactions and victims, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate.

For multiple offenses arising from separate transactions with different victims, partially concurrent sentences should be imposed, with some portions ordered to run consecutively to reflect the overall criminality.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites Leung Yiu Fai as a comparative sentencing authority involving dishonesty offenses with multiple victims.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG TIK ON
judges: Before: Recorder Maggie Wong, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offences, the court must impose a sentence for each individual count and then apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence properly reflects the defendant's overall criminality.

principles.principle_label: Deterrent sentence for convenience store robberies
principles.paraphrase_en: Robberies targeting convenience store staff heighten the offender's culpability and warrant a deterrent sentence due to public policy considerations.
Separate offences represent additional culpability and sentences can, in principle, be made consecutive, subject to the totality principle preventing wholly consecutive orders.

principles.principle_label: Partially consecutive sentences for series of robberies
principles.paraphrase_en: For a series of deliberately committed robberies, a partially consecutive sentence structure is appropriate to reflect persistence in offending and overall culpability.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Chan Nai Keung and Others
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing approach from Chan Nai Keung regarding the application of the totality principle for multiple offences.
The judgment follows the principle established in Kwok Cheuk Man that robberies at convenience stores heighten culpability and require a deterrent sentence.
The judgment follows the reasoning in Wong Cheung regarding the need for deterrent sentences for robberies targeting convenience stores.
The judgment cites Lam Hoi as a comparative sentencing case involving multiple convenience store robberies with weapons.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TONG CHIU CHUEN
judges: Before: HH Judge A. J. Woodcock

principles.paraphrase_en: The offense of burglary of domestic premises is established under section 11(1)(b) and (4) of the Theft Ordinance when a person enters a building or part of a building as a trespasser with intent to steal therein.
A defendant's timely plea of guilty constitutes significant mitigation that must be given appropriate weight in sentencing.
When determining sentence, the court must consider the defendant's personal circumstances including age, living situation, employment status, and criminal record.
Serious injury sustained by the offender during commission of the offense may be considered as a form of punishment or relevant circumstance in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ATREYA BONEY
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Charles J Chan

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining the overall sentence for multiple offences, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final sentence is just and proportionate, considering any increases already made for aggravating factors like multiple offences.

principles.principle_label: Concurrent vs consecutive sentences for multiple victims
principles.paraphrase_en: When offences involve different victims, it is generally inappropriate to impose a wholly concurrent sentence; some degree of consecutive sentencing may be warranted to reflect the distinct harm to each victim.

principles.principle_label: Mitigation for first dishonesty offence and circumstances
principles.paraphrase_en: The fact that an offence is the defendant's first involving dishonesty, and that it was committed due to difficult personal circumstances such as unemployment, can be considered as mitigating factors in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: CHINA PING AN INSURANCE OVERSEAS (HOLDINGS) LTD v. LUCK GAIN LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Jonathan Chang SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A contract must be interpreted as a whole, considering the factual and legal background at the time of agreement and the practical objectives the parties intended to achieve, rather than focusing solely on individual words.

In an intermediated securities system like Clearstream, securities are immobilized with a custodian, and interests in those securities are traded through accounts. The system operates on a 'no look through' principle where parties have rights only against their immediate counterparty, and payments flow from issuer to the clearing system then to account holders.
