The court may find a factual account improbable based on common sense, such as rejecting that a temporary disconnection of power would cause permanent damage to a shutter.

candidates.case_name: CHINA FAIR LAND (QINGDAO) LTD v. LO SUM SUM
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Isaac Chan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A witness may be truthful and honest, but their evidence can still be unreliable if it concerns subjective matters or events outside their personal knowledge.

A plaintiff can recover loss of rent for a vacancy period if they prove the defendant's breach (e.g., water seepage) actually caused the vacancy by reducing the pool of willing tenants and necessitating repair work.

The plaintiff bears the burden of proving actual causation between the defendant's breach and the claimed loss; a brief vacancy period without such evidence is considered within normal market expectations and not recoverable.

principles.principle_label: Corporate claim for rental loss and general damages
principles.paraphrase_en: A corporate plaintiff is permitted to claim both loss of rent and general damages for property-related breaches.

Parties may agree, for simplicity, that pre-judgment interest runs on the entire awarded sum from a single date, such as the approximate midpoint between the cause of action and the trial, subject to arguments about litigation delay.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows Toy Victory (Asia) Limited in allowing a corporate plaintiff to claim both rental loss and general damages.
The judgment notes that Crystal Bright was distinguished in the reasoning of Toy Victory (Asia) Limited, which it follows.
The judgment cites Century Way Investment Limited as one of the authorities relied upon by Toy Victory (Asia) Limited.

relationships.target_case_name: Well Force Co Ltd v Boomway (Hong Kong) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported 1998 case as an authority referenced by Toy Victory (Asia) Limited.

relationships.target_case_name: Gwong Leung Property Holdings Ltd v Wong Kwok Tsoi
relationships.description: The judgment cites this 2022 District Court case as also referring to the same line of authorities on corporate claims for damages.

candidates.case_name: XU YING v. XU WEIHONG
judges: Before : Hon Cheung and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Service out jurisdiction - Gateway F test
principles.paraphrase_en: For service out of jurisdiction under Gateway F (tort), the applicant must show a good arguable case that a real and substantial tort has been committed in Hong Kong, not merely that a tort was committed here and damage occurred here.
For a claim in malicious falsehood, the plaintiff must plead pecuniary loss; reliance on section 24 of the Defamation Ordinance is not available if such loss is not pleaded.
In determining whether Hong Kong is the appropriate forum for a defamation claim, the court considers whether there has been substantial publication of the defamatory material in Hong Kong.

Leave to serve proceedings out of jurisdiction is a discretionary exercise, subject to the requirement that Hong Kong must be shown to be the appropriate forum for the trial under the Spiliada principles.

relationships.target_case_name: Oriental Press Group Ltd v Google LLC
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the test from Oriental Press Group that a real and substantial tort must be committed in Hong Kong for service out under Gateway F.

relationships.target_case_name: Jameel v Dow Jones & Co Inc
relationships.description: The judgment references Jameel as the source of the real and substantial tort test applied in the Oriental Press Group case.

relationships.target_case_name: Johnson v Taylor Brothers & Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment notes this case as the origin of a principle later endorsed in Kroch and referenced by Lord Steyn.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TANG MAN KIT
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peony Wong in Court

candidates.case_name: LIN HO PIN AND OTHERS v. JAMES TING-YEH YANG
judges: Before: Hon Fung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A contingent liability, by itself, does not constitute actual damage for the purpose of accrual of a cause of action. Damage only occurs when the contingency is fulfilled, unless the contingent liability itself causes an immediate depreciation in the value of other property or results in a measurable loss from entering a transaction.

The time when damage is sustained in a negligence claim depends on the facts. The loss is measured by comparing the plaintiff's actual position with the position they would have been in had the defendant fulfilled their duty of care. Damage occurs as soon as the plaintiff sustains a measurable, relevant loss, which could be immediate or at a later date.

principles.principle_label: Loss of chance as immediate damage
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant can suffer actual loss at the time they act on negligent advice by losing the chance to secure a better outcome (e.g., a better deal). This loss of chance is a separate, tangible loss that arises immediately, even if the full extent of the loss is quantified later.

principles.principle_label: Accrual from breach vs. later event
principles.paraphrase_en: A cause of action in negligence can accrue at the time of the defendant's breach of duty if that breach causes an immediate measurable loss (e.g., failing to register a protective interest), rather than at the later date when the consequences of that breach are fully realized.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Sephton that a contingent liability causes no damage until a claim is actually made, unless it causes immediate depreciation in asset value.
The judgment applies Lord Nicholls's approach from Nykredit that the timing of damage accrual depends on the facts and requires identifying the relevant measure of loss.
The judgment cites Bell as an example where damage accrued immediately upon the solicitor's failure to register a caution, not later when the financial consequence occurred.

relationships.target_case_name: Wardley Australia Ltd v The State of Western Australia
relationships.description: The judgment cites Wardley for the proposition that damage from giving an indemnity arises when the indemnity is called upon, not when the agreement is entered.
The judgment cites Maharaj for the principle that in 'no transaction' cases, the inquiry focuses on when the claimant suffered actual or measurable damage.
The judgment cites Microsoft for the principle that a court is not obliged to accept sworn evidence as probably accurate if it is inconsistent with documents or inherently implausible.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN HO YIN
judges: Before: HH Judge Kathie Cheung

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for criminal damage, the court may consider the limited value of the property damaged and the defendant's payment of full compensation to the victim as mitigating factors.

principles.principle_label: Drug influence as a mitigating factor
principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence that a defendant was under the influence of drugs at the time of the offence, and their subsequent recognition of the drug's impact and need for rehabilitation, can be presented in mitigation to inform the court's sentencing decision.

principles.principle_label: Court's power to call for reports before sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has the discretion, based on the case circumstances and the defendant's condition, to call for reports (e.g., updated medical or psychiatric reports) to ascertain the defendant's latest condition before proceeding to final sentence.

principles.principle_label: Discount for guilty plea despite trial listing
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may still be entitled to the usual one-third sentencing discount for a guilty plea, even if the case was initially set down for trial, depending on the procedural circumstances of the case.

candidates.case_name: LO WAI KEUNG v. HANNOVER RÜCK SE
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The jurisdiction of the Labour Tribunal is determined at the time the claim is filed, whereas the appropriate forum for trial is assessed based on circumstances as they develop, including when issues become clear or when changes occur after filing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SO SIU YUEN
judges: Before: Hon Yau J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must consider whether to make a compensation order or leave the victim to pursue civil claims, balancing the defendant's hardship in paying restitution against the victim's burden in initiating civil proceedings.

principles.principle_label: Court's duty to unrepresented defendant on compensation
principles.paraphrase_en: For an unrepresented defendant, the court should inquire whether the defendant needs legal advice regarding a compensation issue, especially for substantial amounts, and consider adjourning to allow time to obtain representation.
An appellate court will not interfere with a magistrate's findings if they are consistent with established legal principles.
A compensation order is correct if it is consistent with the governing ordinance and relevant case authorities.

relationships.description: The present judgment finds the magistrate's findings to be in line with the principle established in Liu Suet Fun.

candidates.case_name: RE INQUEST INTO THE DEATHS OF 39 PERSONS ARISING OUT OF THE COLLISION BETWEEN LAMMA IV AND SEA SMOOTH ON 1 OCTOBER 2012
judges: Before: Monica Chow, Coroner

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for unlawful killing conclusion
principles.paraphrase_en: In a coroner's inquest, the conclusion of unlawful killing, including where death results from manslaughter by gross negligence, is to be determined on the balance of probabilities (the civil standard), not the criminal standard of proof beyond reasonable doubt.

principles.principle_label: Application of civil standard to prior criminal conviction evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a defendant has already been criminally convicted for conduct relating to the death, that conviction provides evidence that satisfies not only the civil standard of proof but also the now-displaced higher criminal standard for an unlawful killing conclusion at an inquest.

Upon concluding an inquest, a Coroner is required to complete the prescribed Form 12 under the Coroners Ordinance, recording the findings and particulars of the death, and forward it to the Registrar of Births and Deaths for registration.

principles.principle_label: Public interest grounds for ordering an inquest
principles.paraphrase_en: An inquest may be ordered by the court on public interest grounds, including to promote transparency, enhance public confidence in safety regulation, and examine matters not fully addressed in prior inquiries or criminal proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: R (Maughan) v HM Senior Coroner for Oxfordshire
relationships.description: The judgment follows the majority holding in Maughan that all short-form inquest conclusions, including unlawful killing, are determined by the civil standard of proof.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YEUNG LAP WAI
judges: Before: HH Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offences, the court must consider the overall criminality and ensure the total sentence is just and proportionate, not excessive.

The court may order sentences for offences arising from the same transaction or closely connected facts to run concurrently, while ordering sentences for distinct criminal episodes to run consecutively to reflect the total criminality.
A defendant is entitled to a reduction in sentence for pleading guilty, which reflects the utilitarian value of saving court time and resources, and may also indicate remorse.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. CHAN YIP WAN
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Pang JA and Barnes J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must not sentence a young person to imprisonment unless no other suitable way exists, prioritizing rehabilitation over punishment and deterrence for young offenders.
In sentencing, the court must consider all applicable sentencing factors and give them appropriate weight to decide a commensurate sentence.

principles.principle_label: Dual sentencing considerations for serious offences
principles.paraphrase_en: For serious offences, the court must balance public interest (requiring a sentence commensurate with offence seriousness) with the offender's personal circumstances and rehabilitation needs.

principles.principle_label: Review of sentence for public order offences
principles.paraphrase_en: For offences related to public order events, courts must emphasize the importance of preserving public order, with deterrence and punishment being key considerations in large-scale unlawful assemblies.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v. Wong Chi Fung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the sentencing principles regarding public order offences and the need to consider all sentencing factors as established in Wong Chi Fung.
The judgment cites SWS in the context of discussing how an offender's youth may be a mitigating factor in sentencing.
The judgment notes the PM's failure to refer to the authority of SHY when considering the gravity of the offences during the sentence review.

candidates.case_name: SUCCESS LANE DEVELOPMENT LTD v. FERGURSON HONG KONG LTD t/a NEW WORLD MILLENNIUM HONG KONG HOTEL
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Gary C C Lam in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A party may be held accountable for loss or damage to another's property if they have been given prior notice that specific conditions or actions could cause such harm.

Applications for procedural directions (such as expert directions) necessary for the fair resolution of a dispute according to the parties' substantive rights should not be dismissed based solely on a party's past opposition to such directions.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of past conduct to costs
principles.paraphrase_en: A party's past opposition to a procedural step is not a valid ground to dismiss a current application for that step; such past conduct is relevant only to the issue of costs, not to the merits of the application.

candidates.case_name: SILVERCORD LTD v. THE SWATCH GROUP (HONG KONG) LTD
judges: Before: Master Alexander Tang in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.principle_label: Frustration by impossibility of common purpose
principles.paraphrase_en: A contract may be frustrated if a supervening event makes the common purpose of the contract impossible to perform, such as when the fundamental reason for the agreement can no longer be achieved.

principles.principle_label: Interpretation of rent abatement clauses - ejusdem generis
principles.paraphrase_en: The ejusdem generis principle applies to interpret general phrases like 'such other events beyond the control of the Landlord' or 'such other risks' in rent abatement clauses, limiting them to risks of the same kind as those specifically listed, which relate to physical inaccessibility or damage to the premises.

principles.principle_label: Exclusion of pure economic loss from rent abatement
principles.paraphrase_en: A rent abatement clause that requires the landlord to determine the extent of rebatement is unlikely to cover pure economic loss, as the landlord lacks the necessary facts and information to assess such loss.
The interpretation of a contractual clause may be suitable for summary determination if there is sufficient factual material before the court and no real dispute over the relevant background facts.

In assessing whether a liquidated damages clause is a penalty, a common-sense view should be taken, comparing the stipulated sum to the legitimate interest protected (such as monthly rental value), rather than relying on comparisons with practices in other similar contexts.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Krell as an example where a contract was frustrated because the supervening event made the common purpose impossible to perform.
The judgment distinguishes Star Win on the basis that sufficient factual materials exist in the present case to allow summary determination of contractual interpretation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. RAJPAL
judges: Before: HH Judge E Lin

principles.principle_label: Burglary - unauthorized entry with intent to steal
principles.paraphrase_en: For a burglary charge to be established, it must be proven that the defendant broke into a room without authorization and with the intention to steal.
For the alternative charge of criminal damage, the prosecution must prove that the defendant caused the damage to the property in question.

principles.principle_label: Inference of guilt from circumstantial evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may draw an inference that the defendant is responsible for causing damage based on the circumstances of the case, including the location and state of the damage, when supported by sufficient evidence.

Minor contradictions in a witness's account do not necessarily undermine their overall credibility on the substantive issues of the case, especially when their core testimony is consistent with the physical evidence.
A court should not make findings of fact on matters that are not necessary elements of the charge actually before the court, even if such actions are suspected.

candidates.case_name: H & W DEVELOPMENT (HK) LTD (formerly known as EAGLE HERO LTD) v. YEUNG KUK LAM AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge B. Mak in Court

candidates.case_name: HANG YUE ENGINEERING LTD AND ANOTHER v. TSE KING YUEN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Ebony Ling in Court

principles.principle_label: Civil liability from criminal assault finding
principles.paraphrase_en: A finding in criminal proceedings that an assault occurred beyond reasonable doubt can establish the fact of the assault in subsequent civil proceedings, and serious dispute on this point is not tenable.
The causal link between an assault and a claimant's injuries is determined on the balance of probabilities based on the evidence presented.

principles.principle_label: Scope of recoverable costs under ECO/CLCO
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim under section 25(1) of the Employees' Compensation Ordinance and section 3 of the Civil Liability (Contribution) Ordinance can include both the costs paid to the claimant and the legal costs incurred in defending the claim.
The court determines the just and equitable contribution between parties liable for the same damage by considering their respective blameworthiness and causative potency.
A settlement is considered bona fide where the amounts paid, including legal costs, are reasonable and supported by contemporaneous documentary evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Li Shiu To v Cheung Pik Ng and Li Sui Chee
relationships.description: The judgment references this case as containing relevant legal principles.

relationships.target_case_name: Liao Kuo Chun v Win Capital (HK) Ltd & ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for its holdings at specific paragraphs.

relationships.target_case_name: China Ping An Insurance (Hong Kong) Co Ltd v Chong Pin To
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding recoverable legal costs under specific ordinances.

relationships.target_case_name: Wah Kwong Construction Material v Wong Man Yip
relationships.description: This case is cited within the authority of China Ping An Insurance on the scope of recoverable costs.

relationships.target_case_name: Hong Kong Red Cross v The Hong Kong Federation of Youth Groups
relationships.description: This case is cited within the authority of China Ping An Insurance on the scope of recoverable costs.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAW FU WING
judges: Before: His Honour Judge E Yip in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must ensure that the combined sentence for multiple offences is proportionate to the defendant's overall criminality, considering the distinct nature of the offences and their temporal and contextual connection within the same course of conduct.

When a separate charge of absconding is brought, the sentence should generally be made consecutive to the sentence for the substantive offence, unless the sentence for the substantive offence is very long, typically measured in years.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Lo Kam Fai 盧錦輝
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Lo Kam Fai regarding when consecutive sentencing for absconding charges is appropriate.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWUN SUM YEUNG
judges: Before: Hon Yeung Acting CJHC, Cheung and Pang JJA in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SOHAIB MOHAMMAD
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may order forfeiture of items to the Government under section 56(1) of the Dangerous Drugs Ordinance if those items are connected to the drug trafficking offence, even if the defendant claims they are personal items.

Items such as a mobile phone, SIM cards, and cash can be subject to forfeiture if evidence shows they were used in connection with the drug trafficking offence, such as for communication or as part of the operational expenses.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. DIAO JUN-HUA
judges: Before: DHCJ Andrew Bruce, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: In the absence of specific Court of Appeal sentencing guidelines for Gamma-Butyrolactone (GBL), a judge may adopt the approach of imposing a sentence approximately 50% higher than the starting point for benzodiazepines, due to the serious dangers associated with GBL ingestion.
There is no fixed sentencing tariff for money laundering offenses, as the crime can be committed in many varied ways, requiring sentencing to be based on the specific circumstances of each case.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for multiple drug trafficking counts
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple counts of drug trafficking, the court should consider the overall starting point for the total quantity of drugs involved, and then apportion that sentence between the individual counts, with the primary focus being on achieving an appropriate total sentence.

relationships.target_case_name: Attorney General v Chan Chi Man
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in the context of discussing sentencing approaches for drug offenses.
The judgment applies the principles from this case regarding the considerations for sentencing in money laundering offenses.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO WAI TAT
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must present sufficient evidence to establish a specific link between seized property and the commission of an offense before confiscation can be ordered.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for drug trafficking, the court should first establish a baseline starting point based on the quantity of drugs involved.
A defendant's active role as an actual trafficker, rather than a mere courier, constitutes an aggravating factor that warrants an upward adjustment from the initial sentencing starting point.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWOK KEI TAI, KORAY
judges: Before : H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: While rehabilitation is an important consideration for young offenders, the court must balance this with the need for deterrence and punishment, particularly in serious cases like drug trafficking.
When imposing sentences for multiple offenses, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the combined sentence is not excessive, which may involve ordering sentences to run concurrently.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TANG CHIU SING, KELVIN
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for separate money laundering offences
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for a money laundering offence that is factually and legally distinct from other offences (like drug trafficking), the court may impose a consecutive sentence, applying the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence reflects the total criminality.

Key factors in sentencing for money laundering include the amount of money involved, the duration of the offending, the absence of evidence linking the funds to a specific predicate offence, and the sophistication (or lack thereof) of the scheme.

principles.principle_label: Sheer enormity of quantity in drug sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: An exceptionally large quantity of dangerous drugs can constitute a 'sheer enormity' that justifies imposing a sentence at the prevailing statutory limit, even within an established guideline band.

relationships.description: The judgment discusses the application of sentencing guideline bands from Abdallah for drug trafficking offences.
The principle from Sum Ka Wa regarding 'sheer enormity' of drug quantity justifying a sentence at the statutory limit is invoked to support the sentence.
The judgment notes that the applicable guideline band for the quantity of drugs would be the same under both Abdallah and Huang Ruifang (No 3).

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN CHUN HEI
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP and Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The quantity of drugs and the act of attempting to flee or dispose of them are not, by themselves, sufficient to prove trafficking, as they are equally consistent with simple possession. The prosecution must prove trafficking beyond reasonable doubt based on additional evidence.

Imprisonment is not automatically the most appropriate sentence for first-time drug offenders. In suitable cases, the public interest may be better served by prioritizing the offender's rehabilitation, a principle reinforced by the Drug Addiction Treatment Centres Ordinance.
Where a statute provides a specific power of forfeiture for certain cases, the court's general forfeiture power should not be used for those same cases, as the specific provision governs.
Forfeiture proceedings, which affect property rights, must adhere to the constitutional imperative of a fair hearing. They are civil in nature, with the burden of proof resting on the applicant.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the reasoning from Yeung Shek Kin regarding the relationship between general and specific forfeiture powers.
The present judgment applies the principles from Wong Hon Sun emphasizing the need for a fair hearing in forfeiture proceedings due to their impact on property rights.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SIPIKILLI RIDONI
judges: Before: Hon Kwok J in court

principles.paraphrase_en: The first step in sentencing for drug trafficking is to assess the offence's gravity based on the quantity of drugs seized, using the applicable sentencing guideline. For cocaine, the revised guideline sets a range of 16 to 20 years' imprisonment after trial for quantities between 500 and 1,500 grammes.

principles.principle_label: Treatment of international element in sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: An international element in a drug trafficking offence is no longer treated as a standalone aggravating factor. Instead, it should be considered as part of assessing the offender's role and overall culpability.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of aggravating and mitigating factors
principles.paraphrase_en: After determining the starting point based on drug quantity, the court must separately consider whether any further aggravating factors or exceptional mitigating factors apply to the case.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the revised sentencing guideline for cocaine trafficking established in this case.
The judgment adopts this case's holding that the international element of a drug offence relates to the offender's role and culpability, not as a separate aggravating factor.
The judgment notes that the defense relies on this authority, though the specific principle cited is not detailed in the provided paragraphs.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHENG CHI WAI
judges: Before: Hon Yeung VP, Pang JA and M Poon J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offenses, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is not excessive and is proportionate to the overall criminality.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing ratio for drug manufacturing vs. trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: A sentencing guideline suggests that the starting point for a general drug manufacturing offense may be approximately 1.8 times higher than for a drug trafficking offense involving an equivalent quantity of drugs.

The seriousness of a drug manufacturing case, and thus the appropriate sentencing starting point, should be assessed based on the scale of the operation and the quantity of drugs involved, not primarily on their market value.

relationships.description: The judgment discusses an appellant's submission that the cited case does not support the 1.8 times sentencing ratio for drug manufacturing versus trafficking.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUNG HO YIN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP and Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The correct sentencing approach is to first determine the appropriate sentence for each individual offence, and then apply the totality principle to the aggregate sentence to ensure the final overall sentence is not excessive.
It is incorrect to examine the total sentence and work backwards to identify an overall starting point; the proper method is to assess each charge individually first.
Sentences for road traffic offences can properly be made consecutive to sentences for drug trafficking offences where they arise from distinct incidents and involve different types of criminality.
When sentencing for multiple drug trafficking charges, the court may aggregate the total quantity of drugs from related offences and apply a single starting point, adjusting for totality.
A sentence may be enhanced where the offender has previous convictions for the same type of offence, reflecting the need for greater deterrence.

relationships.description: The court found the reference to this case unhelpful as it supported the judge's correct approach of determining individual sentences before applying the totality principle.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE CHUN-PANG
judges: Before: His Honour Judge W.K. Kwok

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for multiple drug types
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for trafficking involving multiple types of drugs, the court may adopt a 'combined approach' to determine the appropriate starting point.

If facts necessitate splitting a single charge into multiple charges, sentences for each offence should be imposed individually and then ordered to run concurrently or consecutively according to the totality principle.

The fact that a defendant might personally consume a small quantity of a drug does not negate the primary purpose of trafficking if the bulk was held for sale, analogous to a hawker consuming unsold stock.
Inconsistent explanations given by a defendant for their actions can undermine the credibility of their defense.

principles.principle_label: Use of ratio test in sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: The ratio test is a valid tool for cross-checking and ascertaining the appropriate sentencing starting point for drug trafficking offences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG CHI HIN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Charles J Chan in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for herbal cannabis
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for possession of cannabis in herbal form, the court may adopt a lower starting point compared to cannabis resin, because herbal cannabis has a lower average concentration of tetrahydrocannabinol.

principles.principle_label: Limit on sentencing discount for herbal cannabis
principles.paraphrase_en: The reduction in the sentencing starting point for herbal cannabis, relative to cannabis resin, should not exceed one year.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles established in Cheung King Kwong regarding the appropriate starting point for herbal cannabis.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSOI FOR
judges: Before: DHCJ Johnny Chan

principles.principle_label: Joint account as property of another
principles.paraphrase_en: A joint bank account held with another person is considered property belonging to that other person for the purposes of the Theft Ordinance.

relationships.description: The present judgment references Hsu Yu Yi for the principle considerations relevant to the legal issue at hand.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOW WAI KWOK
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may order the forfeiture of money or property to the Government if it is satisfied, on the balance of probabilities, that the money or property was used in the commission of or in connection with a drug trafficking offence, regardless of whether a conviction has been secured.
The standard of proof required for a forfeiture order under section 56 of the Dangerous Drugs Ordinance is the civil standard of the balance of probabilities.

principles.principle_label: Inference from possession of unexplained cash
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may infer that a large sum of cash found in the possession of a person involved in drug trafficking, for which no credible legitimate explanation is provided, is connected to the drug trafficking offence.

principles.principle_label: Burden on defendant to explain cash
principles.paraphrase_en: Once the prosecution establishes a prima facie case that cash is connected to drug trafficking, the evidential burden shifts to the defendant to provide a credible explanation for the source and intended use of the cash.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Cheung Wai Man that the standard of proof for forfeiture under DDO s.56 is the balance of probabilities.
The present judgment applies the reasoning from Li Kwok Cheung regarding the inference that can be drawn from the possession of a large sum of unexplained cash by a drug trafficker.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TANG SIU PANG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.principle_label: Admission of guilt in drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's admission under caution that they were paid to drive another person to deliver drugs constitutes evidence of guilt for the joint charge of trafficking in dangerous drugs.

When one defendant admits to being hired and paid by another to drive for the purpose of delivering drugs, and drugs are found in the vehicle they jointly occupied, this supports a finding of joint liability for trafficking.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. GGOLOLA EDWARD
judges: Before: Hon Kwok J in court

principles.paraphrase_en: The first step in sentencing for drug trafficking is to assess the gravity of the offence by identifying the applicable sentencing guideline based on the quantity of drugs seized, and to calculate the starting point mathematically within the prescribed range.

principles.principle_label: International element in drug trafficking sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: An international element in a drug trafficking offence should be considered as part of assessing the offender's role and culpability, rather than treated as a separate and additional aggravating factor.

principles.principle_label: Mitigation for family circumstances in serious offences
principles.paraphrase_en: Family circumstances do not constitute mitigating factors in sentencing for serious offences such as drug trafficking.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for cocaine trafficking quantity range
principles.paraphrase_en: For trafficking in cocaine between 500 and 1,500 grammes, the sentencing guideline after trial is 16 to 20 years' imprisonment.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the revised sentencing guideline for cocaine trafficking established in this case.
The judgment follows the principle from this case regarding how to treat the international element in drug trafficking sentencing.
The judgment notes that the defense relies on this authority, though the specific holding is not detailed in the provided paragraphs.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. PASTANA CARVALHO ANDREZA NARELY
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, M Poon JA and A Pang JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: In drug trafficking offences, maintaining consistency in sentences between courts is a fundamental and long-established principle, crucial for ensuring fairness and predictability in the criminal justice system.

principles.principle_label: Limited weight of personal circumstances in serious drug offences
principles.paraphrase_en: For serious drug trafficking offences, the personal circumstances of the offender generally carry very little weight in the sentencing exercise, as the gravity of the crime is the primary consideration.

While courts may extend mercy to an offender, a just sentence is achieved by properly applying legal principles balanced with personal circumstances; justice is not normally served by departing from proper sentencing principles solely due to sympathy.

principles.principle_label: Review basis for manifestly inadequate sentence
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for review of sentence can be made on the basis that the sentence imposed was wrong in principle and/or manifestly inadequate.

relationships.description: The present judgment treats Herry Jane Yusuph as the leading case on the sentencing approach for drug trafficking, particularly emphasizing the principle of consistency.
The judgment cites Chan Chi-ming v R as the origin, over forty years ago, of the principle emphasizing consistency in sentencing for drug trafficking.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v An unknown person alias Stojanovic Milka and Skopljak Sara
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Stojanovic Milka that personal circumstances count for very little in serious drug trafficking offences.
The judgment cites R v Lau Tak-ming for the celebrated statement that personal circumstances are of minimal relevance in sentencing for offences of this magnitude.
The judgment references Omogo Stephen Okuta as a comparable case to highlight the inconsistency and potential inadequacy of the sentence in the present matter.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FONG YAU HEUNG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP and Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Importation for personal consumption as mitigation
principles.paraphrase_en: When imported dangerous drugs are intended wholly or partly for the offender's personal consumption, and there is no risk of supply to the public, this constitutes a strong mitigating factor that may reduce or negate the sentence enhancement typically applied for the international element of the offence.

principles.principle_label: Proportionality of sentence enhancement for importation
principles.paraphrase_en: The enhancement of a sentence for the aggravating factor of international importation of dangerous drugs must be just and proportionate, with the level of enhancement generally guided by the quantity of narcotics involved.

principles.principle_label: Absence of aggravating international element in certain importations
principles.paraphrase_en: The aggravating factor of an international element may not be invoked in some importation cases, such as where the importation involves no elaborate or secretive arrangements and the substance is not a commonly known dangerous drug.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Choi Chun Wo that sentence enhancement for importation must be sufficient for deterrence and proportionate.
The judgment cites Dong Juan as an example of a case involving importation of a specific quantity of drugs for trafficking.
The judgment applies the reasoning from Ko Wai Shing that the international element may not be aggravating in certain circumstances of importation.
The judgment follows the principle stated in Ngai Ho Ting that importation for personal consumption is a significant mitigating factor.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. POON CHI KEUNG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of totality must be considered when imposing sentences for multiple offences to ensure the total sentence is not excessive and is proportionate to the offender's overall criminality.

A sentencing judge has discretion to order sentences to run wholly concurrently, wholly consecutively, or partly consecutively and partly concurrently, based on the circumstances of the case and the offender.
The 'one transaction' rule for sentencing is not rigid and must be applied sensitively to the individual facts of the case and the offender.

principles.principle_label: Separate and distinct offences for sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: Where offences are separate and discrete in nature, even if connected, consecutive sentences may be justified.

relationships.description: The respondent argues the case should be distinguished from the present one due to different charges and facts.
The applicant cites this case to support the argument that partly consecutive sentences can be imposed for distinct offences like drug and driving crimes.
The applicant cites this case as an example where part of a sentence for drug driving was ordered to run consecutively to sentences for other distinct offences.
The respondent cites this case as a precedent where wholly consecutive sentences were imposed for the same two offences arising from a similar factual background.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. VU NHAN KHIEN
judges: Before: Hon Kwok J in court

principles.principle_label: International element in drug trafficking sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for drug trafficking, the presence of an international element is not treated as a separate aggravating factor. Instead, it is considered as part of assessing the offender's role and overall culpability when determining the appropriate starting point for the sentence.

The degree of sentence enhancement for drug trafficking should correspond to the seriousness of the offence and the extent of its harmful impact on Hong Kong. The purpose of such enhancement is to achieve a deterrent effect.
When determining sentence, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final sentence is just and balanced, even if the offence warrants a severe sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Lee Ming Ho regarding how international elements in drug trafficking should be treated in sentencing.
The judgment applies the sentencing principles from Singh Gursevak concerning the rationale for sentence enhancement in drug trafficking cases.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Lam See Chung Stephen
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lam See Chung Stephen in support of the application of the totality principle in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG HO TAK
judges: Before: DHCJ Bruce, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for drug trafficking offenses, it is essential to accurately determine the specific role played by the accused, as culpability varies even within roles like couriers or storekeepers.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Herry Jane Yusuph that accurate characterization of an accused's role is critical for sentencing in drug trafficking cases.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HUI KWAN HO
judges: Before: Hon Kwok J in court

principles.paraphrase_en: Drug trafficking is considered a serious societal evil, and courts must impose deterrent sentences regardless of the offender's personal background or circumstances to avoid encouraging vulnerable groups to participate.

principles.principle_label: Training Centre Order inapplicability for serious drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: It is not in the community's interest to sentence young offenders convicted of serious drug trafficking offences to a Training Centre Order, as such leniency would undermine the deterrent purpose of sentencing.
When an offender's role is assessed as that of a mere courier, the sentencing starting point should be based on the baseline category for couriers, with no upward adjustment required for their role.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v CHAU Tsz-tim
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and applies the sentencing principles established in Chau Tsz-tim regarding the seriousness of drug trafficking and the inappropriateness of Training Centre Orders for such offences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN YUK KWAN
judges: Before: Recorder Martin Hui, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who enters a timely and early guilty plea is entitled to a full one-third discount from the starting point of the sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles established in Ngo Van Nam regarding the discount for a guilty plea.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CELESTIN HENSLEY FRANCO
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's timely guilty plea is a significant mitigating factor that should be taken into account during sentencing, as it demonstrates remorse and saves court resources.
A defendant's agreement to the summary of facts at the time of a guilty plea establishes the factual basis for the offence and is binding for sentencing purposes.
The act of unlawfully bringing a dangerous drug into Hong Kong constitutes trafficking under the law, regardless of whether further distribution occurs.
Possession of a dangerous drug for the purpose of trafficking, evidenced by concealment and quantity, establishes the offence of trafficking.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NOR UMI SABIHA BINTI TAURAN TURKI
judges: Before: DHCJ Bruce, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for drug trafficking offenses, it is essential to accurately determine the specific role played by the accused in the criminal enterprise, as culpability varies even within roles like courier or storekeeper.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Herry Jane Yusuph that accurately characterizing the accused's role is critical for sentencing in drug trafficking cases.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHENG CHI SHING
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's admission under caution and in a video-recorded interview, detailing the operational methods, pricing, and reward structure of drug trafficking, constitutes strong evidence of the trafficking intent and activity.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing relevance of prior trafficking convictions
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's previous convictions for trafficking in dangerous drugs are a material factor to be considered by the court during sentencing for a subsequent trafficking offence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. POOTORNPHAI JAKKRIT
judges: Before: DHCJ Bruce, SC

principles.principle_label: Sentencing discretion for drug trafficking roles
principles.paraphrase_en: Sentencing guidelines for drug trafficking, even when designed for lower-level participants like couriers and storekeepers, do not remove the court's discretion to set a starting point above or below the arithmetically calculated figure based on the specific circumstances of the defendant's role and actions.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG TSZ KIN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: While rehabilitation is an important consideration for young offenders, the court must also consider the prevalence of drug trafficking among youth and emphasize that young age alone is not a strong mitigating factor.
When imposing sentences for multiple charges, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is appropriate and not excessive.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Ma Ming for the proposition that a defendant's frank confession providing the only evidence for charges may justify minor adjustments to the total sentence under the totality principle.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU WING WA
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: The court is not required to obtain a pre-sentencing report for defendants under 21 years old, as per statutory provision, but retains discretion in sentencing.
Rehabilitation generally plays an important role in reforming young offenders, though this must be balanced against other sentencing considerations.

principles.principle_label: Judicial notice of drug trafficking patterns
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may take judicial notice of the common pattern of young persons being involved in drug trafficking offenses.
The court must send a clear deterrent message regarding drug trafficking offenses, even when dealing with young offenders.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG WING LUN
judges: Before: Recorder Martin Hui, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may adopt a combined approach to sentencing based on the total quantity of drugs involved, rather than sentencing separately for each individual smaller quantity.
The combined approach to sentencing based on total drug quantity is properly applicable where the drugs involved in multiple charges are of a similar kind.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing approach established in Yip Pik Kwai regarding sentencing based on total drug quantity.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI WA
judges: Before : H.H. Judge G. Lam

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. RASHIDI MDENGE RUKIA YUSUFU
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP and Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal against a forfeiture order constitutes an appeal against the entire sentence, not just the forfeiture component.

relationships.description: The judgment references a Single Judge of the Court of Appeal's prior refusal of leave to appeal against sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HUANG RUIFANG
judges: Before: Recorder Martin Hui, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: The fundamental policy in sentencing for drug trafficking offenses is to impose a deterrent sentence, which is implemented through established sentencing tariffs for specific dangerous drugs.
The sentencing tariff applicable to trafficking in cocaine follows the tariff established for trafficking in heroin.
Whether an international element in a drug trafficking case constitutes an aggravating factor is determined based on the specific facts and circumstances of the case.

principles.principle_label: Mitigation limited in serious drug cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In serious drug trafficking cases involving large quantities of dangerous drugs, the personal background of the defendant carries very little weight as a mitigating factor.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the Court of Appeal's holding in Abdallah that the sentencing tariff for cocaine trafficking follows that for heroin trafficking.
The present judgment applies the principle from Fong Yau Heung that the international element's status as an aggravating factor depends on the facts and degree of the specific case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI KA-MING
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM SIU FUNG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: The Honourable Mr. Justice D. Yau in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing enhancement for repeat drug offenders
principles.paraphrase_en: A sentence for drug trafficking must be enhanced to strengthen the element of deterrence when the defendant is a repeat offender for similar drug-related offences.
A defendant may be granted a reduction in sentence for making a genuine attempt to assist the police in apprehending other offenders, even if the assistance does not yield a material outcome.

principles.principle_label: Starting point for money laundering sentence
principles.paraphrase_en: The starting point for sentencing in a money laundering offence should be determined with reference to the amount laundered and the defendant's knowledge and participation in the predicate offence.

The decision to impose sentences concurrently or consecutively is determined by the totality principle: sentences should be concurrent if the sentence for one offence can comprehend the criminality of the other; otherwise, they should be at least partly cumulative to avoid understating the total criminality.

Where a defendant commits separate offences (e.g., drug trafficking and money laundering) that involve distinct criminal conduct and harms, wholly concurrent sentences may be insufficient to reflect the full criminality, warranting at least partly consecutive sentences.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the passage quoted and adopted by the Court of Appeal in Ngai Yiu Ching regarding the totality principle for determining concurrent or consecutive sentences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG HANG WAI
judges: Before: DHCJ Bruce, SC

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG WING HONG, RICKY
judges: Before: Recorder Derek Chan, SC

principles.principle_label: Cross-check methods for cocktail drug sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining the appropriate starting point for sentencing in cases involving a mixture of different drugs (cocktail drugs), the court may employ three cross-check methods: the absurdity test, the conversion test, and the ratio test.

principles.principle_label: Application of the ratio test for cocktail drugs
principles.paraphrase_en: The ratio test for sentencing in cocktail drug cases involves calculating the percentage composition of each drug within the total seized quantity. The court then uses these proportions to assess the overall starting point for the sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NIGEL PATRICK STEVENSON
judges: Before: DHCJ Bruce, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: In drug trafficking sentencing, it is essential to determine the specific role and level of involvement of the accused, as culpability varies even among those who might broadly be characterized as couriers.

The sentencing guidelines for drugs like cocaine use the role of a courier or storekeeper as the analytical starting point, but the court must assess the precise nature of the accused's involvement within that category.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for role enhancement
principles.paraphrase_en: To sentence an accused on the basis of a more serious role than a mere courier (such as being part of a criminal conspiracy or acting for reward), the prosecution must prove that enhanced role beyond reasonable doubt.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Herry Jane Yusuph that determining the accused's precise role is critical in drug trafficking sentencing.
This judgment applies the Court of Appeal's reasoning that even within the 'courier' category, there is a range of culpability that must be accurately characterized for sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FU CHUN KIT AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge Ada Yim in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in criminal trials
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution bears the legal burden of proving all elements of a criminal charge beyond a reasonable doubt.
If the prosecution fails to meet the standard of proof beyond a reasonable doubt for any element of the offence, the defendant must be acquitted.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOW HOI YAM AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: For defendants under 21 years old, the court is not required to obtain a pre-sentencing report before sentencing, as provided by statute.
Rehabilitation, rather than punishment, is generally an important principle in sentencing young offenders to facilitate their reform.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for drug trafficking by young adults
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must emphasize through sentencing that young age does not provide immunity from serious punishment for serious offenses like drug trafficking, given the prevalence of such offenses among young adults.
When imposing sentences for multiple charges, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is appropriate for the total criminality involved.
A timely guilty plea and the defendant's young age are valid mitigating factors that warrant a reduction from the starting point of a sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOI MAN SUM
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits the offence of trafficking in a dangerous drug under section 4(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Ordinance when they are found in possession of a dangerous drug with the intent to sell or supply it to others, regardless of whether the defendant claims it was for personal use.
A defendant's guilty plea to a charge, coupled with agreement to the Summary of Facts, is sufficient for the court to enter a conviction.
A statement made by a defendant under arrest and caution regarding the purpose of possessing dangerous drugs is admissible as evidence in proceedings.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. JAVIER DUVAN AMADO PEREZ
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant seeking an extension of time to file an appeal must provide a reasonable and credible explanation for the delay; an explanation based on a misunderstanding of the proceedings or an unsubstantiated claim is insufficient.
Where a forfeiture order is made without objection from the defence counsel during the original proceedings, as evidenced by the court record, a subsequent appeal against that order is without merit.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KO CHUN YIN
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM KWOK-WING
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. GOMEZ BECERRA DEIBER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Sham

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN MAN-YUK
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: The court has discretion under the Criminal Procedure Ordinance to sentence a defendant under 21 years old without obtaining a pre-sentencing report.
Rehabilitation, rather than punishment, is generally an important consideration in sentencing young offenders to facilitate their reform.

Despite the general rehabilitation principle, the court must also consider the need for deterrence in drug trafficking cases involving young offenders, given the prevalence of such offenses among young adults.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHU HO WA AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and A Pang JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who provides evidence for the prosecution in a related case may be entitled to a further discount on their sentence, even when appealing substantially out of time.
The Chief Executive of the HKSAR has the power under the Criminal Procedure Ordinance to refer a case to the Court of Appeal for consideration of sentence reduction following a petition.
A formally dismissed application for leave to appeal, following a notice of abandonment, concludes that appeal process unless revived through another statutory mechanism.
Unlawfully trafficking in a dangerous drug, specifically methamphetamine hydrochloride (Ice), is an offence under the Dangerous Drugs Ordinance.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CEDENO MEJIAS ANGEL RAFAEL
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP and Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing afresh on appeal based on revised sentencing guidelines, the appellate court is not bound by the original judge's approach under the previous guidelines.

principles.principle_label: Role and culpability in drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: An international drug courier who transports substantial quantities of drugs across continents for distribution plays a grave role with high culpability in drug trafficking offenses.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YUE HIN FUNG
judges: Before: Hon Tam J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN CHI HANG
judges: Before: DHCJ Bruce, SC

candidates.case_name: SATHAIAH SUBBAIAH v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge Bruno Chan :

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for torture claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must examine decisions of the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board regarding torture and non-refoulement claims with rigorous examination and anxious scrutiny.

principles.principle_label: Grounds for judicial review of Board decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: A proper application for judicial review of a Torture Claims Appeal Board decision must identify specific grounds for relief, such as procedural unfairness, error of law, or irrationality.

The Director of Immigration must assess multiple applicable grounds for a non-refoulement claim, including risk of torture, risk to absolute rights under the Hong Kong Bill of Rights (such as right to life), and risk of persecution.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU CHI FUNG
judges: Before: Hon Pang, Poon and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Mere presence insufficient for riot liability
principles.paraphrase_en: Simply being present at the location of an unlawful assembly or riot is not sufficient to establish guilt for the offence of taking part in a riot or aiding and abetting it. Liability requires some intentional act in furtherance of the riot.

principles.principle_label: Conduct constituting participation in a riot
principles.paraphrase_en: A person can be considered a rioter and liable to arrest if they encourage, promote, or take part in a riot through words, signs, gestures, or by wearing the badge or insignia of the rioters.
The court must be careful to avoid treating innocent passers-by who are inadvertently caught up in an unlawful assembly or riot as guilty of an offence merely because they were present at the scene.

relationships.description: The judgment cites the Queensland Court of Appeal's decision in R v Cook to support the principle that mere presence is generally insufficient for liability in an unlawful assembly or riot.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSANG CHUN KIT
judges: Before: HH Judge A. J. Woodcock

principles.principle_label: Deterrence over rehabilitation in protest cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In sentencing for offenses arising from public protests that involve violence, the need for deterrence generally takes precedence over the principle of rehabilitation, particularly when there is a context of increasing unrest and large-scale violent protests.

The statutory detention period for a training centre order is not a fixed three-year term; it is a maximum of three years, with a minimum of six months, followed by a potential supervision period of up to three years.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Wong Chi Fung
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in that case that deterrence overrides rehabilitation in sentencing for offenses related to violent public protests.

candidates.case_name: FAEEM AHMAD v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge Michael WONG :

principles.principle_label: Role of court in non-refoulement judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: In judicial review of non-refoulement claims, the court's role is limited to scrutinizing the Board's decision for errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality; it does not reassess the claim afresh or evaluate new evidence not presented to the primary decision-makers.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of new evidence in judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant cannot rely on materials not produced before the Board or Director to argue that those decision-makers erred in not considering them, unless there is a positive duty on the decision-makers to elicit such materials on their own initiative.

The decision-maker only needs to consider internal relocation if the applicant has first established that they qualify as a refugee under Article 1A(2) of the Refugee Convention, meaning they have shown a real risk of persecution in their home area.

General assertions or propositions of law cannot assist an appellant if they fail to provide the requisite particulars related to the specific facts of their case; vague and general grounds cannot establish a valid challenge.
For the court to grant leave to apply for judicial review, the claim must be reasonably arguable and enjoy realistic prospects of success.

relationships.target_case_name: Trinh Thuy Huyen v Torture Claims Appeal Board

principles.paraphrase_en: The power to appeal by way of case stated should be used appropriately and only in clear and obvious cases where a magistrate's decision is erroneous in law or in excess of jurisdiction, to respect the finality of acquittals.

A challenge to a magistrate's factual findings in a case stated appeal must be based on there being no evidence to support them or that they were findings no reasonable magistrate could make, not merely on a disagreement with the conclusion.

The administration of justice should generally be conducted in public, as this safeguards against arbitrariness and maintains public confidence, requiring court proceedings to be held in open court accessible to the press and public.

principles.principle_label: Bail test for national security offenses
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering bail for offenses endangering national security, the court must assess whether there are sufficient grounds to believe the defendant will not continue to commit acts endangering national security if released.

principles.principle_label: Social media influence as bail consideration
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's substantial social media following and use of platforms to make accusations against law enforcement agencies may demonstrate resolve and determination to continue acts endangering national security, which is relevant to bail decisions.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the bail principle for national security offenses established in the cited CFA judgment.

principles.principle_label: Judicial review grounds for administrative decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: A court reviewing an administrative board's decision will assess whether there was any error of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality in the decision-making process.
In certain contexts, such as non-refoulement claims, the court applies an enhanced standard of Wednesbury unreasonableness when assessing the rationality of an administrative decision.

principles.principle_label: State protection and internal relocation in non-refoulement
principles.paraphrase_en: In non-refoulement claims, a decision-making body is entitled to find that an applicant does not face a real risk of persecution or torture if effective state protection is available or if the applicant can safely relocate within their country of origin.
The burden rests on the applicant in a judicial review to demonstrate that the administrative decision contains a reviewable error of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

principles.paraphrase_en: The seriousness of an offense under the National Security Law must be evaluated based on the specific circumstances of the case, which vary from case to case, and involves categorizing the offense as either serious or minor before determining the penalty within the applicable tier.

There are limits to how useful comparisons with other cases are for sentencing, as utility depends on whether the facts or contexts are comparable; if not comparable, such comparisons provide minimal assistance.

principles.principle_label: Role of Mainland law in NSL interpretation
principles.paraphrase_en: Mainland law may in principle inform the construction of the National Security Law or its provisions, but the relevance, extent, and method of reference depend on the actual circumstances of the case before the court.

The proper sentencing approach under NSL 21 involves first categorizing the offense as serious or minor, then determining the penalty within the applicable tier, with any adjustments for individual justice occurring at the second step.

principles.paraphrase_en: For offences where the primary sentencing purposes are punishment and deterrence, an immediate custodial sentence is generally required unless there are very exceptional and rare circumstances.

principles.principle_label: Inapplicability of community service orders for deterrent sentences
principles.paraphrase_en: Even if an offender is suitable for a community service order, it is generally not appropriate to impose one for an offence that clearly calls for a deterrent sentence.

principles.principle_label: Limited relevance of good character and remorse
principles.paraphrase_en: An offender's good character and remorse generally do not justify departing from imposing a deterrent sentence where one is required.
When sentencing for unlawful assembly, the court must fully grasp the gravamen of the offence, recognizing that the public hazard it targets is often more extensive than the apparent acts committed.

principles.principle_label: Misinterpretation of violence in unlawful assembly
principles.paraphrase_en: It is an error to assess an unlawful assembly as 'mild' or 'not serious' simply because it involved no physical conflict or property damage; the court must consider the broader public hazard and potential for harm.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Wong Chi Fung that unlawful assemblies involving violence generally require deterrent custodial sentences.

principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Appeal may intervene and review a sentence when it is found to be wrong in principle and manifestly unduly lenient, even if based on the trial magistrate's factual findings.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for incitement to unlawful assembly
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for incitement to unlawful assembly, the court must assess the offender's culpability by considering the circumstances at the time of the offence, including the context, target location, method, and the risk posed to law and order, not merely the actual consequences.

principles.principle_label: Purpose of sentencing for incitement offences
principles.paraphrase_en: A sentence for incitement must carry sufficient punishment and deterrence to reflect the gravamen of the offence, which is to prevent the commission of crime, and cannot be tilted excessively towards the offender's personal circumstances.

principles.paraphrase_en: Shining laser beams at police officers, capable of harming their eyes, constitutes prohibited conduct under Section 18 of the Public Order Ordinance.
The use of torchlight against police officers can also constitute prohibited conduct under Section 18 when it occurs in a context where others nearby are shining laser beams at the police.

For an unlawful assembly under Section 18, the prosecution must prove that the accused assembled together with others to perform the prescribed (prohibited) conduct; individual provocative conduct alone is insufficient.

An assembly is fluid; the ebb and flow of prohibited conduct among participants does not necessarily extinguish the apprehension of a breach of the peace or constitute a discontinuation of the assembly.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the law as clearly set out in Lo Kin Man.

principles.paraphrase_en: Freedom of expression is a fundamental right in a democratic society and includes the expression of ideas that may be offensive to the majority or critical of government institutions and officials. Constitutional protection extends to peaceful expression but does not cover involvement in violence or threats of violence.
When persons assembled act in concert in a prohibited manner that is objectively likely to cause the stipulated fear, their conduct falls outside the constitutional protection for peaceful assembly.

When a restriction on freedom of assembly or expression is imposed by law or policy, the court must examine whether the restriction is consistent with the principle of proportionality, including at the operational level of enforcement.

Whether conduct is 'insulting' is a question of fact to be determined by applying the ordinary everyday meaning of the word, which includes treating someone with insolence, contempt, indignity, derision, dishonour, or offensive disrespect.

The offence of unlawful assembly is preventive; it does not require an actual breach of the peace to have occurred. It is sufficient if a person present at the scene reasonably fears that a breach of the peace would occur if no preventive action is taken.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Ng Kung Siu for the principle that freedom of expression is fundamental and includes offensive speech and criticism of government, but does not protect violence or threats of violence.

The judgment cites Leung Chung Hang Sixtus for the principle that assembly loses constitutional protection when persons conduct themselves in a prohibited manner objectively likely to cause the stipulated fear.

The judgment cites To Kwan Hang for the principle that the offence of unlawful assembly is not inconsistent with the right of peaceful assembly and does not protect those who conduct themselves in a prohibited manner.
The judgment cites Chow Nok Hang, which adopted Brutus v Cozens, for the definition and factual determination of 'insulting' conduct.
The judgment references Brutus v Cozens indirectly via Chow Nok Hang for the ordinary meaning approach to determining 'insulting' behaviour.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing consideration for social disturbance offences
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for offences committed during or in the context of a social disturbance, the court must consider all relevant facts including the defendant's actions, the time, place, surrounding circumstances, and the potential reactions or consequences at the scene.

principles.principle_label: Failure to consider relevant sentencing factors
principles.paraphrase_en: A sentencing court errs in principle if it fails to take into account the prevailing social setting and the specific circumstances of the offending when determining sentence.

principles.principle_label: Application of sentencing principles across offence types
principles.paraphrase_en: Sentencing principles developed for one type of offence (e.g., unlawful assembly or desecration of the national flag) apply with equal force to other offences committed against the backdrop of a social disturbance.

A sentence that is so far outside the range of what could reasonably be considered appropriate is manifestly inadequate and does the defendant no favor, even if it results in replacing a non-custodial sentence with a custodial one.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG MAN YUEN AVERY AND OTHERS

principles.paraphrase_en: A spontaneous demonstration may override statutory prior notification requirements only in special circumstances where an immediate response to a current event is warranted, and a delay would render the response obsolete. This exception does not apply if participants engage in illegal conduct.

The government has a positive duty to take reasonable and appropriate measures to facilitate the right of peaceful assembly, but this duty is not absolute and depends on all the circumstances of the particular case.

The police must be afforded a degree of discretion in taking operational decisions during assemblies, as they have access to information and intelligence not available to the general public and face complex, rapidly evolving situations.

The police have a duty to prevent demonstrations from deteriorating into riots and crimes to protect others from violence and harm, and to protect the rights and freedoms of others. Interferences with freedom of assembly are in principle justified for these purposes.

The right of peaceful assembly does not extend to those who conduct themselves in a disorderly, intimidating, insulting, or provocative manner intended or likely to cause a reasonable fear of a breach of the peace. Protesters have no right to resort to violence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Kudrevicius that spontaneous demonstrations may override prior notification requirements in special circumstances where an immediate response is needed.

principles.principle_label: Evidential burden for honest belief defence
principles.paraphrase_en: When an accused raises a defence of honest belief but does not testify, they bear only an evidential burden to point to evidence supporting the inference of that belief. Once this evidential burden is satisfied, the prosecution must disprove the belief beyond reasonable doubt.

The reasonableness of a claimed honest belief is merely a factor to consider when determining whether the accused could possibly have held that belief. The key issue is whether the accused actually held the belief, not whether the belief itself was objectively reasonable.

A defence of honest belief can be rejected if there is insufficient evidence to prove the accused held the belief, if the factual circumstances contradict the suggestion of such a belief, and if objective evidence does not support the premise on which the belief is said to be based.
Conduct that obstructs a police officer in the execution of their duty is unlawful unless the defendant can establish a lawful excuse for that conduct.
