principles.paraphrase_en: The equitable jurisdiction to set aside unconscionable bargains cannot be definitively described in all possible situations; it depends on the specific circumstances.
An applicant must establish at least an arguable case, and preferably substantial grounds, for the first essential element of an unconscionable bargain claim to succeed.

principles.principle_label: Duress of the person - causation standard
principles.paraphrase_en: For duress involving threats to the person, the complainant only needs to prove the illegitimate pressure was a reason for entering the contract; the court will presume it induced the contract unless evidence shows it contributed nothing to the decision.

principles.principle_label: Duress of the person - no requirement of no practical alternative
principles.paraphrase_en: In cases of duress by threats to the person, the complainant is not required to demonstrate they had no practical alternative but to enter the contract.

principles.principle_label: Affirmation of contract entered under duress
principles.paraphrase_en: A contract entered under duress is voidable, not void. The aggrieved party may affirm it through voluntary acts with full knowledge of the circumstances, or by failing to take steps to set it aside after escaping the duress. Affirmation requires an unequivocal act.

relationships.target_case_name: Commercial Bank of Australia v. Amadio
relationships.description: The judgment cites Amadio for the proposition that the equitable jurisdiction for unconscionable bargains cannot be definitively described in all situations.
The judgment cites Blomley v. Ryan in the same context as Amadio regarding the scope of unconscionable bargain jurisdiction.
The judgment applies the principle from Mir v Mir that affirmation of a contract entered under duress requires an unequivocal act.

candidates.case_name: PAN SUTONG v. BANK OF CHINA LTD (Trading as “Bank of China Limited Shenzhen Branch”)
judges: Before: Hon Linda Chan J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Adults with full mental capacity are legally bound by documents they sign, unless they can prove a recognized legal vitiating factor. The party seeking to disown the document must specifically plead and prove the facts constituting that vitiating factor.

principles.principle_label: Illegitimate pressure from threat of proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: A threat to commence legal proceedings can constitute illegitimate pressure for duress if either (a) the threatened proceedings would be an abuse of process or malicious prosecution, or (b) the threat is based on past unlawful action.

When assessing whether an allegation of duress raises a bona fide dispute, the court considers the two core elements of duress (compulsion of will and illegitimacy of pressure) and relevant factors such as access to independent advice, time to consider the documents, and steps taken to avoid the contract.
There is a general legal principle against bringing two separate claims before the court seeking the same relief.

relationships.target_case_name: Ming Shiu Chung & ors v Ming Shiu Sum & ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that parties must plead and prove specific facts to disown signed documents.

relationships.target_case_name: Sheikh Tahnoon Bin Saeed Bin Shakhboot Al Nehayan v Ioannis Kent (aka John Kent)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority that a threat of abusive or malicious proceedings can amount to illegitimate pressure.

relationships.target_case_name: Times Travel (UK) Ltd v Pakistan International Airlines Corp
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority that a threat of proceedings based on past unlawful action can constitute illegitimate pressure.
The judgment cites this case as authority for the general principle against bringing two claims for the same relief.
The judgment cites this case as authority for the general principle against bringing two claims for the same relief.

relationships.target_case_name: Beneficial Finance Corp Limited v East Coast Printed Circuits Pty Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the general principle against bringing two claims for the same relief.

candidates.case_name: CHAN BENAMI HOLDINGS LTD v. CHI CHI HUNG, KENNETH
judges: Before: Master James Kwan in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: In the absence of genuine prejudice, courts should generally allow amendments to pleadings to ensure the real issues in dispute are adjudicated, rather than letting procedural technicalities prevent parties from presenting their true claims or defenses.
The central element in proving economic duress is establishing the illegitimacy of the pressure exerted. Not all commercial pressure, even if substantial or overwhelming, is inherently illegitimate.
A party's inaction and delay in responding to a situation of duress can constitute an affirmation of the contract, thereby waiving the right to later avoid it.

Where a contract is entered under economic duress (an unjustified threat to breach), the aggrieved party has a choice to affirm or avoid the contract. Affirmation can occur through conduct, such as making payments without protest and delaying a claim for restitution.

The court has a discretionary power under section 49(1)(a) to fix a post-judgment interest rate different from the standard judgment rate. The mere existence of this power does not mean it must be exercised in every case.

relationships.description: The judgment adopts the principle stated by Ng J regarding the general approach to amendments of pleadings in the absence of real prejudice.

relationships.target_case_name: VSC Building Products Co Ltd v Kono Insurance Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment notes that Ng J in Hsu Ming Chi cited this case for the principle that procedural rules should not block the adjudication of real disputes.

relationships.target_case_name: Esquire (Electronics) Ltd v Hong Kong and Shanghai Banking Corp Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment references the holding of Stock JA (cited in the HSBC case) that the key to economic duress is proving the illegitimacy of the pressure.

relationships.target_case_name: North Ocean Shipping Co Ltd v Hyundai Construction Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Mocatta J that a threat to break a contract without legal justification can amount to economic duress, and that affirmation can occur through conduct like delay.
The judgment follows the reasoning of Ng J that the power to fix a non-standard post-judgment interest rate is discretionary and not automatically exercised.

candidates.case_name: RE DAI YUMIN
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A creditor must take all reasonable steps to personally serve a statutory demand, including conducting searches to find residential addresses for service, before resorting to substituted service by advertisement.

principles.principle_label: Arbitration clauses and bills of exchange
principles.paraphrase_en: A cheque constitutes a separate contract from the underlying agreement, and an arbitration clause in the underlying agreement will not cover disputes on the dishonoured cheque unless it contains a plain manifestation of such an intention.

principles.principle_label: Construction of arbitration clauses for bills
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether an action on a bill of exchange falls within an arbitration clause is a question of construction of the clause in the context of the whole agreement and factual matrix, aiming to find the parties' intention.
Rational businessmen generally do not intend to forgo their right to sue in court on a dishonoured cheque, which is a separate and independent right from any underlying dispute.

For duress of the person, the complainant only needs to prove the illegitimate pressure was a reason for entering the contract; the court will presume inducement unless evidence shows the pressure contributed nothing to the decision.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the requirement to take reasonable steps for personal service of a statutory demand.
The judgment applies the test from this case for determining whether all reasonable steps were taken to bring a statutory demand to a debtor's attention.

relationships.target_case_name: CA Pacific Forex Ltd v Lei Kuan Ieong
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle established in this case that an arbitration clause must expressly include bills of exchange to cover disputes over a cheque.
The judgment applies the 'plain manifestation' test from this case for determining if an arbitration clause rebuts the presumption against arbitrating bills of exchange disputes.

relationships.target_case_name: Nova (Jersey) Knit v Kammgarn Spinnerei GmbH
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the competing principle applicable to bills of exchange in the context of arbitration.

relationships.target_case_name: Fiona Trust & Holding Corporation v Privalov
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes this case, noting it did not concern bills of exchange and that a different principle applies to such instruments.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YUEN HOI LAM
judges: Before: Hon Pang, M Poon and Anthea Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must generally present all evidence intended to prove the charge before closing its case. Departing from this rule is highly exceptional and only permissible if it causes no prejudice to the defendant and does not render the trial unfair.

The rule against splitting the prosecution case exists to ensure a fair trial by allowing the defendant to know the full scope of the case against them before deciding whether to testify and how to mount their defence.

The principle in Browne v Dunn generally requires a party to cross-examine a witness on any significant contradictory evidence they intend to later rely on or criticize, to give the witness a chance to respond.

relationships.description: The judgment discusses the principle from Browne v Dunn regarding the obligation to cross-examine a witness on contradictory evidence.

candidates.case_name: CHENG HUNG KIT AND OTHERS v. TASTY CATERING GROUP LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Linda Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Amendment introducing post-writ cause of action
principles.paraphrase_en: A party cannot amend pleadings to introduce a cause of action that accrued after the date the writ was issued.

relationships.target_case_name: Wing Siu Co Ltd v Goldquest International Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Wing Siu that a cause of action not existing at the writ's issue date cannot be introduced by amendment.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. QUINTERO BERNAL DIEGO-JHOVANNY
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For trafficking in cocaine, the quantity determines the sentencing bracket and starting point. For quantities between 600 and 1,200 grammes, the starting point is between 20 and 23 years' imprisonment.
A sentencing court should consider whether a defendant was subjected to coercion or threats when committing the offence, as a relevant mitigating factor.

principles.principle_label: Limits on weight given to stress and fear
principles.paraphrase_en: Considerable care must be taken not to give undue weight to a defendant's 'stress and fear' as a mitigating factor, to avoid creating an impression that it justifies the criminal conduct.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the sentencing guidelines for trafficking in cocaine established in Abdallah.
The judgment applies principles from this case to determine the offender's role for sentencing purposes.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Wan Hoi Ming
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that coercion or threats should be considered in sentencing.
The judgment cites this case as the authority applied in Secretary for Justice v Wan Hoi Ming regarding coercion in sentencing.
The judgment applies the caution from this case against giving undue weight to stress and fear as a mitigating factor.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. QUINTERO BERNAL DIEGO-JHOVANNY
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge may issue a preliminary ruling to clarify the legal elements of a defence, such as duress, to assist counsel in determining whether sufficient evidence exists to put that defence before a jury. This ruling does not pre-judge the facts or decide whether the defence will ultimately be left to the jury.
An appellate court may examine whether a judge's preliminary ruling inadvertently exerted undue pressure on a defendant, leading them to abandon a defence they wished to advance.

On appeal, the correctness of a judge's preliminary ruling on the defence of duress can be reviewed, including the judge's analysis of elements such as immediacy of threat, continuing threat, taking evasive action, and voluntary participation.

candidates.case_name: RE LI XIAOMING
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Au JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for duress defense
principles.paraphrase_en: The party raising a defense of duress (whether physical or economic) bears the burden of establishing that there is a bona fide dispute regarding the duress claim.

principles.principle_label: Immateriality of capacity in subsequent acknowledgments
principles.paraphrase_en: When a debtor acknowledges personal liability in subsequent documents, the capacity in which they signed those documents is not relevant to establishing that liability.
An appellate court will not interfere with a trial judge's evaluation of the evidence and factual findings unless a palpable error in assessment is demonstrated.

candidates.case_name: GUANGHUA SS HOLDINGS LTD v. CHEN JIANAN
judges: Before: Master James Kwan in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: For a claim of economic duress to succeed in a contractual context, the claimant must prove the pressure applied was illegitimate; mere commercial pressure or a strong bargaining position is insufficient.

Significant or overwhelming commercial pressure exercised by a party in a powerful bargaining position is a common feature of business dealings and does not, by itself, constitute illegitimate pressure for the purpose of establishing economic duress.

relationships.target_case_name: Pao On v Lau Yiu Long
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority for the principle that commercial pressure alone is insufficient to establish economic duress in a contractual situation.

relationships.target_case_name: Esquire (Electronics) Ltd v Hong Kong and Shanghai Banking Corp Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that the key to proving economic duress is establishing the illegitimacy of the pressure exerted.

candidates.case_name: CHEN WU v. XIE FEI
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The party alleging that their consent to a signed document was vitiated by duress bears the legal burden of proving that duress occurred.

principles.principle_label: Standard for duress of the person
principles.paraphrase_en: In cases involving duress of the person (threats to life, health, or liberty), the complainant only needs to prove that the illegitimate pressure was a reason for entering the contract. The court will conclude it induced the contract unless evidence shows the pressure contributed nothing to the decision.

principles.principle_label: Threshold for establishing duress of the person
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish duress of the person, the complainant must demonstrate a threat to life, health, or liberty. It is insufficient to show pressure from a third party not caused by the other contracting party, especially when the complainant had avenues to seek help.

The test for undue influence is whether, on the balance of probabilities, the transaction was procured by an abuse of trust and confidence reposed by the subservient party in the dominant party. Invoking an evidential presumption of undue influence can be a source of confusion.
A person of full age and ordinary understanding is bound by documents they have signed, unless they can prove a recognized legal basis (such as duress or undue influence) that vitiates their consent.

relationships.target_case_name: Ming Shiu Chung v Ming Shui Sum
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that a person is bound by documents they sign unless they prove a legal basis vitiating consent.

relationships.target_case_name: Li Sau Ying v Bank of China (Hong Kong) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Final Appeal's test for determining whether a transaction was procured by undue influence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. THUO PENINAH WANGARI
judges: Before: Hon M Poon JA in Court

candidates.case_name: NG TUNG v. GRAND WAYFAIR INVESTMENT COMPANY LTD
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: For economic duress to be established, the pressure applied must be illegitimate. Mere commercial pressure or inequality of bargaining power, even if significant, is not sufficient by itself to constitute duress.

The principle of economic duress requires a 'special disadvantage' that seriously impairs the innocent party's ability to judge their own best interests, and the other party must know or ought to know of this disadvantage and its effect.

For a court to set aside an agreement on grounds of duress or undue influence, there must be evidence that the other party had actual, imputed, or constructive knowledge of the illegitimate pressure or undue influence being exerted.

relationships.target_case_name: Crescendo Management Pty Ltd v Westpac Banking Corporation
relationships.description: The judgment cites McHugh JA's formulation of the 'special disadvantage' requirement for establishing economic duress.

relationships.target_case_name: Campbell Discount Co Ltd v Bridge
relationships.description: The judgment references Lord Radcliff's authority regarding the rejection of generalized equitable principles against enforcing legal rights.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. GWALA NHLANHLA JOHANNES
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An offender must generally be sentenced according to the laws and sentencing guidelines that were in force at the time the offence was committed.

relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the general principle stated in Choi Wing Ka regarding the temporal application of sentencing laws.

candidates.case_name: SUN HUNG KAI CREDIT LTD v. GUAN WENNA
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Ko in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Adults with ordinary understanding are bound by documents they sign unless a recognized legal basis exists to vitiate their consent or unless enforcing the document would constitute unconscionable conduct justifying equitable relief.
To disown a signed legal document, the party must specifically plead and prove through evidence the facts constituting the particular vitiating factor relied upon.

A company can only act through individuals, and under common law, principles of agency and vicarious liability allow a company to be held liable for the acts and mental states of its agents and employees, just as a human principal would be.

principles.principle_label: Enforcement of contracts for unlawful purpose
principles.paraphrase_en: A court will not assist in enforcing a contract if it was made with the intention of using the subject matter for an unlawful, immoral, or public policy-offending purpose, even if the agreement itself is not unlawful on its face.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Ming Shiu Chung for the principle that facts constituting a vitiating factor must be pleaded and proved to disown a signed document.
The judgment discusses Alexander v Rayson as authority for the principle that a court will not enforce contracts made with a fraudulent purpose.

candidates.case_name: LIU TSAN CHING v. PAK TIM YING TIFFANY
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Joseph Vaughan in Court

candidates.case_name: RE LI XIAOMING
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The applicability of all Ladd v Marshall criteria for admitting new evidence on appeal may be questioned in specific procedural contexts, such as when a recusal application was decided by a judge through private correspondence without formal evidence from other parties.

A recusal application may be rejected on grounds of lateness and lack of substantiation, particularly where the applicant fails to provide a timely or acceptable explanation for the delay in making the application despite prior knowledge of the relevant circumstances.

The capacity in which a person signs a document may be immaterial to determining their liability if sufficient contemporaneous documentary evidence supports the opposing party's case regarding personal guarantee of liabilities.

principles.principle_label: Leave to appeal on procedural issues
principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to appeal to the Court of Final Appeal on procedural issues is rarely granted and generally requires the appeal to clearly raise a question of law of great general or public importance, where the outcome would depend on resolving that question.

principles.principle_label: Question of great general or public importance
principles.paraphrase_en: A question is not of great general or public importance if it is fact-sensitive and premised on the particular circumstances of a case, or cannot be answered in the abstract without reliance on specific facts.

relationships.description: The judgment questions whether all criteria from Ladd v Marshall apply in specific procedural contexts like recusal applications decided through private correspondence.

candidates.case_name: 張鵬舉 v. 原翠美
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Leung in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal against a master's summary judgment requires the appellate court to conduct a fresh assessment of whether the defendant has a real prospect of successfully defending the claim or there is some other compelling reason for a trial.
In commercial contexts, particularly among traders, binding agreements can be formed orally, and their terms can be subsequently varied by further oral agreement between the parties.

A common trade practice exists where goods are initially delivered under a bailment (loan for display), which subsequently converts into a contract of sale if the bailee's customer decides to purchase the goods.
A contemporaneous signed document (like a borrowing note) created after an oral agreement serves as key evidence for ascertaining the terms of the parties' agreement and any outstanding obligations.

candidates.case_name: 莫懿 V. 宏大香港資產管理有限公司 AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: There is no legal requirement that consideration provided by a promisee must move directly to the promisor.

principles.principle_label: Triable issues standard for summary judgment
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's case on a defense such as duress raises a triable issue for trial if the evidence is not incredible or incapable of belief, preventing summary judgment.

relationships.target_case_name: Yue Tai Plywood & Timber Co Ltd v Far East Wagner Construction Ltd & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that consideration need not move directly from promisee to promisor.

candidates.case_name: WEALTHY TECH GROUP HOLDINGS LTD v. YUNG MAY FONG BETTY AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Eugene Fung SC in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant raising duress as a defense must provide timely, specific, and adequately particularized allegations to demonstrate an arguable case that an agreement was procured under duress.
A challenge to the validity of a settlement agreement based on alleged lack of consideration must be substantiated to be considered arguable.

candidates.case_name: FAIRWING INVESTMENT LTD v. CHAU SAU NGOR AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for beneficial ownership
principles.paraphrase_en: The starting presumption is that beneficial ownership follows legal ownership. A party claiming a beneficial interest different from the legal title bears the burden of proving this on the balance of probabilities.

principles.principle_label: Requirements for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a common intention constructive trust, a claimant must show: (i) a common intention between the parties at the time of purchase (or later) that beneficial ownership differs from legal ownership; (ii) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention; and (iii) that it would be unconscionable for the legal owner to deny the claimant's interest.

principles.principle_label: Two limbs of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust can arise under two limbs: first, based on evidence of an express agreement or understanding between the parties; second, based on an inference of common intention drawn from the parties' conduct, which may include direct financial contributions to the purchase.

When assessing whether a witness is truthful, the court must proceed with caution, recognizing the fallibility and potential unreliability of human memory, and that recollection can be affected by unconscious bias.

principles.principle_label: Illegitimacy of pressure in economic duress
principles.paraphrase_en: The key element in proving economic duress is establishing the illegitimacy of the pressure exerted. Not all pressure in commercial bargaining is illegitimate; it becomes so when it involves a 'special disadvantage' that seriously impairs a party's ability to judge their own best interests, and the other party knows or ought to know of this.

relationships.target_case_name: Yu Man Fung Alice v Chiau Sing Chi Stephen
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case as a recent reminder on the cautious approach required when assessing witness credibility due to the malleability of memory.
The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that equity follows the law and the burden is on the claimant to show beneficial ownership differs from legal title.
The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that equity follows the law and the burden is on the claimant to show beneficial ownership differs from legal title.
The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that equity follows the law and the burden is on the claimant to show beneficial ownership differs from legal title.

relationships.target_case_name: Crescendo Management Pty Ltd v Westpac Banking Corporation
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Australian authority for the proposition that economic duress requires a 'special disadvantage' affecting a party's judgment, not merely a difference in bargaining power.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. QUINTERO BERNAL DIEGO-JHOVANNY AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

candidates.case_name: GLOBAL SPARKLE HOLDINGS IV LTD v. GE YIYANG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.principle_label: Discharge of guarantee by creditor's breach of duty
principles.paraphrase_en: A guarantee may be discharged if the creditor breaches a legal duty owed to the debtor, and the guarantor relied on the performance of that duty when giving the guarantee.

principles.principle_label: Creditor's freedom to pursue repayment sources
principles.paraphrase_en: A creditor is generally entitled to choose freely from available sources of repayment and is not required to enforce one security before demanding payment from a guarantor.

principles.principle_label: No general discharge for prejudicial creditor conduct
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no general principle that irregular or prejudicial conduct by the creditor, which does not amount to releasing the principal debtor, discharges the surety.

principles.principle_label: Narrow ground for discharge based on bad faith
principles.paraphrase_en: A guarantee may be discharged on a narrower ground involving fraud or bad faith conduct by the creditor that goes beyond mere prejudice or unfairness to the guarantor.

principles.principle_label: Essential elements of injurious conduct defence
principles.paraphrase_en: For the defence of injurious conduct to succeed absent a breach of legal duty, the creditor's conduct must involve more than mere prejudice and likely involve elements of fraud or bad faith, given the creditor's general entitlement to choose recovery methods.

relationships.description: The judgment interprets Watts v Shuttleworth narrowly, confining it to situations where the creditor breaches a legal duty to the debtor that the guarantor relied upon.

relationships.target_case_name: Bank of India v Transcontinental Commodity Merchants Ltd and Patel
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that irregular or prejudicial creditor conduct does not generally discharge a surety unless it releases the principal debtor.

candidates.case_name: THE HONGKONG AND SHANGHAI BANKING CORPORATION LTD v. KING WAI PIECE GOODS COMPANY LTD AND OTHERS

principles.paraphrase_en: A party who misrepresents the contents or effect of a contractual document to the other party may be prevented from enforcing the contract according to its written terms.
To establish economic duress, the claimant must prove the pressure exerted was illegitimate; mere commercial pressure from a powerful bargaining position is not by itself illegitimate.

A person of full age and understanding who signs a document with legal effect is generally bound by it, even if they signed without knowing its contents, unless a recognized legal basis exists to invalidate that apparent consent.
In extreme and narrowly confined circumstances, a court may find a signed document not binding if an onerous and unusual clause was not properly drawn to the signatory's attention.

relationships.target_case_name: Tin Wan Tung v Wong See Yin
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case for the principle that misrepresentation of a document's contents can prevent enforcement according to its terms.

relationships.target_case_name: Esquire (Electronics) Ltd v Hong Kong and Shanghai Banking Corp Ltd

relationships.target_case_name: DBS Bank (Hong Kong) Limited v Sit Pan Jit

candidates.case_name: 储惠敏 v. KWOK CHING CHING AND ANOTHER

principles.paraphrase_en: A document is a sham if the parties did not intend it to create the legal rights and obligations it appears to create, and their conduct demonstrates they did not treat it as such.
For duress to succeed, the complainant must prove that the illegitimate pressure was a reason for entering the contract; it is not necessary to show there was no practical alternative but to enter.

principles.principle_label: No presumption of undue influence in spousal transactions
principles.paraphrase_en: A transaction that appears disadvantageous to a wife is not presumed to have been procured by undue influence; there may be other valid reasons for her decision.
Generally, a person who signs a legal document is bound by it, and claiming not to have understood its contents is not a defence, absent fraud or other vitiating factors.

principles.principle_label: Definition of money lender under MLO
principles.paraphrase_en: Merely holding an interest in a money lending company and advancing a substantial loan to a stranger is insufficient to prove a person is a money lender within the meaning of the Money Lenders Ordinance.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Midland Bank Plc v Wyatt that a document is a sham if the party never intended to create the beneficial interest it purported to create.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN HOI WING

principles.paraphrase_en: The defense of duress is not available if the defendant had a reasonable opportunity to report the threatening persons to the police but failed to do so, instead choosing to avoid them by moving between locations.

principles.principle_label: Jury instruction on reasonable possibility of defense
principles.paraphrase_en: The jury must be instructed that if the defendant's account supporting a defense is possibly true, they must return a verdict of not guilty.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for mixed drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for trafficking in multiple types of drugs, a combined approach may be used where the quantity of one drug (e.g., ICE) is considered under established guidelines, while other substances (e.g., midazolam) may be disregarded for the purpose of determining the starting point.

relationships.description: The judgment references the sentencing guidelines from Ching Kwok Hung for determining the starting point based on the quantity of ICE involved in drug trafficking.

candidates.case_name: ZHANG, FENGQIU v. CHAN, KA YUNG AND ANOTHER

principles.paraphrase_en: A foreign judgment may be enforced in Hong Kong when the defendant was properly notified of the foreign proceedings and given a fair opportunity to present their case, and the judgment is final and conclusive.
A party alleging duress in the signing of a document bears the burden of providing evidence to substantiate that serious allegation.

principles.principle_label: Finding of fact based on absence of contrary evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may make a positive factual finding where the evidence supports it and no contrary evidence is presented to rebut it.

relationships.target_case_name: JSC BTA Bank v Mukhtar Kabulovich Ablyazov
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the legal requirements for enforcing foreign judgments as set out in this authority.

candidates.case_name: HUNG TAT A. I. GARMENT MACHINERY COMPANY LTD v. HONG KONG MELT BLOWING PRODUCTION COMPANY LTD AND ANOTHER

principles.principle_label: Assessment of evidence relevance and probative value
principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence consisting of bare assertions without explanation of their basis or timing lacks sufficient probative value to be considered for a just resolution of the dispute.
Delay in legal proceedings inherently constitutes prejudice to the innocent party, as it requires them to divert resources from trial preparation to address late-filed matters.

relationships.target_case_name: Times Travel (UK) Ltd v Pakistan International Airlines Corpn
relationships.description: The judgment applies the UK Supreme Court's formulation of the elements required to establish economic duress.

candidates.case_name: APPOTECH LTD v. 深圳今日芯科技有限公司

principles.paraphrase_en: Courts should adopt a more flexible and ready approach to finding the existence of consideration in contracts, in line with the modern trend of upholding parties' bargains.
The party alleging that a threat was made bears the burden of proving it with evidence; mere association or ties between individuals does not establish that another party had notice of the threat.
Statements made in a commercial context must be interpreted objectively, considering the surrounding circumstances and the reasonable reaction of the parties.
An injunction is not an appropriate procedural mechanism to summarily determine a substantive dispute that would normally require a full hearing.

relationships.target_case_name: Williams v Roffey Bros & Nicholls (Contractors) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle from Williams v Roffey that courts should be more ready to find consideration.
The judgment applies the reasoning of Cheung J regarding the modern approach to consideration and upholding bargains.

candidates.case_name: YEUNG SUI YEE v. TSANG YEE MING
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Richard Khaw SC in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle governing illegality in Hong Kong contract law is the 'reliance rule' from Tinsley v Milligan. An agreement may be found unlawful and unenforceable if a party must rely on an illegal act (such as a false representation) to establish their claim or enforce the agreement.

When a subsequent agreement varies an earlier one, the court must determine the scope of the variation. The earlier agreement may continue to govern aspects not expressly revised or replaced by the subsequent agreement.

principles.principle_label: Claim for use and occupation without agreement
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for arrears of rent or mesne profits for the use of property requires a contractual or other legal basis for payment. If no agreement exists regarding payment for use, and the governing agreement permits free use, no such claim can succeed.

relationships.target_case_name: Tse Chun Wai v Leung Kwok Kin Joseph
relationships.description: The present judgment follows its holding that the 'reliance rule' from Tinsley v Milligan is the applicable principle for illegality in Hong Kong.
The judgment applies the 'reliance rule' established in this English case as the governing principle for illegality.

relationships.target_case_name: Best Sheen Development Ltd v Official Receiver
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an authority where 'development scheme' agreements involving false representations were held unlawful.

relationships.target_case_name: Chung Mui Teck v Hang Tak Buddhist Hall Association Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as part of a line of authorities concerning unlawful 'development scheme' agreements.

relationships.target_case_name: Kwan Hung Shing & Anor v Fong Kwok Shan Christine & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites this recent case as further authority on the unenforceability of agreements requiring reliance on illegal acts.

candidates.case_name: ROBERT VON PALACE KOLBATSCHENKO AND ANOTHER v. LEE, IO VAI IVAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Houghton, SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Judgment on admission of trust relationship
principles.paraphrase_en: A judgment can be entered based on a clear admission of a legal relationship (such as a trusteeship) even if the precise quantum of liability remains disputed or unascertained.

Assertions of duress or coercion made a considerable time after the alleged pressured event, and without corroborative evidence of the alleged intimidatory effect, are unlikely to be accepted as invalidating an otherwise clear admission or agreement.

A proprietary injunction can properly extend to cover monies withdrawn from identified accounts and their traceable proceeds held in other specified accounts, based on a good arguable case that the original funds were held on trust.

principles.principle_label: Joinder of parties based on tracing
principles.paraphrase_en: It is appropriate to join additional parties as defendants where there is evidence that monies, which arguably belong to the plaintiffs, have been transferred to accounts held by those proposed parties.

candidates.case_name: SPEEDY FINANCE LTD v. ZHANG XIAOPING
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung J in Court

principles.principle_label: Extension of time for interim payment
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for an extension of time to comply with an interim payment order is governed by the principles applicable to relief from sanctions, requiring the court to consider all relevant circumstances, including the reasons for the delay, the length of the delay, and the prejudice to the parties.
An interim payment order may be made where the court is satisfied that, if the action proceeded to trial, the plaintiff would obtain judgment for a substantial sum of money against the defendant.

candidates.case_name: DM v. COMMISSIONER OF CUSTOMS & EXCISE AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrines of res judicata and issue estoppel apply to judicial review proceedings, and there is no distinction between public and private law for these purposes.

principles.principle_label: Abuse of process via collateral attack
principles.paraphrase_en: A civil or judicial review claim that seeks to re-litigate essential factual elements already adjudicated upon in criminal proceedings constitutes an abuse of process as a collateral attack on the prior decisions.

principles.principle_label: Threshold for abuse of process in collateral attack
principles.paraphrase_en: To determine if a claim is an abusive collateral attack, the court examines whether the essential elements of the current claim were already adjudicated upon in prior proceedings, not whether a different outcome in the current claim would inevitably have changed the prior result.
The application of issue estoppel or abuse of process principles is not absolute and involves a discretionary exercise, which may allow exceptions to prevent injustice.

For a positive operational duty on state authorities to arise under Article 4 of the Bill of Rights (BOR4), it must be shown that the authorities were aware, or ought to have been aware, of circumstances giving rise to a credible threat of a breach.
cited_statutes: Hong Kong Bill of Rights Ordinance, Article 4

relationships.target_case_name: R (DN (Rwanda)) v Secretary of State for the Home Department
relationships.description: The judgment applies Lord Carnwath's analysis confirming that res judicata and issue estoppel principles apply in judicial review proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Thrasyvoulou v Secretary of State for the Environment
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that there is no distinction between public and private law for the purposes of res judicata.
The judgment cites the CFA's holding that the Government's framework is an acceptable method to comply with its duty under BOR4.

candidates.case_name: SHAUN WINSTON JUSTIN BOWERS v. MARBURY RIDGE LTD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Barma JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A promise to advance a loan in the future can constitute sufficient consideration for a new loan agreement that consolidates and replaces a prior loan agreement, even if no new money is immediately advanced. The creditor's forbearance of its right to earlier repayment under the original agreement also provides consideration.
A contract must be construed as a whole and against its factual matrix, even if it contains inconsistencies or is poorly drafted, to ascertain the parties' intended meaning.

An appellate court is generally reluctant to interfere with a trial judge's evaluation of evidence and findings of fact, even when based on affidavit and documentary evidence, unless the judge made a palpable error.

candidates.case_name: TSANG YUEN WANG t/a Second Brother（二師兄） v. LEUNG WANG HONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Connie Lee in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court will only accept the evidence of a witness found to be unreliable when it is corroborated by documentary evidence or other independent findings.

Where a party's oral evidence regarding a contractual term is directly contradicted by the express written term of the agreement, the court will not find that oral evidence to be credible or reliable.
The court may accept a party's alternative (fall-back) legal position put forward during closing submissions if it finds that position to be reasonable in light of its factual determinations.

candidates.case_name: LI KWAN HO v. TSOI KA YIN
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Alan Kwong in Court

candidates.case_name: HUNG TAT A. I. GARMENT MACHINERY COMPANY LTD v. HONG KONG MELT BLOWING PRODUCTION COMPANY LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Isaac Chan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Whether an item is separately chargeable depends on the parties' intention, assessed through their communications and the context of the overall agreement.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for invoice enforceability
principles.paraphrase_en: The party asserting an invoice is enforceable bears the burden of proving it on its face value.

principles.principle_label: Lawful act economic duress - justification of demand
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a threat is lawful, the illegitimacy of the threat is determined by focusing on whether the underlying demand is justified.
Damages are recoverable only for losses that were within the parties' reasonable contemplation or for which the party in breach assumed responsibility.

relationships.target_case_name: Not explicitly named in provided paragraphs
relationships.description: The judgment cites the Court of Final Appeal's holding on remoteness of damage in a conveyancing context to illustrate the principle of reasonable contemplation.

candidates.case_name: TASTY CATERING GROUP LTD AND ANOTHER v. CHENG HUNG KIT AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Kwan Ag CJHC, Cheung JA and Chu JA in Court

relationships.target_case_name: Wing Siu Co. Ltd v. Goldquest International Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment references Wing Siu Co. Ltd as a case that followed the Eshelby principle.

candidates.case_name: FU TAT CREDIT LTD v. LAU WAI MAN
judges: Before: Hon H. Au-Yeung J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person of full age and understanding is legally bound by documents they sign, even if they did not read or understand the contents, unless they can establish a recognized legal basis to disavow the document.
A witness's lack of forthrightness on specific matters does not necessarily undermine their overall credibility if they are generally honest.

principles.principle_label: No vicarious liability for independent conduct
principles.paraphrase_en: A party cannot be held responsible for the conduct of another person unless it is pleaded and established that the other person was acting on their behalf.

relationships.target_case_name: Ming Shiu Chung & Others v Ming Shiu Sum & Others
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that a signatory is generally bound by a signed legal document.
The present judgment cites this case as relevant authority on the issue of enforceability under the MLO.

candidates.case_name: RE CHANCHAL
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge To in Court

candidates.case_name: ZHAN YIFENG v. PANG KA SHING
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Appeal from Master's decision - de novo hearing
principles.paraphrase_en: In an appeal against a Master's decision, the Court conducts a fresh consideration of the matter and is not restricted by the scope of the notice of appeal or the absence of a cross-appeal.

principles.principle_label: Real prospect of success for defence
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may find that a defendant has a real prospect of successfully establishing a defence, such as duress or that a transaction was a sham, based on the version of events presented in the defendant's evidence.

principles.principle_label: Knowledge of proceedings via service on agents
principles.paraphrase_en: A party may be found to have knowledge of legal proceedings, such as an injunction, if the relevant order was served on that party's agents or counterparties in a transaction, as it is highly likely the information would be communicated.

relationships.target_case_name: Bank of Qingdao Co., Ltd. v OOO Securities (HK) Group Limited
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in that case regarding the nature of an appeal from a Master's decision.

candidates.case_name: 張鵬舉 v. 原翠美
judges: Before: Hon Chu VP and Cheung JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may order an appellant to provide security for costs if the appellant would be unable to pay the respondent's costs of the appeal if unsuccessful due to impecuniosity.

principles.principle_label: Security for costs - undue delay/expense basis
principles.paraphrase_en: Security for costs may also be ordered if the respondent can prove they would likely face undue delay or incur undue expense in enforcing a costs order made in the appeal.

principles.principle_label: Discretionary nature of security for costs
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has discretion in ordering security for costs and may consider other relevant factors, such as the apparent strength of the appeal, to determine what is just.

principles.principle_label: Security for costs - injustice to successful respondent
principles.paraphrase_en: It can be prima facie unjust to allow an appeal to proceed without security for costs where the respondent would face difficulty enforcing a costs order against the appellant, especially where issues have already been determined against the appellant at trial.

relationships.target_case_name: Laerdal Medical Limited v Hong Kong Haocheng International Trading Limited
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Laerdal Medical that allowing an appeal without security can be unjust where enforcement of costs would be difficult.

candidates.case_name: LUI MING LOK v. NG IM FONG LORETTA
judges: Before: Hon Cheung, Yuen and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Void vs voidable marriage for mental incapacity
principles.paraphrase_en: Under modern matrimonial legislation (specifically by the time of the Matrimonial Causes Ordinance 1973), lack of consent due to unsoundness of mind or mental disorder can only render a marriage voidable, not void.

A marriage involving an insane person may not be declared null and void if the marriage is beneficial to that person, such as where the other spouse provides care, and third parties should not be permitted to interfere with it.

principles.principle_label: Party's right to assert own incapacity
principles.paraphrase_en: A party may bring proceedings to annul a marriage on the ground of their own mental incapacity at the time of the marriage, as such incapacity invalidates the marriage contract due to want of consent.

In statutory interpretation, where a general enactment covers a situation for which specific provision is made elsewhere in the legislation, it is presumed the situation is intended to be dealt with by the specific provision.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Browning v. Reane for the principle that a beneficial marriage to an insane person may not be declared void and to illustrate the historical common law rule.
The judgment cites Turner v Meyers for the principle that a party can assert their own past mental incapacity to annul a marriage for want of consent.

candidates.case_name: LUI MING LOK (雷明樂) v. NG IM FONG LORETTA (伍艷芳), the executrix of the estate of LUI KWAN CHEUNG (雷均祥), Deceased under the Grant of Probate No. HCAG016896/2015
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Lord Neuberger of Abbotsbury NPJ

principles.principle_label: Interpretation of 'unsound mind' in marriage voidability
principles.paraphrase_en: The phrase 'of unsound mind or subject to recurrent fits of insanity' in the relevant marriage ordinance should be interpreted according to its ordinary meaning to cover any type of mental incapacity that could invalidate a marriage, subject only to specific exclusions expressly stated elsewhere in the legislation.

Where a statute expressly lists specific categories (like 'lunatics' and 'idiots') in one provision, those categories are excluded from the scope of a separate, more general provision (like 'unsound mind') through the interpretative principle of expressio unius exclusio alterius.

principles.principle_label: Statutory language determines who may challenge marriage
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether a marriage is void or voidable under the Marriage Ordinance depends on the plain language of the relevant sections; there is no implied restriction limiting the ability to argue a marriage is void only to a party to that marriage.

principles.principle_label: Distinction between void and voidable marriages
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal effect of a marriage found invalid on certain grounds can be either void (treated as never having existed) or voidable (valid until annulled), and this distinction is determined by the specific statutory wording.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as historical authority discussing the meaning of 'unsound mind' in the context of incapacity.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YEUNG FONG CHU
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J

principles.principle_label: Elements of administering stupefying drug with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: The offense of administering a stupefying or overpowering drug requires proof that the defendant administered such a drug to another person with the specific intent to commit an indictable offense.

principles.principle_label: Attempted murder requires intent to kill
principles.paraphrase_en: For a conviction of attempted murder, the prosecution must prove the defendant took a step towards killing another person with the specific intent to cause death.

principles.principle_label: Wounding with intent as alternative to attempted murder
principles.paraphrase_en: Wounding with intent can be charged as an alternative count to attempted murder, requiring proof of wounding coupled with the intent to cause grievous bodily harm.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEN KEEN (alias JACK CHEN) (陳克恩)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Gummow NPJ

principles.principle_label: Jurisdiction for defendant costs under CCCO s.4
principles.paraphrase_en: Section 4 of the Costs in Criminal Cases Ordinance grants jurisdiction to award costs to a defendant only when the criminal process, or a discrete phase of it, has concluded or terminated in the defendant's favor.

principles.principle_label: Construction of 'is not tried' in s.4
principles.paraphrase_en: The phrase 'is not tried' in section 4 of the CCCO does not apply where a trial has started but is aborted with a new trial to commence; such a defendant is still being tried and the process has not ended.

principles.principle_label: Purpose of costs awards under CCCO
principles.paraphrase_en: Costs awarded under the CCCO are not punitive but are intended to compensate a party for expenses properly incurred in the proceedings, as per the principle in section 15(a).

principles.principle_label: Limitation on costs for interim successes
principles.paraphrase_en: The CCCO does not grant power to award costs for a defendant's interim success within a trial stage (such as winning a voir dire) if that success does not itself conclude or terminate that trial stage in the defendant's favor.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Charlesworth for the proposition that a person is not 'tried' or put in jeopardy until a verdict is reached, supporting the interpretation of 'is not tried' in CCCO s.4.
The judgment cites this case alongside Charlesworth as authority on the meaning of being 'tried' or put in jeopardy in the context of double jeopardy.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN CHU LEUNG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and Anthea Pang JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Right of silence and permissible cross-examination
principles.paraphrase_en: Cross-examination of an accused person is permissible to test the validity of a claim they have made (such as feeling unwell or the circumstances of an event), provided it does not touch upon their right to remain silent about the occurrence of an offence, the identity of participants, or their roles.

The prosecution must not invite the jury to find a witness more credible or less likely to fabricate evidence solely based on their status as a police officer. Arguments must center on the logic and circumstances of the evidence, not the witness's occupation.

A jury is entitled to accept part of a witness's testimony while rejecting another part. However, if a prosecution witness is found unreliable on a material point, the jury must be extra careful before accepting any other material evidence from that witness against an accused.

relationships.description: The judgment references Lam Sze Nga for the principle that cross-examination to determine if an accused availed himself of the right of silence can be permissible.

The judgment applies the distinction from Petty & Maiden to determine that certain cross-examination did not touch on the right of silence as it was not about the offence, participants, or their roles.

candidates.case_name: CHAN SAU WAI SHIRLEY v. JEN KIT JOSEPHINE, the administratrix of the estate of the Deceased AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Court

principles.principle_label: Test for mental capacity to marry
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal test for determining whether a person has the mental capacity to enter into a marriage is the same as for any other contract. The party alleging lack of capacity must prove that the individual's mind was so unsound at the time of the marriage as to be incapable of giving valid consent.

All persons of lawful age are presumed to have the mental capacity to enter into contracts, including marriage. The burden of proof lies on the party seeking to set aside the contract to rebut this presumption by demonstrating a lack of sufficient mental capacity.

principles.principle_label: Voidable vs. void marriage for lack of consent
principles.paraphrase_en: A marriage is voidable, not void, if one party's consent was invalid due to unsoundness of mind. Such a marriage remains legally valid unless and until it is annulled by a court through a nullity petition.

Absent a formal petition for nullity, a court generally has no jurisdiction to grant a declaration that a marriage was void from its inception. The proper procedure to challenge a marriage on grounds of incapacity is through nullity proceedings.

An expert's psychiatric opinion on a person's mental capacity is of limited assistance if it is based on non-medical, disputed facts. The expert's conclusions are conditional upon the court's factual findings, and if those facts are not proven, the opinion may require reconsideration.

relationships.description: The judgment references this case in the context of discussing legal proceedings concerning an individual's capacity to marry.
The judgment cites this case for the legal principles regarding the court's jurisdiction to declare a marriage void and the distinction between void and voidable marriages.

candidates.case_name: CAP10 /2020 ANGELA CHEN v. WAI WAI CHEN
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Discovery before defence in civil actions
principles.paraphrase_en: The general rule in civil litigation is that a party is not entitled to obtain discovery from the opposing side before filing their defence.

principles.principle_label: Executor's duty to avoid costly probate litigation
principles.paraphrase_en: In probate matters, executors have a duty to make every reasonable effort to avoid costly litigation concerning a will, which can include the early disclosure of evidence from the will's preparer.

The principle that legal professional privilege does not apply between parties who share a joint interest (as established in cases like Russell v Jackson) is confined to situations where all parties are claiming under the same will, not where beneficiaries are advancing claims under competing wills.

General correspondence with a solicitor regarding a will does not, by itself, constitute evidence of the will's attestation and execution for the purposes of the exception to privilege established in Re Fuld.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Larke v Nugus that executors have a duty to avoid costly probate litigation, which can justify early disclosure of a will preparer's evidence.
The judgment distinguishes the joint interest privilege principle from Russell v Jackson as inapplicable to a case involving a disputed will with beneficiaries claiming under competing wills.
The judgment follows the reasoning in Chinachem that general solicitor correspondence does not constitute evidence of a will's attestation and execution.
The judgment cites the exception to legal professional privilege established in Re Fuld concerning evidence of a will's execution.

candidates.case_name: THE SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. TAM KIT-I
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice French NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: An application for a confiscation order is 'concluded' for the purposes of section 2(16A) of the Organized and Serious Crimes Ordinance (OSCO) when the judge decides not to make the order, regardless of the reason for that decision.
A restraint order under OSCO is a temporary, protective measure intended to freeze assets pending the making of a confiscation order, and is not designed to apply indefinitely.

principles.principle_label: Trigger for discharge of a restraint order
principles.paraphrase_en: The discharge of a restraint order under section 15(5)(b) of OSCO is triggered when the related confiscation order application is 'concluded' as defined in section 2(16A).

principles.principle_label: Absconding not limited to post-arrest/charge flight
principles.paraphrase_en: A finding that a person has 'absconded' under section 8(1)(a)(ii) of OSCO is not contingent on that person having been arrested or charged prior to their departure from the jurisdiction.

The statutory regime for restraint and confiscation orders under OSCO is distinct from a general interlocutory injunction, as OSCO expressly authorises such orders even in the absence of pending criminal proceedings against the asset owner, specifically catering for cases where a person has absconded.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that a restraint order is a temporary measure not intended to apply indefinitely.
The judgment distinguishes the OSCO statutory regime from the facts of this case, which involved an interlocutory injunction where there was no pending event for the money to be held.

candidates.case_name: LIU SHIH TENG, THE ADMINISTRATOR OF THE ESTATE OF LIU HAK WING, DECEASED v. HKCC DOTCOD LTD t/a DOTCOD SEAFOOD RESTAURANT & OYSTER BAR
judges: Before : Hon Marlene Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to amend pleadings should be granted unless the proposed amendment is bound to fail.

principles.principle_label: Leave to appeal against case management
principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to appeal against a case management decision requires an arguable case with a reasonable chance of success that the judge erred in exercising discretion.

principles.principle_label: Occupiers' liability vs common law negligence
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim under occupiers' liability legislation does not preclude a co-existing claim in common law negligence, which may arise from a different relationship or duty.

principles.principle_label: Duty of care from invitor-invitee relationship
principles.paraphrase_en: A special invitor-invitee relationship, such as between a bar/restaurant and its patron, can impose a general common law duty to take positive steps to protect the invitee from foreseeable dangers, including those arising from intoxication.

For a defendant to be held to have assumed responsibility for a claimant's risk-taking, there must be particular facts or specific reliance beyond mere foreseeability of injury and occupation of the premises.

relationships.target_case_name: Tsang Wing Kwai v Tsang Wing Fai
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for procedural guidance regarding written submissions and paper disposal of summonses.

relationships.target_case_name: Waan Chuen Ming v Lo Kin Nam t/a Kar Kin Engineering & Supplier Co & anor
relationships.description: The judgment notes the Court of Appeal found it unnecessary to decide on the juridical basis for distinguishing between occupancy and activity duties.
The judgment references this case as the origin of the general principle of negligence liability, preceding the specific rules on occupiers' liability.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that an invitor-invitee relationship creates a special duty of care on drinking establishments to protect patrons from intoxication dangers.
The judgment references dissenting judgments in this case which supported an affirmative duty on occupiers to prevent foreseeable injury from alcohol consumption.

candidates.case_name: LIU SHIH TENG, the administrator of the estate of LIU HAK WING, deceased v. HKCC DOTCOD LTD t/a DOTCOD SEAFOOD RESTAURANT & OYSTER BAR
judges: Before: Hon Marlene Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The purpose of providing particulars in pleadings is to inform the opposing party of the case they must meet, prevent surprise at trial, enable preparation of evidence, limit the generality of claims and evidence, define the issues for trial and discovery, and restrict the party from departing from their pleaded case without leave.

principles.principle_label: General approach to amendment of pleadings
principles.paraphrase_en: In the absence of real prejudice, courts should generally allow amendments to pleadings to adjudicate on the real issues between parties, and procedural rules should not prevent parties from raising their genuine claims or defences.

principles.principle_label: Test for granting leave to amend
principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to amend pleadings before trial should be readily granted unless the new claim based on the proposed amendment is bound to fail. The court may consider the merits of the case only when they are readily apparent without requiring prolonged investigation.
The degree of particularity required in pleadings is determined by the need to provide a fair and sufficient indication of the case being brought and that the opposing party has to meet.
The common law duty of care that gives rise to a claim in damages for negligence is determined by reference to the tests established in Caparo Industries Plc v Dickman.

relationships.target_case_name: VSC Building Products Co Ltd v Kono Insurance Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that courts should allow amendments to pleadings to adjudicate on real issues absent prejudice.
The judgment applies the test from this case that leave to amend should be granted unless the new claim is bound to fail.

relationships.target_case_name: Sel-Imperial Ltd v The British Standards Institution
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case regarding the need to balance generosity in amendments against unfairness to the opposing party.
The judgment cites this case in discussing the balance between allowing amendments and preventing unfairness.

relationships.target_case_name: Caparo Industries Plc v Dickman & ors
relationships.description: The judgment follows the tests from this House of Lords case for determining the duty of care in negligence.

relationships.target_case_name: Luen Hing Fat Coating & Finishing Factory Ltd v Waan Chuen Ming
relationships.description: The judgment applies this leading local authority which adopted the Caparo approach to duty of care.

candidates.case_name: A v. B
judges: Before: Hon Au and G Lam JJA in Court

candidates.case_name: RE RAFZAAL AHMED
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Bruno Chan in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Extension of time criteria for judicial review appeals
principles.paraphrase_en: When deciding whether to extend time to appeal a refusal of leave for judicial review, the court must consider: (1) the length of the delay, (2) the reason for the delay, (3) the prospects of the intended appeal, and (4) any prejudice to the respondent if extension is granted.
A delay exceeding nine months in filing an appeal against a refusal of leave for judicial review is considered very substantial.

principles.principle_label: Medical evidence requirement for delay explanation
principles.paraphrase_en: A bare medical appointment slip, without details of the patient's condition or how it affected their ability to act, is insufficient to substantiate a medical reason for a procedural delay.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites Re MK as authority for the criteria governing extensions of time for appealing a refusal of leave for judicial review.

relationships.target_case_name: Chu On Fong Winter v The Appeal Panel (Housing)
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case as authority for the criteria governing extensions of time for appealing a refusal of leave for judicial review.

relationships.target_case_name: Islam Raja Rais v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case as authority for the criteria governing extensions of time for appealing a refusal of leave for judicial review.
The present judgment cites this case as authority for the criteria governing extensions of time for appealing a refusal of leave for judicial review.
The present judgment cites this case as authority for the criteria governing extensions of time for appealing a refusal of leave for judicial review.

candidates.case_name: H v. L
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Supervisory court refusal as strong policy consideration
principles.paraphrase_en: When a supervisory court refuses to remedy an alleged defect in an arbitral award, leaving the final award undisturbed, this creates a strong policy consideration favoring enforcement of that award in other jurisdictions.

principles.principle_label: Illegality and enforcement under Money Lenders Ordinance
principles.paraphrase_en: For a loan agreement to be found illegal and unenforceable under the Money Lenders Ordinance, the party alleging illegality must prove that the agreement has a closer connection to Hong Kong than to any other jurisdiction involved in the transaction.

The reasons provided in an arbitral award do not need to be elaborate or lengthy; they must be read and understood in the proper context of how the issues were argued before the tribunal, considering arbitration's private and confidential nature.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAW SIU KIN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for dangerous driving offenses, the court's primary focus should be on the culpability of the defendant's driving conduct, rather than on personal mitigating factors like good character or clear record.

principles.principle_label: Dominant sentencing principles for dangerous driving
principles.paraphrase_en: The dominant sentencing principle for dangerous driving cases is general deterrence, with principles of retribution and denunciation also being relevant considerations.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles established in HKSAR v Lee Yau Wing regarding the focus on culpability and the dominance of general deterrence in dangerous driving cases.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOW WAI MAN
judges: Before: His Honour Judge E Lin

principles.paraphrase_en: A driver has an absolute duty to ensure no accident occurs, with a heightened standard of care because they are in control of a powerful instrument capable of causing serious injury or death.

principles.principle_label: Standard of the prudent and reasonable driver
principles.paraphrase_en: A competent, prudent, and reasonable driver must be alert to the possibility that pedestrians may not comply with traffic signs and lights.

Where a driver exceeds the legal speed limit and evidence shows that compliance with the limit would have allowed them to stop in time to avoid a collision, the breach can be causally linked to the accident.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SINGH GURINDER
judges: Before: HH Judge C P Pang

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHENG TSE HONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

candidates.case_name: MARINA BAY SANDS PTE LTD v. TSANG YEUK KWAN
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Le Pichon in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A party's prior awareness of an asymmetric jurisdiction clause is a relevant factor when assessing their conduct in subsequent litigation concerning forum choice.

principles.principle_label: Conduct in forum non conveniens applications
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may view unfavorably a party's conduct in raising a forum non conveniens objection late in proceedings, especially when it results in wasted judicial resources, and such conduct is a factor in the court's exercise of its discretion.

principles.principle_label: Case-specific application of forum non conveniens
principles.paraphrase_en: Decisions on forum non conveniens and the risk of inconsistent decisions are highly fact-sensitive and must be interpreted in the context of the specific case in which they were made.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as having considered authorities on the admission of fresh evidence concerning post-trial events.

relationships.target_case_name: Lammas Global Corporation v Barclay’s Bank (Suisse) SA & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment notes that the decision in this case must be viewed against its specific factual backdrop, implying it may not directly govern the present facts.

relationships.target_case_name: Rambas Marketing Co LLC v Chow Kam Fai David
relationships.description: The judgment references a specific passage from this case, which was subsequently cited in DP World Djibouti.

relationships.target_case_name: DP World Djibouti FZCO v China Merchants Port Holdings Company Limited
relationships.description: The judgment notes that this case cited and applied the relevant passage from Rambas Marketing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. POON CHI KEUNG
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. GUO RONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who enters a guilty plea may be entitled to a reduction in sentence, with one-third being a recognized potential discount.
When sentencing for multiple offences, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is just and proportionate to the defendant's overall criminality.
Where multiple offences arise from the same occasion or a series of connected events, the court should consider imposing concurrent sentences.

In applying the totality principle, the court assesses whether the total sentence reflects the defendant's overall culpability, considering that violence in one offence may already be accounted for in the sentence for another connected offence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM HO WING
judges: Before: HH Judge Yiu

principles.paraphrase_en: A starting point of two years' imprisonment is appropriate for the offense of dangerous driving causing death where there is evidence of deliberately jumping a red light.

Where a driver does not deliberately jump a red light but fails to slow down when approaching a pedestrian crossing and proceeds through a red light, a lower starting point of 18 months' imprisonment may be adopted for dangerous driving causing death.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG FUK WANG
judges: Before: DHCJ Eric Kwok, SC

candidates.case_name: FINE TALENT FINANCE LTD v. THE ESTATE OF LEUNG PAK WAI, DECEASED AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for mental incapacity
principles.paraphrase_en: The party alleging that a person lacked mental capacity to enter into a contract bears the burden of proving that lack of capacity.
At common law, factors that can invalidate a legal document include fraud, mistake, misrepresentation, non est factum, duress, undue influence, and lack of mental capacity.
To disown a legal document based on a vitiating factor, the specific facts constituting that factor must be pleaded and established by evidence.

When a third party (like a bank) is sued on a contract allegedly procured by undue influence, the court must consider: (a) if undue influence is proven; (b) if the third party was put on inquiry; and (c) if so, whether the third party took reasonable steps to ensure no undue influence existed.

The doctrine of constructive notice in undue influence applies specifically to surety/guarantee transactions where the wrongdoer (typically the borrower) improperly procures the complainant to act as guarantor, due to the one-sided nature of such transactions.

relationships.target_case_name: Ming Shiu Chung v Ming Shiu Sum
relationships.description: The judgment applies its holding that common law vitiating factors for contracts include lack of mental capacity and must be pleaded and proven.

relationships.target_case_name: Wing Hang Credit Limited v Hui Chun Kit Benjamin & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites the case for the principle that a plea of non est factum requires positive evidence that consent to the document was truly lacking.

relationships.target_case_name: Wing Hang Bank v Liu Kam Ying
relationships.description: The judgment applies the three-stage test from this case for determining a third party's liability when undue influence is alleged.

relationships.target_case_name: Bank of China (Hong Kong) Ltd v Wong King Sing
relationships.description: The judgment applies its classification of undue influence into actual (Class 1) and presumed (Class 2) types.

candidates.case_name: 史超 v. 均富有限公司 AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge David Chan in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Threshold for valid reason to use VCF
principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant seeking to use video conferencing facilities must provide a valid reason, but the required threshold for establishing such a reason is not high.

principles.principle_label: Holistic balancing approach to VCF applications
principles.paraphrase_en: When deciding whether to permit the use of video conferencing facilities, the court should conduct a holistic exercise balancing all relevant countervailing interests, rather than focusing narrowly on whether a threshold for a valid reason has been met.

principles.principle_label: Medical evidence requirement for inability to travel
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim that a party cannot travel to attend trial must be supported by clear medical evidence stating an inability to travel, not merely evidence that travel might worsen symptoms.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Anthony Chan J's ruling in Re Nobility School Limited regarding the holistic balancing approach to applications for video conferencing facilities.

candidates.case_name: RE LYM
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Chambers (Not Open to the Public)(By Paper Disposal)
