principles.paraphrase_en: The court possesses inherent jurisdiction to appoint a guardian for an incompetent adult based on the principle of promoting the adult's best interests and welfare, particularly when statutory provisions are inadequate.
The inherent jurisdiction regarding incompetent adults is not founded solely on the doctrine of necessity, but on the broader principle of safeguarding their best interests and welfare.

When exercising inherent jurisdiction to appoint a guardian, the court may derive guidance from the criteria and provisions set out in the relevant statutory scheme, even if that scheme does not directly apply.
Where an ordinance creates a right or remedy that has no independent existence and specifies a particular method for pursuing it, courts tend to confine persons to that specified method and forum.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Hing Cheong & Anor v Wah E Investment Ltd & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a statutory scheme may provide an exclusive method for pursuing a right it creates.

relationships.target_case_name: St. Helens Borough Council v PE
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this English case that the inherent jurisdiction for incompetent adults is based on their best interests, not merely necessity.
The judgment references this case in tracing the historical development of the basis for the court's inherent jurisdiction.

candidates.case_name: RE LYM
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Chambers (Not Open to the Public)

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SHUM HON YIN DOMINIC
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge N Wong

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant can be convicted based on their own guilty pleas and admissions to the charges.
The act of throwing an object at a person, even if it misses, can constitute an assault if it causes the victim to apprehend immediate unlawful violence.
Attempting to break into a secured premises, such as a security booth, with the intent to commit an offence inside, constitutes the offence of attempted burglary.
Robbery is established when a person, while committing theft, uses force on any person in order to obtain the property or to overcome resistance to its being taken.
The use of a weapon such as a wooden stick to threaten, follow, and ultimately strike a victim while demanding money is an aggravating factor in the commission of robbery.

candidates.case_name: AMPOFO MOSEN ANTI v. NG CHING MEI
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Andrew Li in Court

principles.principle_label: Loss of earning capacity for unlawful workers
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff who was not in lawful employment at the time of the incident and whose immigration status prohibits lawful employment cannot establish an evidential basis for a claim of loss of future earning capacity.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for speculative earnings
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for loss of earnings based on potential employment in another country or in a specific profession requires documentary proof of the alleged average salary; mere assertion is insufficient.

relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the same reasoning as Kumara Debanama regarding the inability to claim loss of earning capacity when the plaintiff was not lawfully employed.
The court applies the principle from Kumara Debanama that a person with asylum seeker or refugee status cannot be lawfully employed in Hong Kong, thus negating a claim for loss of future earnings.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. LI KWOK WING AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Deliberate acts of harassment, insult, or intimidation against witnesses in court proceedings constitute serious criminal contempt because they interfere with the due administration of justice and cause fear to the witnesses.

principles.principle_label: Protection of witnesses and administration of justice
principles.paraphrase_en: The due administration of justice must not be compromised, and protecting witnesses from intimidation is essential for maintaining the rule of law.
When sentencing for contempt involving witness intimidation, the court may consider factors such as the defendant's culpability and the harm caused, including the impact on the witnesses.

relationships.description: The judgment references sentencing guidelines under the UK's Criminal Justice and Public Order Act 1994 as a useful comparative guide for approaching sentence.

candidates.case_name: CHOW KIN HANG ALI v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE FOR AND ON BEHALF OF FOOD AND ENVIRONMENTAL HYGIENE DEPARTMENT
judges: Before: Hon Chu VP, Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a claim under the Employees' Compensation Ordinance, 'injury by accident' requires a distinct accident as the cause and a personal injury as the effect. The claimant must prove both elements.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for employment accident
principles.paraphrase_en: The claimant bears the burden of proving, on a balance of probabilities, that they suffered a personal injury by an accident that arose out of and in the course of their employment.
In an employees' compensation claim, allegations of employer negligence or breach of employment contract are irrelevant to establishing entitlement under the Ordinance.

Compensation under the Employees' Compensation Ordinance is not payable for periods of absence where the employee has already received full salary, as this would constitute impermissible double recovery.

principles.principle_label: Proof of loss of earning capacity
principles.paraphrase_en: To claim compensation for loss of earning capacity, the claimant must prove that the injury caused a loss of earning capacity, which is not established if the claimant continues to perform duties well and employment termination is unlikely.

relationships.target_case_name: Sit Wing Yi Sibly v Berton Industrial Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Final Appeal case for the principle that 'injury by accident' encompasses distinct cause (accident) and effect (injury).
The judgment applies the procedural principle from this decision that a respondent cannot raise new matters on appeal without a respondent's notice.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG YAN KIN
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J

candidates.case_name: RE NLS
judges: Before: Hon Lok J in Chambers (Not open to public)

principles.principle_label: High threshold for Part II MHO orders
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must exercise its jurisdiction to make orders under Part II of the Mental Health Ordinance cautiously, as such orders deprive a person of the civil right to manage their own property and finances, with grave consequences for human dignity and self-esteem. Cogent evidence and a high threshold are required.

principles.principle_label: Test for mental incapacity in property management
principles.paraphrase_en: The distinction relevant to mental capacity is between delegating financial management tasks and lacking the capacity to understand financial decisions. A finding of incapacity is made when a person cannot understand what is happening with their finances and must depend entirely on others.

The court may accept an expert's assessment even if standard tests like the MMSE were not used, provided the expert improvised a suitable method to assess the specific individual's capacity, especially where the standard test has known limitations and was refused by the subject.

principles.principle_label: Focus on functional deficits in capacity assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: In assessing capacity, particularly in neurocognitive disorders, the court must focus on the individual's functional abnormalities and what they cannot do, even if some cognitive abilities are retained. Retained simple abilities may be insufficient for managing complex property and affairs.

Each case must be determined on its own facts. The court should focus on the evidence in the present case rather than relying on judicial comments made about an expert in other cases, as there may be various reasons for such past comments.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG WAI KUEN v. PRELIMINARY INVESTIGATION COMMITTEE OF THE MEDICAL COUNCIL OF HONG KONG
judges: Before: Hon Chow J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The Preliminary Investigation Committee's power under section 11(8) of Cap. 161E to decide whether to refer a complaint to an inquiry panel involves a broad discretion to consider all presented information and determine if there is sufficient evidence of a reasonable prospect of a misconduct finding.

principles.principle_label: Standard for PIC referral to inquiry
principles.paraphrase_en: The PIC must decide not to hold an inquiry if, after considering all information, it finds insufficient evidence to show a reasonable prospect of the medical practitioner being found guilty of professional misconduct.

When assessing alleged misconduct against an individual doctor, the PIC may properly consider that the patient was under the care of a team of doctors and nurses, and whether the evidence sufficiently implicates the individual practitioner as distinct from the team's collective responsibility.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority to support the proposition that the scope of duties covered by hospital staff in controlling patients is very wide.

candidates.case_name: CHIN LING WAH by her son and next friend SHUM LUI v. SHUM CHAU
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Jonathan Chang SC in Court

principles.principle_label: Proof of disability for next friend appointment
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no procedural requirement for formal medical evidence to prove a party's disability for the purpose of appointing a next friend. The determination is typically made by the party themselves or their caregivers, potentially with solicitor advice, without court inquiry.

When property is held by two or more persons as joint tenants or tenants in common, the court has three options: order partition under section 4 of the Ordinance, order sale under section 6, or refuse to make any order.
Whether an order for sale is beneficial to all co-owners must be determined objectively by the court, not by the dissent or assertions of opposing co-owners.

relationships.description: The judgment follows the approach from Masterman-Lister that no formal medical evidence or court inquiry is required to determine disability for next friend appointment.

candidates.case_name: LCSJ v. LSHA AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Chu VP, G Lam JA and Chow JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal under Order 55 is a rehearing, not a retrial. The appellate court does not typically rehear evidence or permit re-cross-examination of witnesses; its role is to review the original decision for errors.

New evidence cannot be introduced on appeal except with the Court of Appeal's leave, and such leave is only granted exceptionally if the evidence meets the stringent criteria established in Ladd v Marshall.

A notice of appeal must specify the grounds of appeal and the relief sought. An appellant cannot rely on grounds or seek relief not specified in the notice, except with the leave of the Court of Appeal.

principles.principle_label: Appellate interference with discretionary case management
principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Appeal will not lightly interfere with a trial judge's discretionary case management decision. An appellant must demonstrate the judge erred in legal principle or was plainly wrong, which is a very high threshold.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from Ladd v Marshall governing the exceptional circumstances under which new evidence may be admitted on appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: United Muslim Association of Hong Kong v. Yusuf Yu
relationships.description: The judgment applies the authority to support the requirement that grounds of appeal and relief sought must be specified in the notice of appeal.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAN XUFENG
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.principle_label: Elements of arson with intent to endanger life
principles.paraphrase_en: The offense of arson with intent to endanger life under the Crimes Ordinance requires the destruction or damage of property by fire without lawful excuse, with the intent or recklessness regarding the destruction/damage, and with the intent or recklessness regarding the endangerment of life.

principles.principle_label: Admission as evidence of actus reus
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's admission to setting a fire, combined with physical evidence like a lighter, can establish the actus reus of arson.
The property destroyed or damaged by fire must be identified and its ownership established, such as belonging to a public body like the Hong Kong Housing Authority.

The offense requires that the arson was committed with the intent to endanger life or with recklessness as to whether life would be endangered, which can be inferred from the location and circumstances, such as fire set outside a residential unit with occupants inside.

candidates.case_name: POON CHI FAI v. POON CHI HUNG, WILLIAM AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Mr Recorder William Wong, SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for mental incapacity
principles.paraphrase_en: The party alleging that a person lacked the mental capacity to execute a legal instrument bears the burden of proving that lack of capacity.

principles.principle_label: Test for mental capacity in transactions
principles.paraphrase_en: The test for determining whether a person had the mental capacity to perform a juristic act is whether they understood the nature and effect of the particular transaction in question.

principles.principle_label: Degree of understanding relative to transaction
principles.paraphrase_en: The degree or extent of understanding required for a person to have mental capacity is relative to the particular transaction; more complex or significant transactions require a higher degree of understanding.
An allegation that a person was mentally incapable of executing an instrument is a serious matter and must be supported by cogent evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: To Lee Wah Samuel v Yum Huin Ming
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that the burden of proof lies on the party alleging a lack of mental capacity when signing an instrument.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Chun Hei David v Ma Tung Lily
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the test for mental capacity and the principle of relative understanding as established in this case.
The present judgment cites this English case as authority applied in the Chan Chun Hei David case regarding the test for mental capacity.
The present judgment cites this English case as authority applied in the Chan Chun Hei David case and discusses its guidance on the required understanding for a transaction.

The present judgment cites this case as an example where comprehensive evidence was required to determine mental capacity, and the finding of sufficient capacity was made on the balance of probabilities.

candidates.case_name: AHMED SIDDIQUE v. HONG KONG SAR GOVERNMENT
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Jonathan Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An immigration detainee has a duty to cooperate with authorities in obtaining travel documents to facilitate removal, even while a non-refoulement claim is pending. Lack of cooperation can affect the assessment of whether detention remains reasonable.

The reasonableness of immigration detention must be assessed considering both legal and practical impediments to removal. Authorities must proactively address legal impediments, while detainees must not create or prolong practical impediments through non-cooperation.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the observation from Johnson Benjamin regarding how an ongoing non-refoulement claim interacts with the duty to cooperate with removal preparations.

candidates.case_name: ASIM HAFIZ TAHIR MAHMOOD v. DIRECTOR OF IMMIGRATION AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The legal authority for detaining an individual can shift between different provisions of the Immigration Ordinance depending on the stage of the immigration process, such as during consideration of a deportation order or after its issuance.

principles.principle_label: Applicable principles for habeas corpus in immigration detention
principles.paraphrase_en: The established legal principles governing applications for writs of habeas corpus in the context of immigration detention are summarized in the Court of Appeal's judgment in Harjang Singh, and these principles should be applied to the specific circumstances of the case.

The fact that a detainee has a pre-existing medical condition does not, in itself, invalidate the detention if there is no evidence that appropriate medical attention is unavailable or not being provided by the custodial authority in accordance with its policy and practice.

relationships.target_case_name: Harjang Singh v Secretary for Security
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and applies the summary of principles for habeas corpus in immigration detention cases as set out by the Court of Appeal in Harjang Singh.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHENG HIU TUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MIO CHIU HING
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.principle_label: Detention centre order inappropriate for serious drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: For offences involving trafficking of dangerous drugs on a serious scale, a detention centre order is generally wrong in principle as a sentencing option.

principles.principle_label: Quantity of drugs indicates offence gravity
principles.paraphrase_en: The quantity of dangerous drugs involved in a trafficking offence is a key factor in assessing the gravity of the offence for sentencing purposes.

principles.principle_label: Training centre order also inappropriate for serious drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: For serious drug trafficking offences, a training centre order is also wrong in principle, similar to a detention centre order.

principles.principle_label: Adherence to normal sentencing guidelines for serious offences
principles.paraphrase_en: For serious offences, there must be compelling reasons to depart from the normal sentencing guidelines, and such departures are not warranted without exceptional circumstances.

candidates.case_name: 吳振權 v. 香港特別行政區政府
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Medical superintendent's factual basis for discharge order
principles.paraphrase_en: The medical superintendent's determination that sufficient factual basis exists to make a conditional discharge order under section 42B of the relevant ordinance is a matter for their discretion, not for the court to decide.

principles.principle_label: Appropriate forum for reviewing discharge order
principles.paraphrase_en: The merits of a conditional discharge order should be reviewed by the statutory Tribunal under section 59B of the ordinance, not by the court in a separate application.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. L.S.Y.
judges: Before: HH Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: The totality principle in sentencing requires the court to consider whether the aggregate sentence for multiple offenses is just and appropriate, taking into account the overall criminality and the relationship between the offenses.

When offenses occur on the same day or during the same incident, the court must consider their connection when applying the totality principle, as the commission of one offense may add to the culpability of another, especially when victims differ.

candidates.case_name: TSO TAK LEE v. TSO CHUI YAN ADMINISTRATRIX OF THE ESTATE OF KAM YUK FUNG, DECEASED AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Lisa Wong J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court should apply a three-question framework when investigating whether a testator possessed the requisite testamentary capacity at the time of making the will.

A testator's decision to distribute their estate unequally among close relatives does not, by itself, indicate irrationality or lack of testamentary capacity; unequal distribution can be perfectly rational.

An elderly or infirm testator is not required to provide a detailed explanation for making unequal provisions in their will; imposing such a requirement would constitute undue interference with testamentary freedom.
The principle of testamentary freedom is a facet of the broader right to self-determination, which is underpinned by human rights protections.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Estate of Au Kong Tim
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the three-question framework for investigating testamentary capacity established in that case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG CHI HUNG
judges: Before: HH Judge K Lo

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM YING YUEN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Charles J Chan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offenses arising from the same incident, the court must determine a fair starting point for each charge while considering the overall sentence to avoid double punishment and ensure the total sentence is just and proportionate.
Drink driving is regarded by the courts as a very serious offense due to its potential to cause fatal consequences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BRAR HARVIR SINGH AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peony Wong

principles.paraphrase_en: In sentencing for dangerous driving offences, the defendant's criminal record, particularly multiple convictions for similar offences, constitutes an aggravating factor. The court's primary focus is on the defendant's culpability and the seriousness of the consequences.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for attempt to pervert justice
principles.paraphrase_en: The normal sentence for attempting to pervert the course of justice is immediate imprisonment. A non-immediate custodial sentence requires the presence of abnormal circumstances. Factors such as a clear record, good background, a single lapse, and future good conduct are relevant considerations but are not typically considered abnormal for this type of crime.

When assessing relative culpability among co-defendants in a joint enterprise, the court considers who was the mastermind, who derived the primary benefit from the plan, and the nature of their interactions. Panic arising from a defendant's own irresponsible conduct is not a mitigating factor.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Kwan Wan Ki regarding criminal record as an aggravating factor in dangerous driving sentencing.
The present judgment applies the basic sentencing principles for attempt to pervert the course of justice established in AG v Yeung Kwong-chi.

candidates.case_name: KRISHAN KUMAR v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for non-refoulement decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: A court reviewing a decision of the Torture Claims Appeal Board will not intervene unless an error of law or procedural unfairness is identified in the Board's decision-making process.

Findings of the Board are not subject to challenge unless they are so unreasonable that no reasonable decision-maker could have reached them, meeting the high threshold of Wednesbury unreasonableness or irrationality.
The principle of non-refoulement, which prohibits returning a person to a territory where they would face persecution, is derived from Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the legal principles established by the Court of Appeal in Re: Kartini regarding the review of non-refoulement decisions.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FU CHUN KIT
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge Ada Yim in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The primary factor in sentencing for offenses like causing grievous bodily harm by dangerous driving is the defendant's culpability, which is assessed by examining both the objective dangerousness of the driving and the defendant's moral blameworthiness.

principles.principle_label: Danger of driving under influence of drugs
principles.paraphrase_en: Driving under the influence of drugs is inherently dangerous as drugs impair the complex skills required for driving, including physical coordination, judgment, and mental condition, thereby necessitating deterrence to protect public safety.

When sentencing an offender who is already serving a prison term for another offense, the court must consider the total cumulative effect of all imprisonment terms to ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate.

principles.principle_label: Aggravating factor of offending on bail
principles.paraphrase_en: Committing an offense while on court bail for another matter is an aggravating factor in sentencing, though it may not necessarily affect the starting point for the specific offense being sentenced.

When offenses are of a different nature, even with the totality principle in mind, sentences may properly be ordered to run consecutively, particularly where one offense was committed while on bail for another.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Chu Wing Yin Christine
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principle from that case, which identifies culpability (based on objective dangerousness and moral blameworthiness) as the dominant factor in sentencing for dangerous driving offenses.

The present judgment applies the totality principle as reiterated in that case, requiring consideration of the cumulative effect of all imprisonment terms when the offender is already serving a sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SIT KIN WA (also known as SIT KIN WAH)
judges: Before: HH Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: The specific intent to cause harm can be formed during the course of an incident, not necessarily requiring premeditation before the confrontation begins. The court examines the sequence of events and the defendant's actions to determine when intent was formed.
The presence of a time interval between obtaining a weapon and using it allows for reflection, which can negate claims of impulsive action and support a finding of deliberate intent.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FENG JINMING
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of wounding under section 19 of the Offences against the Person Ordinance requires proof that the defendant unlawfully and maliciously wounded another person.

The offence of criminal damage under sections 60(1) and 63(2) of the Crimes Ordinance requires proof that the defendant, without lawful excuse, destroyed or damaged property belonging to another, intending such destruction or damage or being reckless as to whether such property would be destroyed or damaged.

principles.principle_label: Elements of reckless arson endangering life
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of arson being reckless as to whether life would be endangered under sections 60(2), (3) and 63(1) of the Crimes Ordinance requires proof that the defendant destroyed or damaged property by fire, and was reckless as to whether the life of another would be thereby endangered.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWAN CHUNG KI
judges: Before: DHCJ Eric Kwok, SC

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. LAW SIU KIN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. TAM YING YUEN
judges: Before: Hon D Pang, M Poon and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Aggravating factors in drunk driving sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for dangerous driving causing death, the court must specifically consider and reflect in the sentence any relevant aggravating factors, such as a high alcohol level (tier 3). Failure to do so constitutes an error in principle.

principles.principle_label: Mitigation limits for serious traffic offences
principles.paraphrase_en: For serious traffic offences involving a conscious and voluntary decision to take an unjustifiable risk (like drunk driving), personal or family circumstances should generally be disregarded as mitigating factors.

principles.principle_label: Review sentence discount for dashed hope
principles.paraphrase_en: On an application for review of sentence where the respondent is near their release date, the court may grant a further discount to account for the 'dashed hope of pending release'.
When ordering sentences for multiple charges to run concurrently, the overall sentence must still properly reflect the seriousness and any aggravating circumstances of the individual charges.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Wong Chi Fung that failing to consider a relevant aggravating factor in sentencing constitutes an error in principle.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG WAI CHUN
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: The starting point for sentencing in dangerous driving cases is an immediate custodial sentence. The sentence length is determined by assessing the offender's culpability and the harm caused, with the maximum penalty being 7 years' imprisonment on indictment. Alternative sentencing options are only available in exceptional circumstances.

principles.principle_label: Limited weight of good character in dangerous driving
principles.paraphrase_en: In dangerous driving cases where the focus is on the culpability of the driving, a defendant's clear criminal record and general good character carry minimal weight in sentencing.

principles.principle_label: Dominant sentencing principles for dangerous driving
principles.paraphrase_en: The primary sentencing consideration for dangerous driving offences is general deterrence, with the principles of retribution and denunciation also being relevant factors.

When sentencing for multiple offences that are not concomitant (not arising from the same act or transaction), the sentences should not be made wholly concurrent. The court should impose partially concurrent sentences while considering the totality principle.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Chan Chi Chiu Louis regarding the limited weight of good character and the dominant sentencing principles in dangerous driving cases.

candidates.case_name: LAI KWOK KEUNG v. LEUNG WAN MING AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Wong J

principles.paraphrase_en: When the defense objects to the admissibility of evidence, such as a confession, the court must conduct a voir dire (a trial within a trial) to determine whether the evidence should be admitted.

In a trial for trafficking in a dangerous drug, if the jury is not sure the defendant is guilty of trafficking, the judge should direct them to consider the alternative verdict of simple possession, provided the evidence supports it. Failure to give this direction is an error.

A public body or inferior tribunal acts without or in excess of jurisdiction if it makes a finding of fact that is unsupported by any evidence or is based on a view of the evidence that no reasonable decision-maker could hold.

A decision is procedurally improper if it violates the rules of natural justice or fails to comply with the procedural rules set out in the enabling legislation. This includes situations where the decision-maker is biased or where a fair-minded observer would perceive a real possibility of bias.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG ZHI
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Special direction requirement for disputed admissions
principles.paraphrase_en: The requirement to give a special direction to the jury regarding the reliability of a defendant's admission (such as Specimen Direction 39.1) is not contingent on whether a formal voir dire hearing was conducted on the issue of voluntariness.

principles.principle_label: Trigger for special direction on admissions
principles.paraphrase_en: A special direction to disregard admissions must be given if evidence emerges during the trial that suggests the admissions may have been obtained through oppressive conduct or by means likely to render them unreliable, even if such issues were not formally raised at the trial's outset.

relationships.description: The judgment references and applies the guidance contained in Specimen Direction 39.1 regarding jury directions on disputed admissions.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ALI HAIDAR
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof in criminal cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must prove the defendant's guilt beyond reasonable doubt; the jury must convict only if they are sure of guilt and acquit if they are not sure.

The defendant is entitled to an acquittal if the defence put forward is or may be correct, or if the evidence gives rise to a reasonable doubt, even if the jury does not positively believe the defence evidence.

principles.principle_label: Application of standard to all defence issues
principles.paraphrase_en: The direction on the standard of proof applies equally to all aspects of the defence, including issues of oppression and fairness, and the reliability of evidence such as video-recorded interviews.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HERNANDEZ BORJAS RAUL ORLANDO
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Before admitting expert opinion evidence, the court must first determine if the subject matter is outside ordinary experience and requires specialized knowledge, and then assess whether the facts and assumptions underlying the opinion are themselves admissible and sound.

An expert witness may rely on a body of work by other specialists in the same field as an exception to the hearsay rule when forming their opinion, provided this forms the basis of their specialized knowledge.

Psychological validity tests designed to detect 'faking good' or 'faking bad' are not relevant where the defendant has no identifiable mental disorder or condition that such tests are intended to assess.

An expert opinion must be supported by identifiable evidence within the expert's analysis (such as specific personality profiles) and cannot be based solely on the defendant's assertions which the expert has accepted without independent verification.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. IP WAI HUNG
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant is not entitled to an additional sentencing discount beyond the standard one-third discount merely because the prosecution's case relies solely on the defendant's own confession.

When offenses are committed in different locations, involve different victims, and occur on different dates, consecutive sentences should generally be imposed, subject to the overall totality principle.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Ma Ming that a sole confession does not warrant an extra sentencing discount.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MUHAMMAD, ASHIQ
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LO PING KEUNG
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who makes a frank confession that provides the sole evidence supporting the charge is not entitled to an additional sentencing discount beyond the standard one-third discount for a guilty plea.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in HKSAR v Ma Ming regarding the limitation on sentencing discounts for confessions.

candidates.case_name: SANG CHEOL WOO v. YOO SHIN CHOI (naturalized name CHARLES C. SPACKMAN)
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Court

principles.principle_label: Enforcement of foreign judgments - finality requirement
principles.paraphrase_en: A foreign judgment must be final and conclusive to be enforceable in Hong Kong, subject to exceptions where the foreign proceedings violate Hong Kong notions of substantial justice or public policy.
The public policy exception preventing enforcement of a foreign judgment is narrow and must be applied restrictively.
For the application of CA760, it is sufficient that two or more persons are held jointly liable for a tortious act; it is not necessary that all defendants in the original action are held liable.

A defendant having taken all available appellate steps within a foreign legal system, even if unsuccessful, does not inherently render the proceedings unfair, as final appellate courts often have limited power to review factual findings.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that final and conclusive foreign judgments are enforceable subject to exceptions like natural justice.
The judgment cites this case alongside others as authority for the enforcement principles regarding foreign judgments.
The judgment cites this case for the principles governing the enforcement of foreign judgments and the public policy exception.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG HO LUN
judges: Before: HH Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may accept a defendant's confession as evidence for an offense that would otherwise be difficult for the prosecution to prove.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Wong Tik On regarding the acceptance of confessions for offenses difficult to prove.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YEUNG KWOK FAI, ANDY
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.paraphrase_en: For an unsophisticated and opportunistic theft offence, the appropriate sentencing starting point before considering aggravating features is 12 months' imprisonment.

principles.principle_label: Starting point for mobile phone theft
principles.paraphrase_en: A sentencing starting point of 15 months' imprisonment is appropriate for the theft of a mobile phone.

principles.principle_label: Discount for confession as sole evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who makes a frank confession that provides the only evidence supporting the charge is not entitled to a sentencing discount beyond the standard one-third discount for a guilty plea.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Wong Kang Sun that 12 months is the starting point for unsophisticated, opportunistic theft.
The judgment applies the sentencing starting point of 15 months for mobile phone theft established in Liu Lin Feng.
The judgment cites Ngo Van Huy as another case applying a 15-month starting point for mobile phone theft.
The judgment applies the principle from Ma Ming that a confession providing the sole evidence for a charge does not warrant an extra discount beyond one-third.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ASIF AHFAQ MEHMOOD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Campbell‑Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The hearsay rule exception established in R v Myers allows for the admission of a third party's out-of-court confession that is against that party's own penal interest, provided it contains admissions that are relevant to exculpating the defendant.

principles.principle_label: Requirement for clear admissions against interest
principles.paraphrase_en: For the Myers hearsay exception to be engaged, the third-party statement must contain clear and positive admissions that are against the declarant's own penal interest and are capable of assisting the defendant's case.

principles.principle_label: Exclusion of ambiguous or non-incriminatory statements
principles.paraphrase_en: Statements by a third party that are ambiguous, non-committal (such as 'I am not clear'), or that do not contain positive admissions against the declarant's interest or directly assist the defendant, do not satisfy the requirements for the hearsay exception.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the well-established hearsay rule exception from Myers for admitting third-party confessions against penal interest.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Ho Kin Hang Kenneth
relationships.description: The judgment follows the application of the Myers principles as previously applied in this Hong Kong case.
The judgment cites this case as another local authority where the Myers principles have been followed.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI CHEUK WAH
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and Anthea Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of confessions from controlled deliveries
principles.paraphrase_en: A confession or admission obtained during a controlled delivery operation is not automatically inadmissible. The prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that it was made voluntarily and not as a result of oppression, threats, or inducements.

A court has a residual discretion to exclude otherwise admissible evidence if its prejudicial effect outweighs its probative value, or if the evidence is so unreliable that a properly directed tribunal could not safely convict upon it.

principles.principle_label: Voluntariness of participation in controlled delivery
principles.paraphrase_en: For a defendant's participation in a controlled delivery to be considered voluntary, they should be advised that they are free not to participate and that their conduct during the operation may be used as evidence against them.

principles.principle_label: Fairness in using evidence from instructed actions
principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant acts under the instructions of law enforcement during an operation (like a controlled delivery), the fairness of relying on evidence of those instructed actions must be carefully assessed by the court.

relationships.description: The present judgment clarifies that Choi Chi Tak did not establish a principle that evidence from a controlled delivery is inadmissible per se, but rather addressed the fairness of relying on evidence from instructed actions.

The judgment references the ruling in Yano Hiroki regarding the need to advise a defendant of their freedom not to participate in a controlled delivery and the potential evidential use of their conduct.
The judgment cites Payne as an illustration of how a court considers breaches of procedural rules when exercising its discretion to exclude a confession.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v LEE KIN YIP
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for possession of offensive weapon
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for possession of an offensive weapon, a court may impose a lower sentence if the weapon is a relatively small domestic knife and there is a possibility it was intended for self-defense, especially if the defendant has no prior custodial sentence.

principles.principle_label: Discount for confession providing sole evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant is not entitled to an additional sentencing discount beyond the standard one-third discount merely because their frank confession constitutes the only evidence supporting the charge.

relationships.description: The judgment references the case for its sentencing approach regarding possession of a small domestic knife where self-defense is a possibility.
The judgment adopts the Court of Appeal's disapproval of granting an extra sentencing discount when a confession is the sole evidence for the charge.

candidates.case_name: YAU LAI WAH v. WONG KAN YU
judges: Before : Hon Kwan VP, Cheung and Chu JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in civil cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In civil cases, a judge must decide whether a fact is more likely than not to have occurred. If the judge finds it more probable than not, it is treated as having occurred; if not, it is treated as not having occurred. The judge cannot remain undecided and must rule for one party, though the burden of proof may resolve the issue if the party bearing it fails to satisfy the judge.

principles.principle_label: No adverse inference from failure to give oral evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a party's case is found to be inherently improbable, the court is not required to draw an adverse inference from the opposing party's failure to give oral evidence, even if that party had filed a signed witness statement.

principles.principle_label: Rejection of presumption of loan from payment to stranger
principles.paraphrase_en: The presumption of an implied obligation to repay arising from a payment made to a stranger, as established in Seldon v Davidson, is not good law and should not be followed.

relationships.description: The judgment states that Seldon v Davidson was wrongly decided and should not be followed, rejecting its presumption of an implied obligation to repay from a payment to a stranger.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU KA HO
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee J

principles.paraphrase_en: Whether there has been undue delay in prosecution is determined on a case-specific basis, considering the nature and scale of the investigation required.
After determining individual sentences for each charge, the court must consider the overall sentence by applying the totality principle.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the totality principle as established in HKSAR v Chiu Ping Kei when determining the overall sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG SIU LEUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow

candidates.case_name: XIE JING FENG v. HONG KONG SAR GOVERNMENT
judges: Before: Hon Lam VP, Barma JA and G Lam J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Under section 10(6) of the Fugitive Offenders Ordinance, the documents required to be produced and authenticated before the committal court are the 'supporting documents' specified in that provision, and these do not include the formal request for surrender transmitted through diplomatic channels.
The legal authority for a committal court to commit a person for surrender derives from the authority to proceed issued by the Chief Executive, not from the request for surrender itself.

principles.principle_label: No duty to produce request to court
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no legal requirement for the formal request for surrender to be produced to the committal court as part of the extradition proceedings.

Under section 10(6)(b)(iii) of the Fugitive Offenders Ordinance, the committal court need only be satisfied that the evidence presented by the requesting state would establish a prima facie case according to Hong Kong law, not the law of the requesting state.
An extradition hearing is not a trial and should not be conducted as one; the full trial will take place in the requesting country according to its laws.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows Chuen Sau‑kam in holding that the applicant has no right to insist on formal production of the extradition request to the committal court.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Governor of Pentonville Prison, ex parte Budlong
relationships.description: The judgment cites Budlong, as referenced in Chuen Sau‑kam, regarding the documents required before a magistrate in extradition proceedings.
The judgment applies the principle from Thongchai that the general prosecution duty of disclosure does not apply to requesting states in extradition proceedings.
The judgment cites Osman's application as additional authority that disclosure duties do not apply in extradition proceedings.
The judgment applies Schmidt's principle that an extradition hearing is not a trial and should not be turned into one.

relationships.target_case_name: Yat Tung Investment Co. Ltd. v. Dao Heng Bank Ltd.
relationships.description: The judgment cites Yat Tung for the wider abuse of process principle applicable to habeas corpus proceedings.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE CHEUK HO
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An employee commits theft when they dishonestly appropriate property belonging to their employer with the intention of permanently depriving the employer of it, which can be established through actions such as falsifying records and transferring funds to personal accounts.
A voluntary confession by the defendant to the victim regarding the commission of the offense can constitute strong evidence supporting a guilty plea.

The specific methods used to carry out theft, such as falsifying instructions, inflating salaries, and fraudulently inducing cheque endorsements, are relevant factual particulars that establish the means of appropriation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SIU HIU FUNG
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Admissions as insufficient proof of knowledge
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's admission of ownership of certain items cannot, by itself, prove knowledge of the specific dangerous drugs in a separate parcel, nor can it establish a propensity for drug trafficking, nor can it prove possession of a different quantity of drugs in a separate count.

If the court is sure a defendant made a verbal admission and confirmed it in a post-recorded statement, it must then assess whether those admissions were or may have been made as a result of threats, inducements, misconduct, or promises that would render reliance on them unsafe.

candidates.case_name: CHEN JINHUI v. HUANG YUEXIA, the person appointed to represent the estate of WONG KAM SAN, Deceased AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Chu VP and G Lam and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Admission of new evidence on appeal - Noble v Owens principle
principles.paraphrase_en: New evidence may be admitted on appeal if it demonstrates that evidence given at trial was false, even if the evidence could have been obtained with reasonable diligence before trial, provided it is decisive for the appeal.

principles.principle_label: Admission of new evidence on appeal - Ladd v Marshall criteria
principles.paraphrase_en: New evidence may be admitted on appeal under the Ladd v Marshall principles if it: (1) could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence for use at trial; (2) would probably have an important influence on the result; and (3) is apparently credible.

principles.principle_label: Admission of post-trial events as new evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence of matters that occurred after the trial may be admitted as new evidence on appeal if relevant to the issues in the appeal.

Admissions by trial witnesses that they gave false evidence at trial may constitute admissible new evidence on appeal, particularly when such admissions undermine the factual basis of the trial judgment.

principles.principle_label: Duty of full disclosure when seeking to adduce new evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: When applying to adduce new evidence on appeal, parties must not selectively present only favorable portions of the evidence but should disclose relevant contradictory evidence that provides context to the new evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment references Noble v Owens as one legal basis for admitting new evidence that shows trial evidence was false.
The judgment cites Ladd v Marshall as an alternative traditional test for admitting new evidence on appeal.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM HOI KIT
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A trial judge's summing-up to the jury must be assessed and understood in its entirety, not by isolating individual parts.

In assessing whether a sentence for drug trafficking is manifestly excessive, the court must consider the totality of relevant factors including the quantity of narcotics, the offender's role and culpability, and any aggravating features.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Tam King Hon that a summing-up must be read as a whole.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN CHIN CHING
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant makes an out-of-court statement containing both incriminating and exculpatory parts (a mixed statement), both parts are admissible as evidence of the truth of their contents.

principles.principle_label: Weight of exculpatory parts in mixed statements
principles.paraphrase_en: A fact-finder may attach less weight to the exculpatory parts of a mixed statement, but cannot automatically disregard them or attach no weight to them.

principles.principle_label: Direction on defendant's credibility when relying on interview
principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant does not give evidence but relies on an exculpatory video-recorded interview, the trial judge must direct the jury (or themselves) not only on the defendant's lower propensity to commit crimes but also on the defendant's higher credibility.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof and defence version
principles.paraphrase_en: In a criminal trial, the judge must not simply choose between the prosecution and defence versions. The defendant must be acquitted if the defence version might reasonably be true.

relationships.description: The judgment cites R v Sharp for the principle that both parts of a mixed statement are evidence of the truth of their contents.
The judgment cites this case for the principle that exculpatory parts of a mixed statement cannot automatically be given no weight.

The judgment applies the principle from this case that a material irregularity occurs if the judge fails to give a direction on the defendant's higher credibility when relying on an exculpatory interview.
The judgment follows the established principle from this case that the defendant must be acquitted if the defence version might be true.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BUGTAQUEN MERLYN QUILA
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: Theft under section 9 of the Theft Ordinance requires the dishonest appropriation of property belonging to another with the intention of permanently depriving the other of it.

A defendant's admission to the victim regarding the commission of the offense, including details of the stolen items and their disposal, can form a sufficient basis for conviction when combined with other evidence.

principles.principle_label: Conviction on plea and agreed facts
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may be properly convicted of an offense when they plead guilty and agree to an amended summary of facts that establishes all elements of the crime.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM WING CHUEN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Kathie Cheung

principles.principle_label: Consecutive vs concurrent sentencing for separate offences
principles.paraphrase_en: Separate and distinct offences should, in principle, receive consecutive sentences. However, the court may order concurrent sentences when the offences are closely connected in time and location, applying the totality principle to ensure the total sentence properly reflects the overall criminality.

The totality principle requires the court to consider whether the aggregate sentence for multiple offences is just and appropriate in reflecting the defendant's overall criminal conduct, even when individual sentences for separate offences might technically be consecutive.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHALIQ SALMAN (also known as KHALIA SALMAN)
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who intends to plead guilty after trial dates are fixed but before the first day of trial is entitled to a sentencing discount ranging from 20% to 25%.
A defendant who pleads guilty at the earliest opportunity should receive the customary one-third discount from the appropriate sentencing starting point.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the discount range established in Ngo Van Nam for guilty pleas entered after trial dates are fixed but before trial begins.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. “M”
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When evaluating a defendant's assistance to the authorities for sentencing purposes, the assessment should be based on whether the information provided was 'of practical use', not limited to whether it produced a tangible result like an arrest or prosecution.

The prosecutor plays an important role in the procedure for assessing a defendant's assistance, and the involvement of a senior independent officer reviewing the investigation officer's assessment lends credibility to the process and reduces the risk of disputes.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Kilima regarding the prosecutor's role and procedures for assessing a defendant's assistance to authorities.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MUHAMMAD BILAL
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge L C Cheng

principles.principle_label: Analogy to forged credit card sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for offences involving counterfeit banknotes, the court may draw an analogy to the sentencing approach for tendering forged credit cards, as both involve furthering the aims of a larger criminal syndicate and causing financial loss, though victims of counterfeit notes have less recourse for recovery.

When sentencing an offender for multiple offences committed on different dates against multiple victims, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is appropriate and not excessive.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. THONG HIU KIN
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YUN YU
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge Wong Sze-lai, Lily in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for multiple drug types
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for offences involving multiple types of dangerous drugs, the court should adopt a combined approach rather than an individual approach, considering the quantity and sentencing guidelines for each drug in relation to the base drug.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing adjustment for secondary drug quantity
principles.paraphrase_en: The increase to the sentencing starting point for a secondary drug type should be determined by judicial discretion, considering the quantity of that drug, its sentencing guidelines, and comparing them with the base drug's guidelines.

principles.principle_label: Application of sentencing tests in drug cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The court should examine the results of the absurdity test, conversion test, and ratio test when determining the appropriate sentence for offences involving multiple dangerous drugs.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the combined sentencing approach and methodology established in Islam SM Majharul for cases involving multiple drug types.
The court follows the guidance from paragraph 27 of Islam SM Majharul regarding the exercise of sentencing discretion when dealing with multiple drugs.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU KA YIU
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge D Yau in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSUI KING BUN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Jason Wan

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offence of fraud under section 16A of the Theft Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant, by deception, dishonestly obtained property belonging to another with the intent to permanently deprive.

The offence of theft under section 9 of the Theft Ordinance is established by proving the defendant dishonestly appropriated property belonging to another with the intention of permanently depriving the other of it.
An admission made by a defendant under caution, acknowledging the commission of the offence and the dishonest intent, is admissible as evidence against them.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SO MAN HO
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offences arising from the same incident, the court should apply the totality principle to determine a global starting point that reflects the overall criminality, rather than simply adding individual sentences.
A timely guilty plea entitles the defendant to a reduction in sentence from the starting point determined by the court.
A defendant's previous convictions may justify an enhancement (increase) to the sentence that would otherwise be imposed.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites Ma Ming for the principle that a court may make minor sentence adjustments when a defendant's frank confession provides the only evidence supporting a charge.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM WAI FUNG
judges: Before: Hon M Poon J

principles.paraphrase_en: For trafficking in cocaine between 200 and 400 grammes, the established sentencing tariff is between 12 and 15 years' imprisonment.
In sentencing for drug trafficking offences, the court affords very little credit to mitigating factors other than a defendant's guilty plea.
The primary determinant of sentence in drug trafficking cases is the weight of the narcotics involved.
A defendant who pleads guilty is entitled to a full one-third discount from the starting sentence.

candidates.case_name: CHEN JINHUI v. WONG KAM SAN, by his guardian ad litem, HUANG YUEXIA AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon G Lam and Chow JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judgment and its factual findings from earlier proceedings, whether civil or criminal, are generally inadmissible as evidence in subsequent proceedings, unless the party against whom the finding is sought to be used is bound by it due to estoppel per rem judicatum.

principles.principle_label: Exception to Hollington principle for factual summaries
principles.paraphrase_en: The Hollington principle may not exclude parts of a prior judgment that contain summaries of factual evidence (such as witness testimony) presented in the earlier proceedings, provided that evidence relates to issues in the current proceedings.

principles.principle_label: Hearsay rule abolition in civil proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: The exclusionary rule against hearsay evidence does not apply in civil proceedings, as it has been abolished.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of new evidence not pre-decided by leave grant
principles.paraphrase_en: Granting leave to adduce an item as new evidence does not automatically determine or preclude subsequent challenges to its admissibility; the issue of admissibility remains open for argument.

relationships.target_case_name: Capital Century Textile Company Limited v Li Dianxiao
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from Capital Century regarding the Hollington principle and the abolition of the hearsay rule in civil proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Zillion Global Ltd v UBS AG
relationships.description: The judgment cites Zillion Global Ltd as further authority supporting the abolition of the hearsay rule in civil proceedings.
The judgment references Ladd v Marshall in the context of discussing the categorization and admissibility of new evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAW NGO HIN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge L T Pang in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOI YE IN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SINGH POPINDER AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Anthea Pang JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Leave requirement for mixed law and fact grounds
principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal ground that involves a challenge to a trial judge's ruling on the admissibility of evidence or a 'no case to answer' submission is considered a ground of mixed law and fact, not pure law. Therefore, leave to appeal is required before such a ground can be argued.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. OKAFOR CHRISTIAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A jury may be invited to compare a defendant with a person shown in a photographic image (such as CCTV footage) and conclude they are the same person, based on observable attributes like clothing, build, and other visual characteristics.

There are specific circumstances in which, subject to judicial discretion and appropriate directions, a jury may be invited to conclude the defendant committed the offence based on a photographic image from the crime scene.
When a jury compares a defendant with a person in a security photograph, they are not acting as experts but are performing an ordinary fact-finding task using their own observation.

principles.principle_label: No invariable rule for specific direction
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no absolute rule that a jury must always be given a specific direction on the dangers of relying on photographic comparison evidence; the need for such a direction depends on the circumstances of the case.

principles.principle_label: Warning not required for non-sole/non-witness evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A specific 'resemblance evidence' warning is not required where the CCTV evidence is not the sole basis for identification and does not involve a witness giving resemblance evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that there are circumstances where a jury may be invited to identify a defendant from a crime scene photograph.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that it is permissible for a jury to compare a defendant with a person in a security photograph.
The judgment applies the holding that there is no invariable rule requiring a specific direction on the dangers of photographic comparison evidence.
The judgment follows this case in endorsing the approach in Downey as correct regarding directions on photographic evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YIP SIN WAH
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Woodcock in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal from a magistracy is conducted as a rehearing on the evidence, but the appellate court will only overturn the magistrate's findings of fact or credibility assessments if those findings are plainly wrong.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of circumstantial evidence for identification
principles.paraphrase_en: Circumstantial evidence surrounding communications (like texts and images) sent before and after a material event can be admissible to assist in evaluating the reliability of identification testimony.

principles.principle_label: Inference from combined direct and circumstantial evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A court is entitled to draw an irresistible inference of identity by combining credible direct identification evidence (like voice recognition) with admissible circumstantial evidence (like related messages and call content).

principles.principle_label: Avoidance of confirmation bias in evidence evaluation
principles.paraphrase_en: Using circumstantial evidence (like message content showing a sender's emotional state) to support the credibility of identification evidence does not constitute improper confirmation bias, provided the findings are based on the evidence itself and not on unsupported assumptions about a party's likely motives.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SO CHIN CHING AND OTHERS
judges: Before: HH Judge Kathie Cheung

principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence from an identification parade is admissible if the prosecution proves beyond reasonable doubt that the parade was conducted in a fair and impartial manner, and there are no discretionary grounds for exclusion.

A witness's evidence can be accepted as reliable and credible for certain aspects (e.g., the occurrence of an attack and injuries) while being rejected for other aspects (e.g., identification of specific individuals), with weight attached accordingly.

Identification evidence is not reliable if it is based solely on a person's build without facial features, especially when other available evidence contradicts a key identifying characteristic (like a tattoo) mentioned by the witness.

Witnesses whose evidence is not challenged or criticized by the defence, and who give clear and straightforward testimony, are to be found credible and reliable, with full weight attached to their evidence.

principles.principle_label: Voluntariness and fairness of identification parade
principles.paraphrase_en: An identification parade is valid if the suspect participates voluntarily, the officer conducting it is credible, and any procedural instructions are treated as a guide to ensure the witness is certain before making an identification, rather than as mandatory rules.

relationships.description: The judgment references the principles from this case regarding when a jury may conclude a defendant committed an offence based on a photographic image.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SINGH POPINDER AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge D Yau in Court

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of identification after viewing photos
principles.paraphrase_en: The viewing of photographs of suspects by a witness prior to an identification parade does not automatically render subsequent identification evidence inadmissible. The court must assess the individual circumstances of the case, including the risk of a 'displacement effect' where the witness identifies the person from the photograph rather than from memory of the event.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of witness reliability for prior acquaintance
principles.paraphrase_en: A key factor in assessing the reliability of identification evidence is determining the truthfulness of a witness's claim of prior acquaintance with the defendants. If the witness genuinely knew the defendants before the incident, their ability to identify them may be based on pre-existing memory rather than on subsequently viewed images.

The fairness of an identification parade's procedure is assessed by examining how it was conducted and the suspect's reactions and choices during the process. Evidence from a fairly conducted parade is admissible for the jury's consideration.

principles.principle_label: Prosecution burden for admissibility of contested evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution bears the burden of proving beyond reasonable doubt that contested identification evidence is sufficiently reliable to be placed before the jury for their consideration on the general issue.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the legal principles from R v Phillips concerning the admissibility of identification evidence following a witness's viewing of photographs.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ASHRIF MEHMOOD
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Pang & Zervos JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: No case to answer ruling without reasons
principles.paraphrase_en: At the stage of a 'no case to answer' submission, it is neither necessary nor desirable for a judge to provide detailed reasons for the ruling.

principles.principle_label: Turnbull principle on poor identification evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: When the quality of identification evidence is poor, the judge should withdraw the case from the jury unless there is other evidence that supports the correctness of the identification.

principles.principle_label: Prosecution case at its highest for jury consideration
principles.paraphrase_en: In a 'no case to answer' assessment, the judge must consider if, putting the prosecution case at its highest, a reasonable jury could properly convict on the evidence.

principles.principle_label: Judge's role in assessing evidence for jury withdrawal
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge should only withdraw a case from the jury if the evidence is of such a tenuous character that it cannot safely support a conviction.

principles.principle_label: Appellate review of no case ruling
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court, in reviewing a 'no case to answer' ruling, will assess whether the case was properly left to the jury based on the evidence available at the half-time stage.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the Turnbull principle concerning the handling of poor quality identification evidence.
The judgment cites the Hong Kong Archbold 2019 edition as a reference source for legal principles.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ARIF AQIB AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Anthea Pang JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's participation in a joint enterprise can be established through inference from proven facts, even in the absence of direct evidence of their specific role or actions.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for joint enterprise
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that a defendant was part of a joint enterprise, which is a very high standard of proof requiring careful determination.
Allowing one's car to be used by others to travel to the scene of an attack can constitute assistance in a joint enterprise, sufficient for criminal liability.

principles.principle_label: Role of jury in assessing evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: It is for the jury to decide, based on the facts they find proven, whether a defendant's presence amounted to mere presence or intentional participation in a joint enterprise.
Apparent inconsistencies in evidence, such as footage from a different location, can properly be left for the jury to resolve if covered in the judge's summing-up.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOY KAM KUEN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: In a judge-alone trial, the judge is not required to give an explicit Turnbull warning to themselves, provided the judgment demonstrates they were mindful of and applied the Turnbull considerations regarding the reliability of identification evidence.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of defence challenges to identification
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the defence raises issues that could reasonably undermine the reliability of identification evidence, the court must consider those issues when deciding whether to rely on that evidence.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of time taken for identification
principles.paraphrase_en: The time a witness takes to make an identification at a properly conducted parade, even if several minutes, is not in itself unusual or indicative of a problem, provided the parade comprises participants of similar appearance.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the key principles for addressing identification evidence established in Turnbull.
The judgment applies the holding that a judge-alone trial does not require an explicit Turnbull self-warning if the judge shows mindfulness of Turnbull considerations.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SIDHU, BALVEER SINGH
judges: Before: DHCJ E Lee in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SINGH BALBIR
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Francis Cheng

principles.paraphrase_en: Identification evidence must be reliable for a court to safely rely on it; if found unreliable, it cannot form a safe basis for conviction.
Lies told by a defendant regarding an alibi do not necessarily amount to a serious fabrication intended to deceive the court, and their significance must be assessed.
The prosecution must prove its case against a defendant beyond reasonable doubt for a conviction to be sustained.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ALE MAGAR KRISHPOO AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Alice Chan

principles.paraphrase_en: An eyewitness identification made under chaotic and confusing circumstances, where the witness was unable to pay sufficient attention to the perpetrator's features, may be considered unreliable and insufficient to support a conviction without corroborating evidence.

principles.principle_label: Insufficiency of suspicious circumstances for common intention
principles.paraphrase_en: Mere presence in suspicious circumstances, such as being masked at the scene of an attack, is insufficient by itself to establish a common intention to commit the offense; a shared intention must be positively inferred.

principles.principle_label: Unsafe reliance on subsequent identification parade
principles.paraphrase_en: If the initial identification of a defendant during the offense is potentially inaccurate, a subsequent identification in a parade cannot be relied upon, as the witness may be selecting based on a prior belief rather than genuine recognition.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. VERGNES CLEMENT VINCENT CHRISTOPHE
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo in Court

candidates.case_name: WONG HON LUNG V. CHIEF EXECUTIVE OF HKSAR
judges: Before: Hon Chow JA (sitting as an additional judge of the Court of First Instance) in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: In considering an application for leave to apply for judicial review, the judge is not required to extensively examine the applicant's materials to identify or formulate potentially arguable grounds for judicial review.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ALI HAIDER
judges: Before: The Honourable Madam Justice Toh in Court

candidates.case_name: RE AMMAR ABDLMOEEN HUSSEIN IBRAHIM
judges: Before: Hon Cheung and Au JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Judicial review standard for administrative decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: A court will not interfere with the factual findings of an administrative decision-maker (like the Director or a Board) unless an error of law or procedural unfairness is demonstrated.

principles.principle_label: Extension of time for appeal requirement
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for an extension of time to appeal must demonstrate a realistic prospect of success in the intended appeal.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. GURUNG ASHIS AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: For an identification parade to be admissible, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that it was conducted fairly. A key aspect of fairness is ensuring the lineup consists of individuals who share the suspect's general physical characteristics, including ethnicity or race, to avoid undue suggestion.

A court can assess the fairness of an identification parade by examining the personal particulars of the actors in the lineup, such as their surnames, to determine if they reasonably share the suspect's ethnic or racial characteristics.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for admissibility of evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: When the admissibility of evidence, such as that from an identification parade, is challenged, the prosecution bears the burden of proving its admissibility beyond a reasonable doubt.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KRISHAN, KUMAR AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: The Honourable Madam Justice Toh in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a witness, especially a police officer whose credibility is central to the case, is found to have told a blatant lie under oath in court, this significantly undermines the overall credibility and reliability of their entire testimony.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSE KI CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court

principles.principle_label: Chain of custody requirement for exhibits
