principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must prove that the exhibits examined by an expert are the same items seized from the defendant. A failure to maintain this chain of custody can impair the defendant's ability to mount a full defense.

principles.principle_label: Standard for appellate review of factual findings
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not interfere with a magistrate's factual findings and acceptance of evidence, such as expert testimony, unless they are demonstrably wrong or unreasonable.

principles.principle_label: Proof of intent for possessing offensive weapon
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offense of possessing an offensive weapon, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant possessed the item with the plain intention of using it to injure another person.

relationships.description: The present judgment notes that the Court of Appeal in Cheung Wai discussed and criticized the conclusion in Chu Kam To but did not declare its legal principle wrong.
The judgment references the legal principle from Chu Kam To regarding the consequences of missing exhibits, which was later discussed in HKSAR v Cheung Wai.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SULDAAN, YUSUF SULDAAN
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal under section 113 of the Magistrates Ordinance is conducted as a rehearing of the original evidence, with the appellate court having the power to admit further evidence under its statutory authority.
In a rehearing, the appellate court must independently assess the evidence and determine whether it proves the essential elements of the offence to the required standard of proof.

principles.principle_label: Appellate court's independent conclusion on evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The appellate court is both entitled and obligated to reach its own conclusions on contested issues of fact and law based on its review of the evidence, without being limited to determining whether the trial court's conclusion was open to it.

relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle established in Chou Shih Bin regarding the nature of an appeal under section 113 of the Magistrates Ordinance as a rehearing.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Ip Chin Kei & Others
relationships.description: The judgment applies the standard from HKSAR v Ip Chin Kei regarding the appellate court's duty to independently assess evidence on rehearing.

relationships.target_case_name: Histollo Pty Ltd v Director-General National Parks & Wildlife
relationships.description: The judgment cites the reasoning of James J in Histollo to support the appellate court's authority to reach independent conclusions on evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. DASHPAL SINGH
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.paraphrase_en: A court can find that a defendant committed an offence based on a photographic image from the crime scene if the image is sufficiently clear to allow a comparison with the defendant in the dock, subject to appropriate judicial directions.
The prosecution must prove the identity of the offender beyond reasonable doubt, and the court must be satisfied so that it is sure based on all the evidence and circumstances of the identification.

principles.principle_label: Inference of identity from distinctive clothing
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may draw the inference that the same person appears in multiple instances of CCTV footage if the individual is seen wearing the same distinctive combination of clothing items within a short time frame, making it inherently improbable that two different persons would share the same attire.

principles.principle_label: Limits of similarity in clothing evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: Where clothing seen in CCTV footage is merely similar to an item found in a defendant's possession, but specific identifying features (like a zipper) cannot be confirmed, the court cannot conclude they are the same item.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from that case regarding when a jury (or judge) can base a finding of identity on a photographic image.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ARIF AQIB
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence from an identification parade may be excluded if the decision to hold the parade was fundamentally unfair, such as where there was no prior evidence linking the suspect to the offense or the scene, rendering the suspect's inclusion in the parade unjustified from the outset.

An identification parade may be unfair if the 'Brief Facts' provided to the suspect are materially incomplete or misleading, particularly by omitting key contextual details (like the alleged role of a vehicle) that the suspect would need to understand the full basis of the identification attempt.

The reliability of an identification can be undermined if a witness's account becomes more detailed or changes only after participating in the parade, as this suggests the identification process itself may have contaminated or influenced the witness's memory.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v ABAD WILBERT MAGAN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge C H Li in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must prove all elements of the burglary charge beyond reasonable doubt; the court may acquit if the evidence does not establish the necessary elements, such as intent to commit an offense within the building.
A conviction requires the prosecution to prove all elements of the offense beyond reasonable doubt.
The court may find prosecution witnesses honest and truthful if they have tried their best to recount what they know.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SANJAY, KUMAR AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may properly find that a person was employed as an odd job worker if that is the only reasonable and irresistible inference that can be drawn from the proven facts.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEN JINGWEI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: HH Judge Casewell in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must prove the defendant's involvement in the alleged offense beyond a reasonable doubt. If the evidence of key witnesses is accepted, it should be capable of supporting a conviction on all charges.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of witness credibility and reliability
principles.paraphrase_en: The central issue in resolving charges often hinges on the credibility and reliability of witnesses. A realistic approach must be taken, recognizing that inconsistencies can exist even with truthful witnesses and that minor discrepancies not central to the main issues do not necessarily invalidate the evidence.

principles.principle_label: Factors negating proof beyond reasonable doubt
principles.paraphrase_en: A combination of factors, such as the possibility of a displacement effect in identification, a short observation period, and the witness being a co-conspirator, can mean the standard of proof beyond a reasonable doubt is not met, leading to an acquittal.

principles.principle_label: Defendant's right to refuse identification parade
principles.paraphrase_en: If defendants indicate an unwillingness to take part in in-person identification parades, the identification may proceed by way of photo identification.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KO KANG SUK AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: A fact-finder is permitted to independently compare video footage or photographs and determine whether the person depicted is the defendant.
The prosecution must prove every element of an offence against each defendant to the criminal standard of beyond reasonable doubt.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Tagao Saudee Abad that a fact-finder may perform its own identification exercise from video or photographic evidence.
The judgment applies the principle reaffirmed in Riasat-Ali, which itself followed the earlier Tagao Saudee Abad decision on identification from video evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAI TIN HO, ZAFAR AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge may answer a jury's question by providing a written reply after discussing the proposed answer with counsel in open court, without necessarily calling the jury back into court, provided this does not violate the principle of open and transparent justice.

A procedural irregularity in handling a jury question does not automatically constitute a material irregularity warranting appeal if the court can fully assess the un-redacted question and determine no miscarriage of justice occurred.
Even if a material irregularity occurred during trial, the appellate court may still apply the proviso to dismiss the appeal if satisfied that no miscarriage of justice actually resulted.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for armed robbery in private premises
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for a robbery in private premises involving multiple assailants acting in concert, pre-planning, use of weapons including firearms, and violence causing terror to a lone victim, the starting point must adequately reflect these serious aggravating features.

On an application for review of sentence, the appellate court will find a sentence manifestly inadequate and wrong in principle if the sentencing judge failed to properly account for significant aggravating factors that substantially increase the seriousness of the offence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Gorman as the leading authority on the extent of judicial disclosure required for jury communications after retirement.
The judgment applies the principle from R v Lamb that providing a written answer to a jury question is an acceptable alternative to calling them back to court.
The judgment cites Leung Wai Hung as a case that referenced the R v Lamb approach to jury question procedure.
The judgment distinguishes the present case from Lee Yi Choi because the full un-redacted jury question was available for assessment here.
The judgment cites Mo Kwong-sang for the appropriate sentencing starting point for a single-assailant robbery in private premises with violence and weapons.

candidates.case_name: RE HUANG GUOZHEN
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Jonathan Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When applying for substituted service of a bankruptcy petition based on evasion of service, the applicant must provide specific grounds for the claim of evasion and detail the efforts made to locate the debtor.
Proper service of an amended petition can, in principle, cure defective service of an original petition, provided the amended service itself is valid.
A ground for opposing a bankruptcy petition based on undue influence in the underlying debt is not suitable for summary determination and requires fuller examination.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NJIE EBOU
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge N Wong

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's act of running away upon seeing a police officer can constitute strong evidence of guilt, provided no innocent explanation for the flight exists. The reasonable inference is that the defendant sought to avoid discovery of illicit items in their possession.
A perceived shortfall in direct evidence can be addressed by considering the totality of highly indicative circumstantial matters, which together may support a finding of guilt.

relationships.description: The present judgment finds the discussion in this case useful regarding inferences that can be drawn from a defendant's conduct, such as fleeing from police.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. IP TUNG TAI
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

candidates.case_name: THE SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. TAM KIT-I
judges: Before: Hon Cheung and Chow JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A restraint order under the Organized and Serious Crimes Ordinance is a temporary measure to freeze assets pending the potential making of a confiscation order, aimed at preserving property value for potential future confiscation.
An application for a confiscation order is 'concluded' for the purposes of a restraint order when the court decides not to make the confiscation order sought.
For a confiscation order to be made against a person under the relevant provision, it must be established that the person has 'absconded' within the statutory meaning.
For a person to be found to have absconded to evade justice, proceedings must be instituted or imminent and within that person's knowledge at the time of departure.
A departure does not constitute absconding if it is neither sudden nor secret, and if the person was not aware of impending proceedings against them at the time of departure.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HA BUT YEE
judges: Before: Hon Macrae Acting CJHC, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Scope of proceeds of crime for confiscation
principles.paraphrase_en: For the purpose of making a confiscation order, the proceeds of crime are not limited to the specific transactions detailed in the charge to which the defendant pleaded guilty, but can encompass all benefits derived from the criminal conduct as defined in the admitted facts.

A defendant who pleads guilty and admits to a summary of facts is bound by the scope of the criminal conduct as described therein, and cannot subsequently argue that the confiscation order should be limited to a narrower subset of transactions.

candidates.case_name: THE SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. TAM KIT-I
judges: Before: Hon Cheung, G Lam and Chow JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must determine the correct interpretation of the statutory provisions governing when a person is considered to have absconded for the purposes of a confiscation application under the Organized and Serious Crimes Ordinance.

When a court is not satisfied that a person has absconded and therefore finds section 8(1)(a)(ii)(B) of the Ordinance inapplicable, leading to a decision not to make a confiscation order, a legal question arises regarding whether the application should be considered concluded and whether restraining orders should be discharged.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM CHUNG YIU CHARLES
judges: Before: The Honourable Mr. Justice D. Yau in Court

principles.principle_label: Determination of realisable amount under OSCO
principles.paraphrase_en: The realisable amount for a confiscation order under the Organized and Serious Crimes Ordinance is the total value of all realisable property held by the defendant at the time the order is made, unless reduced by obligations having priority as specified in the statute.

The phrase 'at any time' in the context of obligations having priority under the confiscation regime refers only to obligations that arose before the making of the confiscation order, not after, as the recoverable amount must be determined prior to the order's issuance.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof on defendant regarding property interests
principles.paraphrase_en: In confiscation proceedings, the defendant bears the burden of proving, with cogent evidence and on the balance of probabilities, the truth and accuracy of assertions regarding their interest in property or that property is not realisable.

principles.principle_label: Treatment of gifts and property under effective control
principles.paraphrase_en: Property held by a third party (such as a spouse) may still be considered realisable property if it constitutes a gift caught by the ordinance or is subject to the effective control of the defendant.

principles.principle_label: No general discretion to refuse confiscation order to avoid injustice
principles.paraphrase_en: The court does not possess a general discretionary power to refuse to make a confiscation order on the ground that its making would result in injustice.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes the situation from that in Lung Wai-hung, finding no comparable injustice to a victim company that would warrant not making a confiscation order.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ZHENG ZHI LONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge R Wong

principles.principle_label: Scope of realizable property under confiscation
principles.paraphrase_en: For the purposes of a confiscation order under the relevant ordinance, realizable property includes all assets held by the defendant, both within Hong Kong and overseas.

The court's determination in a confiscation application is confined to the specific property identified by the prosecution in that application, even if other property falls within the statutory definition of realizable property.

principles.principle_label: Avoidance of double counting in valuation
principles.paraphrase_en: Where there is a possibility that the value of an asset has already been accounted for through another source in the calculation of the defendant's benefit, the court should, as a matter of fairness to the defendant, exclude that asset to prevent double counting.

principles.principle_label: Determination of property held by defendant
principles.paraphrase_en: A court must make a factual finding, based on the evidence, as to whether specific assets constitute property held by the defendant for the purposes of a confiscation order.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG PUI TING HEIDI
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge A J Woodcock in Court

principles.principle_label: Determination of proceeds from specified offence
principles.paraphrase_en: The court determines the value of proceeds from a specified offence by examining the best available records of the defendant's net income from that offence, rather than relying on amounts from related money laundering charges which may be less accurate or comprehensive.

principles.principle_label: Burden and standard of proof in confiscation proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: In confiscation proceedings following conviction, the court must apply the applicable standard of proof when determining the amount of benefit received from the specified offence, and the prosecution bears the burden of proving that amount.
The court will not deduct amounts from a confiscation order unless the defense presents credible evidence supporting such deductions, considering the applicable standard of proof.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YIK SIU-HUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Anthea Pang J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court determines the amount of benefit received by a defendant from criminal conduct for the purpose of making a confiscation order, based on the evidence presented.
When making a confiscation order, the court must assess whether the defendant's realizable assets at a specified date are sufficient to cover the benefit amount.

candidates.case_name: WONG, CHI HUNG v. LO, WING PUN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Gary C C Lam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The terms of a contract are ascertained by an objective test, meaning a party can be bound if the other party actually and reasonably believed they had the requisite intention, even if subjectively they did not.

A contract that is unenforceable under its proper law will not be enforced by a Hong Kong court, irrespective of the place of performance or whether the foreign law is a penal law that would not be directly enforced.

Violation of foreign laws in the actual performance of a contract may render it unenforceable in Hong Kong courts, even if such violation was not initially required or intended, with the approach being flexible and considering the seriousness of the illegality.

principles.principle_label: No subversion principle in unjust enrichment
principles.paraphrase_en: Restitutionary remedies for unjust enrichment are not available where their effect would be to subvert the agreement made by the parties in a valid contract.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of foreign law to public policy
principles.paraphrase_en: Foreign law provisions, such as Article 157 of the PRC Civil Code concerning restitution after invalidity, can be relevant to whether granting a claim for unjust enrichment would be contrary to public policy, even if the agreement is not expressly governed by that foreign law.

relationships.target_case_name: Shanghai Tongji Science & Technology Industrial Co Ltd v Casil Clearing Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the 'no subversion principle' established in this case to argue that unjust enrichment cannot subvert a valid contract.
The judgment notes that counsel urged the adoption of its flexible, holistic approach to foreign illegality, and that it may provide guidance.

relationships.target_case_name: Lesnina H DOO v Wave Shipping Trade Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment is cited by counsel in an attempt to urge a flexible approach to the defence of foreign illegality.

relationships.target_case_name: Monat Investment Ltd v All Person(s) in Occupation of No 16 Ma Po Tsuen
relationships.description: The judgment is cited by counsel alongside Lesnina in support of adopting a flexible approach to foreign illegality.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. CAI KANG GANG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Wong J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Requirement for settled intention to prosecute
principles.paraphrase_en: For a restraint order to be justified under the relevant legislation, there must be a settled intention to commence criminal proceedings; a conditional intention dependent on obtaining further evidence is insufficient.

The powers to make a restraint order must be exercised with the primary objective of preserving realizable property so that its value can be made available to satisfy any confiscation order that may be made against the defendant.

principles.principle_label: Grounds for making restraint order - proceeds of crime
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may be satisfied to make a restraint order where it finds that the respondents received proceeds of fraud, and transactions in the relevant accounts bear the obvious hallmarks of money laundering activities.

principles.principle_label: Grounds for making restraint order - risk of dissipation
principles.paraphrase_en: A restraint order is justified where there is an obvious risk that the realizable property (such as funds in bank accounts) will be dissipated.

The court possesses the discretion to vary a restraint order, and a key factual consideration in exercising that discretion is whether an intervener can establish the bona fides of their claim (such as a judgment debt) to the restrained assets.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a settled intention to prosecute is required for a restraint order, not a conditional one.

relationships.target_case_name: International Associated Attorneys Ltd v Eurostock Energy Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment references this case, which was heavily relied upon by counsel, in the discussion concerning the court's discretion to vary a restraint order.

candidates.case_name: THE SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. TAM KIT-I
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Tang NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: The legal consequence of a judge's decision not to make a confiscation order based on a finding that the respondent had not 'absconded' under section 8(1)(a)(ii)(B) of OSCO must be determined, specifically whether this results in the application being 'concluded' under section 2(16A)(a) and thereby discharges the restraint order.

principles.principle_label: Nature of appeal against restraint order
principles.paraphrase_en: When a person against whom OSCO proceedings have been instituted appeals a judge's refusal to discharge a restraint order, the jurisdictional and procedural nature of that appeal (whether criminal or civil) must be determined.

candidates.case_name: WONG, CHI HUNG v. LO, WING PUN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Gary C C Lam in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The no-subversion principle, which prevents restitutionary claims from undermining contractual arrangements, does not automatically apply to bar a claim for restitution arising from a contract that is unenforceable due to foreign illegality. The court must examine the specific authorities rather than relying solely on textbook commentary.

principles.principle_label: Distinction between unenforceable and invalid contracts
principles.paraphrase_en: A contract that is unenforceable due to foreign illegality remains distinct from a contract that is invalid. The legal consequences, including the availability of restitutionary remedies, may differ based on this distinction.

In assessing the effect of foreign regulations, a court is entitled to make a factual finding that assets 'may' be confiscated under those regulations, as opposed to finding they 'must' be confiscated, based on evidence of how the regulations are generally enforced in practice.

A party challenging the trial judge's refusal to accept their expert evidence must demonstrate a reason why the opposing party's expert evidence should not be preferred; mere disagreement is insufficient.

relationships.description: The court expresses a conclusion that differs from the commentary on the no-subversion principle found in Chitty on Contracts.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SO WING YIU
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow

principles.principle_label: Dealing with property under restraint order
principles.paraphrase_en: Knowingly dealing with property that is subject to a court-issued restraint order constitutes a criminal offense.
A defendant's serious medical condition does not generally justify a sentence reduction, but may exceptionally be considered as an act of mercy in specific circumstances.

principles.principle_label: General principle on health and sentence
principles.paraphrase_en: The courts do not, as a general rule, reduce a sentence based solely on the defendant's health condition.
When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court must consider the total cumulative sentence to ensure it is just and appropriate.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. TAM KIT-I
judges: Before: Hon Campbell‑Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may certify that a point of law of great and general importance is involved in a decision, enabling an appeal to the Court of Final Appeal on that specific legal question.

A key legal issue concerns the consequence under the Organized and Serious Crimes Ordinance when a judge decides not to make a confiscation order based on a finding that the respondent had not 'absconded' as defined.

A legal question arises as to whether a judge's decision not to make a confiscation order results in the application being 'concluded' under the statutory definition, which may lead to the automatic discharge of a related restraint order.

principles.principle_label: Nature of OSCO restraint order appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: A point of law exists regarding whether an appeal against a refusal to discharge a restraint order under the Organized and Serious Crimes Ordinance is criminal or civil in nature for jurisdictional and procedural purposes.
If an appeal against a restraint order decision is determined to be criminal in nature, a court's exercise of civil jurisdiction over that appeal may be a nullity.

candidates.case_name: O KIN TUEN v. COMMISSIONER OF CUSTOMS AND EXCISE
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Courts should exercise great caution and be very reluctant to interfere with prosecutorial decisions or the criminal investigation process via judicial review, absent wholly exceptional circumstances. Courts should not second-guess when investigations should end or when a suspect can be dismissed from inquiry.

principles.principle_label: Interpretation of seizure and retention powers
principles.paraphrase_en: The power conferred by a statutory provision permitting seizure of material authorizes retention only for as long as is reasonably necessary for the investigation for which the warrant was issued.

principles.principle_label: Character of seized items as evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal treatment of seized items depends on their character as evidence. Items that are themselves evidence of the commission of the suspected offence are distinguishable from items that merely show a state of affairs (like account balances) and for which alternative evidence (like witness testimony) could be obtained.

Where the first two steps of a proportionality analysis are satisfied and a wide margin of discretion exists, the court will not lightly insist on the use of a potentially less intrusive or more protective alternative measure simply because it is available.

relationships.target_case_name: Tam Sze Leung v Commissioner of Police
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle from this case regarding the court's reluctance to second-guess the termination of investigations.
The present judgment distinguishes the seized money in this case from the deposit receipts in Lai Hing, as the money is itself evidence of the offence.
The judgment cites the common law principle from Ghani regarding the reasonable necessity of retention as consistent with the statutory interpretation.

candidates.case_name: LAI CHEE-YING v. SECRETARY FOR SECURITY
judges: Before: Hon Anthony Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Interpretation of 'deal with' under NSL Schedule 3
principles.paraphrase_en: The term 'deal with' property under Schedule 3 of the National Security Law includes the exercise of voting rights attached to shares that are designated as offence-related property.

principles.principle_label: Scope of property held by a person
principles.paraphrase_en: Property is considered 'held by' a person if that person holds any interest in it, as defined under the National Security Law.

principles.principle_label: Rule of law principle in national security offences
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of the rule of law must be adhered to in preventing, suppressing, and punishing offences endangering national security, requiring that convictions and punishments only follow acts constituting offences under the law.
A definition from one statute for a particular purpose is not determinative of the meaning of the same or similar expression in a different statutory document, as context dictates interpretation.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: The meaning of a word or expression must be determined within the specific textual and factual context of the statute being construed, even if similar language appears elsewhere.

principles.paraphrase_en: Constitutionally protected property rights, including those attached to shares, must be given real and substantial weight when interpreting provisions of the National Security Law.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheermark Investment Ltd v Director of Lands
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Cheermark that definitions from one statute do not control the meaning of similar terms in a different statutory context.

candidates.case_name: TAM SZE LEUNG AND OTHERS v. COMMISSIONER OF POLICE
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may exercise its discretion to determine a judicial review application even if the specific relief sought has become academic, particularly in public law cases where the duties of public bodies are ongoing and affect parties beyond those immediately before the court.

principles.principle_label: Dual purpose of money laundering provisions
principles.paraphrase_en: The legislative scheme against money laundering serves two primary purposes: first, to deprive criminals of the proceeds of their crimes, and second, to impose a duty to report knowledge or suspicion of such proceeds to authorities to aid investigation.

A statutory immunity from the offence of dealing with property known or believed to represent proceeds of crime exists where a person has made a suspicious transaction report and obtained consent from an authorized officer to deal with the property.

relationships.target_case_name: Chit Fai Motors v Commissioner for Transport
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principles from this case to justify hearing a judicial review application even where the specific relief has become academic.
The judgment follows this authority's interpretation of the dual purposes of the money laundering legislative scheme.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Yeung Ka Sing Carson
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as further reference for the interpretation of the money laundering legislative scheme.

relationships.target_case_name: Chief Officer, Customs & Excise, Immigration & Nationality Service v Garnet Investments Ltd
relationships.target_neutral_citation: Guernsey Court of Appeal, Judgment 19/2011, 6 July 2011
relationships.description: The judgment cites this foreign authority regarding the operational purpose of the consent regime in providing immunity from criminal liability.

candidates.case_name: CHENG YIU TONG v. THE COMMISSIONER OF POLICE
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.principle_label: Legal basis for No Consent regime
principles.paraphrase_en: The No Consent (LNC) regime is not an ultra vires exercise of power and does not require specific statutory authorization for freezing accounts, as it operates within the police's general statutory powers and duties under the Police Force Ordinance.

principles.principle_label: Test for applying No Consent mechanism
principles.paraphrase_en: The No Consent mechanism applies only where a financial institution knows, believes, or suspects that property (such as bank funds) are proceeds of crime or terrorist property. Absent such knowledge, belief, or suspicion, the mechanism does not apply.

principles.principle_label: Principle of necessity, proportionality, and reasonableness
principles.paraphrase_en: The use of the No Consent mechanism in each case must be necessary, proportionate, and reasonable. This is an underlying principle that must be seriously considered to justify its application.
A No Consent order may be extended as long as there appears to be justification in the exceptional circumstances for continued extensions, even if a considerable length of time has passed.

principles.principle_label: Individual assessment on facts and merits
principles.paraphrase_en: Each case involving the No Consent mechanism must be decided on its own specific facts and merits, with proper justification provided for its use.

relationships.target_case_name: Tam Sze Leung v Commissioner of Police
relationships.description: The present judgment notes the first-instance decision which held the LNC regime ultra vires, but this view was overturned on appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: Tam Sze Leung v Commissioner of Police
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the Court of Appeal's ruling that the LNC regime is not ultra vires, has a proper purpose, and meets the 'prescribed by law' requirement.

candidates.case_name: THE SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. TAM KIT-I
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice French NPJ

candidates.case_name: RE MR TIMOTHY WYNN OWEN KC
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP (sitting as an additional Judge of the Court of First Instance) in Court

principles.principle_label: Principle of legality and fundamental rights
principles.paraphrase_en: Human rights and fundamental legal principles, such as the right to property and privacy, cannot be overridden by legislation except through express wording or necessary implication. When such rights are engaged, appellate courts should provide guidance.
cited_statutes: Basic Law of the Hong Kong SAR Articles 6, 105
Hong Kong Bill of Rights Article 14

principles.principle_label: Threshold for adverse costs order in leave applications
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for leave to appeal should only attract an adverse costs order if it is so wholly without merit that no reasonable applicant could view it as having a reasonable prospect of success. Merely coming close to lacking merit is insufficient.

relationships.description: The judgment cites dicta from this case to illustrate that cases which appear clear-cut may not be so upon full argument.
The judgment applies the reasoning and threshold test for adverse costs orders established in this cited authority.

candidates.case_name: WONG, CHI HUNG v. LO, WING PUN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Kwan Ag CJHC, Au and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Unenforceability due to foreign illegality in performance
principles.paraphrase_en: A contract may be rendered unenforceable in Hong Kong courts if its performance violates the laws of the place where performance is required, even if such violation was not initially intended or required by the contract.

principles.principle_label: Restitution for unjust enrichment independent of contract
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for unjust enrichment can arise independently from a contract, requiring proof that the defendant received an enrichment at the claimant's expense which is unjust and ought to be redressed, such as due to total failure of consideration.

principles.principle_label: Governing law for unjust enrichment claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The law governing an unjust enrichment claim is determined by Hong Kong's conflict of laws rules, specifically by identifying the law with the closest connection to the obligation to make restitution, which may differ from the proper law of any related contract.

A defense of illegality under foreign law to an unjust enrichment claim requires examining whether the underlying transaction, as a whole, is tainted by illegality under the law of the place where performance was required.
A contract tainted by foreign illegality may remain valid in substance but be unenforceable in a Hong Kong court; the refusal to enforce does not necessarily render the contract void ab initio.

relationships.description: The judgment applies and discusses the principles from Ryder concerning the unenforceability of contracts tainted by foreign illegality.

relationships.target_case_name: Ralli Bros v Compania Naviera Sota y Aznar
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle from Ralli Bros that a contract will not be enforced if performance is illegal under the law of the place of performance.

relationships.target_case_name: Emeraldian Limited Partnership v Wellmix Shipping Ltd & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes Emeraldian as a different type of case under the Ryder principles and criticizes its reliance by the lower court.

relationships.target_case_name: Tae Kim Chang v Official Receiver
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case regarding the historical application of the Bretton Woods Agreements to render certain exchange contracts unenforceable in Hong Kong.
The judgment cites Diplock LJ's opinion on contracts governed by foreign law that are illegal under that law.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ROBIAH SITI
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

candidates.case_name: SITI RUMANI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is limited to examining decisions for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality; it does not involve reassessing the evidence or making factual findings anew.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary decision-makers responsible for assessing evidence and making factual determinations regarding risk of harm, state protection, and internal relocation in non-refoulement claims.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the limited grounds on which a court may intervene in non-refoulement decisions through judicial review.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MUSAH HAKEEM TAHIRU alias TAHIRU MUSA HAKEM
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, M Poon and A Pang JJA in Court

candidates.case_name: TAM KIT I v. THE SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Hon Cheung and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Composition of appellate court for interlocutory vs final orders
principles.paraphrase_en: The number of judges required to hear an appeal depends on whether the appeal is against an interlocutory order or a final order. An appeal against an interlocutory order may be heard by a two-member Court of Appeal, whereas an appeal against a final order generally requires a three-member Court of Appeal unless specific consent provisions apply.
Parties may jointly consent to an appeal being heard and determined by two Justices of Appeal, as provided for under specific statutory provisions.

candidates.case_name: ABDUL RAHAMAN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge P. Li :

principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant in non-refoulement proceedings is responsible for seeking language assistance according to their own resources and capabilities, and the court will assess their actual ability to obtain such help based on the evidence.

The absence of legal representation before the Torture Claims Appeal Board does not constitute an irregularity if the applicant had prior legal assistance in preparing their case to the Director of Immigration and there was no substantial change in their case before the Board.
The court may infer that an applicant was able to find language assistance from the fact that they filed comprehensive, typewritten legal documents in English.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: The court may infer that an applicant was able to find language assistance from the fact that they filed comprehensive, typewritten legal documents in English.

principles.paraphrase_en: There is no general obligation for the Torture Claims Appeal Board to translate its decision to an applicant; the onus is on the applicant to seek understanding through available assistance.

candidates.case_name: DIOCARES MARILOU BANGSOY v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD/ NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement under Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol prohibits returning a person to a territory where they would face a risk of persecution.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for Board decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: A court will not interfere with a decision of the Torture Claims Appeal Board unless it identifies an error of law, procedural unfairness, or findings that are Wednesbury unreasonable or irrational.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority on the applicable legal principles.

candidates.case_name: ALI ZULFIKAR v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

candidates.case_name: FAIYAZ AHMED alias SHAJAHAN SHAIKH v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When the Torture Claims Appeal Board dismisses an appeal following a claimant's valid withdrawal or abandonment, made as a deliberate and informed decision, the appeal ceases to exist and the Board becomes functus officio, losing jurisdiction to take further action on that appeal.
A torture claim rejected by the Director of Immigration is considered finally determined when any appeal lodged against that rejection has been disposed of.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the Court of Appeal's holding in Re Das Purnima Rani regarding the Board becoming functus officio after a valid withdrawal.

candidates.case_name: RE LE THI HUONG
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge To in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Extension of time factors for appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: When deciding whether to extend the time limit for appealing a refusal of leave to apply for judicial review, the court considers four factors: the length of the delay, the reasons for the delay, the merits of the intended appeal, and any prejudice to the respondent if extension is granted.

principles.principle_label: Role of court in non-refoulement judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: In judicial review of non-refoulement claims, the court's function is not to reassess the claim or evidence anew. The court's role is limited to scrutinizing the decision of the primary decision-makers for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary decision-makers responsible for assessing evidence, making factual findings, and determining risk of harm, state protection, and internal relocation viability in non-refoulement claims.

principles.principle_label: Requirement for grounds in judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant for judicial review must state proper grounds demonstrating how the decision-maker erred in public law, such as by identifying errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality. Bare, vague, or unsupported assertions are insufficient.

relationships.target_case_name: Jagg Sing and Torture Claims Appeal Board/non-Refoulement Claims Petition Office
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the four-factor test established in Jagg Sing for considering an extension of time to appeal.

candidates.case_name: LE THI HUONG v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge C P Pang :

principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant for judicial review must clearly state the specific public law grounds (such as error of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality) on which they are challenging the decision. Vague or unsupported assertions without identifying an error in the public law sense do not provide a reasonably arguable basis for review.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for non-refoulement claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must examine decisions on non-refoulement claims with rigorous examination and anxious scrutiny to ensure there is no error of law, irrationality, or procedural unfairness, and that high standards of fairness have been applied.

principles.principle_label: Burden on applicant to demonstrate error
principles.paraphrase_en: The burden is on the applicant in a judicial review to demonstrate that the decision-maker made an error of law, acted irrationally, or was procedurally unfair. In the absence of such a showing, the court will not find a reasonably arguable challenge to the decision.

principles.paraphrase_en: A court's ruling on public interest immunity is not final and must be monitored throughout the trial, with reviews conducted as necessary to protect the interests of the defence.

A witness's evidence can be accepted as honest and truthful overall, even if it contains some flaws arising from misinterpretation or misunderstanding, provided the witness held an honest belief in its truthfulness and conducted a bona fide analysis.

An officer's honest and bona fide belief in the truthfulness of investigation information, based on a professional analysis of the results, is sufficient to support the validity of their actions and recommendations.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the procedural steps laid down in this case for handling public interest immunity matters.
The judgment cites this Court of Final Appeal authority, though the specific legal principle referenced is not detailed in the provided paragraphs.

principles.paraphrase_en: A party to a contract cannot assert rights that arise as a consequence of its own breach of an obligation owed to the other party.

A bank's duty of confidentiality to its customer is subject to four exceptions: disclosure under legal compulsion, a duty to the public to disclose, disclosure required by the bank's own interests, or disclosure with the customer's express or implied consent.

principles.principle_label: No duty to issue consent letters
principles.paraphrase_en: A mortgagee bank has no duty to issue consent letters to allow a mortgagor to lease the mortgaged property, nor a duty to act reasonably in deciding whether to issue such consent.

For the prevention principle to apply, the breach relied upon must be a breach of the specific contract that the party is seeking to enforce, not a breach arising from a separate contractual or legal relationship.
A plaintiff is entitled to choose which parties to sue and is not required to sue all persons who may be liable for the same cause of action.

relationships.target_case_name: Kensland Realty Ltd v Whale View Investment Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Kensland Realty that a party cannot benefit from its own wrong to analyze the prevention principle defence.
The judgment cites the established exceptions to a bank's duty of confidentiality as set out in the Tournier case.
The judgment applies the procedural rule from Hong Kong Civil Procedure that a plaintiff need not sue all potentially liable parties.

candidates.case_name: THE HONGKONG AND SHANGHAI BANKING CORPORATION LTD v. YEUNG FUK KWONG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Mr Recorder William Wong, SC in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Reliance on oral representations against written terms
principles.paraphrase_en: A sophisticated party, particularly one with legal training and business experience, cannot reasonably claim to have relied on an oral representation that directly contradicts the clear written terms of a formal agreement they have signed, especially when they had the opportunity to review those terms.

principles.principle_label: Bank's right to enforce strict legal terms
principles.paraphrase_en: A bank's prior indulgence or forbearance towards a customer does not create a legitimate expectation that it will not later enforce its strict legal rights under loan documents when circumstances deteriorate.

A defence based on misrepresentation or mistake can be summarily dismissed if the court finds the factual basis for the defence is not credible and incapable of being believed, making a trial on those points unnecessary.

principles.principle_label: Timing of protective writs and causation
principles.paraphrase_en: The issuance of a protective writ containing only claims for relief, without factual allegations, and served long after its issuance and after a separate action has commenced, is insufficient evidence to establish that the writ caused the commencement of the later action.

It is not unreasonable or irrational for a bank, which has commenced legal proceedings seeking vacant possession of mortgaged properties, to refuse consent for the continuation of existing tenancies on those properties.

relationships.target_case_name: Kensland Realty Ltd v Whale View Investment Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the established principle that a party in breach of a contractual obligation cannot assert rights arising from that breach.

candidates.case_name: 郭卓堅 v. 政府醫衛局局長盧寵茂先生
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Burden for interim injunction against government enforcement
principles.paraphrase_en: When seeking an interim injunction to restrain a government authority from enforcing a law, the applicant generally must establish more than just a serious issue to be tried. Typically, they need to show a strong prima facie case that the law is invalid, unless they can demonstrate they would suffer such serious and irreparable harm from enforcement that an injunction would be just and convenient despite not meeting this higher burden.

principles.principle_label: Judicial review focus on legality not desirability
principles.paraphrase_en: In a judicial review challenge to a government decision, the court's inquiry is confined to assessing the legality of the decision, not its merits or desirability.

principles.principle_label: Interpretation of statutory provisions in context
principles.paraphrase_en: The correct interpretation of a specific statutory provision must be considered within the context of the legislative scheme of the ordinance as a whole, particularly other relevant sections.

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining whether a 'prohibited group gathering' exists under the Regulation, the court must first consider whether there is sufficient physical proximity between the alleged gatherers. Only if physical proximity is established should the court consider other factors such as common purpose, prior organization, interaction, and duration.

The interpretation of a penal provision must be consistent with the principle against doubtful penalization, meaning any ambiguity should be resolved in favor of the accused to avoid imposing criminal liability without clear legal basis.

A law must satisfy the principle of legal certainty by being adequately accessible to citizens and formulated with sufficient precision to enable them to regulate their conduct, as required by international human rights jurisprudence under the phrase 'prescribed by law'.

A regulation restricting gatherings is rationally connected to the maintenance of public health when it implements social distancing measures and limits the number of people gathering, with common purpose being a relevant consideration in determining the existence of a gathering.

In some cases, an interference with a right may be so great that neither proportionality analysis nor margin of discretion are meaningful, and a right may be held to be encroached if the impugned measure destroys the 'essence of the right'.

relationships.target_case_name: R (Dolan) v Secretary of State for Health and Social Care and Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites the UK Court of Appeal's observations that lockdown measures did not breach human rights provisions under the European Convention on Human Rights.

judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Anthony Chow in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for prevention principle
principles.paraphrase_en: A party alleging that the other party's act of prevention justified termination of a contract bears the burden of proving that such wrongful instruction or act occurred.
A witness's credibility is assessed based on their demeanor, consistency with contemporaneous documents, and plausibility of their account when tested under cross-examination.

A party's resignation or termination is only justified if the other party's instruction would, if complied with, cause the first party to breach professional duties (e.g., commit professional negligence or breach of confidence).

principles.principle_label: Evaluation of factual assertions against human nature
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may reject a factual assertion if the alleged conduct is inconsistent with ordinary human behavior, such as sending messages that contradict the alleged instructions.
