principles.principle_label: Court's discretion to proceed despite non-compliance
principles.paraphrase_en: The court retains the discretion to consider an application on its merits even if a party fails to comply with procedural directions, such as filing required submissions.

The court has the discretion to determine the language of the proceedings and judgments, considering factors such as the language used in prior hearings and court documents, while making arrangements for interpretation if needed.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOW HANG TUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Collateral challenge to administrative notices in criminal proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a statutory notice is directed specifically at an individual (a 'same person' case), the legislative intent is that the individual must challenge the notice via available appeal or judicial review procedures, not by raising its legality as a defence in subsequent criminal proceedings for non-compliance.

principles.principle_label: Elements of offence under Schedule 5 s.3(3)(b) IR
principles.paraphrase_en: For an offence of non-compliance with a notice under section 3(3)(b) of Schedule 5 to the Implementation Rules, the prosecution does not need to prove as a fact that the organization named in the notice was actually a foreign agent. The validity of the notice is not an element of the offence open to challenge in the criminal trial.

principles.principle_label: Validity of notice based on facial appearance
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence under the relevant provision requires only that the notice appears valid on its face and has not been quashed by judicial review. The court in criminal proceedings does not inquire into its substantive legality.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Boddington as not being a 'same person' case where collateral challenge was permissible.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v The Incorporated Owners of No 10 Bonham Strand
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that individuals specifically directed by an order cannot challenge its reasonableness collaterally in criminal proceedings.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN WAN YIU
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.paraphrase_en: The purpose of an activity (such as operating a stall) for determining whether a legal exemption applies is not assessed based solely on the duration of the activity or the specific time spent waiting for participants.
When hearing an appeal against sentence, the court's primary consideration is whether the sentence imposed violates any sentencing principle or is manifestly excessive.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWOK WING KIN AND OTHERS
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Tang NPJ
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Leave to appeal requirement in criminal cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In criminal cases, leave to appeal to the Court of Final Appeal will not be granted unless the lower court or the Appeal Committee certifies that the decision involves a point of law of great and general importance, or that substantial and grave injustice has occurred.

A 'prohibited group gathering' under the Prevention and Control of Disease (Prohibition on Gathering) Regulation is a gathering of more than four persons in a public place during a specified period, which is prohibited by section 3 of the Regulation.
cited_statutes: Prevention and Control of Disease (Prohibition on Gathering) Regulation s.3
Prevention and Control of Disease (Prohibition on Gathering) Regulation s.6

principles.principle_label: Offence of participating in prohibited gathering
principles.paraphrase_en: Section 6 of the Prevention and Control of Disease (Prohibition on Gathering) Regulation creates an offence for anyone who participates in, organizes, or is involved in a prohibited group gathering.
cited_statutes: Prevention and Control of Disease (Prohibition on Gathering) Regulation s.6

principles.principle_label: Legal basis for emergency health regulations
principles.paraphrase_en: The Prevention and Control of Disease (Prohibition on Gathering) Regulation was validly issued by the Chief Executive in Council as a public health emergency regulation under the authority of section 8 of the Prevention and Control of Disease Ordinance.
cited_statutes: Prevention and Control of Disease Ordinance s.8

principles.paraphrase_en: The legislative objective of the Prevention and Control of Disease (Prohibition on Gathering) Regulation is to ban group gatherings to prevent, protect against, delay, or control the incidence or transmission of the COVID-19 virus.

candidates.case_name: 吳振權 v. 香港特別行政區政府
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.paraphrase_en: When challenging an administrative decision made under statutory powers, if the decision-maker is assumed to have held the necessary view to trigger those powers, the primary conventional ground for judicial review is an assertion of irrationality in the public law sense.
The legal threshold for successfully proving that an administrative decision is irrational in the public law sense is a high one, making such challenges difficult to establish.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YU CHUN WING
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Definition of 'party room' under Prevention and Control of Disease Regulations
principles.paraphrase_en: The definition of 'party room' in the relevant Directions is not limited to premises actively being hired at the material time. It includes premises that are ordinarily maintained for hire for holding social gatherings, regardless of whether a specific gathering at the time is private or involves payment.

principles.principle_label: Magistrate's duty in explaining Criminal Procedure Ordinance s.65C
principles.paraphrase_en: When a magistrate proposes to rely on section 65C of the Criminal Procedure Ordinance to admit facts without calling witnesses, it is necessary to explain the legal consequences of this procedure to an unrepresented defendant to ensure they understand and their rights are protected.

A magistrate should not find a defendant to be a dishonest witness solely based on a disagreement over the interpretation of a legal term, as this conflates a legal dispute with an assessment of credibility.

principles.principle_label: Magistrate's role in managing unrepresented defendant
principles.paraphrase_en: A magistrate must maintain neutrality and an open attitude when assisting an unrepresented defendant, ensuring they have a proper opportunity to present their case and obtain favourable testimony, and should avoid taking on the role of the prosecution.

candidates.case_name: DENNIS KWOK HON MING v. POON SUI CHEONG ALBERT AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Cheung JA, Barma JA and Harris J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.paraphrase_en: The prevention principle prevents a party from benefiting from their own wrongful act, but it does not apply where the act in question (such as removal of a manager) was legally justified under an implied term of the agreement.
An appellate court applies different standards when reviewing findings of primary fact versus reviewing inferences or conclusions drawn from those primary facts.
A court-ordered share buy-out remedy is only available if unfair prejudicial conduct by the majority shareholders has been established.

relationships.target_case_name: Kensland Realty Ltd v Whale View Investment Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Kensland Realty to explain the essence of the prevention principle.

relationships.target_case_name: Young Ivy Shui Heung v Yau koon Sum
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example of the well-known appellate approach to intervening in factual findings.

relationships.target_case_name: Ting Kwok Keung v Tam Dick Yuen
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case regarding the appellate court's approach to different types of factual findings.

relationships.target_case_name: So Kai Hau v YSK2 Engineering Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case, which itself cites Todd v Adams, on the appellate standard for reviewing factual inferences.

candidates.case_name: ISLAM SAIFUL v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge (Non-Refoulement Claims) Lewis Law :
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Ladd v Marshall inapplicability to new evidence in judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: The rules established in Ladd v Marshall for admitting new evidence do not apply when an applicant seeks to file new evidence with the Court of First Instance in support of an application for leave to apply for judicial review against the Torture Claims Appeal Board, where that evidence was not previously considered by the Board.

principles.principle_label: Limited role of CFI in non-refoulement judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of First Instance does not conduct a fresh examination of the merits of a non-refoulement claim in a judicial review application; its role is confined to reviewing the lawfulness and fairness of the Board's decision-making process.

relationships.description: The judgment clarifies that the rules from Ladd v Marshall do not apply to the specific context of introducing new evidence in a judicial review leave application against the Torture Claims Appeal Board.

relationships.target_case_name: Chidomere Christian Okechukwu v TCAB/NRCPO & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment follows the recent Court of Appeal holding that Ladd v Marshall rules are inapplicable to new evidence filed at the judicial review leave stage against the Board.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TANG NGOK KWAN (鄧岳君), TSUI HON KWONG (徐漢光)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Chan NPJ
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Foreign agent status as essential element
principles.paraphrase_en: For an offence under Schedule 5 section 3(3) of the relevant ordinance, the prosecution must prove as an essential element that the person or organization served with a notice was, as a matter of fact, a foreign agent as defined in Schedule 5 section 1. It is not sufficient for the prosecution to prove only that the Commissioner of Police reasonably believed them to be a foreign agent.

principles.principle_label: Right to challenge notice validity as defence
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the validity of an administrative notice is an essential element of a criminal offence, a defendant is entitled to challenge that validity as part of their defence in the criminal trial. Such a challenge is not an impermissible collateral attack.

principles.principle_label: Presumption in favour of collateral challenge
principles.paraphrase_en: There is a strong presumption in favour of permitting a defendant to challenge the validity of an administrative act or decision during a criminal trial where that validity is an essential element of the offence. This presumption can only be displaced by compelling reasons derived from statutory construction.

relationships.description: The present judgment clarifies and limits the application of the 'same person' exception from Chow Hang Tung, rejecting its use to preclude a defendant from challenging the validity of a notice that is an essential element of the offence.

The judgment follows the principle from Chow Hang Tung that while collateral challenges may sometimes be excluded by statute, there is a strong presumption in favour of permitting them when validity is an essential element of the offence.

candidates.case_name: LAW YEE MEI v. CHIEF EXECUTIVE OF HONG KONG SAR AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Judicial review scope for public health measures
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in reviewing public health regulations is limited to determining whether they breach established principles of public law, not to express general views on the policy.

principles.principle_label: Qualification as property agent not a basic right
principles.paraphrase_en: The aspiration to qualify as a licensed property agent does not constitute a basic human right protected under the Basic Law or the Hong Kong Bill of Rights Ordinance.

principles.principle_label: Access to markets not a basic right
principles.paraphrase_en: Access to wet markets or supermarkets for purchasing food and daily necessities, even if causing inconvenience, cannot be characterized as a basic right.
Public health regulations, such as vaccine pass rules, will be upheld if they are justifiable and proportionate under public law principles.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. PERSONS CONDUCTING THEMSELVES IN ANY OF THE ACTS PROHIBITED UNDER PARAGRAPH 1(a), (b), (c) OR (d) OF THE INDORSEMENT OF CLAIM
judges: Before: Hon Anthony Chan J in Chambers
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Test for contra mundum injunction necessity
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must apply a test of necessity or utility when considering a contra mundum injunction, requiring that the injunction is necessary because the defendants' illegal conduct cannot be effectively restrained without it.
When assessing the necessity of an injunction, the court must consider (1) whether it provides greater deterrence than existing criminal law, and (2) the ease of enforcement against law-breakers.
Any injunction must be limited to terms that are genuinely necessary and address serious concerns, not exceeding what is required.

principles.principle_label: Criminal penalty severity as injunction consideration
principles.paraphrase_en: The court should question the utility of an injunction when the criminal sanction for the targeted act is already severe, particularly when it exceeds potential contempt penalties.

An injunction contra mundum is exceptional because it departs from the general rule that final injunctions only bind parties to the proceedings, potentially compromising normal procedural protections for defendants.

relationships.description: The judgment references authority for the principle that a person cannot be subject to court jurisdiction without notice enabling them to be heard.
The judgment cites authority supporting the requirement that injunction terms must be 'really necessary and address serious concerns'.
The judgment references authority discussing when civil courts should grant injunctions for acts lightly penalized under criminal law.

candidates.case_name: DBS BANK (HONG KONG) LTD v. HONOUR ELITE CORPORATION LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers (by paper disposal)
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing procedural matters, a court may consider whether a defence raised is substantial in nature, not merely technical or insubstantial.

candidates.case_name: HUANG QINGZHAN v. CHINA DING YI FENG HOLDINGS LTD
judges: Before: Hon G Lam & Chow JJA and Anthony Chan J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.paraphrase_en: The prevention principle, which prevents a party from benefiting from its own wrong, can apply where one party's actions or failures cause the other party's inability to perform a contractual obligation.

Where a contract specifies payment to a registered account, the paying party is not obligated to use alternative payment methods if a registered account exists, even if technical issues prevent payment into that account.

relationships.target_case_name: Kensland Realty Ltd v Whale View Investment Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the prevention principle as explained by Ribeiro PJ in Kensland Realty Ltd v Whale View Investment Ltd.

candidates.case_name: KWOK CHEUK KIN v. SECRETARY FOR HEALTH
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.paraphrase_en: When there is a conflict between general and specific statutory provisions, the specific provisions take precedence. A broadly worded general power cannot be used to override the clear intention of specific provisions.

principles.principle_label: Statutory interpretation as a coherent whole
principles.paraphrase_en: Legislative instruments must be interpreted as a coherent whole, considering the full structure and context of all relevant provisions, not by isolating general provisions to create a desired outcome.

The government's power to regulate a specific regime, including creating exemptions, must be found within the specific ordinance establishing that regime. It cannot seek to source such power from a different, separate ordinance.

principles.principle_label: Power to designate vs. power to invalidate
principles.paraphrase_en: A statutory power for the Secretary to designate categories of exempted persons does not imply a separate, unstated power to invalidate certificates that otherwise meet specific statutory criteria for exemption.

principles.principle_label: Leave to amend judicial review grounds
principles.paraphrase_en: The decision to grant leave to amend grounds in a judicial review after initial leave has been granted depends on what is fair and appropriate to determine the real dispute, considering factors like the reason for delay, the nature and merit of the amendment, and any prejudice.

relationships.description: The judgment references 'Bennion on Statutory Interpretation' for reasons why a legislature may delegate legislative power, such as during a sudden emergency.
The judgment cites the treatise to note that the 'no fettering' principle typically applies to individualized discretions like licensing, not to categorical policy decisions.

candidates.case_name: SOREMI INVESTMENTS LTD v. CHINA NATIONAL GOLD GROUP HONG KONG LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Eugene Fung J in Chambers (Open to the Public)
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.paraphrase_en: A 'matter' requiring a stay of court proceedings under an arbitration agreement is a substantial issue that is legally relevant to a claim or defence in the proceedings, and which can be determined by an arbitrator as a discrete dispute. It does not include issues that are peripheral or tangential to the subject matter of the legal proceedings.

In a claim for dishonest assistance, the determination of dishonesty is not dependent on establishing a breach of contract. References to a contract in pleadings do not necessarily make contractual breach an essential element of the dishonest assistance claim.

Whether disputes in a legal action fall outside the scope of an arbitration agreement is a matter for judicial determination based on whether the disputes constitute 'matters' covered by the agreement.

The prevention principle (that a person cannot take advantage of their own wrong) cannot be invoked to rewrite factual circumstances or to artificially deem a contract terminated for the purpose of avoiding an arbitration agreement.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the definition of 'matter' for arbitration stay purposes as stated by Sundaresh Menon CJ in Tomolugen.

relationships.target_case_name: Kensland Realty Ltd v Whale View Investment Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes the present facts from Kensland Realty, finding that the prevention principle cannot be applied to rewrite the factual circumstances regarding contract termination.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHANG HOO CHANG
judges: Before: Hon Wong J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Presumption of innocence and burden of proof
principles.paraphrase_en: The defendant has the right to plead not guilty and is presumed innocent. The legal burden to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt rests entirely on the prosecution, and the defendant is entitled to require the prosecution to strictly prove its case.

A defence is not automatically considered frivolous or vexatious merely because it arises from the defendant's misunderstanding of the law. However, a defence may be classified as such if it involves making indiscriminate false accusations against the prosecution or relies on sheer sophistry.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the reasoning from HKSAR v Ball, John Edward regarding what constitutes a frivolous or vexatious defence.

candidates.case_name: NGUYEN THI NHUNG v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD/ NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.paraphrase_en: When an applicant for judicial review applies to withdraw their application, the court may proceed to deal with the withdrawal on the basis of the applicant's stated original intention, even if the applicant cannot subsequently be contacted.

When a judge accedes to an applicant's request to withdraw an application for leave to apply for judicial review, and there is nothing else outstanding in the action, the correct way to conclude the matter is to order a dismissal of the application, rather than leaving the action unresolved.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Re Chu Woan Chyi that the correct disposal after a withdrawal is to dismiss the application.

candidates.case_name: TSANG HO MING v. COMMISSIONER OF POLICE AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Threshold for police powers under s.54
principles.paraphrase_en: The applicant argues that section 54(1) of the Police Force Ordinance sets an insufficiently low threshold by requiring only that a police officer finds a citizen 'acting suspiciously' without context, rather than requiring a founded suspicion that the person is engaged in criminal activity.

principles.principle_label: Distinction between restriction and deprivation of liberty
principles.paraphrase_en: A restriction of personal freedom, such as being temporarily prevented from leaving a premises during a police operation, does not necessarily constitute a deprivation of liberty that engages constitutional protections against arrest, detention, or imprisonment.

principles.principle_label: Scope of Basic Law Article 28 right
principles.paraphrase_en: The right to freedom of the person under Basic Law Article 28 is not absolute and does not permit a person to resist or obstruct the due execution of a lawful duty by a public officer.

principles.principle_label: Objective facts required for police suspicion
principles.paraphrase_en: For police to lawfully invoke powers under section 54, there must be objective facts upon which they can base the requisite suspicion or reasonable belief connecting a person to criminal activities.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tze Yam v Commissioner of Police
relationships.description: The present judgment cites Wong Tze Yam (No.2) as a precedent where a similar constitutional challenge was raised regarding police powers and the distinction between restriction and deprivation of liberty.

relationships.target_case_name: Lai Man Lok v Director of Home Affairs
relationships.description: The judgment notes that Lai Man Lok applied the principle from Wong Tze Yam (No.2) regarding the non-absolute nature of the freedom of the person under Basic Law Article 28.
The judgment references the interpretation from Wong Tze Yam (No.1) regarding the need for objective facts to found police suspicion under section 54.

candidates.case_name: 董旺建 v. 香港特別行政區行政長官及另一人
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Judicial review leave requirement for proper grounds
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for leave to apply for judicial review must identify comprehensible and proper grounds of review that allege specific breaches of public law duties, not merely express personal opinions or regurgitate legal terms.

principles.principle_label: Failure to identify grounds as sufficient for refusal
principles.paraphrase_en: Failure to identify any comprehensible and proper grounds of review is, by itself, a sufficient basis to refuse leave to apply for judicial review, without needing to consider other potential issues like delay or standing.

principles.principle_label: Test for granting leave for judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to apply for judicial review will be refused if the application is not reasonably arguable and has no realistic prospect of success.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the reasoning in the earlier decision that the applicant failed to identify proper grounds of review as required by the Rules of the High Court.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHENG KIN FAI
judges: Before: Hon Wong J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169)

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for unsuccessful not guilty plea
principles.paraphrase_en: A magistrate is not required to impose a sentence enhancement under section 12(3) of the relevant ordinance when a defendant who pleaded not guilty is convicted after trial, provided the magistrate does not find the defence to have been frivolous or vexatious.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of innocence and burden of proof
principles.paraphrase_en: An accused person has the right to plead not guilty, which is grounded in the presumption of innocence. The legal burden of proving the offence rests entirely on the prosecution, and the accused is entitled to require the prosecution to prove its case strictly.

principles.principle_label: Retention of legal protection despite non-payment
principles.paraphrase_en: A person's failure to pay a fixed penalty as required by regulations does not automatically result in the loss of the legal protection afforded by the principle of presumption of innocence and the prosecution's burden of proof.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v TABOR DEXTER VALERA
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Terence Wai

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for animal cruelty offences
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no fixed starting point for sentencing in animal cruelty cases; the sentence must be determined based on the specific facts and circumstances of each case, including the method of operation, motive, type of animal victim, and defendant's background, with a focus on deterrence.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Fung Chi Hoi
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the sentencing approach established in Fung Chi Hoi, which rejects a fixed starting point and emphasizes a fact-specific, deterrent sentence for animal cruelty offences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU KWAN LING
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peony Wong in Court

principles.principle_label: Mitigation and demand for endangered species
principles.paraphrase_en: The absence of direct evidence of poaching by the defendant is not a mitigating factor in sentencing for offenses involving endangered species. The critical factor is the defendant's demand to possess, control, and sell such species, as this demand itself drives the depletion of populations.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. CHEUNG KIM LUN
judges: Before: Hon Pang, M Poon and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Definition of 'accident' in Road Traffic Ordinance
principles.paraphrase_en: The term 'accident' in section 56 of the Road Traffic Ordinance (Cap. 374) is not limited to unintended occurrences. It can include deliberate acts that result in an unfortunate event or an event outside the ordinary course.
A collision caused by a deliberate act, such as deliberately driving a vehicle at a person or object, can still constitute an 'accident' under the relevant road traffic legislation.

The duty to report an accident under section 56 serves important practical purposes, including enabling assessment of injuries and damage, facilitating medical or veterinary attention, and allowing immediate police investigation and evidence preservation.

relationships.description: The judgment indicates that the requirement for an occurrence to be unintended, as suggested in Morris, is not an essential element of 'accident' in subsequent cases.

relationships.target_case_name: Chief Constable of Staffordshire v Lees
relationships.description: The judgment adopts the analysis from this case that a collision caused by a deliberate act can be regarded as an 'accident'.

candidates.case_name: GALLETIS DOMINICA DOSCA v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD/ NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Hon Chow JA and Ng J in Court

principles.principle_label: No absolute right to free legal representation in USM
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant under the Unified Screening Mechanism does not possess an absolute right to free legal representation throughout all stages of the non-refoulement proceedings.

The assessment of whether procedural fairness was breached involves examining the specific circumstances, including whether the claimant had legal assistance at earlier stages, whether decisions were translated, and whether the adjudicating body was fully aware of the relevant documents.
The risk of persecution in non-refoulement claims is assessed with reference to the non-refoulement principle under Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees.
cited_statutes: Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees

candidates.case_name: AUTOMOBILI LAMBORGHINI S.p.A. v. JARVIS MOJO LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: DHJC Kent Yee in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Notice requirement for ex parte proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may proceed with an application in the absence of a party if satisfied that the absent party had proper notice of the hearing and chose not to attend.
An interim injunction may be granted pending the determination of a summons for interlocutory injunctions, with directions given for the filing of evidence.
Goodwill in a jurisdiction for trademark or passing off claims can be established through extensive promotion, distribution, and sales of products under the marks over a significant period.
Registered trademarks can become distinctive of a plaintiff's specific products through their use in commerce.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHAW KIM SUN
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Macrae VP and Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge must direct the jury that if the defence's account is or may be true, they must acquit the defendant.
In a case based on circumstantial evidence, the jury should be directed that if there is a reasonable explanation of the facts consistent with innocence, they must find the defendant not guilty.
Legal principles from cases decided solely on expert evidence may not apply where the case involves additional, non-expert evidence.
A person's right of silence can be infringed in two distinct ways, as established in Lee Fuk Hing v HKSAR.

principles.principle_label: Irrelevance of right of silence principles
principles.paraphrase_en: Principles concerning the right of silence are not relevant where the impugned questions and answers were admissible, presented without judicial comment, and the defence itself wanted the evidence before the jury.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Henderson as it concerned cases turning solely on expert evidence, unlike the present case which had additional evidence.
The judgment cites Boyes v MacLeod as an example of a Scottish case involving uncautioned interviews following an incident.
The judgment cites Lee Fuk Hing as the leading local authority on the two ways a right of silence can be infringed.
The judgment cites R v P as the source of the approved direction on circumstantial evidence used in England and Wales and Hong Kong.

candidates.case_name: VU NGOC DAN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.principle_label: Time extension for judicial review appeals
principles.paraphrase_en: When deciding whether to extend time for appealing a refusal of leave to apply for judicial review, the court will consider specific factors, including the length and reasons for the delay, the merits of the underlying case, and the potential prejudice to the parties.

An appeal against a Director's decision under the non-refoulement petition scheme must be filed within 14 days after the Notice is deemed served. Service is deemed effective on the second day after posting.

principles.principle_label: Role of court in non-refoulement judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of a non-refoulement claim is not to reassess the evidence or facts de novo. The primary fact-finders are the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board. The court intervenes only to scrutinize the lawfulness of the Board's decision, not to substitute its own assessment.

principles.principle_label: Standard for late filing of appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: The Board must apply a high standard of fairness when considering a late appeal. The applicant bears the burden to provide sufficient information and evidence for the Board to assess the appeal's merits and any special circumstances that might justify the delay.

relationships.target_case_name: AW and Director of Immigration & Anor
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principles established in AW regarding the factors to consider when extending time for an appeal against refusal of leave for judicial review.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG HIN MING
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee J

principles.paraphrase_en: A trial judge's assessment of a witness's credibility, based on observing their demeanor and evaluating the reasonableness and logic of their testimony, is a crucial finding of fact that an appellate court should be slow to overturn.

In a case relying solely on the evidence of an accomplice witness of flawed character and suspect credibility, with little independent corroboration, an appellate court may intervene if the trial judge's reasoning in assessing that witness is defective and renders the assessment unreliable.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE PAK TO
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.paraphrase_en: The general principle is that a one-third reduction in sentence is typically the maximum discount for a timely guilty plea, and exceeding this discount requires exceptional circumstances.

principles.principle_label: Victim spared testimony as sentencing factor
principles.paraphrase_en: When a guilty plea spares a victim from the ordeal of giving evidence in court, this factor should be considered within the established framework for plea-based sentence reductions, not as an automatic justification for exceeding standard discount limits.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Chow Yuen Fai regarding the maximum usual discount for a timely guilty plea and the requirement for exceptional circumstances to exceed it.

candidates.case_name: NINTENDO CO., LTD v. CHEN YUAN NING AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kent Yee in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The fundamental aim of damages in intellectual property infringement cases is to place the injured party in the financial position they would have occupied had the wrongful act not occurred.

principles.principle_label: Applicability of patent damages principles to copyright
principles.paraphrase_en: The principles for assessing compensatory damages established in patent infringement cases are generally applicable to copyright infringement cases, subject to specific statutory considerations like additional damages or conversion damages.
A company cannot be held liable for infringing acts that occurred before its date of incorporation, as it did not exist as a legal entity at that time.

principles.principle_label: Apportionment of damages for pre-incorporation acts
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a defendant company is incorporated after the commencement of infringing activities, damages payable by that company must be apportioned to exclude liability for the period before its incorporation.

relationships.target_case_name: General Tire and Rubber Co v Firestone Tyre and Rubber Co. Ltd.
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and applies the compensatory damages principle established in General Tire for assessing damages in intellectual property infringement cases.

candidates.case_name: CHEUNG TAK WING v. COMMUNICATIONS AUTHORITY AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Chu JA and Barma JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The term 'government policy' encompasses the general principles, guidelines, statements, or plans that guide a government in managing public affairs and conducting its business.

principles.principle_label: Freedom of expression and political advertising
principles.paraphrase_en: A challenge to an exemption allowing government political advertising cannot logically succeed on the basis that it violates a right not to receive such advertising, when the challenger accepts that the underlying general ban on political advertising itself does not infringe freedom of expression.

relationships.description: The judgment references definitions from dictionaries or other sources to interpret the natural meaning of the term 'policy'.

candidates.case_name: PARMINDER SINGH v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement under Article 33 of the Refugee Convention protects individuals from being returned to a country where they would face a risk of persecution.
cited_statutes: Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees and its 1967 Protocol

principles.paraphrase_en: A decision of an administrative board can only be successfully challenged on judicial review if it contains an error of law, involves procedural unfairness, or is Wednesbury unreasonable/irrational.

relationships.description: The present judgment references the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority on the applicable legal principles.

candidates.case_name: UGBE KENNETH NGOZI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement under Article 33 of the Refugee Convention protects individuals from being returned to a country where they would face a risk of persecution.
cited_statutes: Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees and its 1967 Protocol

principles.paraphrase_en: A decision of an administrative board, such as the Torture Claims Appeal Board, can only be successfully challenged on judicial review if it contains an error of law, procedural unfairness, or is Wednesbury unreasonable/irrational.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority on the applicable legal principles.

candidates.case_name: GURUNG KUMAR v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement, as established in Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol, prohibits the return of a person to a territory where they would face a risk of persecution.
cited_statutes: Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees and its 1967 Protocol

relationships.description: The present judgment references the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority on the applicable legal principles.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. AL HAMD WAEEL ALI MAHMOUD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Ivy Chui

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for smuggling endangered species, the court must consider the quantity and value of the contraband, as well as the defendant's role, such as being a courier.
In determining an appropriate sentence, the court may refer to sentences imposed in previous cases involving similar charges and factual circumstances to ensure consistency.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case as a comparable sentencing precedent for a similar offence involving transporting a large quantity of dried seahorses.
The present judgment cites this case as another relevant sentencing precedent for similar charges.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SIRICHEUNVICHIT PAWAT
judges: Before: His Honour Judge E Lin in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining culpability and sentence, the court may consider elaborate methods of concealment as evidence of detailed planning and a disregard for the law or welfare of others.

In imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offenses, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is just and proportionate to the offender's overall criminality.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIAQAT ALI KHAN
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Clement Lee

principles.paraphrase_en: Guilty knowledge (mens rea) can be inferred from the defendant's actions, such as knowingly making a false declaration about the nature of goods to customs authorities to conceal their true, prohibited nature.

A defendant's active role in providing templates and advice for customs clearance, rather than acting as a mere labourer or courier, is relevant to establishing their level of involvement and knowledge in an offence.

The use of multiple languages during police investigation and procedures does not necessarily invalidate the process; procedural justice can still be maintained if the defendant had a fair opportunity to understand and participate.

candidates.case_name: ANACHEBE CHIBUZOR REUBEN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement under Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol prohibits returning a person to a country where they would face a risk of persecution.
A court will not interfere with an administrative board's decision unless it finds an error of law, procedural unfairness, or that the decision is Wednesbury unreasonable or irrational.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority.

candidates.case_name: GURUNG KUMAR v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Ng J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a claim of persecution or torture to succeed, the alleged ill-treatment or threats must reach a minimum level of severity. Factors considered include the frequency and nature of past threats and whether actual injury resulted.

principles.principle_label: Requirement for evidence supporting fear of harm
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant's subjective fear of being killed or seriously harmed upon return must be supported by objective evidence. Mere speculation by the claimant is insufficient to establish a well-founded fear.

principles.principle_label: Court's duty of care to unrepresented litigants
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has a duty of care towards an unrepresented litigant who does not understand the language of proceedings or legal terminology. This duty is not discharged merely because the litigant sought informal help from a friend.

Where a decision against a non-refoulement claimant is based purely on an adverse credibility finding, procedural fairness may require the court to intervene, especially if the claimant had no other avenue for appeal on that point.
The assessment of risk of persecution in non-refoulement claims is conducted with reference to the principle under Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol.
cited_statutes: Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SO WAI HANG
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSANG KAI YIU
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits an offence by importing specimens of species listed in Appendix II of the Protection of Endangered Species of Animals and Plants Ordinance (Cap. 586) without complying with the statutory requirements.
Species listed in Appendix II of CITES are those whose trade must be controlled to prevent them from facing the threat of extinction.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG JUSTIN
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for an additional offence related to the main criminal conduct, the sentence for that additional offence should generally be ordered to run consecutively to the sentences imposed for the substantive offences.

A sentencing court must consider the totality principle when imposing consecutive sentences to ensure the final aggregate sentence is not excessive and properly reflects the defendant's overall criminality.

principles.principle_label: Global sentencing approach for multiple offences
principles.paraphrase_en: When dealing with multiple offences, the court should first adopt a global approach to determine an overall starting point that reflects the total criminality, before considering individual sentences for each charge.
A defendant is entitled to a reduction in sentence for entering a timely guilty plea, which is applied from the appropriate starting point determined for the offence.

candidates.case_name: GALLETIS DOMINICA DOSCA v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge P. Li :

principles.principle_label: No absolute right to legal representation in USM
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant under the Unified Screening Mechanism does not possess an absolute entitlement to free legal representation throughout all stages of the non-refoulement proceedings.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of procedural fairness in USM
principles.paraphrase_en: The determination of whether procedural unfairness exists in a non-refoulement claim requires a holistic assessment of the circumstances, including the availability of prior legal assistance, translation of decisions, and the adjudicator's awareness of the relevant documents.

relationships.description: The judgment references a prior authority (footnote 12) for the proposition that there is no absolute right to free legal representation in the Unified Screening Mechanism.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LOR HAO HING
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's statement made under police caution, where they admit to elements of the offense and their motivations, constitutes admissible evidence that can be used against them in court.

The physical acts of rushing at a victim, pushing them into a corner and onto the floor, and covering their mouth and nose with a towel, causing pain and injury, can constitute the prohibited conduct (actus reus) for offenses involving assault.
Evidence of a defendant's prior suspicious conduct towards the victim, such as approaching them on a previous occasion, can be admitted as relevant background to the incident in question.

candidates.case_name: CHI YUM BUDDHIST HALL ASSOCIATION LTD v. YU LIK WAI WILLIAM AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Jonathan Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Amendments to pleadings should be allowed before the limitation period expires if they serve to clarify the genuine issues in dispute between the parties or to correct procedural errors.

The acquiescence branch of proprietary estoppel arises when one party acts under a mistaken belief about their rights, and another party, aware of both this mistaken belief and their own conflicting rights, does not assert their rights. It applies if the first party would suffer a detriment if the second party later enforced their rights.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Ramsden v Dyson as an authority illustrating the application of the principle of acquiescence-based proprietary estoppel.

candidates.case_name: CHEUK CHI WAI AND ANOTHER v. HSU MAN NAI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Kay Seto in Chambers

candidates.case_name: NG YAU SING AND OTHERS v. FALCON INSURANCE COMPANY (HONG KONG) LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Andrew Li in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Costs scale discretion for sanctioned payments
principles.paraphrase_en: When a sanctioned payment within District Court jurisdiction is accepted in a High Court claim, this constitutes an exceptional circumstance justifying the court's discretion to order costs to be taxed on the District Court scale under Order 22 rule 20 of the Rules of the District Court.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle established in Wong Chim Hing regarding exceptional circumstances for costs scale when accepting sanctioned payments.

candidates.case_name: MAN YU SHING SIMON v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kent Yee in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for adverse possession
principles.paraphrase_en: A person claiming adverse possession without documentary title bears the burden of proving an intention to exclude everyone, including the true owner, from the land.
To establish adverse possession, the claimant must demonstrate evidence of enclosures or exclusive physical control over the land throughout the statutory limitation period.

When multiple squatters are in simultaneous possession, they acquire title as joint tenants by default, and a single squatter cannot claim title alone; possession must be singular, though it can be held jointly.

principles.principle_label: Consequence of failing to prove adverse possession
principles.paraphrase_en: If a claimant fails to prove adverse possession, they cannot invoke statutory provisions that extinguish the owner's title, and the owner's claim for recovery of possession and damages succeeds.

principles.principle_label: Requirement of exclusive and singular possession
principles.paraphrase_en: Adverse possession requires proof of exclusive and singular possession for the entire statutory period; without it, the owner is not dispossessed and the claim fails.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM MAN YIN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peony Wong

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for money laundering
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining a sentencing starting point for a money laundering offence, the court must consider the total amount of money involved, the scale and degree of planning, and the presence of aggravating features such as an international element.

principles.principle_label: Delay in proceedings as a mitigating factor
principles.paraphrase_en: A delay between arrest and plea is merely one factor to be considered in sentencing, and comparison with delays in other cases with different factual backgrounds is unhelpful. An additional discount for delay is not warranted unless the defendant demonstrates specific rehabilitation progress or suffers substantial stress beyond the normal uncertainty of facing punishment.
Each case must be decided on its own specific facts, and comparisons with the factual matrices of other cases are of limited utility.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Wan Kwok Keung as a precedent for a sentencing starting point in a money laundering case involving a large sum but a simple, non-syndicated operation.

The judgment distinguishes the present case from Lam Ka Sin on material factual grounds, noting the latter involved a single, failed transaction under coercion, whereas the present case involved more active and sustained conduct.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHAW KIM SUN
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Keith in Court

principles.principle_label: Belief vs. objective value in research
principles.paraphrase_en: In assessing whether a defendant believed their research had potential value, the jury's determination of the objective lack of value of the research is highly relevant to informing that assessment of belief.
The proper remedy for a question that should not have been asked in cross-examination is to prevent that line of questioning in a retrial, not to exclude all evidence on the broader topic.

A court, including a jury in a jury trial, may take judicial notice of a fact if it is so well known that proving it formally would waste time and resources. The test is whether the jury members, as residents of Hong Kong, would commonly know the fact.

candidates.case_name: NAEEM MUHAMMAD v. HONG KONG SAR GOVERNMENT
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining whether a period of immigration detention has become or will become unreasonable and thus unlawful, the weight assigned to specific factors may change over an extended period of detention.

The principles governing the lawfulness of immigration detention, as summarized in relevant Court of Appeal authority, should be adopted and applied as appropriate to the specific circumstances of the individual case.

relationships.target_case_name: Harjang Singh v Secretary for Security
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principles summarized by the Court of Appeal in Harjang Singh for assessing the lawfulness of immigration detention.

candidates.case_name: CHAN KWOK KUEN v. LAI YAT HO AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: An injunction granted under section 729(a) of the Companies Ordinance must correspond precisely to the specific conduct or failure to act identified in section 728(1)(a) or (b) of the same Ordinance.

Failure to disclose relevant legal principles or jurisdictional limitations to an ex parte judge constitutes serious material non-disclosure, even when such non-disclosure is legal rather than factual in nature.

principles.principle_label: Client responsibility for legal advisers' errors
principles.paraphrase_en: A lay client should not automatically bear adverse consequences for errors made by their legal advisers, particularly when those errors are legal rather than factual.
An injunction should only be granted when it is necessary, and its scope must be proportionate to the specific conduct it seeks to address.

relationships.target_case_name: Sanju Environmental Protection (Hong Kong) Ltd v Wang Lishan
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that courts have wide jurisdiction to grant prohibitory or mandatory injunctions.

candidates.case_name: TECHNIC STAR UNIVERSAL LTD v. TINY LOVE LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Richard Khaw SC in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A party providing discovery at a very late stage must furnish the court and the opposing side with comprehensive and precise information covering: the reasons for the delayed disclosure; the origin and creators of the documents; their relevance to the issues in the case; and the availability of the document creators for cross-examination at trial.

The duty to supply goods of satisfactory quality under the Sales of Goods Ordinance is strict. It is not a valid defence for the seller to argue that they exercised all possible care or that they relied on an assurance from their own supplier.

Where purchase orders have been placed, a party commits an anticipatory breach of contract by indicating, for any reason, that the orders cannot be performed. This demonstrates an intention not to produce the goods, which the other party can then accept as a repudiation.

A claimant in an action for conversion or detinue must properly plead and prove their title to sue, specifically their possessory title with an immediate right to possession of the goods. The absence of such a plea is fatal to the claim.
A party commits a repudiatory breach of contract by instructing the other party to withhold the shipment of goods under existing purchase orders, thereby preventing performance.

relationships.target_case_name: Hong Lok School Limited & Anor v. Chow Sai Yiu & Anor
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principles set out in Hong Lok School regarding the information required from a litigant giving late discovery.

relationships.target_case_name: Young & Marten Ltd v McManus Childs Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this House of Lords authority to support the principle that specifying a particular manufacturer does not exclude the implied warranty of satisfactory quality.

candidates.case_name: HUI CHUN PING v. HUI KAU MO
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Lord Hoffmann NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: The limitation rule that time does not run against a trustee in possession of trust property applies only to express or consensual trusts (category 1), not to constructive trusts arising from fraud or breach of duty (category 2).

The fiction that a fiduciary holds property acquired in breach of duty on constructive trust serves the specific purpose of preventing the fiduciary from profiting from the property, not for general limitation purposes.
Legal fictions and deeming provisions are created for particular purposes and should not be automatically extended to other legal contexts without justification.

principles.principle_label: No discretion in constructive trust imposition
principles.paraphrase_en: The imposition of a constructive trust on a fiduciary who acquires property through breach of duty is not a matter of judicial discretion but follows settled legal principles applied to the facts.

relationships.target_case_name: Williams v. Central Bank of Nigeria
relationships.description: The judgment follows the majority Supreme Court decision rejecting the argument that limitation periods apply equally to consensual and constructive trusts.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v. Hon Kam Wing
relationships.description: The judgment references this Hong Kong case where a bribe recipient was treated as a category 1 trustee for limitation purposes.

relationships.target_case_name: Attorney-General for Hong Kong v. Reid
relationships.description: The judgment discusses this Privy Council decision that established an employer's proprietary interest in bribes received by an employee.

relationships.target_case_name: Gwembe Valley Development Co Ltd v. Koshy (No 3)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the distinction from this English case between category 1 and category 2 trustees for limitation purposes.
The judgment expresses doubt about dicta suggesting judicial discretion in imposing constructive trusts, stating they were wrong.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG KA SHING
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton
candidates.topic_label: Dangerous Driving and Road Traffic Offences

principles.paraphrase_en: When imposing multiple sentences, the court must consider the overall combined sentence to ensure it is just and fair, and may order sentences to be served partly consecutively.

principles.principle_label: Mandatory disqualification for dangerous driving offenses
principles.paraphrase_en: For a first conviction of causing death by dangerous driving, there is a mandatory driving disqualification of at least 5 years, and for causing grievous bodily harm by dangerous driving, at least 2 years, unless special reasons exist to reduce or avoid disqualification.

principles.principle_label: Special reasons exception to mandatory orders
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may only order a shorter disqualification period or no disqualification for specified dangerous driving offenses if special reasons are established.

Upon conviction for causing death or grievous bodily harm by dangerous driving, the court must order the offender to attend and complete a driving improvement course, unless special reasons justify not making such an order.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG YEE KI
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge David Cheung in Court
candidates.topic_label: Dangerous Driving and Road Traffic Offences

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for dangerous driving causing death
principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Appeal has adopted the sentencing guidelines from Cooksley, emphasizing that due to the serious consequences of dangerous driving causing death, a deterrent sentence is necessary.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of dangerous driving - excessive speed
principles.paraphrase_en: Driving at a speed that is excessive for the prevailing road conditions and layout, such as on a single-lane two-way road near a T-junction, can constitute dangerous driving.
Driving while the driver's attention is avoidably distracted, for example by using a mobile phone (particularly if hand-held), is an aggravating factor in dangerous driving cases.
The commission of other offences at the same time as dangerous driving, such as driving without a license, while disqualified, or without insurance, constitutes an aggravating factor.
When imposing sentence, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Poon Wing Kay
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the sentencing principle and guideline from Cooksley as adopted by the Court of Appeal in this case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI TAI WAI
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court
candidates.topic_label: Dangerous Driving and Road Traffic Offences

principles.paraphrase_en: The sentencing principles established for the offense of dangerous driving causing death are also applicable to the offense of dangerous driving causing grievous bodily harm.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Lee Yau Wing
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principle from this case to the offense of dangerous driving causing grievous bodily harm.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWOK CHING KAN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge L C Cheng in Court
candidates.topic_label: Dangerous Driving and Road Traffic Offences

judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court
candidates.topic_label: Dangerous Driving and Road Traffic Offences

candidates.topic_label: Dangerous Driving and Road Traffic Offences

candidates.case_name: SCHUTZ, GILLES YVES RENE v. HAI CLOUD EXCHANGE (HK) LTD
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A party seeking release from the implied undertaking not to use disclosed documents for collateral purposes must demonstrate cogent and persuasive reasons. The court will only grant release in special circumstances where no injustice would be caused to the person who gave discovery.

When a party seeks to disapply Hong Kong law in favor of foreign law for a tort claim, they bear the burden of providing sufficiently cogent evidence to justify that displacement. Failure to meet this burden leads to Hong Kong law being applied.

Whether a duty of care has been breached, including the point at which a defendant gained knowledge putting them on notice of suspicious transactions, is an evidential issue that generally cannot be determined at an interim stage but requires a trial.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Florence Tsang Chiu Wing
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Florence Tsang that release from the implied undertaking requires special circumstances and absence of injustice.

candidates.case_name: ZEUS AIRCRAFT OWNER 2 LTD AND ANOTHER v. POLAR PAY LTD
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge Harold Leong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The elements of 'good faith' and 'without notice' in the bona fide purchaser defense are not to be considered separately in practice, as it is difficult to conceive of a situation where a defendant lacks notice yet still acts in bad faith.

principles.principle_label: Unifying test for bona fide purchaser
principles.paraphrase_en: The overarching test for the bona fide purchaser defense is whether, considering all facts known to the defendant, the defendant acted in a commercially unacceptable manner.

A defendant's conduct is not commercially unacceptable merely because a transaction lacks a one-to-one correspondence in fund movements, especially where an industry-wide settlement practice is known to exist and is recognized by regulatory authorities.

principles.principle_label: Industry practice as evidence of good faith
principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence that a particular business practice is common within an industry and known to the relevant regulatory authority can support a finding that a defendant acted in good faith and without notice.

principles.principle_label: Consequence of finding no commercially unacceptable conduct
principles.paraphrase_en: If a court finds no evidence that a defendant acted in a commercially unacceptable way, it must conclude on the balance of probabilities that the defendant acted in good faith and without notice, defeating claims based on the lack of such a defense.

candidates.case_name: OPERA NATIONAL DE PARIS v. SUN BON INTERNAITONAL LOGISTIC LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge B Mak in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWAN KIM FUNG
judges: Before: Hon Tam J in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing primary factor for money laundering
principles.paraphrase_en: The primary factor in sentencing for a money laundering offence is the total amount of the proceeds involved, not the personal benefit gained by the defendant or others.

For money laundering offences, established sentencing starting points exist based on the amount laundered, such as approximately 3 years for amounts between HK$1-2 million, and approximately 4 years for amounts between HK$3-6 million.
Although no formal sentencing guidelines exist for dealing with the proceeds of an indictable offence, specific factors must be considered when determining the appropriate sentence.

principles.principle_label: Discount for guilty plea and mitigating factors
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's guilty plea warrants a standard one-third discount on the sentence, and further reductions may be granted for a clear record, personal circumstances, and willingness to make restitution.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary of Justice v Wan Kwok Keung
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Wan Kwok Keung that the sentence for money laundering should primarily reflect the amount laundered.
The present judgment applies the sentencing starting point ranges and relevant sentencing factors established in Hsu Yu Yi.

candidates.case_name: CHEUNG YIU LEUNG v. CHEUNG CHONG MONEY EXCHANGE O/B CHEUNG CHONG LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Isaac Chan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The legal framework for analyzing an unjust enrichment claim involves addressing four specific questions, as established by the Court of Final Appeal.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of reasonable response to doubt
principles.paraphrase_en: When a doubt exists, the claimant must respond reasonably to that doubt. The assessment of 'reasonableness' is based on good faith and whether the response resolved the doubt, not on a negligence standard.

principles.principle_label: Inadequate investigation not waiver of inquiry
principles.paraphrase_en: An investigation carried out in good faith to address an initial inquiry, even if inadequate or negligent, does not necessarily constitute a waiver of inquiry or cancel the underlying mistake.

principles.principle_label: Basis for change of position defence
principles.paraphrase_en: The defence of change of position hinges on the fundamental justice or injustice of enforcing a restitutionary claim. Courts must apply principled analysis, not discretionary sympathy, to prevent the defence from becoming a case-by-case discretionary tool.

relationships.target_case_name: Shanghai Tongji Science & Technology Industrial Co Ltd v Casil Clearing Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the four-question framework for unjust enrichment claims established by this Court of Final Appeal authority.

relationships.target_case_name: Globenet Droid Ltd v Hong Kong Hang Lung Electronic Co
relationships.description: The present judgment follows and agrees with the holding that the 'reasonable response' to a doubt is assessed by good faith and resolution of the doubt, not negligence.
The judgment cites this case as an example where inadequate investigation in good faith did not amount to a waiver of inquiry.

relationships.target_case_name: Credit One Finance Ltd v Yeung Kwok Chi & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that the change of position defence requires analysis based on principle, not discretionary sympathy.

candidates.case_name: LI XIUHUA v. FT ELECTRONIC CO LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Suen SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may potentially establish a defense of ministerial receipt by demonstrating they received funds as an agent of another party, holding those funds on trust for that principal, which can negate a proprietary claim against them.

Where traceable proceeds are paid into a bank account operated as a running account with frequent withdrawals and deposits, and where there are competing equitable interests from innocent contributors, the first-in-first-out rule from Clayton's Case may apply instead of the principle of subordination to determine attribution of payments out.

relationships.description: The judgment references the first-in-first-out rule or presumption established in Clayton's Case for attributing payments out of a running bank account with mixed funds.

candidates.case_name: REMX LIMITED Plaintiff and LAE TECHNOLOGIES HONG KONG LIMiteD 1st Defendant LEGACY TRUST COMPANY LIMITED 2nd Defendant
judges: Before: Master Alexander Tang in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A person who receives assets as a trustee can still be subject to a claim for unjust enrichment; the status as trustee is not a complete bar to such a claim.

principles.principle_label: Constructive trust upon notice of termination
principles.paraphrase_en: When a party holding funds receives notice that the relationship between the payer and the intended recipient has been terminated and that the funds are to be recalled, that party holds the funds on trust for the payer and has no right to transfer them to the original intended recipient.

principles.principle_label: Quistclose trust upon failure of purpose
principles.paraphrase_en: Where money is transferred for a specific purpose and that purpose fails, the recipient holds the money on a resulting trust (Quistclose trust) for the transferor.

principles.principle_label: Agent's duty regarding funds subject to equitable interest
principles.paraphrase_en: An agent who becomes aware that funds cannot be used for their designated purpose and are subject to a trust claim or equitable interest of another party cannot deal with those funds in a manner inconsistent with that interest.

In determining the defendant's enrichment for a restitution claim, the court must consider not just the amount received but also any liabilities the defendant incurred as a consequence of receiving the money.

relationships.target_case_name: Skandinaviska Enskilda Banken AB (Publ) v Conway
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Privy Council authority for the principle that a trustee can be sued for unjust enrichment and for the approach to measuring enrichment.

relationships.target_case_name: Jeremy Stone Consultants Ltd v. National Westminster Bank plc
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English High Court case as judicial authority recognizing defenses like good faith change of position and ministerial receipt in unjust enrichment claims.

candidates.case_name: LI CHUNG HONG ERIC v. TUNG CHUN WAI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kent Yee in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Trading cryptocurrency with a well-known and commonplace platform at the material time does not, by itself, constitute commercially unacceptable behavior or sharp practice.
A finding of bad faith requires damaging evidence and cannot be based merely on a lack of care or failure to consider risks with the benefit of hindsight.

principles.principle_label: Defense of bona fide purchaser is fact-specific
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether the defense of being a bona fide purchaser for value without notice is established must be decided on the specific facts of each individual case.

relationships.target_case_name: Leung Lily v Fan Kwan Wang
relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes the cited case by emphasizing that the defense of a bona fide purchaser must be decided on its own specific facts.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. RAHAYU NIKE
judges: Before: HH Judge A N Tse Ching in Open Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offenses, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is appropriate and not excessive, considering all the circumstances of the case.
Sentences for offenses occurring on different dates and involving different victims or distinct criminal acts should technically be ordered to run consecutively.

principles.principle_label: Aggravation for multiple offenses in short period
principles.paraphrase_en: Committing multiple offenses within a short timeframe, such as one month, is an aggravating feature that must be reflected in the sentence.

relationships.description: The judgment references this case as potentially helpful guidance regarding the handling of charges related to money transfer arrangements.

candidates.case_name: 安徽海牛進出口貿易有限公司 v. SMART STAR HONOR LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Master James Kwan in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who has already filed a defence cannot rely on an unpleaded defence to resist an application for summary judgment.
Enrichment at the expense of another is generally established by direct transfer, but can also be proved if the defendant's benefit is traceable to the plaintiff's asset.

principles.principle_label: Trial requirement for regulatory compliance assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether a party acted in a commercially acceptable way for a licensed operator, for purposes of determining good faith, requires exploration at trial and cannot be determined solely on affirmation evidence.

principles.principle_label: Standard for triable issue on bona fide purchaser defence
principles.paraphrase_en: A court cannot conclude that no triable issue exists regarding a bona fide purchaser for value without notice defence unless it can be properly determined at trial after full discovery and oral evidence.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the general principle laid down in Kaefer regarding the prohibition of relying on unpleaded defences in summary judgment applications.

relationships.target_case_name: Lipkin Gorman (A Firm) v Karpnale Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the House of Lords' principle on establishing enrichment at the expense of another through direct transfer or traceable assets.

candidates.case_name: RE NERICO BROTHERS LTD
judges: Before: Hon Linda Chan J in Court

candidates.case_name: OPERA NATIONAL DE PARIS v. SUN BON INTERNATIONAL LOGISTIC LTD

candidates.case_name: JUMBO FAIR LTD AND OTHERS v. PACM SERIES 235 LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kenneth Wong in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: When exercising discretion on costs, the court must consider all relevant circumstances, including those listed in Order 62 rule 5 of the Rules of the High Court, to determine a fair and just outcome.
cited_statutes: Rules of the High Court O.62 r.5

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may order costs to be paid on a full indemnity basis when it is fair and just in light of all circumstances, even if a party's conduct does not rise to the level of being spurious.
If a tender is found invalid, the party making that tender should not recover the portion of costs attributable to that invalid tender, even if the invalidity does not imply bad faith.
For a tender to be valid, the mortgagor must produce sufficient evidence to demonstrate that the tender sum was readily available at the time the tender was made.

candidates.case_name: CHEN YUNG NGAI KENNETH AND ANOTHER v. OLDHAM, LI & NIE (A Firm)
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may release a party from the implied undertaking not to use disclosed documents for collateral purposes, but the applicant must show cogent and persuasive reasons. Release is only granted in special circumstances where it would not cause injustice to the disclosing party.

Not all documents in a solicitor's file belong to the client. Certain documents, such as the solicitor's own books of account and certain instructions or authorities sent during the retainer, belong to the solicitor.

principles.principle_label: Distinction between open court and chambers
principles.paraphrase_en: Hong Kong law maintains a distinction between hearings in open court and hearings in chambers, with specific statutory provisions governing each.
For certain legal purposes, such as establishing a connection for discovery, it is not necessary to make an actual finding of nomineeship; a prima facie case to that effect is sufficient.

The torts of champerty and maintenance are concerned with officious intermeddling in litigation and do not apply where the funder has a legitimate common interest in the outcome of the litigation that justifies supporting it.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Slade v Abbhi as being based on a different rule (CPR 31.22) that refers to 'a hearing held in public' rather than 'open court'.
The judgment applies the principle from Unruh v Seeberger that champerty and maintenance require officious intermeddling and exclude cases with a legitimate common interest.

candidates.case_name: TAI SHING LEE VICTOR t/a LUCKY RENMINBI EXCHANGE FOREIGN CURRENCY CO v. WU KA YU JENNIE
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Chu VP and Anthony Chan J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish that a party acted as an agent, evidence must show they held themselves out as acting for and on behalf of the principal in the specific transactions, not merely that the principal existed at the time.
In the absence of direct documentary proof on a critical issue, a judge is entitled to draw inferences from available contemporaneous documents to establish relevant facts.

principles.principle_label: Exception to counsel's conduct binding party
principles.paraphrase_en: In criminal cases, a party may not be bound by their counsel's conduct if flagrant incompetence deprives them of a fair trial, but this principle is generally not applied in civil disputes.

relationships.target_case_name: Progetto Jewellery Co Ltd v Lau Chiu Ying
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition regarding exceptions to a party being bound by their counsel's conduct.
The judgment cites this case as the authority for the exception to the general rule on counsel's conduct, as referenced in Progetto Jewellery.
